dialogue.french: Bonjour Jeanne, comment allez- vous ?
dialogue.english: Hello Jeanne, how are you (how go- you)?

dialogue.french: Bien, et vous ?
dialogue.english: Well, and you?

dialogue.french: Ca va très bien, merci.
dialogue.english: (It goes) Very well, thanks.

dialogue.french: Je vous présente ma fille, Chloé.
dialogue.english: Let me introduce (I present you) my daughter, Chloe.
dialogue.pronunciation: zhuh voo prayzaeh't ma feeyy kloh- ay

dialogue.french: Bonjour, Chloé. Comment ca va ?
dialogue.english: Hello, Chloe. How are you (it goes)?

notes.content: In most French words, the final consonant is silent. For example, vous sounds like [voo]. And comment is generally pronounced [kohmaeh']- as in line 5. But if the following word starts with a vowel, then the normally silent final consonant of the first word is pronounced and "linked" to the second word: comment_allez [kohmaeh'-talay]. This link is called a liaison. Listen to the difference in the pronunciation of comment in sentences 1 and 5. We indicate the liaison with an underline _ between the two words.
bonjour literally means good (bon) day (jour). It can be used as the equivalent of good morning but also as a formal way of saying hello throughout the day until around 6 pm.

allez comes from the irregular verb aller, to go, the verb used to enquire about someone's health or well- being (instead of to be in English: How are you?). Allez is the form used with the formal address for you (vous) when speaking to people other than close friends or family.
va is the third- person singular (he/she/it) of aller.
presenter means to present but, in this context, to introduce. Je vous présente is the equivalent of May I introduce / I would like to introduce, etc.

Comment ça va ? How are you doing? is equivalent to Comment allez- vous ?, but slightly more familiar. Here, Jeanne (the female equivalent of the masculine forename Jean) is talking to her friend's daughter, so she uses a less formal phrase.

translate.question: Bonjour, comment allez-vous ?
answers: Hello, how are you?
Good morning, how are you?

translate.question: Ça va très bien.
answers: I'm very well.
It goes very well.

translate.question: Comment ça va, Jean ?
answers: How are you, Jean?

translate.question: Je vous présente ma fille.
answers: Let me introduce my daughter.

translate.question: Chloé va très bien.
answers: Chloe is very well.

fill.question: How are you?  ........ allez-vous ?
fill.full_answer: Comment allez-vous ?

fill.question: Very well, thank you. Ça . très bien, . . . . . .
fill.full_answer: Ça va très bien, merci.

fill.question: Let me introduce Jeanne and Chloé. Je vous . . . . . . . Jeanne . . Chloé.
fill.full_answer: Je vous présente Jeanne et Chloé.

fill.question: Well, and you? . . . et vous ?
fill.full_answer: Bien et vous ?

fill.full_answer: Ma première leçon.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: French has two forms of address: formal and familiar. We'll start by learning the honorific, or formal, mode, marked by the pronoun vous, because this is the one you are most likely to encounter when visiting France and interacting with people you don't know. (In other French- speaking countries, such as Canada, the informal address, marked by tu, is used more frequently.) Which form to use in which circumstances is a much debated issue, but we've adopted the rule of thumb that tu is strictly for family and friends.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: Congratulations! In a short space of time, and without too much effort, you've learned some important - and useful - words and phrases. Remember that the best way to make progress is to learn a little every day. That's the secret of the Assimil method!

extras.title: Numbers
extras.content: un [ah']

dialogue.french: Monsieur, madame, vous désirez ?
dialogue.english: Sir, Madam, [what] would you like (you desire)?

dialogue.french: Deux cafés et deux croissants, s'il vous plaît.
dialogue.english: Two coffees and two croissants, please (if it pleases you).
dialogue.pronunciation: du kafay ay du khwashohr seelvoolpay

dialogue.french: Non, je préfère une tartine beurrée pour le petit-déjeuner.
dialogue.english: No, I prefer a slice of buttered bread for (the) breakfast.
dialogue.pronunciation: noh oeh fet zhuh prayfehr ewn tarteen buhray poor luh petee dayzhuhnay

dialogue.french: Donc, deux_expressos, un croissant et une tartine ?
dialogue.english: So, two espressos, one croissant and one slice of buttered bread?
dialogue.pronunciation: doh'k duhz- expresso ah' khwashohr ay ewn tarteen

dialogue.french: Oui, c'est ça. Le croissant est pour moi et la tartine pour elle.
dialogue.english: Yes, that's right (this-is it). The croissant is for me and the bread for her.
dialogue.pronunciation: wee say sa luh khwashohr ay poor mwah ay la tarteen poor el

notes.content: Remember that the final s of a noun is generally not pronounced, so the plural form often sounds exactly the same as the singular. Likewise, the final x of deux and the final z of désirez are silent: [duh] and [dayzeeray].
Note the liaison: deux_expressos becomes a single sound. The x is pronounced as a [z]: [duhz- expresso].
Notice that est, is, and et, and, are pronounced in exactly the same way.
un café is both a beverage (a coffee) and the place where you drink it (a café). If you order un café in France, you will get a shot of espresso without milk, also called un expresso or un express.
Although s'il vous plaît ("if it pleases you") may seem very formal, it isn't. It just means please.

une tartine beurrée (or simply une tartine) is a slice of buttered bread, a popular breakfast alternative to a croissant. Beurré is the adjective formed from le beurre, butter. In this sentence, it 'agrees' with the noun une tartine, which is feminine, so we add another - e: beurée. More on the question of agreement later.
le déjeuner means lunch, so le petit- déjeuner (literally "small lunch") is breakfast. Logical!

All French nouns have a gender: they are either masculine or feminine. A masculine noun is indicated by the articles un/le, a/the, and a feminine noun by une/la. Whenever you learn a new noun, make sure you learn its gender. Note that French does not have a separate word for one, so un café can mean a coffee or one coffee, depending on the context.

translate.question: Je préfère un croissant pour le petit-déjeuner.
answers: I prefer a croissant for breakfast.

translate.question: Un café, s'il vous plaît.
answers: One/A coffee, please.

translate.question: Oui, c'est ça.
answers: Yes, that's right.

translate.question: La tartine est pour elle.
answers: The slice of bread is for her.

translate.question: Et l'expresso est pour moi.
answers: And the espresso is for me.

fill.question: One/A coffee and a/one slice of buttered bread. . cafe et ... tartine
fill.full_answer: Un café et une tartine

fill.question: The croissant and the slice of bread. croissant et .. tartine.
fill.full_answer: Le croissant et la tartine.

fill.question: Two croissants, please. croissants,
fill.full_answer: Deux croissants, s'il vous plaît

fill.question: No, in fact, I prefer an espresso.  ... , ... , ... , je ... ... un expresso.
fill.full_answer: Non, en fait, je préfère un expresso.

fill.question: Good morning, madam and sir. Bonjour, ... ... ...
fill.full_answer: Bonjour, madame et monsieur

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: France is a huge country - the largest in western Europe- with an incredibly varied landscape ranging from the rugged granite cliffs of Bretagne, Brittany, to the lush olive groves of southern France. We will learn more about the French regions as we progress: today we've met a Breton and a woman from the area known as le Midi, another word for the south of France. (If you arrive by train in the Belgian capital, Brussels, one of the two main stations is Bruxelles- Midi, so- called because it is in the south of the city.)

extras.title: Numbers
extras.content: trois [trwah]

dialogue.french: Je me présente : je m'appelle 'Sophie Mercier.
dialogue.english: [Let me] introduce myself (I introduce me): I am (I call- myself) Sophie Mercier.
dialogue.pronunciation: zhuh muh prayzoh't zhuh mahpel sohfe mayhrsay

dialogue.french: Et voici mon ami Loïc Le Gall. Il est breton.
dialogue.english: And here is my friend Loic Le Gall. He is Breton.
dialogue.pronunciation: ay wwahsee moh'- amee lo- eek luh gal eel ay bruhoh'

dialogue.french: Et vous, êtes- vous bretonne aussi ?
dialogue.english: And you, are you Breton, too?
dialogue.pronunciation: ay voo et voo bruhton ohsee

dialogue.french: Pas du tout, je suis de Nîmes, dans le Midi de la France.
dialogue.english: Not at all, I am from Nîmes, in the south of France.
dialogue.pronunciation: pah dew too: zhuh swee duh neem doeh' luh meedee duh la froeh's

dialogue.french: Mais maintenant nous sommes parisiens.
dialogue.english: But now we are Parisians.

notes.content: Most final consonants are not pronounced. That is why the second r in Mercier is silent: [mayhrsryay].
Note the liaison: mon_ami is pronounced [moh"amee].

appeler, to call. Je m'appelle literally means "I call myself," but is equivalent to My name's ... French can sometimes seem very formal (s'il vous plait for please, see Note 3 Lesson 2), but you'll soon get used to it.

We saw in the last lesson that you can ask a question simply by raising the intonation of an affirmative phrase. Here is a more formal - but very simple - way of forming a question with the verb être (to be): you simply switch the verb and pronoun Vous êtes Breton ? ⇒ Êtes- vous Breton ?
Remember that every noun is either masculine or feminine. But some nouns, particularly those describing men and women, have two forms: bretonne is the feminine form of breton.
The first person pronoun je, I, does not take an initial capital unless it is the first word of the sentence.
Now you know the main forms of être: je suis, il est, vous êtes, nous sommes, I am, he is, you are, we are.

extras.content: trois [trwah]

dialogue.french: Pardon, est- ce que 2 vous_avez l'heure ?
dialogue.english: Excuse me (pardon), do you have the time (hour)?
dialogue.pronunciation: pardon' es kuh vooz- avay luhr

dialogue.french: Je n'ai pas 3 de montre mais je pense qu'il 4 est midi 5 dix.
dialogue.english: I don't have a watch, but I think (that) it is ten past noon (noon ten).
dialogue.pronunciation: zhuh nay pa duh moh"truh may zhuh poeh"s keel ay meedee dees

dialogue.french: Mais comment le 6 savez- vous ?
dialogue.english: But how do you know that (how it you- know)?

dialogue.french: Parce que j'ai rendez- vous avec ma femme à midi ...
dialogue.english: Because I'm meeting (I have an appointment with) my wife at noon ...
dialogue.pronunciation: parskuh zhay roeh"dayvoo avek ma fam ah meedee

dialogue.french: ... et elle a toujours dix minutes de retard !
dialogue.english: ... and she is (has) always ten minutes (of) late!
dialogue.pronunciation: ay el ah toozhoor dee meenewt duh ruhtar

notes.content: Le final - z of avez and savez is silent. The same rule applies to rendez in the next sentence.
In line 2, dix is pronounced [dees] because it comes at the end of the sentence. But in 5, it is followed by another word, so the final x is silent [dee meenewt].
Remember that the final - s is (almost) never pronounced.
L'heure (it's feminine) literally means the hour, but we also use the word when asking the time. Avez- vous l'heure ?, Do you have (i.e. can you tell me) the time?
Another very common way of asking a question is to put est- ce que (literally, "is it that") before the verb. So Avez- vous l'heure ? and Est- ce que vous avez l'heure ? have the same meaning.
To form the negative, you need two words: ne (or n' before a vowel or an h) directly before the verb, and pas directly after it: Je n'ai pas l'heure, I don't have the time.
French does not have a neutral pronoun such at it. You have to use either il or elle, depending on the gender of the noun. In neutral contexts, such as when you are telling the time, always use il.
We saw in the last lesson that le Midi is the south of France. The word also means noon ("midday").
Here, the article le means it or that. Notice that it is placed before the verb, not afterwards, as in English.

translate.question: Est- ce que vous avez l'heure ?
answers: Do you have the time?

translate.question: J'ai rendez- vous avec ma fille.
answers: I'm meeting my daughter.

translate.question: Ma femme n'a pas de montre.
answers: My wife does not have a watch.

translate.question: Il a toujours dix minutes de retard.
answers: He's always ten minutes late.

translate.question: Je pense qu'il est midi.
answers: I think it's noon.

dialogue.pronunciation: exkewzay mwah zhuh shersh luh maytrob 1 sah meeshel

dialogue.english: Sorry, madam, I'm not from here.
dialogue.pronunciation: dayzohlay madam zhuh nuh swee pa deesee

dialogue.english: But wait a minute, I have a map of the city.
dialogue.pronunciation: may atoerday ewn meenewt zhay ah ploer du la veel

dialogue.english: Here [we are]: cross the boulevard and turn (to) left at the fountain.
dialogue.pronunciation: wahlah traversay luh booluhvar ay toomay ah gohsh ah lah foh'ten

dialogue.french: Merci beaucoup pour votre aide !
dialogue.english: Thanks very much (a lot) for your help!

notes.content: French does not have a separate verb form to express a present continuous action (e.g. I am looking). Instead, you use the simple form (I look), which makes life much easier!

le Metropolitain, known universally as le métro, is the underground railway system (subway) of major cities such as Paris, Marseille or Lyon. The word is also used to refer to a specific station: le métro Châtelet, the Châtelet metro station, as well as to the train itself. (You can also ask for une station de métro, a metro station.)

As we will see throughout this course, a substantial portion of English vocabulary is derived from French, and, in many cases, the imported words or expressions are quite formal. For instance, while desole is the root of desolate and desolation, it is an ordinary, everyday word equivalent to the Germanic- origin sorry: je suis desole, I'm sorry.

You will notice that French people use madame and monsieur a lot when addressing strangers. This is not as formal as it might sound in English, which lacks a convenient, polite way of catching someone's attention.
une ville, a city or a town. French makes no difference between the two.

voilà is the companion word to voici (lesson 3 line 2). In principle, we use voici to designate something or someone located close to us (like here is/are) and voilà for something farther away (there is/are). But in practice, we use voilà in both instances. For instance, Voilà le métro, Here's the metro station / train. We can also use it at the beginning of a sentence to present something or provide information. We'll see voilà and voici again in different contexts in the coming weeks.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: The Saint- Michel district of Paris is synonymous with the Quartier latin, or Latin Quarter (so- called because it is home to the Sorbonne University, where classes were originally taught in Latin). The Place Saint- Michel is a central meeting point, dominated by a 19th century monumental fountain. Leading off it is the Boulevard Saint- Michel, famed for its bookstores and cafés.

dialogue.english: Where are your bags (your luggage)?

notes.content: bonsoir, good evening, is the formal greeting used after 6 pm. (See Lesson 1 note 1).
Remember that the definite article I' replaces le or la in front of a word beginning with a vowel or an h. Un hôtel is masculine.
le nom, the name. The word generally refers to a person's surname, the proper term of which is le nom de famille, family name. Le prénom (literally "pre- name") is the term for the first name.

grand is an adjective meaning big or large. Adjectives 'agree' with the gender of the noun they qualify, so we say un grand lit, a large bed, but une grande chambre. You see now why learning noun genders is so important.

Here's another type of agreement: pronouns also need to change depending on whether the noun is plural or singular. Vos is the plural of votre: votre nom, your name, but vos bagages, your bags. We'll learn more about agreements later on.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: When you start learning a language, you often get the feeling that people are speaking at breakneck speed! In many cases, this is because several words are run together to form a single sound. In French, this is often due to the liaison, which can make four words such as nous avons une chambre sound like two - or even one: [nooz- ahvoh'zewn sheeh'bruh]. Remember that we do exactly the same thing in English (I want to be a star - > [eyewonabeeva- star]). You'll soon get used to listening out for phrases rather than individual words.

notes.content: Gender: This is possibly the biggest difficulty facing English speakers when they start learning French. Every noun is either masculine or feminine. Masculine nouns take the articles le, the, and un, a, an, feminine nouns take la and une. There is no neuter pronoun, like it in English. There are some basic rules for determining the right gender: the words for woman, wife, grandmother, etc. are feminine (we already know une femme and une fille), while those for man, boy, etc. are masculine. So far, so logical. But why is coffee (le café) masculine and a slice of bread (une tartine) feminine? The simple answer is: because! In fact there are some patterns, which you will learn to recognise. But basically you will have to rely on your memory. We suggest you start by writing down every noun you learn, together with both articles (definite and indefinite), under one of two columns. Here's a short list to help you get started: Masculine nouns: un/le boulevard, un/le café, un/le croissant, un/l'hôtel, un/le lit, un/le métro, un/le plan. Feminine nouns: une/la chambre, une/la tartine, une/la fille, une/la fontaine, une/l'heure, une/la minute, une/la montre, une/la nuit, une/la salle de bains. One of the reasons why it is so important to remember genders is that adjectives 'agree' with the noun they describe, and therefore have both a masculine and a feminine form (we saw this briefly in lesson 6: un grand lit / une grande chambre). We'll learn more about agreements very soon.

Verbs: French verbs are divided into three groups, depending on the ending of the infinitive. (Unlike English infinitives, which are formed with to, French infinitives consist of a single word). By far the largest group comprises verbs ending in - er. We have already seen quite a few of them, including appeler, to call, chercher, to look for, désirer, to want, penser, to think, présenter, to introduce, tourner, to turn, and traverser, to cross. As in English, French verb endings change depending on the 'person' doing the action (think, but she thinks). Let's see what we have learned already: je pense, I think, vous pensez, you [formal] think, and nous pensons, we think. As you can see, there are more forms than in English, which generally has just two in the present tense, but you will soon get the hang of them. Remember, too, that there are no continuous verb forms in French, so je cherche means both I look for and I am looking for. We have also seen some parts of a key verb, être, to be, which is irregular. Remember: je suis, I am, il/elle est, he/she/it is, and vous êtes, you are. This verb is very important because it is used as an auxiliary, as we will see later on. Another major irregular verb is aller, to go: je vais, I go, il/elle va, he/she goes, nous allons, we go, vous allez, you go, ils/elles vont, they go. Both être and aller are used as auxiliaries. Lastly, remember that you need two words, ne (n' before a vowel) and pas, on either side of a verb to form a negative: Je n'ai pas l'heure, I don't have the time; Il n'est pas breton, He's not Breton. That's enough for the time being. But just think what you're already capable of saying in French after just six lessons!

The Alphabet: As in English, each letter of the French alphabet has a name, which we use when spelling words aloud. Here is a table to help you pronounce the letter names. a[ah] j[zhee] b[bay] k[kah] c[say] l[el] d[day] m[em] e[uh] n[en] f[ef] o[oh] g[zhay] p[pay] h[ash] qbetween [koo] and [kew] i[ee] r[er] s[ess] w[doobluh-vay] t[lay] x[eeks] u[ew] y[ee grek] v[vay] z[zed]. A couple of the letters are unusual. For instance, y is i grec or Greek i (which is what the Romans used to call it) and w is double v instead of double u' (w is rarely used in French). When spelling a word or name with a double letter, for example, Bailly, you can either repeat the letter (l, l) or say double I' (as in English). Lastly, remember that the pronoun je, I, does not take a capital letter unless it is at the beginning of a sentence.

Vocabulary: French is a Romance language, and most of its vocabulary comes from Latin, which can make it sound rather formal or 'starchy' to an English speaker. But this is not the case. It's simply that there are very few Anglo- Saxon words in French. Whereas English often has pairs of words, such as kingly and regal, that have similar meanings but slight nuances, French has only one. You'll soon get past the impression of formality. French and English share some of the same roots, so many words are identical. But beware of what we call false friends' - words that look the same in both languages but have different meanings. For example, we saw the word retard, which means late. (Although our word retard has the same etymology, the meaning is certainly not the same!) However, you may recognise retard as the root of our word tardy, so there are definite links. Lastly, in these early stages, be very careful of French words that look the same as their English counterparts but that are pronounced differently. A classic example is Jean, which is pronounced [zəehr] and is a man's name. It may be useful to underline these tricky words or note them down, just to remind yourself.

extras.title: Review dialogue
extras.content: 1 - Hello Chloe, how are you? 2 - Very well, thanks. 3 And you? 4 - Very well. Do you have the time? 5 - Sorry, I don't have a watch. 6 But I think it's noon. 7 - How do you know? 8 - Because I have a meeting with my daughter 9 and she's always late. 10 - Thanks very much for your help. Don't worry: after only a couple of weeks of regular practice all these new things - pronunciations, word orders, genders and so on - will be almost second nature!

dialogue.french: Bonjour mademoiselle, est- ce que votre père est à la maison ?
dialogue.english: Good morning (Good- day), miss. Is your father at home (Is it that your father is at the house)?

dialogue.french: Non, monsieur ; il est au bureau aujourd'hui
dialogue.english: No, sir; he is at the office today.

dialogue.french: À quelle heure est- ce qu'il rentre après le travail ?
dialogue.english: (At) what time (hour) [does] he come back after (the) work?

dialogue.french: Oh, pas avant huit heures normalement.
dialogue.english: Oh, not before 8 o'clock (hours) normally.

dialogue.french: Vous voulez l'adresse de son bureau ?
dialogue.english: [Do] you want the address of his office?

dialogue.french: Oui, s'il vous plaît.
dialogue.english: Yes, (if you) please.

dialogue.french: Attendez un moment, je cherche mon carnet d'adresses.
dialogue.english: Wait a moment, I [am] looking for my address book.

dialogue.french: Voilà. Sept rue Marbeuf, dans le huitième.
dialogue.english: There [it is]. 7 rue (street) Marbeuf, in the 8th [district].

dialogue.french: Merci beaucoup, mademoiselle. Au revoir.
dialogue.english: Thank you very much, miss. Goodbye.

dialogue.french: De rien, monsieur. Au revoir.
dialogue.english: You're welcome (of nothing), sir. Goodbye.

notes.content: We have seen that there are fewer prepositions in French than in English. Thus à la can meanto the, at the or in the, depending on the preceding verb. We say à la if the noun is feminine: à la maison, at home and au if the noun is masculine: au bureau, at the office. Remember, all nouns are either masculine or feminine.
We know that your means day (as in bonjour, good day, or, more generally, hello). The adverb aujourd'hui means today.
Remember that the present tense, temps présent in French translates both the simple and continuous forms of our present tense: je cherche, I look for and I'm looking for.

un carnet is a common word that crops up in many guises. The basic meaning is a notebook but, if you take the Paris metro, you can buy un carnet de tickets, a book of tickets, which is cheaper than buying tickets individually. Un carnet d'adresses, an address book. (Notice that une adresse has only one "d").

beaucoup is an adverb of quantity that translates much, many (and a lot of), making no distinction between countable and uncountable nouns: beaucoup de temps, much time; beaucoup de croissants, many croissants. Merci beaucoup, Thanks very much; Thanks a lot.

translate.question: Est- ce que votre pere est a la maison aujourd'hui ?
answers: Is your father at home today?

translate.question: Il est au bureau, monsieur.
answers: He's at the office, sir.

translate.question: Voici l'adresse : il habite dans le sixieme.
answers: Here is the address: he lives in the sixth [district].

translate.question: Merci beaucoup, monsieur. - De rien, mademoiselle.
answers: Thank you very much, sir. - Don't mention it, miss.

translate.question: A quelle heure est- ce qu'il rentre ? - Pas avant sept heures.
answers: What time does he come home? - Not before 7.00.

fill.full_answer: Mon père est à la maison et ma fille est au bureau.

fill.full_answer: Vous voulez l'adresse ? Attendez, je cherche mon carnet.

fill.full_answer: Je vais au bureau et après je rentre.

fill.full_answer: Vous voulez l'adresse de mon père ? - Oui, s'il vous plaît.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: The three major cities in France (Paris, Lyon, Marseille) are divided into districts called arrondissements (from the adjective rond, round). Paris has 20 arrondissements numbered from the first, le premier, to the twentieth, le vingtième. In everyday conversation, it is usual to omit the word arrondissement after the figure. Elle habite dans le septième, She lives in the seventh [district]. In Belgium, un arrondissement is a subdivision of one of the country's ten provinces. And in Switzerland, the word refers to an administrative area in the canton of Bern.

extras.title: Numbers
extras.content: Remember to read aloud the numbers at the beginning of each lesson and at the top of each page.

dialogue.french: Ce 1 monsieur 2 s'appelle Jérôme Laforge et cette 1 dame est sa femme.
dialogue.english: This gentlemen is called (calls himself) Jerome Laforge and this lady is his wife.

dialogue.french: Ils sont 3 la mairie pour demander une nouvelle carte d'identité pour leur fils.
dialogue.english: They are at the town hall to (for) ask [for] a new identity card for their son.

dialogue.french: Quel âge a 3 cet 4 enfant ?
dialogue.english: How old is (what age has) this child?

dialogue.french: Il a huit 5 ans, monsieur.
dialogue.english: He is (has) eight years [old], sir.

dialogue.french: Et il s'appelle Laforge ? Est- ce que c'est votre enfant ?
dialogue.english: And he is called (calls himself) Laforge? Is it [he] your child?

dialogue.french: Oui, monsieur.
dialogue.english: Yes, sir.

dialogue.french: Bien. Et il habite chez 5 vous ?
dialogue.english: Fine. And he lives with (at) you?

dialogue.french: Mais évidemment, avec moi et sa mère !
dialogue.english: (But) obviously, with me and his mother.

notes.content: this or that is ce for a masculine noun and cette for a feminine noun. Ce garcon, this boy, cette femme, this woman. But if a masculine noun begins with a vowel or a silent h, we write cet cet_ami, this friend; cet_homme, this man. (cette and cet are pronounced the same). You see why it's so important to learn the gender of each and every noun!
un monsieur is used as a noun in formal French to mean a gentleman. (We saw this word used as a greeting in Lesson 2 line 1. )

Don't confuse the preposition à (at, to, etc.) with a, the third person singular of the verb avoir (to have). The pronunciation is the same but the grave accent ( ) shows the grammatical difference between the two words.

In French, you have your age, whereas in English you are your age: elle a dix ans, she is 10 years old; quel age a cet enfant ?, how old is this child? Unlike in English, we always add ans, years after the age: vingt ans, 20 years old.

chez [shay] means basically "home of": chez moi, my place; my home (it comes from the Latin word for house, casa). But chez can also mean the shop of; chez le boulanger: at the baker's. We'll see some "abstract" uses of chez later on in the course.

translate.question: Quel age a cet enfant ? - Il a neuf ans.
answers: How old is this child? - He is nine years [old].

translate.question: Ce monsieur s'appelle Jerome et cette dame s'appelle Marie.
answers: This gentleman is called Jérôme and this lady is called Marie.

translate.question: J'habite chez un ami a Lyon.
answers: I am living at a friend's in Lyon.

translate.question: Est-ce que c'est votre carte d'identite?
answers: Is this your identity card?

translate.question: Oui, evidemment !
answers: Yes, obviously!

fill.full_answer: Ce monsieur, cette dame et cet enfant sont bretons.

fill.question: I'm doing my job, that's all. Je . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . c'est tout.
fill.full_answer: Je fais mon travail, c'est tout.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: La mairie (from the personal noun un maire, a mayor) is the equivalent of a town hall. (In major cities, each arrondissement has its own mairie, and the principal administrative centre is called l'hôtel de ville). La mairie plays a vital part in community life: citizens register births, marriages and deaths there, and they also renew a carte nationale d'identité, or national identity card, (see next lesson), which has to be produced for official purposes (voting, identity checks, etc.) and, sometimes, when picking up a registered letter or parcel, etc.

dialogue.french: D'accord '. Je fais mon travail, c'est tout.
dialogue.english: OK. I'm doing my job, that's all.

dialogue.french: Est- ce que vous_avez le formulaire B- 52 ?
dialogue.english: Do you have (the) form B- 52?

dialogue.french: Oui monsieur, nous l'avons.
dialogue.english: Yes, sir, we have it (we it have).

dialogue.french: Et l'imprime A- 65 ?
dialogue.english: And the printed [form] A- 65?

dialogue.french: Ca aussi, nous l'avons.
dialogue.english: That also, we have it.

dialogue.french: Ah bon ? Mais est- ce que vous_avez son passeport 2 ?
dialogue.english: Oh really (good)? But do you have his passport?

dialogue.french: Bien sûr. Nous_avons même 3 sa 2 photo.
dialogue.english: Of course. We have even his photo.

dialogue.french: Très bien. Alors je vous fais 4 la carte. Voilà.
dialogue.english: Very well. So I'll do (am- doing) you the card. Here it is.

dialogue.french: Réglez à la caisse 5, s'il vous plaît.
dialogue.english: Pay at the cash desk, please.

dialogue.french: Zut ! 6 Je ne trouve 4 pas mon portefeuille !
dialogue.english: Damn! I can't find (not find not) my wallet!

notes.content: d'accord is used to express agreement or consent (it's also the origin of the English word accord). In everyday speech, however, many French speakers simply use the universally understood OK (or even OK, d'accord!)

Unlike English, the possessive adjective in French agrees with the gender of the object possessed, not the owner. In the previous lesson, we learned son bureau. Why son? Because le bureau is masculine. Here, we have son passeport and sa photo (une photo), both of which "belong" to the young boy. But what matters, as far as the possessive adjective is concerned, is the gender of the noun. We'll go into greater detail in Lesson 14.
même has several meanings: here it means even. Followed by que it means the same as. For the time being, simply remember this usage. Other forms will crop up later.

Notice how the present simple tense in French can express an immediate future (je vous fais, I'm doing = I will do) as well as the auxiliary can (je ne trouve pas, I don't find = I can't find). Don't worry about the rule for the time being; just remember these two examples.
regler is a formal way of saying to pay (similar to our settle up). Une caisse is a cash desk, checkout, etc.
Zut! I a mild, inoffensive expletive to express annoyance, similar to our damn, heck, etc.

new_words.english: I make / will make for you

extras.content: un [ah']

dialogue.french: Pourquoi 'allons- nous au marché ? Il est trop cher.
dialogue.english: Why are we going (go we) to the market? It's too expensive.

dialogue.french: Parce que tous 2 les placards sont vides !
dialogue.english: Because all the cupboards are empty.
dialogue.pronunciation: parskuh too lay plahkar soh' veed

dialogue.french: Nous_avons besoin de 3 tout 2 : de fruits et de légumes,
dialogue.english: We (have) need (of) everything: (of) fruit, (of) vegetables,

dialogue.french: mais aussi de pain, de fromage, de beurre, de crème, de jambon, et...
dialogue.english: but also (of) bread, (of) butter, (of) cream and...

dialogue.french: Bon, je comprends. Il n'y a rien à manger. Allons- y.
dialogue.english: OK (good), I understand. There's nothing to eat. Let's go (there).
dialogue.pronunciation: ... koh'prc' eh elnyah- ryah' ah meh'zhay aloh'zee

dialogue.french: Bonjour madame. Qu'est- ce que je peux faire pour vous ?
dialogue.english: Good morning madam. What can I do for you?

dialogue.french: Je vais acheter une livre 4 de cerises, un kilo de pommes,
dialogue.english: I am going (go) to buy a pound of cherries, a kilo of apples,

dialogue.french: trois cents grammes de champignons, s'ils ne sont pas trop chers,
dialogue.english: 300 grams of mushrooms, if they're not too expensive,

dialogue.french: quelques 5 bananes, deux kilos de pommes de terre et un peu de lait frais.
dialogue.english: a few bananas, two kilos of potatoes (apples of earth) and a little fresh milk.

notes.content: pourquoi (literally: "for- what"): why. Parce que, because, is always spelled with two words.

When used as an adjective, tout, all / every, has to "agree" in number and gender with its noun: tout le fromage, all the cheese (masculine singular); toute la journée, all day (feminine singular); tous les fruits, all the fruit (masculine plural); toutes les pommes, all the apples (feminine plural). It is also a pronoun (line 2): J'ai besoin de tout, I need everything.
avoir besoin de, to need. Note that in very formal English, we use exactly the same construction: to have need of.

une livre, a half kilo (500g, almost equivalent to one pound). To order by weight, you can also ask for un kilo or the number of grams: huit cents grammes, 800 grams. (Une livre is also used for a pound sterling.)
quelque, some / a few. It takes an 's' in the plural. Quelques champignons, a few mushrooms. Quelqu'un, somebody; quelque chose, something. Quelque part, somewhere.

new_words.english: if they are not too expensive

answers: We are going to the market because we need fruit and vegetables.

answers: What can I do for you today?

answers: She's going to take three kilos of potatoes.

translate.question: Il n'y a rien à manger à la maison.
answers: There's nothing to eat at home.

translate.question: J'ai besoin de quelques pommes et d'une livre de cerises.
answers: I need a few apples and a pound of cherries.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: There may seem to be lots of detail, but don't try to remember everything. We always repeat important points several times in different situations so that you become familiar with them automatically. Just relax and enjoy yourself! And remember, we don't try to explain everything at once.

dialogue.french: Et avec ceci ? Vous voulez des' fraises, peut- être ?
dialogue.english: And with that? [Do] you want some strawberries, perhaps?

dialogue.french: Est- ce que je peux les goûter ?
dialogue.english: Can I taste them (Is it that I can them taste)?

dialogue.french: Allez- y 2. N'hésitez pas ! Toutes mes fraises sont bio 3.
dialogue.english: Go ahead (go- there). Don't hesitate. All my strawberries are organic.

dialogue.french: Mm, c'est vrai, elles sont vraiment délicieuses. Elles sont_à combien 4 ?
dialogue.english: Mm, it's true, they're really (truly) delicious. How much are they?

dialogue.french: Le prix ? Elles ne sont pas chères 5. Seulement quinze_ euros le kilo.
dialogue.english: The price? They're not expensive. Only 15 euros a (the) kilo.

dialogue.french: Non, c'est trop cher 5. J'ai tout 6 ce que je veux pour l'instant.
dialogue.english: No, it's too expensive. I have all (everything it) that I want for the time being (the instant).

dialogue.french: Alors, ça vous fait vingt- six_ euros soixante s'il vous plaît.
dialogue.english: Right (Then), that makes (you) 26 euros 60 please.

dialogue.french: Voilà trente.
dialogue.english: Here [is] 30.

dialogue.french: Et voici votre monnaie. Bonne journée.
dialogue.english: And here's your change. [Have a] good day.

notes.content: des in this context is an indefinite article. It is often translated by some in English: Je veux des fraises, I want some strawberries, but, depending on the context, it can also be omitted (I want strawberries). We now know all three indefinite articles: un (masculine), une (feminine) and des (plural for both genders).

Allez- y is a useful expression whenever you want someone to go ahead and do something, try something out, walk in front of you, etc. It is translated by Go on / Go ahead, etc. It can also be used in the first person plural: Allons- y, Let's go and with the familiar form of you, which we will learn in due course.
bio is an shortened form of biologique (literally "biological"). It means organic. Because the adjective is idiomatic, it does not agree with the noun.

combien (de), how much / how many? Combien de kilos ?, How many kilos? Combien de beurre ?, How much butter? The phrase Ils / Elles sont à combien ? is a common, idiomatic way of asking the price of something.

As we have seen, cher - like all adjectives - agrees in number and gender with its noun. Masculine adjectives ending in "er" are changed to feminine by putting a grave accent over the "e" and adding a final, silent "e": le beurre n'est pas cher, la livre de fraises est cher; the butter isn't expensive; the pound of strawberries is expensive. When used as an adverb, cher always takes the masculine form: C'est très cher, it's very expensive. As we progress, we'll learn more about how the masculine gender takes precedence over the feminine.
tout is used as an indefinite pronoun, meaning everything. See lesson 11 note 3.

notes.content: Qu'est-ce means What is thislthat. But in everyday French, we usually add ... que c'est (literally ... that it is), sa we say qu'est-ce que c'est ... ?. There is no difference in meaning, and the answer starts with C'est ...

We know that adjectives usually come after the nouns they qualify: un téléphone portable, a mobile phone. But some are placed before the noun. That is the case with nouveau (feminine: nouvelle), new. We will learn more about these exceptions later on. (Incidentally, the French word for a phone with computer functions is... un smartphone).

pouvez and peux are respectively the second person plural and the second person singular of the verb pouvoir, can / to be able to. The first person plural is pouvons. Notice that the verb which follows pouvoir is in the infinitive: vous pouvez essayer mon téléphone, you can try my phone.

Some English verbs that are followed by a preposition (pay for, listen to, look at, etc.) are expressed with a single verb in French: écouter la musique, listen to music; payer un repas, to pay for a meal. There are no hard- and- fast rules on adding these prepositions, so the best way to remember any differences between French and English is to write them down.

marcher mea nsto walk (it's the origin of our word march), but the verb is used colloquially in the sense of to function, to work. Cette montre ne marche pas, This watch isn't working. The expression Ca marche ! is used idiomatically to indicate that the person being addressed understands or agrees. - Deux cafes s'il vous plait. - Ca marche ! - Two coffees please. - Coming up!

There are two ways of saying I know in French. We say je sais when referring to a fact or some other abstract thing, and je connais for a person or a place. Je connais sa mère, I know his/her mother. Nous ne connaissons pas cette ville, We don't know this town. I sait beaucoup de choses, He knows many things. Both verbs are irregular, and the infinitives are savoir and connaître. We'll see them again later on.

notes.content: Possessive adjectives: Look at these examples: my father, mon père; my mother, ma mère; my brother, mon frère; my sister, ma sœur. The possession word (adjective) changes if the singular noun is masculine or feminine. For the plural, the word is mes [may] irrespective of the gender: mes parents, mes enfants. For your, our and their, we have one singular form and one plural form, irrespective of gender: votre carte, your card; votre père; vos parents; notre bureau; nos amis; leur adresse; leur maison; leurs photos. (The plural s is silent unless the noun begins with a vowel; leurs amis.) Be careful: the rule for his and her (its) is slightly different from English, where we say his if the possessor is masculine and her if feminine. In French however, the words for his/her/its are adjectives and must therefore agree with the noun they qualify. So son bureau can mean: his or her office; we use son because bureau is masculine. In the same way sa mère can be his or her mother. The plural is ses: ses enfants, his or her children. There is one exception to these rules of agreement: if the qualified noun begins with a vowel, we use the masculine adjective to make pronunciation easier. e.g. *sa amie would be difficult to pronounce (rather like "a apple"); so we say son_amie and mon_amie, allowing the context to show whether the friend is male or female.

Verbs ending in -er: In the last set of lessons we have seen many different verbs with infinitives ending in -er. This is the largest category of French verbs, with literally thousands of "members:" Let's look at an example: aimer; to like I to love: j'aime, il/elle aime, nous aimons, vous aimez, ils/elles aiment (the final -ent is silent). As we mentioned in the first lesson, there is also an informal mode of address, tu, which we'll see later on. This is the pattern for all -er verbs. Do you remember these? acheter to buy, commander to order, demander to ask, écouter to listen to, envoyer to send, essayer to try, habiter to live, marcher to walk, to function (idiomatic), parler to speak, payer to pay (for), regarder to look at, rentrer to return, téléphoner to phone. Note that the past participle, seen briefly in lesson 9, is formed simply by dropping the final r of the infinitive and putting an accent on the final e: trouver → trouvé; aimer → aimé, payer → payé, etc. The pronunciation does not change.

faire and pouvoir: Now that you know aller and être, here are two more important irregular verbs: - Faire translates two English verbs: to do and to make: je fais, il/elle fait, nous faisons, vous faites, ils/elles font Qu'est que nous pouvons faire pour vous ?, What can we do for you? Je fais quelque chose à manger, I'm making something to eat. - Pouvoir also has more than one meaning, though all of them are connected to the idea of ability or possibility: can, to be able, may, etc. je peux, il/elle peut, nous pouvons, vous pouvez, ils/elles peuvent: Est-ce que vous pouvez prendre des cerises au marché s'il vous plaît ?, Can you get some cherries at the market please? Vous ne pouvez pas faire ça, You're not allowed to do that. (The noun le pouvoir means power.)

C'est: It is: We have seen two ways of saying it is: c'est and il est (or elle est with a feminine noun). There is a difference between the two. We use c'est to explain what a thing (or who a person) is: Qu'est- ce que c'est ? - C'est une photo de famille. What is it? - It's a family photo. Regardez, c'est mon cadeau, Look, that's my present. or to say: It's me - C'est moi; It's you - C'est vous; or if the noun is qualified by an adjective: C'est un bon ami, He's a good friend. The plural is ce sont. Ce sont mes bons amis, They are my good friends. Il / elle est is used to refer to a noun just mentioned: Où est le beurre ? - Il est sur la table. Where is the butter? - It's on the table. Où est votre femme ? - Elle est au marché Where is your wife? - She's at the market. We also use il est if we are talking about the weather: Il pleut, It's raining. Il fait beau, It's fine. or telling the time: Il est huit heures et demie, It's half past eight.

Adjectives: 5.1 Masculine and feminine forms: As a rule, we form the feminine singular of an adjective by adding an e to the masculine: petit (small) ⇒ petite; blond (blond / fair- haired) ⇒ blonde; fort (strong) ⇒ forte. It's important to remember that some masculine adjectives end in -e and do not therefore change when used with a feminine noun: un placard vide, an empty cupboard; un téléphone (portable), a mobile phone. But if the masculine ending is -e, we do add an e: occupé (busy) → occupée; fatigué (tired) → fatiguée. Masculine adjectives ending in -er form their feminine by replacing the ending with -ère: dernier (last) → dernière; premier (first) → première. Another group of masculine adjectives ending in -eux replace the x by -se in the feminine: heureux (happy) → heureuse; délicieux (delicious) → délicieuse. There are a couple more rules and a few irregular forms, which we will learn later on. 5.2 Before or after? We know that adjectives are generally placed after the noun they qualify but that some are placed before it. These are the words that describe beauty, age, goodness and size. Think BAGS: Beauty: beau (beautiful), joli (pretty); Age: vieux (old), jeune (young), nouveau (new); Goodness: bon (good), mauvais (bad), gentil (kind / nice), vrai (true); Size: grand (big), petit (small), haut (high / tall), gros (fat). Some of these adjectives are new to you but don't worry: we'll see them all again in the near future. There are few more that do not fall into these categories; we will point them out as we go along.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: So much for the rules. In time you will "feel" the correct usage, so don't try to do too much at once. But from now on, when you memorize the gender of a noun, try adding a simple adjective (grand or petit, for instance) and making it agree: this will help you to acquire the right reflexes very quickly.

notes.content: We know that possessive adjectives have singular, plural, masculine and feminine forms (lesson 7). So, when used in a compound word - monsieur (mon sieur), madame (ma dame), mademoiselle (ma demoiselle), respectively "my sire," "my lady," "my damsel" - they follow the usual agreement rule: monsieur → messieurs, etc. A casual way of greeting a mixed- sex group of people - often heard in cafés and bistros - is Messieurs- dames, bonjour, Good morning ladies and gentlemen. The phrase should be recognised but not used.
A number of French words are identical or very similar to English words but have different meanings. Actual means current, and the adverbial form actualement means currently NOT actually.
Remember that any verb which follows pouvoir, to be able to, is always in the infinitive.

Learning the gender of French nouns is vital, especially since some have two genders, each with a different meaning. Here's an example: le tour (masculine) is a tour whereas la tour means a tower (monument, tower block, etc.) Another example is la livre (see lesson 11 note 4), a pound (sterling, weight), and un livre, a book. Only a handful of these dual- gender nouns are used in everyday conversation, but they have to be learned! We'll look at them in greater detail at the end of the course.

We have already seen two ways to attract attention when asking a question: Pardon (lesson 4) and Excusez- moi (lesson 5). You can also use s'il vous plaît, please, in exactly the same way: S'il vous plaît, où est le métro ? Please can you tell me where the metro is?
Some nouns are singular in English but plural in French, or vice versa. A good example is les toilettes, the toilet. We'll learn more of these words as we progress.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: La place (or Place) de la Concorde is a large public square at the bottom of les Champs- Elysées (literally "Elysian Fields"), one of the main thoroughfares in Paris. It is close to le musée du Louvre (Louvre museum), one of the world's greatest art galleries that houses treasures such as La Joconde (the Mona Lisa), la Vénus de Milo (Venus of Milo) and La liberté guidant le people, ("Liberty Guiding the People," the painting by Eugène Delacroix that symbolises the French republic).

notes.content: When the masculine adjective grand is followed by a word beginning with a vowel, for example architecte, the final -d is pronounced as a t when making the liaison: [groeh'tarsheetekt].

on is the third person singular impersonal pronoun. It has the same meaning as one in English but is much less formal. In practical use, it can replace either nous (we) - On est en retard, We're late- or the passive voice: On appelle Paris "la Ville lumière", Paris is called the City of Light. We'll see this quirky pronoun in greater detail later on.

The adjective grand, big, can be used to mean great, eminent, etc. Like all adjectives, it agrees in gender and number with its noun: un grand architecte (a great architect), une grande ville (a big OR great city), ses grands magasins (its big stores), ses grandes avenues (its major avenues). See Culture Note below. Also, the adjective vieille is the irregular feminine form of vieux (old).
The second person (singular and plural) is used without a pronoun as an imperative: Regardez ! Look! The negative form is regular: Ne traversez pas ! Don't cross!

Just because an adjective ends in e does not necessarily mean that it's in the feminine form. Adjectives such as superbe, superb, facile, easy, malade, ill, and moderne, modern, are both masculine and feminine. See lesson 21.

Likewise, not all nouns ending in - e are feminine. This is particularly true with words relating to professions, such as un architecte, an architect. However, the feminine form can be formed simply by changing the article: une architecte. This is a recent development in the French language, which we'll discuss later in the course.

Some masculine adjectives ending in a consonant are changed to the feminine by doubling the last letter and adding an e: bon → bonne (good), ancien → ancienne (ancient, old). To make the plural, simply add an - s to either form. See lesson 14.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Literally, un grand magasin means a big shop, but the term usually refers to a department store. In Paris, several such stores can be found along les Grands Boulevards near the Opera. Another famous landmark in that neighbourhood is le Cafe de la Paix, which opened in the mid- 19th century and was frequented by famous composers, writers and intellectuals during La Belle Epoque, the period of peace and prosperity that preceded the first world war. Lutèce (Lutetia in Latin) is the name of the Gallo- Roman city that eventually became Paris. Today the toponym can be found in the names of cafes, restaurants, hotels and other business all over France.

notes.content: The interrogative adjective quel is the equivalent of what or which. Quel est votre nom ? What is your name. Like all adjectives, it agrees in number and gender with the associated noun (quelle heure, quelles choses). See lesson 21.
Several English words ending in - ect come from French (via Latin). To find a French equivalent, simply remove the c. Thus a project → un projet, a subject → un sujet, an object → un objet.

il y a (literally "it there has") is an invariable construction that basically means there is or there are: Il y a une exposition, There is an exhibition; Il y a deux choses, There are two things. (Be careful: when used with an expression of time, il y a it means ago: Il y a trois jours, Three days ago. We'll see this again when we learn the past tense).

avoir envie de (the root of our word envy) means to want to, to feel like: J'ai envie de voir mes cadeaux, I want to see my presents. Envie is invariable but the verb avoir has to be conjugated (see lesson 21). The construction is similar in form to avoir besoin de (see lesson 11 note 3).

devoir means both must and to have to (French makes no distinction between the two notions). The verb is irregular: je dois, tu dois, il/elle doit (all three pronounced [dwa]), nous devons ([duhvo"]), vous devez ([duhway]), ils doivent ([dwa]). Any verb immediately following devoir is in the infinitive: il doit aller..., he must/has to go to...
The 24- hour clock is used not only in official or formal settings; it is frequently found in ordinary conversation, too. See lesson 21.

Many words, especially long ones, are abbreviated in everyday usage (the process is known as apocope). Rather than une exposition or le petit- déjeuner, for example, it's common to hear une expo and le petit- déj. Although you should avoid using such familiar terms, it's important to recognise and understand them.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Contrary to what many visitors to France may think (along with many Parisians), Paris is not the only city with cultural treasures aplenty. Of course, the likes of Marseille, Lyon and Toulouse have many spectacular museums and monuments, but so do smaller towns like Lens, which houses an outpost of the Louvre, and Metz, home to part of the Paris- based Pompidou Arts Centre. One of the more original attractions can be found in the former textile capital of Roubaix: La Piscine is an art museum housed in what was once an Art Deco swimming bath, hence the name. Anyone spending time in France should wander off the beaten track to discover its rich and diverse cultural heritage, or le patrimoine culturel.

dialogue.french: Mon agenda 6 est enfin vide !
dialogue.english: My diary is (finally) empty at last!

notes.content: The -ille termination is pronounced [-eevy], more or less as in the English word tea. For example, the old Parisian fortress La Bastille is pronounced [lah basteeyy].

la journée, the day, refers to the period between dawn and dusk: toute la journée means the whole day (or daytime) rather than a specific day or moment in that day, for which we use le jour: le jour de mon mariage, the day of my wedding. A common, and useful, leave- taking expression is Bonne journée, Have a good day.

By now you are familiar with the partitive articles de, de la and des, reviewed in lesson 21. But de also means from (for example II est de Nimes, lesson 3). When talking about a period of time (from... to/till), we say de... à (de neuf heures à dix heures, from 9 until 10 o'clock) but if the following noun is preceded by le, la or les, the partitive becomes du, de la or des, respectively. For example, le lundi et le jeudi → du lundi au jeudi.
regretter, to regret, can be used in conversation when refusing something politely: Je regrette mais je ne peux pas venir demain, I'm sorry but I can't come tomorrow.

charge, the adjective and past participle of charger, to load, is one of les faux- amis, or false friends, discussed in lesson 7. Used in connection with a timetable or programme, it means to have a heavy schedule or to be very busy (i.e. "laden" with work).

un voyage has a broader scope than its English cognate, referring to travel in general (rather than just a sea voyage). It basically means a journey or a trip - Le voyage en bus est très long. The journey by bus is very long - but can also refer to travel in general: une agence de voyages, a travel agency. Be careful not to confuse une journée (see note 1) with a journey.

Other examples of faux- amis (see note 4) include un stage, a course or an internship, and la formation, training or continuing education; and un agenda (line 13), a schedule. It's important to remember these false cognates because they can have very different meanings from one language to the other.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: One of the pleasures of learning a language is discovering how common words or terms are used in culture- specific ways. Take, for example, le pont, meaning a bridge (le pont de la Concorde, for example). The word also refers to the French custom of taking an extra day off work if a public holiday falls, say, on a Tuesday or Thursday, in order to make a long weekend. The expression in this case is faire le pont ("to make the bridge"). Another culture- specific term is la rentrée, which comes from the verb rentrer, to go back in, and generally refers to the start of the new academic year (called officially la rentrée scolaire), when students go back to school. But the term is used more broadly to means any post- holiday "return," such as la rentrée politique, when parliamentarians return after the summer recess, or la rentrée littéraire, the period in September/October when new books are published ahead of the literary award season.

dialogue.english: the broom cupboard: it's free!

notes.content: The adjective beau has several meanings: handsome, beautiful, lovely, great, etc. It is also irregular: the feminine form is belle and the masculine plural is beaux. But because French does not permit a hiatus (a break between the terminal vowel sound of one word and the initial vowel of the following word), for example un "beau endroit, there is another masculine form: bel: un bel endroit. The same rule applies to nouveau/nouvelle (new): nouvel an (new year). Three other adjectives have this additional masculine form. (We have seen a similar construction with ce/cette: ce matin, cette femme, but cet après- midi). Note that une belle- famille (with a hyphen) has nothing to do with beauty: it means a family- in- law, usually consisting of une belle- mère (mother- in- law) and un beau- père (father- in- law).

combien is a useful word (lesson 12 note 4). Remember that it translates both how much and how many. One useful phrase is Ça coûte combien ? or Combien est- ce que ça coûte ? How much does it cost? (from the verb coûter, to cost).

The termination - aine can be added to a cardinal number, generally a multiple of ten or five (dix, quinze, vingt, etc.), to indicate an approximation (une dizaine, about 10, une quinzaine, about 15, une vingtaine, about 20). Note that une douzaine can mean about twelve OR, when buying eggs, a dozen (une douzaine d'œufs).

The difference between the two words for the evening, le soir et la soirée, is the same as for le matin and la matinée. But une soirée can also mean an event held in the evening, such as une soirée dansante, a dinner dance.

The adjective free has two translations: the first, seen in the previous lesson, is libre (i.e. no commitments or engagements: J'ai une soirée libre, I have a free evening); the second is gratuit (absence of cost: Le bus est gratuit ce soir, The bus is free this evening) Moreover, gratuit is the root of the English word a gratuity, something given freely as a gift.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: La Basilique du Sacré Cœur (Basilica of the Sacred Heart) is a famous late nineteenth- century church located atop la Butte Montmartre, the hill overlooking Paris. Built of white stone with a flamboyant Byzantine- style dome, it is sometimes called la grosse meringue ("the great meringue") by its detractors but it is nevertheless one of the most recognisable buildings in the capital.

notes.content: We know that au (to the) is the equivalent of a la for masculine nouns (lesson 8 note 1). Aux is the plural form for both genders: Cet été, nous allons aux États-Unis (masc.) / Je vais aux toilettes (fem.). Like all plural endings, the - x is silent [oh] unless it precedes a vowel.

Country names, like all nouns, are either masculine or feminine. Most of those ending in - e are feminine (la France, la Belgique (Belgium), l'Italie (Italy), etc.); the others are masculine. Some toponyms are plural, notably les États-Unis, the United States. There are a few exceptions, which we will see later.
Adjectives of nationality do not take an initial capital (l'ambassadeur américain, the American ambassador), but nouns of nationality do: un(e) Américain(e), an American man/woman.

The partitive articles du, de la and des give information about the quantity of the noun they precede. They are often translated by some/ any but, in some cases, can be omitted in English. Du and des are the combined forms of de le and de les, respectively (see lesson 12 note 1). Il veut du pain, de la crème et des champignons, He wants (some) bread, (some) cream and (some) mushrooms. If a singular noun following a partitive begins with a vowel (or an h), the article becomes de l', whatever the noun's gender: (de l'argent, masc.; de l'éducation, fem.). Yet another good reason for learning genders!

Despite the final - s, pays can be singular as well as plural (mon pays, my country, nos pays, our countries). The same applies to nouns ending in - x (le prix/les prix, price/prices) and - z, as we will see later on.

vrai(e), true, can also mean real (la vraie réponse, the real answer) The exclamation Ce n'est pas vrai !, It isn't true! can also be used (as in sentence 12) to mean I don't believe it!, You're kidding! In everyday usage, careless speakers tend to drop the negative particle n', but don't be tempted to imitate them!

notes.content: être, avoir and vouloir: These are three of the most common verbs in French. The first two - être and avoir- are main verbs as well as auxiliaries, just like their English counterparts to be and to have. All three are irregular (or "strong") verbs. être,to be: je suis, tu es*, il/elle est, nous sommes, vous* êtes, ils/elles sont. avoir,to have: j’ai, tu as*, il/elle a, nous avons, vous* avez, ils/elles ont. vouloir,to want: je veux, tu veux*, il/elle veut, nous voulons, vous* voulez, ils/elles veulent. *tu is the familiar form (see below). French, like many languages, has both a formal and an informal way of addressing people. The corresponding pronouns are vous (formal) and tu (informal). For the time being, we will concentrate on using vous (the verb is vouvoyer), which is always the case when talking to a stranger or a person to whom we want to show respect. Vous is also the second person plural pronoun, used when addressing more than one person. Like all verbs, the negative of vouloir is formed with two words: ne (or n' before a vowel or mute h) and pas (see lesson 7). Je ne suis pas libre demain, I'm not free tomorrow. Elle n'a pas de projets, She doesn't have any plans. Nous ne voulons pas venir, We don't want to come. The interrogative can be formed in three ways, depending on the context: - simply by raising the voice at the end of an affirmative sentence: Vous voulez venir? You want to come? This is very informal (but also very common); - by adding est-ce que (literally "is it that") before the pronoun: Est-ce que vous voulez venir? Do you want to come? (see lesson 4 note 2). This is the usual construction in everyday spoken French; - by inverting the pronoun and the verb Voulez-vous venir? Do you want to come? This is the most formal of the three constructions. These negative and interrogative forms are used for all French verbs. Choosing the right one will depend on the person speaking and the context. For the time being, go back over the previous lessons and take note of the constructions we have used.

Agreement of nouns and adjectives: We know that adjectives agree in number and gender with the nouns they qualify. Here are some basic rules for identifying noun genders (though there are quite a few exceptions). 2.1 Nouns: In many cases, the gender can be identified by its ending. - Most nouns ending in -age and -eau are masculine. For example, l'âge (age), un stage (a training course), un bureau (an office/a desk), un cadeau (a gift). Two notable exceptions are une image (a picture) and l'eau (water). Another typical masculine ending is - er: le déjeuner (lunch) or - ier, le quartier (the neighbourhood). Both these terminals are often found in the nouns for jobs or professions: un boulanger (a baker), un banquier (a banker), and so on. Finally, nouns that end in - in are generally masculine: un jardin (a garden), le matin (the morning). Possibly the most common exception is la fin (the end). - The majority of nouns ending in -ion and -te are feminine: une région (a region), l'éducation (education), la santé (health), la liberté (freedom, liberty). Two exceptions are le côté (the side) and l'avion (the plane). Likewise, the -ée most ending is usually feminine, for instance la soirée (the evening), une idée (an idea). One of the very few exceptions is un musée (a museum). There are several other "typical" endings, which we will see in lesson 28. 2.2 Adjectives: We have learned the basic rules for the agreement of adjectives, but some of them have an irregular feminine form. Here are three common ones: le lait frais (masc. fresh milk) → une fraise fraîche (fem. a fresh strawberry) - note the circumflex on the i; le vin blanc (masc. white wine) → une carte blanche (fem. a white card); un long moment (masc. a long time/moment) → une longue avenue (fem. a long avenue). To form the plural, simply add the final s. We saw in lesson 19 that a handful of the adjectives coming before a noun have two masculine forms, depending on the first letter of the following word: a vowel (or h) or a consonant. In addition to beau and nouveau, the third commonly used adjective is vieux: un vieux monument (an old monument) but un bel endroit (a pretty place or spot). Note that bel is pronounced in exactly the same way as the feminine form belle. Finally, twenty or so adjectives ending in - e are both masculine and feminine. In addition to superbe, facile, malade and moderne (lesson 16), vide (lesson 18) and honnête (lesson 20), three others worth learning are fable, weak, pauvre, poor, and triste, sad. You will also recognise confortable, comfortable (note the n), drôle, funny (the origin of droll) and sincère, sincere, genuine.

Days, months, and dates: The days of the week are: lundi Monday, mardi Tuesday, mercredi Wednesday, jeudi Thursday, vendredi Friday, samedi Saturday, dimanche Sunday. The final syllable, - di, is from the Latin dies, a day, found in the English equivalents (Monday, etc.). As for the weekend, most people in France (and Belgium) use the same word, le week- end (note the hyphen), rather than the preferred term la fin de semaine (litt. "the end of week"), which is used in Canada. The months of the year are: janvier January, février February, mars March, avril April, mai May, juin June, juillet July, août August, septembre September, octobre October, novembre November, décembre December. None of the day or month nouns take an initial capital (unless they start a sentence) and are all masculine. When giving the date, French uses a cardinal number, not an ordinal, and puts the masculine definite article before the day, not the month, as in English: le lundi dix mai, Monday the tenth of May, le jeudi vingt et un août, Thursday the twenty- first of August.

Telling the time: French has two main words for time: le temps (a measured or measurable period) and l'heure, a feminine noun that literally means the hour but is used to give the precise time. To ask the time, always use the inverted question form (never est- ce que): Quelle heure est- il ? What time is it? (Here il is the impersonal pronoun, performing the same function as it in English.) - There is no equivalent of o'clock. Simply put heures (or heure for 1 am or 1 pm) after the hour: Il est une heure, It's one o'clock, Il est quatre heures; It's four o'clock. - For minutes past the hour, use the cardinal and invert the word order: Il est trois heures dix, It's ten past three; Il est six heures vingt-cinq, It's twenty-five past six. - For minutes before the next hour, use moins (less, minus) after the cardinal: Il est une heure moins dix, It's ten to one, Il est onze heures moins vingt, It's twenty to eleven. - For the quarter-hours, we use et quart and moins le quart, placed after the word heure(s): Il est deux heures et quart, It's a quarter past two, Il est huit heures moins le quart, It's a quarter to eight. Note the difference with the English word order. - The term for half past is et demie, placed after the hour. Demi means half, and, since une heure is feminine, we add a final e (but no s): Il est neuf heures et demie, It's half past nine. - The words for midday (noon) and midnight are midi [meedee] and minuit [minwee]. - French uses the twenty-four hour clock, not only in official contexts such as appointments and travel or broadcasting schedules, but in everyday conversation, too: treize heures 1 pm, quatorze heures 2 pm, quinze heures 3 pm, seize heures 4 pm, dix-sept heures 5 pm, dix-huit heures 6 pm, dix-neuf heures 7 pm, vingt heures 8 pm, vingt et une heures 9 pm, vingt-deux heures 10 pm, vingt-trois heures 11 pm, vingt-quatre heures 12 midnight. The hour is followed simply by the number of minutes (et quart and moins le quart are not used): Il est seize heures trente, It's half past four (in the afternoon); Il est dix- neuf heures quarante, It's twenty to eight in the evening.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: You may feel that there is a lot of detail in today's revision lesson. But in fact, we are going back over things you have already learned and helping you to organise them. With the exception of noun genders, don't try to learn anything by heart for the time being. Just enjoy yourself: there are no exams to pass!

notes.content: The double-l sound is pronounced in several ways. One of the trickiest is -ill- preceded by a consonant, which it is usually pronounced [eev]. We already know fille (fieeyy) and Bastille (basteeeyy). The same applies to grille (greeeyy). But there are exceptions: mille in line 5 is [meef]. We recommend keeping a list of the various pronunciations so as not to get confused.

Most nouns ending in - eu form the plural with - x: un jeu, des jeux (game(s)), un feu, des feux (fire(s)). Another set of nouns with an irregular ending are those ending in - al. The I is deleted and replaced by - ux: un cheval, des chevaux (horse(s)), un journal, des journaux (newspaper(s)). There are a couple more of these irregular forms, which we will learn later on.

tous is the masculine plural of the adjective tout, all. Placed before a noun, it is pronounced [too], just like the singular (tous les jeux, all the games → [too lay zheu]). But if tous comes after a verb, it means all of you/them/ everyone, and is pronounced [toos]: je les aime tous, I like all of them → [zhe layzem toos]. Always check the preceding word before trying to pronounce tous!

Mais non !, literally "But no!", is an emphatic contradiction, equivalent to Of course not! or Not at all! Vous êtes malade ? - Mais non, je vais très bien ! Are you ill? - Not at all, I'm very well! Another common exclamatory expression, in line 9 and line 13, is Au fait, literally "to the fact." Placed at the beginning of a sentence, it means By the way. Au fait, quel est votre nom ? By the way, what's your name?

établis is the first person singular of établir, to establish, to draw up. It belongs to the second major group of verbs, which end in - ir and are conjugated like this: j'établis, tu établis, il/elle établit, nous établis- sons, vous établissez, ils/elles établissent. Other common verbs in this group are choisir, to choose, finir, to finish, remplir, to fill, and réfléchir, to think, reflect. (More details in the next lesson and an overview in lesson 28.)
choisissez is the second person plural and honorific singular form of choisir (see above). Note the pronunciation: [shwahzeessay].

gagner means to win: Elle gagne toujours quand elle joue, She always wins when she plays. But it also means to earn (money): Il gagne cinq mille euros par mois, He earns five thousand euros per month. Note the context whenever you come across this very common verb. Un(e) gagnant(e), a winner (male/female).

un sou is the name of an old French coin. It no longer exists, but the word still crops up in popular expressions –Je n’ai pas un sou, I don't have a penny/a bean; Il est sans le sou, He doesn't have a bean- as well as in the familiar term une machine a sous, a slot machine. (Pay attention to the context: sous is also a preposition meaning under).

notes.content: Pay attention when you come across a French word with the same spelling as an English word: the pronunciation is generally different. For example, la queue and le secret are pronounced [la kuh] and [luh suhkray], respectively.

Remember to always learn the preposition or adverb that usually follows a verb, particularly when the equivalent English verb does not have one. Here, jouer, to play, is followed by a if the object noun is feminine (jouer à la loterie, to play the lottery), au for a masculine singular noun (jouer au loto, to play the lotto) and aux for a masculine or feminine plural: jouer aux échecs, to play chess.

The negative particle pas is followed by de (or d' before a vowel) regardless of whether the following noun is singular or plural: I n'a pas de billets, He doesn't have any tickets; Elle n'a pas d'argent, She doesn't have any money.

The idiomatic adjective sympa is an abbreviated form of sympathique, equivalent to nice, pleasant, etc. Michel est une personne très sympa, Michel's a really nice person. Because the word in an abbreviation, it is invariable in the singular, but it takes a final - s in the plural: Ce sont des personnes très sympas, They're really nice people. Note that sympa is as imprecise as nice - and just as common!

autre is an indefinite adjective, like tout, that is used to give a general description: Choisissez un autre chiffre, Choose another figure. When followed by a plural noun, it is preceded by the partitive d': Choisissez d’autres chiffres, Choose other figures. Now look back at lesson 16 sentence 1, and lesson 18 sentence 5.

dire (pronounced [deer]) is an important irregular verb that translates both to say and to tell. The present tense is je dis, tu dis, il/elle dit, nous disons, vous dites, ils/elles disent. Sami dit qu’il est désolé, Sami says he’s very sorry. Dites-moi votre nom, Tell me your name. We’ll learn more about the subtle differences in construction later on.

tout à fait (“all to fact”) is a common expression of agreement or a confirmation, equivalent to Exactly or Absolutely: Êtes-vous prêt ? – Tout à fait, Are you ready? – Absolutely. It can also be used for emphasis: Je suis tout à fait d’accord, I totally agree. Remember that French does not use an auxiliary to add emphasis (I do like that game), so expressions such as tout à fait are very common.

toujours comes after the verb, in contrast to always: Nous jouons toujours aux mêmes jeux, We always play the same games. And whereas English makes a difference between always and still, toujours covers both notions: Est-ce que tu habites toujours à Toulouse ? Do you still live in Toulouse?

The terms for numbers, figures, digits and numerals are awkward to translate because the terms are not always used precisely. Thus un chiffre, which give us the English word cipher, refers to a written figure or a digit: le chiffre sept translates as the figure 7, and un numéro refers to a specific unit such as a phone number or a house number (numéro de téléphone / de maison. To complicate matters slightly, there is also the word un nombre, which we will see later on.

Second- group verbs ending in - ir are characterised by their present participle, which ends in - issant (finir → finissant, applaudir → applaudissant, réussir → réussissant, etc.): Il choisit tous les numéros finissant par huit, He chooses all the numbers finishing in eight.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Cafés are an essential feature of everyday life in France. The generic name, un café, is subdivided into several categories, notably un tabac (literally tobacco) or un bar- tabac, a café that also sells tobacco products, newspapers, phone cards, postage and tax stamps and lottery tickets. That is where this 23rd lesson takes place. As the name un café implies, the basic drink is coffee, served either as an espresso or with milk (un café crème or un café au lait).

title: Je ne vais pas bien du tout
