notes.content: More about the double- l sound (see lesson 22). If - ll- is preceded and followed by a single vowel, it is pronounced simply as an [l] (mademoiselle, [mahduhmwazel]). When preceded by two vowels, it reverts to a palatised sound (like boy in English), which we have represented here by [yy]. The pronunciation of the previous syllable depends on the vowel combination. Thus oreille and vieille are pronounced [ohrayy] and [vyayy], while feuille is pronounced [feuyy]. Listen to the difference between the vowel sounds in the last syllables of fatigué and oreille. Now play the entire audio track again, repeating aloud feuille, oreille and vieille until you feel comfortable. Then add them to your - ll- list.

qu'est ce qui is an interrogative expression referring to the subject of the sentence: Qu'est- ce qui est important pour vous ? What's important for you? Compare it with qu'est- ce que, which refers to a direct object: Qu'est- ce que vous voulez ? What do you want?

We know that aller, to go, is used to talk about someone's health (see lesson 1). Je vais bien, I'm well. The negative form, Je ne vais pas bien means I'm not well. To ask what is wrong, a doctor will say Qu'est- ce qui ne va pas ? What's not right?, or, more colloquially, What's the matter? / What's wrong ?

avoir mal ("to have bad") is an intransitive expression used to talk about pain or discomfort. It is generally followed by a la (or au/aux) and the painful body part: J'ai mal au ventre ("I have bad to the stomach"), I have a stomach ache. The word order is often different to the English form: Elle a mal a la tête, She has a headache; Il a mal aux pieds, His feet hurt. (Note that there is no distinction between ache and hurt in French.)

une fièvre, literally a fever, is used in everyday speech, generally with de, to mean a high temperature: Mon fils a de la fièvre, My son has a (high) temperature. In a technical context, however, fièvre is translated by the cognate: la fièvre jaune, yellow fever. Note that the Fahrenheit scale is not used in most French-speaking countries (including Canada): all temperatures are in Celsius. So when the doctor says, as in line 6, Vous avez trente- huit (de fievre), You have a temperature of 38°C, the equivalent in Fahrenheit would be 100.4°F.

The masculine noun I'air means the air. But the meaning of the expression avoir I'air (de) is to look like or look as if. Marc a I'air fatigue, Marc looks tired. Elle a I'air d'une femme très sympa, She looks like very a nice woman.

The direct object pronouns are me, me, te/vous, you, le/la, him/herit nous, us, and les, them. In contrast to English, they come before the verb: je les aime, I love them. Nous la voyons, We see her. Remember, though, that there are no neutral pronouns in French, so the last sentence can also mean We see it, if the pronoun is replacing a feminine noun.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Under France's health care system (le système de santé), the cost of medical care is covered by the national social security scheme (la Sécurité sociale, often shortened to la Sécu). In most cases, people are free to choose un médecin traitant, or a general practitioner. Once a medical consultation is complete, the doctor fills in une feuille de soins (literally "a care sheet"), which is forwarded - usually electronically - to the local health insurance branch. Note that un médecin means a doctor (see lesson 17), whereas the word doctor is used only as a title (le Docteur Bellier, for example) or when addressing the practitioner directly: Bonjour docteur.

notes.content: Remember that French is syllable-timed, unlike English, where stress plays a much greater role. For example: restaurant has three evenly-pronounced syllables in French: [res-toh-roeh"] but only two in English: [restront]. Try not to put too much emphasis on any one syllable in French words, however long they may be.

We know that the second person singular and plural are used in the imperative (lesson 16 note 3). The same is true of the first person plural: Déjeunons, Let's have lunch. Faisons vite, Let's make it fast. (Remember that the final - s is silent.) See lesson 18.

Most French words come from either Latin or Greek, whereas at least a third of English words are of Anglo-Saxon origin. Which means that one French word can often be translated in two ways. For example, accompanyer is either to accompany or to go/come with. For many English speakers, used to short phrasal verbs, French can sound very formal. It takes time to get used to this "double" vocabulary, so let's start now!

Don't confuse un quartier, a district or neighbourhood of a city (lesson 21) with un quart, a quarter. The noun can also be used attributively: un restaurant de quartier, a neighbourhood restaurant. (It is also used to refer to a segment of fruit or a cut of meat.)

lequel (masc.), laquelle (fem.) and lesquels/lesquelles (plu.) are pronouns formed by combining the definite articles le/la/les and the interrogative adjectives quel/quelle/quels/quelles, meaning which/what. In this lesson and lesson 22, they are used as interrogatives: J'aime ces trois restaurants. - Lequel est-ce que vous préférez? I like these three restaurants. - Which one do you prefer? Nous cherchons la crêperie. - Laquelle? - We're looking for the pancake house. - Which one? We will see later how to use these compounds as pronouns.

An idiom is an expression whose meaning can't be guessed from its various parts. For example, Ca m'est égal ("It to me is equal") is equivalent to It's all the same to me or I don't mind. Similarly, the interrogative Ca vous dit? (literally: "that you speaks") means something like Do you fancy that? Remember to make a special note of idioms and expressions whenever you come across them, because the meaning is not always obvious.

The adjective droit has two meanings: right (the opposite of gauche, left) and straight. Le genou droit, the right knee; un chemin droit, a straight path. Of course, both adjectives agree with their nouns: l'épaule droite, the right shoulder; deux lignes droites, two straight lines. The expression tout droit, straight ahead, is an adverb and therefore invariable.

venir de, to come from, followed by an infinitive, is an idiomatic way to express the immediate past. It is the translation of to have just + past participle in English: Je viens de voir Elsa, I've just seen Elsa. The expression is not used in the negative and is rarely found in the interrogative.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Une brasserie is a full- service restaurant offering traditional French cuisine throughout the day, and often late into the night. The word actually means a brewery (from brasser, to brew), because brasseries once brewed their own beer. Note how the - ie suffix, which generally denotes a status or condition, is used to create nouns - most of them feminine- indicating an activity or profession: brasser → brasserie; une crêpe (a pancake) → une crêperie (a pancake house), un sandwich → une sandwicherie (a sandwich shop), etc. Similarly, many words for different kinds of businesses end in - erie: une boulangerie (a bakery), une fromagerie (a cheese shop), une crèmerie (a dairy), etc. These are just a few examples of how to expand your vocabulary from a corpus of basic words.

notes.content: The verb suivre, to follow, is the root of la suite, the continuation (i.e. that which follows). The expression Si vous voulez bien me suivre is a formal way of asking someone to follow you. In a less formal setting, the server might say Suivez- moi (Follow me) as they lead you to your table. It's important to recognise the register, or level of formality, that is being used in order to respond accordingly.

convenir is another rather formal verb, meaning to suit, to be convenient/suitable for, etc. For the time being, just remember the interrogative expression Est- ce que ça vous convient ?, Does that suit you? or, more idiomatically, Is that okay with you? And the response Ca me convient / Ca ne me convient pas, That's fine / That doesn't suit me. Remember that idiomatic phrases can be translated in different ways, depending in the context, but the basic meaning is the same.

As we know, the verb avoir is used with several expressions whose English equivalents are formed with to be (J'ai trente ans, I am thirty). Similar constructions include avoir faim, to be hungry (lesson 25), avoir soif, to be thirsty, avoir chaud, to be hot, and avoir froid, to be cold.
Remember that the present simple tense in French can express an immediate future (see lesson 10 note 4). Je vous apporte la carte, I'll bring you the menu.

We saw in note 1 that la suite, from suivre, to follow, means a continuation. But tout de suite is an idiomatic expression meaning immediately, at once. Faites-le tout de suite, Do it straight away. Note the pronunciation [foot- sweet].

In the previous lesson we learned a group of compound pronouns (lequel, etc.). The root word is the interrogative adjective quel, what or, when used discriminatively, which, and its feminine and plural forms: quel plat... ?, which dish, quelle carte... ?, which card/menu, quels légumes... ?, which vegetables... ?, quelles tables... ?, which tables... ? All four adjectives are pronounced identically [kel], unless the following noun starts with a vowel, in which case the two are liaised: quelles églises [kelz_aygleez].
le sang (pron. [soehr]), blood, is the root of the verb saigner, to bleed, and the adjective saignant (fem. saignante) which literally means bleeding but, when applied to a steak, signifies rare.

une addition, literally "an addition," also means a bill in the context of food service. L'addition, s'il vous plaît. The bill, please. A common synonym is la note (pron. [not]). The word for an invoice is une facture.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Most restaurants in France offer both une carte, a full- service menu (hence the expression manger à la carte, to eat à la carte), and un menu (literally "thin" or "slim"), a fixed- price or "slimmed- down" menu. Most menus feature un plat du jour, a dish of the day. And since the wine list describes the full selection of bottles on offer, rather than a short selection, the term is la carte (not le menu) des vins. Lastly, French restaurants include a service charge (le service) in their menu prices (service compris, service "understood," i.e. included), but it is customary to leave a tip (un pourboire, literally "for- drink") if the dining experience is satisfactory.

notes.content: Although the initial h of hotel is silent, it is a "voiceless vowel," so the rules governing liaisons apply. Thus the final s of quels runs into the o of hotel: [keizothel]. The same applies to the singular: quel hotel: [keilothel], un hotel: [ah'othel].
Be careful of the verb voler, which means to fly but also to steal. Thus un vol means either a flight or a theft, but hopefully the context will make the meaning clear!

In the transitive form, rater means to miss (a flight, an opportunity, etc.) Ne ratez pas votre avion ! Don’t miss your plane! It does not, however, have the same emotional connotation of loss or absence as the English verb. For this we use another verb, manquer, which we will see later on.

The pronouns celui (masc. sing), celle (fem. sing.), ceux (masc. plur., see lesson 23) and celles (fem. plur.) are similar to the one/the ones (or those) in English, and perform the same function of avoiding the repetition of a noun. If a deux hôtels : celui près de l'aéroport est cher. There are two hotels: the one near the airport is expensive.

un avis (note the pronunciation: [ahnavee / ahnavee]) shares the same root as the English word advice (a credit or debit advice from a bank is un avis de débit/crédit). But the most common meaning is an opinion: Donnez- nous votre avis, Give us your opinion. The word is frequently used in expressions with the possessive adjectives: à mon avis, in my opinion, à son avis, in his or her opinion, etc. The plural is the same as the singular: Les avis sont partagés, Opinions are divided.

As we saw in lesson 15 note 6, some nouns are singular in English but plural in French, and vice versa. The collective masculine noun l'équipement means equipment, but, when used in the plural, les équipements has a much broader meaning, similar to facilities or amenities.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Mince alors ! Like every language, French has many euphemisms that stand in for vulgarisms and swear words. This is especially true of exclamations and expletives. We already know Zut (see lesson 10). Now meet mince, an adjective meaning thin or slender. Used as a polite expletive, however, it replaces a much stronger word, merde, equivalent in meaning to (but slightly less offensive than) shit. The reason for learning this kind of vocabulary is that many native speakers use such words on a daily basis, so it is important to be aware of them. However, we strongly recommend that you avoid using them, at least until you feel totally comfortable in French.

dialogue.french: André joue à la loterie chaque semaine pour devenir très riche.
dialogue.english: Andre plays the lottery every week to become very rich.

dialogue.english: Now I'm hungry and thirsty: let's go and have lunch together.

dialogue.french: Je connais une bonne pizzéria et aussi un restaurant breton dans le quartier : lequel préférez-vous ?
dialogue.english: I know a good pizzeria and also a Breton restaurant in the neighbourhood: which do you prefer?

dialogue.french: Ça m'est égal. Disons celui qui ne me donne pas mal au ventre.
dialogue.english: I don't mind. Let's say the one that doesn't give me a stomach ache.

dialogue.french: Je suis tout à fait d'accord avec vous.
dialogue.english: I absolutely agree with you.

notes.content: The second group of verbs: -ir: Most verbs terminating in - ir form the second largest group of regular verbs, after the - er group seen in lesson 7. The conjugation endings are added to the stem, obtained by dropping the infinitive ending. Here are two very common - ir verbs: choisir, to choose: je choisis, tu* choisis, il/elle** choisit, nous choisissons, vous choisissez, ils/elles choisissent. finir, to finish: je finis, tu finis, il/elle finit, nous finissons, vous finissez, ils/elles finissent. * We will start using the familiar form, le tutoement, in the dialogues from lesson 29 onwards. ** The third person singular is also used with the third person singular impersonal pronoun on. This is true for all verbs. As always, the final consonant (or double consonant) is silent. Note also that the nous and vous forms have an extra syllable (je chois, nous choisissons; tu finis, vous finissez). The negative is formed in the usual way, with ne...pas: je ne finis pas, vous ne finissez pas, etc. The interrogatives follow the same pattern (rising intonation, est ce que, inversion) as for - er verbs: Vous finissez votre cafe ?; Est- ce que vous finissez votre cafe ?; Finissez- vous votre cafe ? (Remember that the inverted interrogative is very formal and rarely used in everyday speech.) The distinguishing feature of regular second group verbs is a present participle ending in - issant (finissant, finishing, etc.). This is important to remember because some - ir verbs actually belong to the third group. We will discuss these later.

Direct object pronouns: Here is the full table of direct object pronouns: me* me, te* you, le / la* him/her/it, nous us, vous you, les them. * The final vowel is elided if the next word in the sentence starts with a vowel. The main difference with English is that these pronouns come before the verb, not after it. Je le veux, I want it. Nous les achetons a l'aeroport, We buy them at the airport. Je l'ecoute tous les jours, I listen to him/her/it every day. Because of the elision, it is important to know the context of the sentence (in our example, je l'ecoute, the direct object pronoun could refer to a proper or personal noun that is feminine (la radio, the radio, la chanteuse, the singer) or masculine (le bruit, the noise, le guide, the guide). Finally, in a construction with an auxiliary like pouvoir or vouloir followed by an infinitive, the pronoun is placed between the two: Je peux le voir, I can see him/it. Nous voulons vous aider, We want to help you.

Interrogative adjectives and pronouns: 3.1 Quel...? Quel can be used with a noun, in which case it serves as an adjective, or as a pronoun to replace a noun: quel (masc. sing.), quelle (fem. sing.), quels (masc. plu.), quelles (fem. plu.) - what/which. Quel âge a votre enfant ? How old is your child? Quelle heure est- il ? What time is it? Quels sont vos projets ? What are your plans? Quelles expositions sont intéressantes ? Which exhibitions are interesting? Of course, the final plural - s is silent unless quels/quelles comes before a word starting with a vowel quels_enfants [kelzohr'fochr], quelles_expositions [kelzekspozeessyohr] or with an h: quels_hôtels [kelzohtel]. 3.2 Lequel...? Quel is used to ask questions about a large group of things or people, but if the group is smaller or the choice relates to a predefined group, the adjective is combined with the definite article: lequel (masc. sing.), laquelle (fem. sing.), lesquels (masc. plu.), lesquelles (fem. plu.) - which one(s). Lequel de ces hôtels préférez- vous ? Which of these hôtels do you prefer? Laquelle des deux montres voulez- vous ? Which of these two watches do you want? Prendre des comprimés, d'accord, mais lesquels ? Take pills, okay, but which ones? J'ai des grandes et des petites bananes. - Lesquelles sont les moins chères ? I have small and large bananas. - Which are the least expensive?

Demonstrative pronouns: celui: This family of pronouns is used to refer to something that has been mentioned previously, thus avoiding repetition: celui (masc. sing.), celle (fem. sing.), ceux (masc. plu.), celles (fem. plu.) - the one(s), those. Quel chemin prendre : celui de droite ou celui de gauche ? Which path to take, the one on the right or the one on the left? Les deux chambres sont chères, mais celle du premier étage n'est pas très confortable, Both rooms are expensive but the one on the first floor isn't very comfortable. Et celles du rez- de- chaussée sont petites. And the ones on the ground floor are small. Les menus des crêperies sont moins chers que ceux des brasseries. The menus at pancake houses are cheaper than those at brasseries.

Avoir instead of "to be": Many expressions describing an emotional or physical state are formed with an auxiliary and a descriptive word. Whereas the majority of such expressions in English use to be and an adjective (I'm hungry), French uses avoir and a noun (J'ai faim). Here are some more examples: avoir faim: -Avez-vous faim? to be hungry; avoir soif: -J'ai très soif. to be thirsty; avoir chaud: -Avez-vous chaud? to be hot; avoir froid: -Il n'a pas froid. to be cold; avoir raison: -Vous avez raison! to be right; avoir tort: -Ils n'ont pas tort. to be wrong; avoir peur: -Je n'ai pas peur. to be afraid; avoir sommeil: -Elle a sommeil. to be sleepy; avoir besoin (de): -Nous n'avons pas besoin de vous. to need; avoir de la chance: -Ils ont vraiment de la chance! to be lucky. Since the descriptive word in all these expressions is a noun, not an adjective, there is no need for agreement in gender or number: J'ai raison, Vous avez raison.

Numbers and figures: cardinals: Let's revise the cardinal numbers, which you have assimilated naturally by reading them at the foot of each page. (If you've forgotten to do so, read on and then go back to the beginning of the book!) un1, deux2, trois3, quatre4, cinq5, six6, sept7, huit8, neuf9, dix10, onze11, douze12, treize13, quatorze14, quinze15, seize16, dix-sept17, dix-huit18, dix-neuf19, vingt20, trente30, quarante40, cinquante50, soixante60, soixante-dix70, quatre-vingts80, quatre-vingt-dix90, cent100. To form numbers above 20, use the multiple of ten followed by the second digit, e.g. 66 = soixante- six. However, this rule does not apply to 70, 80 and 90, which are expressed in base- 20 rather than base- 10: - 70 is 60 + 10 (soixante- dix), so soixante et onze (71), soixante- douze (72), soixante- treize (73), soixante- quatorze (74), soixante- quinze (75), soixante- seize (76), soixante- dix- sept (77), soixante- dix- huit (78), soixante- dix- neuf (79). – 80 is 60+20: quatre-vingts, then quatre-vingt-un (81), quatre- vingt-deux (82), quatre-vingt-trois (83), etc. – 90 is 80+10: quatre-vingt-dix, then quatre-vingt-onze (91), quatre-vingt-douze (92), quatre-vingt-treize (93), etc. This is the system used in France. It takes some practice at first, but soon becomes second nature. By contrast, Belgium and Switzerland have slightly different –and arguably more logical– systems. Both use septante for 70 (thus septante et un for 71, etc.) and nonante for 90 (nonante et un, etc.). In addition, Switzerland often uses huitante for 80. The rules governing the terminal - s on vingt, in particular, the use of hyphens are quite complex, so for the time being concentrate on learning the numbers and acquiring the automatic reflex of seeing them, then saying them aloud. Lastly, there is only one word for 0 in French: zero, which translates both zero and nought, as well as the oh used in phone numbers.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: You'll notice that, for the time being, the English translations are fairly literal. This is deliberate because we want you to concentrate on the French syntax, rather than systematically comparing the two languages. As we progress, however, the translations will become more idiomatic. And now that you are familiar with the basic rules on liaisons, we will no longer mark them with the undertie (vous_avez) which has been used solely to get you used to this particular feature of spoken French.

dialogue.french: Salut', Louis! Comment vas- tu 2 ?
dialogue.english: Hi, Louis! How are you?

dialogue.french: Je vais très bien. Et toi 2, Manon ? La forme ?
dialogue.english: I'm very well. And you, Manon? On [good] form?

dialogue.french: Ça va, merci. Qu'est- ce que tu fais ici ?
dialogue.english: Fine thanks. What are you doing here?

dialogue.french: Mes courses 4. Je cherche une pâtisserie pour acheter une tarte ou un gâteau.
dialogue.english: My shopping. I'm looking for a cake shop to buy a tart or a cake.

dialogue.french: Les Mercier 5 viennent dîner ce soir et je sais que Clément adore les pâtisseries 6.
dialogue.english: The Merciers are coming [to] dinner this evening and I know that Clement loves pastries.

dialogue.french: Je dois aussi trouver un cadeau à leur offrir 7 parce que c'est leur anniversaire 4 de mariage samedi prochain.
dialogue.english: I also have to find a present to give (offer) them because it's their wedding anniversary next Saturday.

dialogue.french: Mais je n'ai pas d'idées précises. Je ne connais 8 pas leurs goûts.
dialogue.english: But I don't have any precise ideas. I don't know their tastes.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que tu sais 8 où je peux acheter quelque chose d'original ?
dialogue.english: Do you know where I can buy something (of) original?

dialogue.french: Il y a un centre commercial là-bas, près du bureau de poste. Tu le vois ?
dialogue.english: There's a shopping (commercial) centre over there, near (to) the post office.

dialogue.french: Dedans, tu as une superbe pâtisserie 6 qui vend des tartes délicieuses pour le dessert,
dialogue.english: Inside, there's (you have) a superb cake shop that sells delicious tarts for (the) dessert

dialogue.french: et des gâteaux 9 délicieux avec des noix, des morceaux 9 de chocolat et de la chantilly.
dialogue.english: and delicious cakes with walnuts, bits of chocolate and whipped cream.

dialogue.french: Excellente idée ! Je sais que nos amis sont très gourmands.
dialogue.english: Excellent idea! I know that our friends are very fond of food.

dialogue.french: Et pour le cadeau 9, qu'est-ce que tu penses d'un tableau 9 ?
dialogue.english: And for the present, what do you think of a painting?

dialogue.french: Plutôt 10 des tisanes, pour faciliter la digestion !
dialogue.english: Herb teas instead, to help [with] digestion.

notes.content: As always, the final s marking the familiar form of the verb (vas, fais, sais, vois, pense, etc.) is silent: [vah], [fay], [say], [vwah], [poeh's], etc.
Salut ! (the origin of salute) is a familiar greeting, equivalent to Hil! As a singular noun, un salut is either a wave (of the hand) or a nod (of the head).
tu is the familiar pronoun for the singular you. It is used when talking to friends, family members and children. Toi, in this context, is the object pronoun.

la forme, meaning the form or shape, is also used to refer to fitness or health, as we saw in lesson 24. Louis est en forme aujourd'hui, Louis is on top form today. The polite expression J'espere que tu es en forme ? I hope you're doing well? is often shortened to just La forme ?, with a rising intonation, in familiar contexts.

You should be familiar with the two meanings of les courses: errands, shopping (lesson 13) and horse races (lesson 22). Similarly, un anniversaire can mean both a birthday and an anniversary. Remember to take special note of these homonyms, which can often be disconcerting.
Unlike English, French surnames do not take an - s in the plural: Monsieur et Madame Mercier → Les Mercier. Of course, this does not affect the pronunciation.
une patisserie refers to both a pastry (the circumflex replacing the letter s) and the shop in which it is bought.
offrir, to offer, can also mean to give when used in the context of a gift. J'offre souvent des cadeaux à mes enfants, I often give my children presents. See also note 9.
See lesson 13 note 6 and lesson 35 for more about the difference between savoir and connaître.

Singular nouns ending in - eau form their plural with a (silent) final x. They include un cadeau (a gift), un bureau (an office), un morceau (a piece, a bit, a slice), un gateau (a cake) and un tableau (a painting).

The adverb plutot, rather, instead, is a useful way of qualifying a statement or expressing a preference: je n'aime pas les tartes, je prefere plutot les gateaux, I don't like tarts, I prefer cakes instead.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Chantilly is a town to the north of Paris, renowned for its chateau and gardens, an extensive library of illuminated manuscripts, and world- famous race horse training stables. But it is arguably better known for the delicacy that bears its name: la crème de Chantilly - or, more commonly la crème chantilly (whipped cream with vanilla and sugar) - as well as for la dentelle de Chantilly (handmade bobbin lace).

title: J'ai un truc à te demander...

dialogue.french: Excuse- moi un instant, Juliette. Je dois répondre au téléphone.
dialogue.english: Excuse me a second (instant), Juliette. I have to answer the phone.

dialogue.french: Allô ? 2 Ah, c'est toi, Antoine. Je t'entends 3 très mal.
dialogue.english: Hello? Ah, it's you Antoine. I can't hear you very well (I hear you very badly).

dialogue.french: Reste où tu es. Je vais descendre au premier étage.

dialogue.french: Tu sais que nous sommes au milieu de la campagne, sans antenne de téléphone !
dialogue.english: You know that we're in the middle of the countryside, without [a] telephone aerial!

dialogue.french: Voilà : est-ce que tu entends ma voix ? Je mets 5 le haut-parleur.
dialogue.english: There: can (is it that) you hear my voice? I'm putting [on] the loud-(high) speaker.

dialogue.french: Oui, c'est bien. J'ai un truc 1 à te demander, Stéphane.
dialogue.english: Yes, that's fine. I've got something (a thing) to ask you, Stephane.

dialogue.french: Vas-y 6. Qu'est-ce que je peux faire pour toi ?
dialogue.english: Go ahead (go there). What can I do for you?

dialogue.french: Peux-tu me prêter ton aspirateur ? J'en ai besoin pour nettoyer ma piscine.
dialogue.english: Can you lend me your vacuum cleaner? I need it to clean my swimming pool.

dialogue.french: Le magasin dans notre village n'en vend pas.
dialogue.english: The shop in our village doesn't (of them) sell [them].

dialogue.french: Je promets de ne pas le perdre ! Tu peux me faire confiance.
dialogue.english: I promise not to lose it! You can trust me (make me confidence).

dialogue.french: Écoute, je veux bien 7 mais ça ne dépend pas entièrement de moi.
dialogue.english: Listen, I'm fine with that (want well) but it doesn't depend entirely on me.

dialogue.french: Je vais demander à Juliette si elle en a besoin.
dialogue.english: I'll go [and] ask (to) Juliette if she (of it) needs [it].

dialogue.french: Bon, c'est d'accord si tu nous le rends avant la fin de la semaine.
dialogue.english: Well (good), she agrees (it's okay) if you return it [to] us before the end of the week.

dialogue.french: Merci. Je te le rends après- demain. À tout à l'heure 8.
dialogue.english: Thanks. I [will] give it back [to] you [the day] after tomorrow. See you later (to all to the hour).

notes.content: un truc is a familiar but very common placeholder word meaning a thing, a whatname, etc.

Allô comes from the English hello and, in France, is used only when answering the phone (in Quebec, it is also a greeting, like Hi!). The word is often found in the business names of emergency call- out services, such as Allo Serrurier for a 24/7 locksmith.

entendre, to hear, along with vendre (lesson 29) and several other verbs in this lesson, provide an introduction to Group Three, which comprises three types of endings divided into sub- categories. Verbs in the first sub- category end in - re. The inflexions are j'entends, tu entends, il/elle entend, nous entendons, vous entendez, ils/elles entendent. Note that the - s is usually dropped from the second person singular in the imperative form (but see note 5).

quitter, to leave, is the origin of the verb to quit. Quittez l’autoroute à la sortie trente, Leave the motorway at exit 30. On the telephone, Ne quittez pas, or Ne quitte pas in the familiar form, means Please hold (“Don’t leave”).

mettre, to put, is an important verb (je mets, tu mets, il/elle met, nous mettons, vous mettez, ils mettent). Unlike English, it does not usually take a preposition (mettre un manteau, to put on a coat).
Vas-y is the familiar form of Allez-y, Go ahead (lesson 12 note 2). The terminal- s is retained in the imperative before the adverbial pronouns y and en to make pronunciation easier (Ivahzee).
vouloir bien (literally "to wish well") is used in polite expressions like Je veux bien or Si vous voulez / tu veux bien to express acceptance (similar to the formal If you will in English).

Used with the present or future tenses, the adverbial phrase à tout à l'heure ("to all to the hour") is equivalent to later on or, as a valediction, See you later: Je reviens dans quelques minutes. - A tout a l'heure. I'll be back in a few minutes. - See you later. It can also be used with a past tense, meaning earlier. (The phrase is probably the origin of the English slang term toodle- oo.)

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: For English- speakers, le tutoiement can be difficult to master at first (When to use it? With whom?). But in most cases, the context will help you: if someone addresses you in the tu form, you can respond likewise. If you're not sure which form of address (tu or vous) is appropriate, always opt for the formal one. For more information, see lesson 35.

extras.title: Numbers
extras.content: cent trente-deux .132

notes.content: Here's another -ll- word. As usual after a double vowel, the phoneme is pronounced [yy]: [booyy-wahr]. It's worth repeating the word several times aloud in order to get to grips with the pronunciation.

We came across puis- je in the exercises in lesson 26. The term, derived from the old interrogative form of je peux, I can, is now used only in polite, formal questions, like may/might in English: Puis- je avoir votre nom ? Might I have your name? Que puis- je faire pour vous ? What may I do for you? (Puis- je cannot be used in questions formed with est- ce que...?, which, by definition, are less formal than the inverted interrogative.)

The adverbial pronoun en was introduced in the previous lesson. It has several functions, notably to avoid repetition of a noun. En is frequently used with the partitives du/de la/des: J'ai du café. Vous en voulez ? / have coffee. Do you want some? See also note 5.

The demonstrative pronouns celui / celle / ceux / celles (lesson 28) can't be used on their own (Les tartes ?) 'aime celles aux fraises). But they can take the suffixes - ci (here, an abbreviation of ici, which has no cedilla under the c) and - la when indicating (or "demonstrating") a specific thing that is either close to or distant from the speaker: Tu veux celui- ci ? Do you want this one (here)? Nous preferons celles- la, We prefer those ones (over there).

hors (pronounced [or]) means out of, away from. Followed by de, it is a prepositional phrase: Il est hors du bureau pour le moment, He's out of the office for the moment. It can also be used in set expressions such as hors de question (see lesson 18), hors de prix ("out of price"), overpriced or outrageously expensive, as well as the culinary term hors d'œuvre, ("out of work"), a starter.

en is also a preposition meaning in. It can be used in phrases indicating either a state - en or, in/af gold- or a situation, en vacances, on holiday. For the time being, simply memorise the phrases you come across. We'll learn more as we go along.

We know that I'argent means money, but the other meaning is silver. In this case, it is preceded by de (la medaille d'argent, the silver medal) or en: Ma nouvelle montre est en argent, My new watch is in silver.

Don't confuse the preposition sur, on, with the adjective sur, safe, secure. Both words (and the feminine adjective sure) are pronounced identically, but the circumflex on the second word makes it easier to tell the difference between them.
tant pis is a set expression meaning Too bad or Never mind. (Pis a literary form of pire, worse/worst, which we will see shortly).

Je vous en prie is the standard response to Merci, Thank you. The familiar (tu) form is Je t'en prie. Although the literal translation, "I pray you," is very formal (and rarely used) in English, the French expression - both the vous and the tu forms- is standard.

notes.content: The ligature œ has no equivalent sound in English. To pronounce un œil, an eye, use the 'open-u' sound found in the verb 'to purr' and add a long 'y' sound (represented by [yy]) at the end: [uh-yy]. Listen carefully to the recording and try to imitate the speaker.
As we know, si means if, but it can also be an intensifier, like so. Ma collègue est si fatiguée qu'elle ne peut plus continuer, My colleague is so tired that she can't continue.

This lesson looks at the second sub- category of third- group verbs; those ending in -oir: devoir, to have to / must; pouvoir, to be able to / can; recevoir, to receive; savoir, to know; and voir, to see. You have already come across most of them, but now you can see that they are conjugated in the same way.

Just like une dizaine, approximately ten (lesson 19 note 3), une centaine means about a hundred. The indefinite plural can be used in the same way as hundreds in English: je reçois des centaines de mails d'une dizaine de collègues, I receive hundreds of emails from ten or so colleagues.

The indefinite pronouns chacun (masculine) and chacune (feminine) are compounds of chaque plus un/une: each one. Je regarde chacun des mails en détail, I look at each email (or, alternatively, each one of these emails) in detail. For emphasis, the pronoun can be placed at the end of the phrase, as in English: Ces bouillories coûtent cent euros chacune, These kettles costs 100 euros each. Don't confuse chacun(e) with chaque, which is followed by a noun: Chaque appareil coûte cent euros, Each device costs 100 euros. Obviously, neither chacun(e) nor chaque has a plural form.

The negative verb form is usually constructed with ne...pas. In some cases, however, pas can be replaced with an adverb, notably jamais and plus. Il ne répond jamais au téléphone, He never answers the phone; Elle ne répond plus à mes messages, She is no longer answering my messages.

Another very common irregular plural is the masculine noun for the eye, l'œil, which becomes les yeux (pronounced yeu). Note also the idiom un coup d'œil, a glance, used with the verb jeter, to throw. (The expression donner un coup de main, used in line 11, is equivalent to the English idiom to give someone a hand.)

The indirect object pronouns are me, te, lui, nous, vous and leur. Max nous donne un coup de main, Max is giving us a hand. Peux- tu me prêter dix euros? Can you lend me ten euros? They are identical to the direct object pronouns, except for the third persons singular and plural: lui and leur. Note that the singular lui can mean him or her. Je lui donne un cadeau: I'm giving him OR her a present. Last, don't confuse the pronoun leur with the possessive adjective, spelled identically: leur travail, their work.
The plural of un mal, a pain, an ache, is des maux. See lesson 24 note 3.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: The use of "franglais"- English words (or words derived from English)- is a perennial hot- button issue. Here's a first example. The word email was imported directly into French decades ago as a noun (but not a verb). Efforts have since been made to find a home- grown equivalent - most notably un courriel, a portmanteau of le courrier électronique (electronic mail). However, many French speakers still prefer email or even mail. A similar debate has been waged over the terms for a smartphone (un smartphone) and tablet (une tablette). If you're unsure whether a word is accepted in French, check le site de l'Académie française, the official guardian of the language. (And don't forget le tacos, which is masculine and singular, despite the s!)

notes.content: For internet addresses, French uses the nouns corresponding to the grammatical function of punctuation signs. Hence a dot is called un point [pwah"], which means a full stop (or period).
Here is another example of the lexical links between French and English. Over the centuries, une garde- robe (plural garde- robes), literally "keep dress", evolved into a wardrobe.

Remember that the second person singular imperative of - er verbs has no final - s (tu entres ⇒ Entre ! you come in ⇒ Come in!). However, for all regular and most irregular - ir and - re verbs, the imperative is identical to the indicative: tu attends ⇒ Attends !
When used with the imperative, the direct object pronouns me and te become - moi and - toi: vous me donnez la clé → Donnez- moi la clé ! tu t'assiés → Assiés- toi !
We saw pas du tout, not at all, used on its own (lesson 3) as an emphatic negative. The phrase can also be used after a verb: je ne les connais pas du tout, I don't know them at all.

Here is another negative construction formed with ne and an adverb (see lesson 32 note 5), in this case rein: Je n'ai rien à faire à la maison, I have nothing to do at home; Il ne voit rien la nuit, He can't see anything at night. Remember that pas is not used in this type of construction, otherwise it would create a double negative.

This is another example of a word that can be singular in French but not in English (lesson 27 note 5): les vêtements means clothes (Ses vêtements sont chics, His/her clothes are smart) whereas un vêtement is an item of clothing. Ce vêtement est trop petit, This piece of clothing is too small. In practice, we would generally use another, more precise word in English (this coat, etc.) but un vêtement is standard French.

mille is a thousand so un millier is approximately a thousand, just like dix / une dizaine (lesson 19 note 3) and cent / une centaine (lesson 32 note 3) . It, too, has a plural form: Des milliers de gens, Thousands of people. And it's masculine, not feminine.

The compound adjective bon marché ("good market") means inexpensive, cheap (with no negative connotation). It is invariable: Voici une liste des hôtels bon marché à Bordeaux, Here's a list of inexpensive hotels in Bordeaux. A synonym is pas cher, not expensive, but in this case, the adjective agrees: des hôtels pas chers.

When used as adjectives, colours generally agree with the noun in number and gender: un manteau bleu, a blue coat, des jupes bleues, blue skirts. Of course, if the masculine adjective ends in - e, it does not change: un pull jaune, une robe jaune.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Le Bon Marche is the name of the world's first- ever grand magasin (department store), which opened in Paris in 1852. Although French uses the verbal expression faire ses courses to talk about shopping for food and other staples, the term faire du shopping is preferred when talking about browsing for and buying other goods, especially clothes. Naturally, purists dislike this import - they insist on faire des achats (to make purchases), while French- speaking Canadians say faire du magasinage- but it is largely (and unfortunately) unavoidable in everyday usage.

notes.content: The terminal -er of the masculine noun l'hiver is pronounced [air], as in cher (rather than [ay], as in aller). Don't forget that the half-vowel h is not pronounced (just like hour in English) so, with en hiver, the final n of en runs into the i of hiver: [œhneevair].
nez is pronounced [nay], like the final syllable of the second person plural of an -er verb: venez: [vuhnay].

This is final sub- category of third- group verbs which end in - ir but are irregular (unlike Group 2 verbs). They include courir, to run; dormir, to sleep; sentir, to feel; servir, to serve; ouvrir, to open; souffrir, to suffer; offrir, to offer (see lesson 29 note 7); and servir, to serve. The endings are, for example, je cours, tu cours, il/elle court, nous courons, vous courez, ils/elles courent.

Normally, the plural of nouns ending in - ou is formed by adding an s (un trou, a hole, des trous). But a handful, including un genou, a knee, un bijou, a jewel, and un caillou [kie- you], a pebble, take an - x. There are seven of these nouns in all, but these three are the most common.
rien can mean both anything or nothing (lesson 33 note 5). Note the place of this pronoun, which, unlike English, comes before the verb: Il ne peut rien faire pour toi, He can't do anything for you.

mi- is a shortened form of demi, half. It is used in a number of expressions, notably à mi- chemin ("half- path"), halfway: Sélestat est à mi- chemin entre Colmar et Strasbourg, Sélestat is halfway between Colmar and Strasbourg.

We know that the adverb y means there, as in il y a. But it can also be used to avoid repeating a noun, just like en (lesson 31 note 2). Tu connais Colmar ? - J'y habite, Do you know Colmar? - I live there (instead of J'habite à Colmar). Like rien (note 3), y comes before the verb. It can also be used in the expression y compris, including. Toute la famille est invitée, y compris ton frère, The whole family is invited, including your brother. In this case, y has no intrinsic meaning.

Here's another very common vernacular word: un copain (feminine: une copine) means a pal, a mate. Depending on the context, the term can be used for a boyfriend/girlfriend. Le copain de Noémie est un artiste qui s'appelle Florian, Noémie's boyfriend is an artist called Florian.

Like une course (lesson 29 note 4), le temps has two meanings: the time and the weather (despite the final s, the word is singular). The context should make it easy to tell the difference, but remember this mnemonic just in case: Je n'ai pas le temps pour ce mauvais temps, I don't have time for this bad weather. (Un temps can also mean a (verb) tense: Le futur simple est un temps de l'indicatif, The future simple is an indicative tense - see lesson 8 note 3).

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Located in eastern France, Alsace is a region with a distinct personality. Bordered by the Vosges mountains (les Vosges, pron. [vohzh]) to the west and the river Rhine (le Rhin, pron. [rahn]) to the east, it is part of the administrative area known as le Grand Est ("the great east"). Alsace has strong cultural ties with its immediate neighbour, Germany. It also has its own Germanic language, l'alsacien or Elsassisch, used alongside French on highway signage and street name signs, and still spoken by some Alsaciens (Alsatians). The region's capital, Strasbourg, is home to the European Parliament.

dialogue.french: Demain est l'anniversaire de ma sœur et elle organise une fête.
dialogue.english: Tomorrow is my sister's birthday and she's organising a party.

dialogue.french: Je vais acheter des cadeaux. Et je vais aussi prendre quelques gateaux.
dialogue.english: I'm going to buy some presents. And I'm also going to get a few cakes.

dialogue.french: Est- ce qu'on peut venir avec toi, s'il te plaît ?
dialogue.english: Can we come with you please?

dialogue.french: Bien sûr, mais mettez vos manteaux : il fait froid dehors.
dialogue.english: Of course, but put your coats on: it's cold outside.

dialogue.french: Tu vas à quelle pâtisserie ?
dialogue.english: Which cake shop are you going to?

dialogue.french: Celle à côté de la poste. C'est celle que je préfère.
dialogue.english: The one next to the post office. That is the one I prefer.

dialogue.french: Mais leurs gateaux sont hors de prix ! Chacun coûte au moins trente euros.
dialogue.english: But their cakes are ruinous. Each one costs at least 30 euros.

dialogue.french: Tant pis : c'est son anniversaire et je vais lui acheter un truc sympa.
dialogue.english: Never mind: it's her birthday and I'm going to buy her something nice.

dialogue.french: Voici les gateaux : je prends celui- ci, aux fraises, celui- là, au chocolat, et aussi quelques sandwichs pour notre déjeuner.
dialogue.english: Here are the cakes: I'm taking this one, with strawberries, this one, with chocolate, and also a few sandwiches for our lunch.

dialogue.french: Les sandwichs sont là- bas, monsieur. Ceux- ci sont très bons, et ceux- là aussi. En fait, tout est bon chez nous.
dialogue.english: The sandwiches are over there, sir. These ones are very good, and those ones too. In fact, everything is good at our place.

dialogue.french: Où sont les sandwichs au jambon ? Je ne les vois pas.
dialogue.english: Where are the ham sandwiches: I don't see them.

dialogue.french: Il n'y en a plus. Mais je peux en commander si vous voulez.
dialogue.english: There are none left. But I can order some if you want.

notes.content: The third group of verbs: -re, -oir and -ir: The third group is the most complex of the three, largely because the component verbs are all irregular. It can be divided into three sub- categories according to the endings: - re, -oir and -ir. 1.1 Verbs ending in -re: prendre, to take: je prends, tu prends, il/elle prend, nous prenons, vous prenez, ils/elles prennent. This sub- category includes common verbs such has attendre, to wait; mettre, to put; and boire, to drink. 1.2 Verbs ending in -oir: savoir, to know: je sais, tu sais, il/elle sait, nous savons, vous savez, ils/elles savent. Several key verbs come under this sub- category, particularly vouloir, to want; and pouvoir, to be able to. 1.3 Verbs ending in -ir: dormir, to sleep: je dors, tu dors, il/elle dort, nous dormons, vous dormez, ils/elles dorment. This sub- category, comprising irregular verbs such as sortir, to go out, souffrir, to suffer; partir, to leave (and repartir, to restart); and ouvrir, to open, is slightly disorienting because it can be confused with the second group of regular verbs. The difference can be seen in the present participle (suffering, leaving, etc.), which ends in - issant for Group 2 but - ant for Group 3. Thus, finir, to finish, and choisir, to choose, are regular Group 2 verbs with the present participle finissant and choisissant, whereas the present participle of the irregular dormir is dormant. The third group also contains one, very irregular - er verb: aller, to go. Since Group 3 verbs are, by definition, irregular, they need to be learned by heart. There are, however, some recognisable patterns, which we shall see later on.

Plural nouns: an overview: By now, you should know how to form the plural of nouns and adjectives. Here is an overview. The basic rule is simply to add a terminal (and silent) -s to the singular: un croissant, deux croissants. There are, of course, several exceptions depending on the word ending: - ail: travail → travaux, work(s) (construction, etc.); - al: journal → journaux, newspaper(s); - eu: jeu → jeux, game(s); - eau: manteau → manteaux, coat(s). Singular nouns ending -s, -x or -z do not change in the plural: un Francais → des Francais, one Frenchman, Frenchmen; une voix → des voix, a voice, voices; un nez → des nez, a nose, noses. The final letter is usually silent, but there are a couple of exceptions, notably un fils/des fils, a son/sons (listen to the dialogue in lesson 28).

y and en: The adverbial pronouns y and en have no direct equivalents in English. The meaning varies according to their function and where they are placed in the sentence. Here are some basic rules: 3.1 y is used to avoid repetition: Le musee ferme dans trois heures. On peut y aller maintenant. The museum closes in three hours. We can go there now. The noun does not have to refer to a place: J'aime mon travail et j'y pense tout le temps. I like my job and I think about it all the time. To form the negative, place the particle before the pronoun: je n'y pense pas. It's important to note that y cannot relate to a person: J'aime Annette : je pense a elle tout le temps. (The verbs in this type of sentence are generally followed by the preposition a: aller a, penser a, etc.). And of course, y is used in the phrase il y a ("it there has") to express the existence of something: Il y a beaucoup de neige cet hiver, There is a lot of snow this winter. 3.2 en replaces a word, often a verb, followed by de: En can be the equivalent of some / any: Avez- vous du lait ? Oui, j'en ai, Do you have any milk? - Yes, I have some. It always comes before the verb. While some is often dropped in this context in English (Yes, I have), en can never be omitted. It can also replace the partitive article (du / de la / des), meaning of or about it/them: Est- ce qu'elle a des enfants ? - Oui, elle en a trois. Does she have any children? - Yes, she has three [of them]. Alain connait bien l'Alsace : il en parle beaucoup. Alain knows Alsace very well: he talks about it a lot. As with y, the negative particle is placed before the pronoun: il n'en parle pas beaucoup. Idiomatically, en is used in the expressions Je vous en prie / Je t'en prie, Don't mention it. Finally, en is also a preposition, usually translated as in: Ils habitent en Bretagne, They live in Brittany. Nous sommes en vacances la semaine prochaine, We're on holiday next week.

savoir and connaître: Both verbs mean to know, but they are used differently. In essence, savoir means to know a fact or how to do something. It is usually used with a clause, introduced by que, qui, ou, quand, etc. For example, Je sais ou habite Emmanuel, I know where Emmanuel lives (note the word order, with the verb before the direct object). Used with an infinitive, savoir means the same as can: Il sait parler l'anglais et le français, He can speak English and French. By contrast, connaître means to know or be familiar with a person or a place. In a sentence, the verb is followed by a direct object noun: Je connais Emmanuel, Nous connaissons Paris. In sum, we would say Je connais Emmanuel mais je ne sais pas ou il habite, I know ("am acquainted with") Emmanuel but I don't know ("am not aware") where he lives. There are a few cases where either verb can be used in a specific context, but these are few and far between.

Indirect object pronouns: Indirect object pronouns are used to avoid repeating a noun. They represent the person on whom an action is being performed. They are: me to me, nous to us, te to you, vous to you, lui to him / her, leur to them. me and te become m' and t' when followed by a vowel. In contrast to English, indirect object pronouns in French always come before the verb. In many cases, they are used with verbs followed by a (écrire à, to write to, donner à, to give to, parler à, to speak to), replacing both the noun and the preposition: Je vais parler au directeur = Je vais lui parler. One slight complication is when the equivalent verb in English takes a direct object: to phone someone / téléphoner à quelqu'un. As a result, I'm phoning my sister is Je téléphone à ma sœur, which becomes Je lui téléphone. That's why it is so important to learn verbs together with their prepositions. Here are some examples: Dites à vos amis de venir → Dites- leur de venir. Tell your friends / Tell them to come; Il donne de l'argent à moi et ma femme → Il nous donne de l'argent. He gives money to me and my wife → He gives us money. The third person pronouns lui and leur replace both masculine and feminine nouns: Il faut demander au patron / à ma femme → Il faut lui demander. J'écris à mes copains / à mes amies → Je leur écris. The pronoun leur is invariable. Do not confuse it with the possessive adjective leur / leurs, which can agree with a plural noun (leurs amis). See lessons 14 and 29 note 7.

Le tutoiement: The "familiar" form of address no longer exists in English (thee and thou disappeared centuries ago), so mastering the equivalent in French, le tutoiement (from the pronoun tu) can be tricky at first. Here are some basic rules: - tu is used when talking with family, close friends, lovers, children and people in the same social group. Young people use tu (the verb is tutoyer) among themselves more so than older people; and you always tutoie pets, animals, God and inanimate objects that might irritate you (computer, car, etc.); - vous (le vouvoiement, vouvoyer) is the formal version, used in everyday interaction with people you don't know. It is also the plural pronoun, used collectively, even with family and friends; - using tu instead of vous outside these contexts can be seen as disrespectful or even rude. Tutoiement can even be a form of insult, for instance when used by two drivers involved in a fender- bender. In practice, however, the situations in which to use the familiar form vary according to a number of factors. For instance, staff in a big company will use vous with superiors and tu with colleagues, whereas employees in a tech firm will all use le tutoiement, regardless of rank or seniority. Similarly, an adult will address a child other than a family member as tu but the child will respond with the vous form. And members of an online forum will tutoie each other even though they have never met. So, when in doubt, stick to the simple guideline that vous should always be used with strangers and in formal situations. You may then be invited to switch to the familiar form (On peut se tutoyer). Always take the lead from the person you are interacting with. Here are the vous and tu forms of each verb group: aimer: vous aimez / tu aimes; finir: vous finissez / tu finis; mettre: vous mettez / tu mets; boire: vous buvez / tu bois; dire: vous dites / tu dis. Two very common familiar phrases are Comment vas- tu ? How are you? and Vas- y ! Go ahead! Go for it! And since tu is the familiar form, it is often used in an informal register. In spoken French, tu as (you have) is frequently shortened to t'as (and tu n'as pas sometimes becomes t'as pas). Needless to say, you should not imitate these colloquialisms but it is important to recognise them because they are commonly used by native speakers.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: You have certainly noticed that the language content of our lessons is becoming a little more idiomatic. This is deliberate because as soon as you start speaking in real- life settings, the people you interact with will rarely make an effort to confine themselves to "formal," academic French. So, without venturing into hard- core slang, we will present you with language that is as realistic as possible while still allowing you to learn at a relaxed pace. Speaking of which, don't forget to pick up the book or listen to the recordings every day, even if you only have ten minutes: C'est en forgeant qu'on devient forgeron, "It's by forging that one becomes a blacksmith," equivalent to the English proverb Practice makes perfect.

title: J'espère que je n'ai rien oublié...

dialogue.french: Ca y est '. J'ai commandé 2 le taxi. On peut partir à l'aéroport dans cinq minutes.
dialogue.english: Here we go. I've ordered the taxi. We can leave for (to) the airport in five minutes.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que tu as fermé 3 toutes les fenêtres et tous les volets ?
dialogue.english: Have you closed all the windows and all the shutters?

dialogue.french: Oui, j'ai tout 4 vérifié, même le grenier. Tout 4 est verrouillé 5.
dialogue.english: Yes, I've checked everything, even the attic. Everything is bolted.

dialogue.french: J'ai vidé le réfrigérateur et le congélateur tout à l'heure 6
dialogue.english: I've emptied the refrigerator and the freezer just now

dialogue.english: and I've closed the door of the cellar.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que tu as vidé les poubelles de la cuisine et la salle de bains ?
dialogue.english: Have you emptied the rubbish bins in the kitchen and the bathroom?

dialogue.french: Mais oui ! Et la corbeille à papier. Ne t'inquiète pas.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que tu as caché mes bagues et mes colliers ?
dialogue.english: Have you hidden my rings and my necklaces?

dialogue.french: J'ai fait tout ce que 7 tu m'as demandé.
dialogue.english: I have done everything that you asked me.

dialogue.french: Hier, j'ai fait aussi des copies de nos passeports et déposé tout sur notre coffre en ligne.
dialogue.english: Yesterday I've also made copies of our passports and deposited the copies on our online safe (trunk).

dialogue.french: J'ai demandé à notre voisine de vérifier notre boîte à lettres et de la vider de temps en temps.
dialogue.english: I've asked our [female] neighbour to check our letter box and to empty it from time to time.

dialogue.french: Moi, j'ai changé les draps, nettoyé 8 la cuisine et lavé la vaisselle.
dialogue.english: [As for] me, I've changed the sheets, cleaned the kitchen and washed the dishes.

dialogue.french: J'ai arrosé les plantes et débranché le four et tous les autres appareils électriques.
dialogue.english: I've watered the plants and unplugged the oven and all the other electrical appliances.

dialogue.french: Heureusement, j'ai pensé à tout et je n'ai rien oublié.
dialogue.english: Fortunately (happily) I've thought of everything and I've forgotten nothing.

dialogue.french: Bonjour, vos passeports et billets d'avion, s'il vous plaît.
dialogue.english: Good morning, your passports and plane tickets, please.

dialogue.french: Oh non ! J'ai laissé mon sac à main sur la table du salon !
dialogue.english: Oh no! I've left my handbag (bag at hand) on the table in (of) the living room!

notes.content: The expression Ca y est ("that there is") is useful for acknowledging that something is about to happen - Ca y est : la pluie arrive, Here we go: the rain is on its way- or has happened: Ca y est : le week- end est terminé, That's it. The weekend is over. As usual, the translation will depend on the context, but the basic idea is that of recognition. As for the pronunciation, the three words are run together in a single sound: [sayay].

This is the perfect tense -le passé composé- of verbs ending in -er. Generally formed with avoir, appropriately conjugated, and the past participle of the verb, it is used to describe an action that was completed ("perfected") in the past. Although similar in structure to the present perfect tense in English (I have ordered), it is not a one- on- one equivalent. (Some verbs form their passé compose with être, as we will see later on.)

The interrogative form of the perfect tense can be constructed, like the present, in three ways: by raising the voice, inverting the auxiliary and pronoun or, as here, with est- ce que: Est- ce que vous avez fermé la fenêtre ? Did you have you closed the window?

When used with the perfect tense, tout is usually placed as an adverb between the auxiliary and the participle (ll a tout fermé, He's closed everything). Remember that the adverb tout is invariable (ll a tout fermé, ils ont tout fermé).

fermer means to close (Le magasin est fermé, The shop is closed) but also to lock. To avoid ambiguity, to lock can also be expressed as fermer a clé ("close to key"). The noun a lock is une serrure. Doors and windows can also be secured with un verrou, a bolt, whence the verb verrouiller (vervoayy), to bolt.

We saw in lesson 30 note 8 that tout a l'heure, later on, could be used in reference to the past. In this case, it refers to an immediate or very recent event. J'ai verifie la cuisine tout a l'heure, I checked the kitchen just now / a feu minutes ago / earlier, etc. Remember that tout is used here as part of the expression, not an adverb, as in sentence 3.

The relative pronoun ce que, first seen in lesson 12, is equivalent to what / which. It functions as the direct object of a subordinate clause, often with a subject pronoun: Je mange ce que je veux, I eat what I want. Prenez ce que vous voulez, Take what you want.

nettoyer is a useful verb that means to clean or clear up, depending on the context. Like fermer, it is often followed by a noun to make the action more specific, whereas English tends to use an entirely different verb. For example, J'ai nettoyé la piscine à l'éponge, I've sponged down the swimming pool. We'll see more of these equivalent constructions (verb + noun in French vs. single verb in English) as we advance. The noun nettoyage a sec means dry cleaning.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Words can teach us a lot about a country's culture and history. This is especially true of eponyms, or words derived from the proper names of real (or imaginary) people. The word for a dustbin, une poubelle, is named for Eugène Poubelle, a 19th- century city administrator who ordered refuse containers to be installed in all Parisian buildings for public health reasons. French is well- endowed with eponyms, notably in science and mathematics, yet many ordinary words originated from people who are often forgotten. For example, un martinet is a type of whip, named for the stern disciplinarian General Jean Martinet; la nicotine is named after the philologist Jean Nicot; while la guillotine was invented by a doctor, Joseph Guillotin, as a "humanitarian" form of execution! Tracking down the etymology of words, particularly eponyms, is an excellent way to build up a vocabulary.

title: J'ai réfléchi à la question du loyer...

dialogue.french: Ca fait deux mois que je cherche un appartement à louer.
dialogue.english: I have been looking for (That makes two months that I look for) a flat to rent for two months.

dialogue.french: As- tu trouve quelque chose ?
dialogue.english: Have you found something?

dialogue.french: Pas encore. C'est plus compliqué que ça, et ce n'est pas fini !
dialogue.english: Not yet. It's more complicated than that, and it's not over (finished)!

dialogue.french: J'ai cherche d'abord dans l'arrondissement ou j'ai grandi , mais sans succès.
dialogue.english: I looked first in the neighbourhood where I grew up, but with no (without) success.

dialogue.french: Ensuite, j'ai élargi 2 mes recherches, 3 et j'ai aussi contacté un agent immobilier.
dialogue.english: Then I broadened by (my) search(es) and I also contacted an estate agent.

dialogue.french: J'ai n'a pas réussi 2 à le joindre par téléphone, mais j'ai rempli 2 le formulaire sur son site
dialogue.english: I didn't manage (succeed) to get (join) him on the (by) phone, but I filled in the form on his site

dialogue.french: et j'ai fourni 2 tous les renseignements nécessaires.
dialogue.english: and I supplied (provided) all the necessary information.

dialogue.french: Il m'a rappelée le lendemain et nous avons choisi 2 plusieurs propriétés à visiter.
dialogue.english: He called me back the next day and we chose several properties to visit.

dialogue.french: Et alors ? Tu as trouvé ton bonheur 4 ?
dialogue.english: Well? Did you find what you were looking for (your happiness)?

dialogue.french: Il y a deux apparts 5 dans mes moyens 6 : l'un est plus petit 7 que l'autre
dialogue.english: There are two flats that I can afford (in my means): one is smaller than the other

dialogue.french: mais il est aussi plus cher parce qu'il donne sur une cour intérieure très calme.
dialogue.english: but it's also more expensive because it gives onto a very calm interior courtyard.

dialogue.french: L'autre, au premier étage, est moins intéressant 7 : il est plus grand 7 mais aussi plus bruyant 7.
dialogue.english: The other, on the first floor, is less interesting: it is bigger (more big) but also noisier (more noisy).

dialogue.french: Il y en a un troisième, le plus spacieux 7, au dernier étage. Il est plus grand 7 que tous les autres
dialogue.english: There is a third one, the roomiest (most roomy), on the last floor. It's bigger than all the others

dialogue.french: et beaucoup plus joli 7, avec une énorme terrasse tout autour et rien au-dessus 8.
dialogue.english: and much prettier (more pretty), with a huge terrace all around and nothing above.

dialogue.french: Mais, malheureusement, le loyer est prohibitif. Il est plus élevé 7 que mon salaire mensuel.
dialogue.english: But, unfortunately, the rent is prohibitive. It's higher than my monthly salary.

dialogue.french: Eh oui, mon amie ! C'est difficile mais il faut vivre au-dessous 8 de ses moyens.
dialogue.english: Oh yes, my friend. It's hard (difficult) but you have (it is necessary) to live below your (one's) means.

dialogue.french: Mais je peux t'aider, peut-être.
dialogue.english: But perhaps I can help you.

dialogue.french: En effet, j'ai réfléchi 2 sérieusement à la question : est-ce que tu peux garantir mon loyer... ?
dialogue.english: Indeed, I've thought seriously about the question: can you guarantee my rent...?

notes.content: In many cases, pronouncing a liaison will depend on who is speaking. The expression pas encore is a good example. A careful speaker will join the two words, pas_encore [pahzohr'kor] whereas someone talking quickly will probably not [pah ohr'kor]. The first is more elegant; the second, more frequent.
Remember not to pronounce the h in bonheur. [boh-nuhr] (the same applies to the antonym malheur, misfortune : [mah-luhr]).

The perfect tense of Group 2 verbs (regular, ending in - ir) is usually constructed with avoir and the past participle, formed by dropping the final - r from the infinitive. For example, grandir (to grow up) → grandi: j'ai grandi, tu as grandi, il/elle a grandi, nous avons grandi, vous avez grandi, ils /elles ont grandi. Simple!

Continuing the series of nouns that can be plural in French but not in English, la recherche has two meanings: research, in which case the noun is usually singular and has a definite article - La recherche scientifique est très importante, Scientific research is very important - and, more generally, a search or an investigation, in which case, it takes an indefinite article but can also be plural: Nous allons continuer nos recherches, We are going to keep searching. For web browsing (see lesson 27), we use un moteur de recherche, a search engine.

le bonheur means happiness or good fortune. Après, trois mariages, elle a enfin trouvé le bonheur : être seule, After three marriages, she finally found happiness: being alone. The word can also be used in idiomatic expressions such as Est-ce que tu as trouvé ton bonheur ?, Have you found what you wanted or were looking for? The opposite of le bonheur is le malheur, unhappiness, misfortune. Remember this proverb: Un malheur n'arrive jamais seul, Misfortunes never come on their own, or, more colloquially, It never rains but it pours. (The h is silent in both words.)

un appartement, a flat or an apartment (notice the difference in spelling), is one of the multisyllabic words that are frequently abbreviated in spoken French (lesson 17 note 7). It becomes un apart, with the final t pronounced.

le moyen, in the singular, is the way or the means: Je connais un bon moyen d'apprendre une langue, I know a good way to learn a language. In the plural, it is equivalent to the singular English noun means, i.e. money. Nous n'avons pas les moyens d'aller en vacances cette année, We don't have the money to go on holiday this year. In this case, avoir les moyens is a good paraphrase for to afford, which has no direct equivalent in French.

By now, you should be familiar with the comparatives of superiority and inferiority, where more is translated by plus and less by moins. In both cases the conjunction than is que. Lyon est plus grand que Toulouse mais plus petit que Paris, Lyon is bigger than Toulouse but smaller than Paris. To form the superlative, simply put the definite article le or la for the singular (depending on the gender of the noun) and les for the plural before plus or moins.

dessus and dessous mean above and below / beneath, respectively. They are used in different forms, but here we see them, with au- , as adverbs. J'habite au troisième étage et ma copine habite à l'étage audessus, I live on the third floor and my girlfriend lives on the floor above. La température est deux degrés au- dessous de zéro, The temperature is two degrees below zéro. (You will have recognised the adjective sous, under). In some cases, au- dessous is replaced with en dessous, but the meaning is exactly the same.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: L'île de France - literally "The Island of France" - is the official designation of the greater Paris area, the most populous of the country's 18 regions, but is often referred to simply as la région parisienne. The toponym is used to form an adjective, francilien(ne) (referring to the Paris region) and a proper noun, un Francilien / une Francilienne, a local inhabitant. L'île de France comprises eight administrative areas, or départements, that are subdivided into two "rings," an inner one (la Petite Couronne, or "small crown") and an outer one, la Grande Couronne, "big crown"). The region is circled by a network of motorways and expressways called... La Francilienne.

title: C'est de la part de qui ?

dialogue.french: Bonjour, j'essaie de joindre Alain Bolland. Est-il au bureau aujourd'hui ?
dialogue.english: Good morning. I'm trying to reach Alain Bolland. Is he at the office today?

dialogue.french: Je ne sais pas, je vais vérifier. C'est de la part de 1 qui ?
dialogue.english: I don't know. I'll check. Who is calling (it's of the share of who)?

dialogue.french: Dites-lui que Madame Lacroix veut lui parler.
dialogue.english: Tell him that Mrs Lacroix wants to speak with him.

dialogue.french: Je l'ai rencontré au Salon de l'automobile et j'ai pris 2 sa carte de visite.
dialogue.english: I met him at the Motor Show and I took his business (visit) card.

dialogue.french: J'ai appris 2 qu'il est le nouveau directeur commercial pour l'Île de France.
dialogue.english: I learned that he is the new sales (commercial) manager for the Île de France (island of France).

dialogue.french: Il m'a dit 3 de l'appeler en début de semaine pour organiser une visite de vos locaux 4.
dialogue.english: He told me to call him at the beginning of the week to organise a visit of your offices (premises).

dialogue.french: Je vous mets en attente. Ne quittez pas je vous prie 5.
dialogue.english: I'm putting you on hold (in wait). Please hold the line (do not leave).

dialogue.french: Je regrette, mais son poste 6 ne répond pas. Il doit être à l'extérieur.
dialogue.english: I'm sorry, but his extension is not answering. He must be out of the office (at the exterior).

dialogue.french: Je ne suis pas surpris parce que nous avons plein de 7 travail en ce moment et l'agenda de Monsieur Bolland est plein 7.
dialogue.english: I'm not surprised because we have loads (full) of work at the moment and Mr Bolland's diary is full.

dialogue.french: Voulez-vous laisser un message ?
dialogue.english: Do you want to leave a message?

dialogue.french: Pouvez-vous lui demander de me recontacter plus tard dans la journée ?
dialogue.english: Can you ask him to contact me again later in the day?

dialogue.french: Entendu 8. Il a vos coordonnées 9, je présume ?
dialogue.english: Of course (heard). He has your contact details, I presume?

dialogue.french: Normalement oui, mais je vais vous donner un numéro où il peut me trouver à tout moment.
dialogue.english: He should do (normally yes) but I'm going to give you a number where he can get (find) me at any time.

dialogue.french: C'est le 07 28 91 12. Attendez, excusez-moi, c'est le 02 à la fin.
dialogue.english: It's (the) 07 28 91 12. Wait, excuse me, it's (the) 02 at the end.

dialogue.french: Si je ne réponds pas c'est parce que je suis en réunion.
dialogue.english: If I don't answer it's because I'm in a meeting.

dialogue.french: J'ai compris. Je lui laisse votre message. Au revoir et merci de votre appel.
dialogue.english: I've understood. I'll leave him your message. Goodbye and thank you for calling (your call).

dialogue.french: Un grand merci pour votre coopération.
dialogue.english: Thank you very much (a big thankyou) for your cooperation.

notes.content: Unlike in English, oo is not pronounced as a long vowel but as two separate ones: [oh- oh]. This is particularly the case with the prefix co- Thus the first syllables of coordonnées and coopération are pronounced [koh- or] and [koh- op], respectively.

une part is a piece or a slice. Voulez- vous une part de gâteau au chocolat ? Do you want a piece of chocolate cake? In a telephone conversation, however, the expression de la part de identifies the person on behalf of whom the call is being placed. The standard question is C'est de la part de qui ?, equivalent in register to Who shall I say is calling? Giving one's name is a sufficient response. The caller can also say, for example, Je l'appelle de la part de Michelle, in which case, the caller may be Michelle herself or someone calling on her behalf.

Here is the perfect tense of certain Group 3 verbs ending in -re, such as prendre, to take, and its derivatives: j'ai pris, tu as pris, il/elle a pris, nous avons pris, vous avez pris, ils/elles ont pris.

Another set of -re verbs, including dire, to say, lire, to read, and écrire, to write, form their past participle with a final (and silent) -t: j'ai dit, tu as dit, il/elle a dit, nous avons dit, vous avez dit, ils/elles ont dit. Naturally, any verbs derived from these three, such as contredire, to contradict, are conjugated in the same way.

The adjective local has the same meaning as in English (un guide local, a local guide). As a noun, however, it means premises. In contrast to English, it can be either singular or plural: un local commercial, des locaux commerciaux, commercial premises. The difference is merely a question of size, with the plural being used for a larger facility. (Remember that nouns and adjectives ending in - al form the plural with - aux.)

We are familiar with je vous en prie (see lessons 31 and 35). In very formal contexts, however, the en can be dropped, particularly if the expression appears at the end of a sentence. Entrez, je vous prie, Please come in. You are unlikely to use je vous prie (and the tu form is very rarely used); however, you will probably hear it in certain circumstances, notably on the phone.

A few nouns have two genders, each with a different meaning. One of the most common is poste. Une poste is a post office (lessons 29 and 35) but un poste means a position or job: Il quitte le poste de directeur en mai, He is leaving the position of director in May. The masculine word also means a switchboard extension in a telephone system: Le poste ne répond pas, The extension is not answering. You can see why we insist on memorising not only the meaning of nouns but also their gender!

The adjective plein (feminine pleine, pronounced [plen]) means full or busy (think: plenty): Les hôtels sont toujours pleins en été, The hotels are always full in summer. The invariable expression plein de means full of and, more broadly, lots of: Elle a plein de choses à faire, She has lots to do.

entendu is the past participle of entendre, to hear: J'ai entendu un bruit, I heard a noise. But the word can be used alone in response to a statement that you acknowledge or agree with. Venez tôt demain. - Entendu. Come early tomorrow. - Will do. Of course, the actual translation will depend on the context but, in every case, avoid the (bad) French habit of saying OK instead of Entendu!

les coordonnées, coordinates, is the geographical term for distances or numbers identifying a position. By extension, it is commonly used to refer to a person's contact details in the general sense. Thus Donnez- moi vos coordonnées can be translated as Give me your address / your contact details / your name and number, etc. If further details are needed, an adjective can be added - for instance, Donnez- moi vos coordonnées téléphoniques or bancaires if you want the person's phone number or bank details.

fill.question: What is his/her name? - I don't know. I didn't take his/her business card after the meeting. son nom - Je ne sais pas: sa après.
