extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Job titles are often hard to translate because corporate structures differ from one country to another. Also, since English is widely used in the business world, many French companies anglicise to some extent their in-house vocabulary. Thus a firm may have both un directeur and un manager, who have different positions even though the second title is the translation of the first! But in most public companies, the top job is the same: le Directeur général, or managing director (also known as the chief executive). In some companies, le DG [day-zhay] as he (or she) is often called, can also be the chair of the company, in which case the title is le Président-directeur général, ("chairman and managing director") or le PDG [pay-day-zhay]. Note that most of the nouns used in French corporate job titles are still masculine, even if the person holding the office is female.

dialogue.french: La France possède une longue tradition cinématographique, et le "septième art" est bien vivant aujourd'hui.
dialogue.english: France has (possesses) a long cinematographic tradition, and the "seventh art" is truly alive today.

dialogue.french: Malgré toutes les plateformes de diffusion 2 et tous les autres supports disponibles,
dialogue.english: Despite all the streaming (broadcasting) platforms and all the other media (supports) available,

dialogue.french: beaucoup de gens vont deux ou trois fois par semaine au cinéma.
dialogue.english: many people go twice or three times a week to the cinemas.

dialogue.french: Et les metteurs en scène 3 et comédiens français sont très appréciés par le public.
dialogue.english: And French directors (putters- on- stage) and actors are much appreciated by audiences (the public).

dialogue.french: Le choix de films est énorme - comédies, aventures, policiers, dessins animés, et cætera.
dialogue.english: The choice of movies is huge: comedies, adventures, detective films, cartoons (animated drawings), etc.
dialogue.pronunciation: ... kohmaydee avaoh'tewr pohleessyay dayssahn aneemay etsaytayrah

dialogue.english: Naturally, there are also a lot of foreign films in the "dark (obscure) rooms,"

dialogue.french: qu'on peut voir en version originale ou en version française (c'est-à-dire doublés).
dialogue.english: which one can see in [the] original [language] version or in the French version (that is to say, dubbed).

dialogue.french: Deux rendez-vous 5 annuels sont indispensables pour les cinéphiles 6 :
dialogue.english: Two annual get- togethers are indispensable for movie movers:

dialogue.french: le festival de Cannes avec sa Palme d'Or, et la soirée des Césars ;
dialogue.english: the Cannes festival with its Golden Palm, and the Césars evening;

dialogue.french: ces prix récompensent le meilleur du cinéma de l'année, notamment le meilleur film et le meilleur scénario.
dialogue.english: these prizes reward the best of the year's cinema, notably best film and best screenplay.

dialogue.french: La semaine dernière, des millions d'amateurs en ont suivi 7 la cérémonie de clôture.
dialogue.english: Last week millions of fans followed the closing ceremony.

dialogue.french: Ils ont vu 8 la maîtresse et le maître de cérémonie présenter les prix aux gagnants.
dialogue.english: They saw the hostess and the host (the mistress and the master of ceremony) present the awards to the winners.

dialogue.french: Et maintenant, le moment que nous attendons tous : le César du meilleur metteur en scène.
dialogue.english: And now the moment that we've all been waiting for: the César for the best director.

dialogue.french: Cette année il est attribué à... Michel Bonnaud pour "Commencez sans moi".
dialogue.english: This year it is awarded to... Michel Bonnaud for his movie Start Without Me.

dialogue.french: Essayant 9 de rester décontracté, le gagnant répond :
dialogue.english: Trying to remain relaxed, the winner answered:

dialogue.french: Merci, je n'ai jamais reçu un prix de la mise en scène 3.
dialogue.english: Thank you. I've never had (receive) a director's award.

dialogue.french: Mais je suis étonné car je n'ai pas tourné un seul film depuis vingt ans.
dialogue.english: But I'm astonished because I haven't made (turned) a single film for twenty years.

dialogue.french: C'est justement pour ça que le jury vous donne cette récompense, mon cher Michel !
dialogue.english: That's exactly (justly) why the jury is giving you this reward, my dear Michel!

notes.content: In a formal register, careful speakers such as actors or newscasters tend to use linkage (see Introduction) more than would otherwise be the case. In this lesson, contrast the liaisons made in deux_ou trois and films_étrangers with je suis étonné in line 16 and sentence 5 in the first exercise, both of which use the non- formal pronunciation found in everyday speech.

Most of this text is in a formal register, as used in the press, for example. In these contexts, French often shuns "ordinary" verbs such as être and avoir, replacing them with fancier synonyms. Here, for example, La France a une longue tradition is rephrased using the regular verb posséder, to possess. Likewise, the cinema is known as le septième art, the seventh art. This tendency to "upgrade" simple concepts can be disconcerting at first, but it soon becomes a familiar trope.

la diffusion comes from the verb diffuser, to diffuse but, more commonly, to spread or broadcast. The noun is used in terms such as la diffusion video, streaming, and la baladodiffusion, podcasting. These are relatively new coinages that have certainly not replaced the imported terms le streaming and le podcasting in everyday speech.

Here is a good example of how nouns are derived from verbs. From the verb mettre, to put, we get un metteur en scène ("a putter in stage"), a film director or a stage director, and la mise en scène, direction.

Another literary device used in formal writing is metonymy, the use of a related term to stand in for an object or concept: les salles obscures ("the dark rooms") is used - always in the plural - to mean cinemas. However, the regular adjectives for dark are sombre (the absence of light, pronounced [sahbrruh]) or fonce (any colour approaching black): Mon nouveau pull est bleu fonce, My new sweater is dark blue.

We know that un rendez- vous means a meeting or an appointment (lesson 4, for example). But it can also be used figuratively when referring to an event attracting large numbers of people, Le Salon de l'automobile est le rendez- vous annuel des tous ceux qui aiment les voitures, The Motor Show is the annual get- together for everyone who loves cars. Because the final letters on both components of this compound noun are - s and - z, respectively, it does not change in the plural (des rendez- vous).

The Latin and Greek origins of French are evident in many everyday words. Here, for example, we have the suffix - phile (from the Greek philos, friend). With its Anglo- Saxon roots, English would translate this as lover: for example un bibliophile, a book- lover; un cinéphile, a film- lover or movie buff. The opposite suffix is - phobe (from phobos, fear): un aquaphobe: someone who is scared of water. These types of words are not considered formal.

suivi is the past participle of the Group 3 verb suivre, to follow. Two other common verbs from the same group with the -i participle ending are finir → fini (to finish/finished) and dormir → dormi (to sleep/slept).
Here's another the past participle of a Group 3 verb: voir → vu, to see → seen. Note also venir → venu, to come → came and avoir → eu, to have → had.

Introduced in lesson 23 note 9, the present participle of all three verb groups ends in - ant: essayer → essayant (try → trying), finir → finissant (finish → finishing), rendre → rendant (give back → giving back). Note how the participle is used as an adjective: les arts vivants, the performing ("living") arts.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: In many French-speaking countries, the cinema is known as le grand écran ("the big screen") and the TV as le petit écran ("small screen"). One of France's top film awards is la Palme d'Or, Golden Palm, presented to the best movie at le Festival de Cannes. And instead of the Oscars, the French movie industry organises its own annual awards, les Césars (named for the sculptor who designed the trophy). Foreign films are popular in France and are generally screened in two versions: en VO (version originale, with subtitles) or en VF (version française, dubbed into French).

notes.content: The familiar register in this lesson is in sharp contrast to the formality of the previous one. For example: the one-word exclamation Bof is a concise idiomatic way of expressing indifference. It can be translated in several ways: Qu'est-ce que tu penses de ce film ? - Bof. What do you think of this movie? Not much!Meh/Whatever, etc.

In this lesson we come across two sets of Group 3 verbs with their past participles: mettre → mis and prendre → pris; along with perdre → perdu, boire → bu, entendre → entendu, and plaire → plu. More details in lesson 42.

We saw the adjective étranger, foreign, in lesson 39. The noun is identical: un étranger / une étrangère. It has two meanings: a foreigner and a stranger (the latter derived directly from French). The context should make the meaning clear -for example, la Légion étrangère, the Foreign Legion. Don't confuse the adjectives étranger and étrange, strange: Quelle question étrange ! What a strange question!

dernier/dernière means both last (lesson 14) and latest. Once again, context is key, because le dernier film de l'acteur can mean the actor's last film (if he made no more movies or is deceased) and the actor's latest film (their most recent performance). If in doubt, ask a movie buff or google the name!

Another familiar usage is nul (line 8). After a noun, the word literally means nil or null and void. But in idiomatic language, it indicates that the thing or person being described is useless, worthless, terrible, etc. Cet acteur est nul, That actor is terrible. Being an adjective, nul agrees with its noun: Cette actrice est nulle.

un truc, a thing (lesson 30 note 1), is used in the same way as in English to express something one likes or is keen on: Ce n'est pas mon/son/leur truc, It's not my/his/her/their thing. Le sport, c'est son truc, He/she is into sports.

We know that si means both if and so (lesson 32 note 1). It can also be used like oui when contradicting a negative statement, thus performing the same function as the repetition of the auxiliary in English: Il n'aime pas le chocolat - Si. He doesn't like chocolate. - Yes he does. To add extra insistence, the word is repeated: Si si, il l'adore, Oh yes he does, he loves it.

The suffix -erie, seen in lesson 25, can designate any place serving a specific function: un billet → une billetterie, a ticket / ticket office, une huile → une huilerie, oil (vegetable, etc.) / an oil mill or factory. Look for root word and work from there.

une bête (the origin of beast) means an animal or a creature. As an adjective, it is used colloquially to mean silly or idiotie. Ton idée est bête, Your idea is silly. A useful expression, in response to an acceptable suggestion, is Ce n'est pas bête: Nous allons vendre notre voiture, - Ce n'est pas bête. We are going to sell our car - That's not a bad idea.

un verre, a glass (lesson 24 line 12) is used socially in the expression prendre un verre, to have / get a drink: Tu veux voir un film ou aller prendre un verre ? Do you want to see a movie or go for a drink?

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Identifying different registers in another language is difficult, particularly in the early stages of learning, and takes quite a bit of practice. This lesson and the previous one are designed to underline some of the differences between formal and informal interactions in everyday French. For the time being, simply listen to the dialogues and do the exercises. You'll soon be able to differentiate between registers and take part actively in conversations using the appropriate language. Continuez le bon travail ! (Keep up the good work!)

dialogue.french: Allô, bonjour. Je suis bien au restaurant "La Fleur d'Argent" ?

dialogue.english: If you have nothing else.

dialogue.english: Alright, I'll note it down. What name?

dialogue.french: C'est bien ça. Vous pouvez me les envoyer par texto.
dialogue.english: That's right. You can text them to me.

notes.content: The terminal -euil has exactly the same sound as œil (lesson 32), so accueil is pronounced [ak-uh-yy]. In the verb accueillir, it precedes the y sound of the double-l, hence [ak-uh-yeer]. It's important to practise this sound, which does not exist in English.

accueillir has many different translations. Basically, it means to welcome, but it can be used in the broader sense of to meet, to pick up, etc. Mon amie Nadège m'a accueilli à l'aéroport, My friend Nadège met me at the airport. The noun un accueil means a welcome, a reception, but the translation will depend on the context: Merci de votre accueil can mean Thanks for making me welcome or Thanks for having me. In a public building, look for the sign Accueil, the reception desk.

The preposition depuis translates both for and since. It is used with the present tense, not the past: Je connais cet homme depuis vingt ans, I have known that man for twenty years. Elle est malade depuis Noël, She has been sick since Christmas.

Here is another common example of apocope (lesson 17 note 7), which mirrors English usage. The noun for a software routine, une application, is generally shortened to une appli, an app. This process of truncating long words is usual in technical contexts such as computing.

un fil, a wire, is often used in reference to telephones, even though landlines have almost vanished. Two common expressions are passer un coup de fil, to make phone call, give someone a ring, and au bout du fil, on the line. Do not confuse the plural les fils with the word for son: un fils / des fils. In the latter case, the singular and the plural are both pronounced [feess], compared with [fee/l for fil / fils.

souhaiter is a formal verb meaning to wish (for) or to hope that. It is common in polite contexts: A quelle heure souhaitez-vous venir ? What time do you wish to come? The noun, un souhait means a desire, a wish. The verbal response to someone sneezing is A tes/vos souhaits ! ("to your desires"), an exclamation inducted into English as Atishoo!

un couvert, from the verb couvrir, to cover, means a place-setting in culinary contexts, hence the expression mettre le couvert, to lay the table. In a restaurant context, the noun refers to a customer or diner (known in the trade as "a cover"). Don't confuse un couvert with un couvercle, meaning a lid or top of a container.

Remember that past participles used as adjectives must agree with their noun, so Gabriel est pris ce soir / Gabriel et Marc sont pris ce soir / Lucie est prise ce soir / Toutes les tables sont prises ce soir.

See lesson 26 note 2. Using an inverted pronoun, rather than est-ce que, to ask a question is formal but quite common in formal social interaction. So instead of Est-ce que cela vous convient ? the restaurant owner enquires Cela vous convient-il ? (Listen to the liaison between the final t and the initial vowel.)
This is our first encounter with the future tense, which, in contrast to English, is formed not with an auxiliary but by changing the verb ending: chanter, to sing, → je chanterai.

Despite the prevalence of English, French has succeeded in coining "native" terms in the field of technology. One example is un texto, an SMS. Donne-moi ton numéro et je t'envoie un texto tout à l'heure, Give me your number and I'll send you an SMS later. Even so, many young people in France opt for the English term un SMS. Which is unnecessary because the verb texter means "to text"!

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Léo Ferré (1916-1993) was a French singer, poet and composer who marked several generations of fans with his music, writing and politics. Ferré, along with his contemporaries Georges Brassens and Jacques Brel, was un auteur-compositeur-interprète ("author, composer, interpreter"), the nearest equivalent to the singer-songwriter typical of Anglo-American music. That tradition, known as la chanson (the song), attaches equal value to the music and the lyrics. More recently, the importance of the written word in French music has spawned several generations of rap and hip-hop artists who have become household names. Song lyrics can be hard to follow for language learners, but the pleasure of choosing a single text and learning it by heart is definitely worth the effort.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que vous avez vidé la corbeille à papier ce matin ?

dialogue.english: No, I've looked but it's not there. But where did I put it?

dialogue.french: Mais attendez, je suis bête ! Je l'ai laissé à l'accueil tout à l'heure.
dialogue.english: But wait. I'm stupid! I left it at the reception desk earlier.

dialogue.french: Heureusement que vous l'avez trouvé !

dialogue.french: Ah bon ? Je suis étonné. Qu'est-ce qu'elle veut ?
dialogue.english: Really? I'm surprised. What does she want?

dialogue.english: She said that she has been trying to get in touch with you for several days.

notes.content: Participles: 1.1 Le participe présent - Present participle: Present participles, seen in lesson 22 note 9 and lesson 23 note 6, are used less than in English because there is no progressive verb form in French. For most regular and irregular verbs, the participle is formed by dropping the ending from the first person plural of the present tense and adding -ant to the root: penser → pensons → pensant; dormir → dormons → dormant; perdre → perdons → perdant. (Remember that verbs ending -cer take a cedilla in the first person plural, and thus in the participle (commencer → commençons → commençant), while -ger verbs take an e: manger → mangeons → mangeant). Present participles are used in two ways. First, as in English, to describe an ongoing action: Les personnes habitant près d'un aéroport souffrent souvent de maux de tête. People living near an airport often get headaches. In this case, the participle does not agree with the noun. A similar usage is in a clause starting the preposition en: En vérifiant la cave, j'ai trouvé la porte ouverte. While a literal translation would be While checking the cellar, it is easier to recast the sentence: When I checked the cellar, I found the door open. The structure can be very different: Il arrive. En attendant, commençons à nettoyer la cuisine. He's on his way. In the meantime, let's start cleaning the kitchen. The other main use of the present participle is as an adjective, in which case it has to agree with the noun: C'est une histoire intéressante. 1.2 Le participe passé - Past participle: In addition to functioning as adjectives, past participles are used to form le passé composé. They are therefore important. • For Group 1 verbs (-er), simply drop the final -r and replace the -e- with -é. Hence commander → commandé, fermer → fermé, etc. There are no exceptions. • The rule for Group 2 (regular verbs ending in -ir with the present participle in -issant) is even simpler: simply drop the last letter: finir → fini, réussir → réussi. • For Group 3 verbs (-re, -oir, -ir with a present participle in -ant), the rules are: -re is replaced by -u: vendre → vendu; attendre → attendu; -oir verbs also end in -u: recevoir → reçu; vouloir → voulu; -ir behave mostly as regular Group 2 verbs: partir → parti; sentir → senti. There are, however, many exceptions, notably frequently used verbs such as prendre → pris; faire → fait; dire → dit; courir → couru; mettre → mis; and, more importantly, the auxiliaries être → été, and avoir → eu. Two other common verbs with irregular past participles are pouvoir → pu, and savoir → su. When you learn a new verb, especially one from Group 3, always check the past participle. Finally, when a past participle is used alone as an adjective, it has to agree in number and gender with its noun: Toutes les tables sont prises, All the tables are taken; Cette robe est vendue en plusieurs couleurs, This dress is sold in several colours.

Le passé composé - the perfect tense: The perfect tense is known as le passé composé because it is "composed" of two parts: an auxiliary and a past participle. It is used in the spoken and written language to translate two English tenses: the past simple and the present perfect. There is an equivalent tense, le passé simple ("simple" because it consists of a single word), which we cover only briefly at the end of this book because it is used chiefly in literature and, occasionally, very formal speech. The usual auxiliary is avoir, although some verbs take être, as we shall see in the next set of lessons. The past participle sometimes has to agree with the direct object of the sentence, but we will look at this rule in more detail later on, particularly because it rarely affects the pronunciation. • Group 1: manger, to eat: j'ai mangé, tu as mangé, il/elle a mangé, nous avons mangé, vous avez mangé, ils/elles ont mangé. • Group 2: finir, to finish: j'ai fini, tu as fini, il/elle a fini, nous avons fini, vous avez fini, ils/elles ont fini. • Group 3: voir, to see: j'ai vu, tu as vu, il/elle a vu, nous avons vu, vous avez vu, ils/elles ont vu. The negative is formed by placing ne ... pas around the auxiliary: je n'ai pas mangé, nous n'avons pas fini, ils n'ont pas vu. The three forms of the interrogative are (i) a rising tone Tu as vu ? (ii) with est-ce que: Est-ce que tu as vu ? or (iii) inversion: As-tu vu ? The perfect tense relates to actions that took place in the past and are now complete. In this context, it is equivalent to the past simple tense in English: J'ai vu le notaire hier, I saw the notary yesterday. But if we remove the time marker hier, the French verb remains the same (J'ai vu le notaire), whereas the English equivalent is either I saw or the present perfect, I have seen the notary. When adverbs such as jamais and toujours are used with le passé composé, the verb is translated with the present perfect: J'ai toujours voulu / Je n'ai jamais voulu apprendre le français. I've always/never wanted to learn French. In some cases, however, particularly with the preposition depuis, which translates both for and since, French uses the present rather than a perfect tense: Elle travaille ici depuis deux mois / depuis l'année dernière. She has been working here for two months / since last year. Last, while English automatically uses the past tense to describe an action or sequence of actions that have taken place and are now complete, French tends to use the present form of the verb. This narrative device, known as le présent historique, brings the events to life and makes them more compelling: Hier, je vais au marché pour faire les courses mais j'oublie mon portefeuille. Yesterday, I went to the market to do the shopping but I forgot my wallet. Of course, le passé composé can also be used in these cases, with absolutely no difference in meaning. To sum up, le passé composé does the work of both the simple past and the present perfect tenses in English and, for that reason, is easier to use. For instance: J'ai vu Max à Noël mais je ne l'ai pas vu depuis. I saw Max at Christmas but I haven't seen him since.

Comparatives and superlatives: • The comparative of superiority is always formed with plus before the adjective and que after it: Londres est plus grand que Paris., London is bigger than Paris. Unlike English, the length of the adjective does not matter: Paris est plus cher que Bruxelles. Paris is more expensive than Brussels. • The same rule applies to comparisons of inferiority, formed with moins ... que: Le film est moins intéressant que le livre. The movie is less interesting than the book. • To form the superlative, simply put the definite article le or la for the singular (masculine/feminine) and les for the plural before plus or moins: Le bus est le moyen le plus rapide d'aller à Lille. The bus is the quickest way to get to Lille; C'est l'arrondissement le moins bruyant de la ville. It's the least noisy district in the city. A handful of adjectives are irregular, notably bon, bien and mauvais: bon, good → meilleur (que), better (than) → le meilleur / la meilleure, the best; bien, good/well → mieux (que), better than → le mieux / la mieux, the best; mauvais, bad → pire (que), worse (than) → le/la pire, the worst. Bien can be confusing at first because it is an adjective but also an adverb. As we will see later, it is invariable when used adjectivally: Elles sont très bien, tes nouvelles chaussures, Your new shoes are very nice. For adverbs, the superlative is formed with le (never la) before the comparative plus: le plus rapidement possible, as fast as possible.

On the phone: Using the telephone can be difficult, even in one's native language, for many reasons. In a foreign language, those difficulties are compounded by rapid speech, an unfamiliar vocabulary and, usually, being unable to see the person you are talking to. And although texting, videoconferencing and other forms of digital communication are omni-present, being able to communicate by phone - téléphone portable (mobile) or fixe (landline)- is still an important skill to learn. (Interestingly, although most communications today are wireless, the familiar expression for a phone call is still un coup de fil!) Here are some of the set phrases used in most conversations. • Allô is used when answering the phone or checking that the person you are calling can hear you. (One variant is Allô oui ?, although some people reply simply Oui ?, which is just as abrupt as Yes? in English.) To enquire about the identity of the caller, use the expression Qui est à l'appareil ? Who is calling? • To confirm that you have reached the right number, say Je suis bien chez ... ? (for a person) or Je suis bien au/à la ... (for a business, an administration, etc.) • If you ask to speak to someone other than the person answering, you will usually be asked: C'est de la part de qui ? Who shall I say is calling? • Before transferring the call, the person will say Ne quittez/quitte pas (Hold the line) or Je vous le/la passe, I'll put you through to him/her, or both phrases together. • If the line is engaged, the message will be La ligne est occupée or Ça sonne occupé. If the person you wish to speak to is busy, you might want to leave them a message: Est-ce que je peux laisser un message ? or have them call you back Est-ce qu'il/qu'elle peut me rappeler s'il vous plaît ? Or you can always say that you will call back later: Je rappellerai plus tard. • If you cannot hear the other person, tell them Je ne vous entends pas, parlez plus fort s'il vous plaît. And if you have difficulty hearing or understanding, use one of these phrases: Je ne vous entends pas bien, I can't hear you very well, or Pouvez-vous répétez lentement s'il vous plaît ? Can you repeat slowly please? One slight difference between French and Anglo-American usage is that phone numbers are written and pronounced in two-digit groups. For example 06 32 72 91 would be given as zéro six / trente-deux / soixante-douze / quatre-vingt-onze. A word of advice: when taking down a number, pause if you hear soixante ... because the next digit could be, for example, deux (62) or douze (72). The same applies to quatre-vingt ... , which might be continued as quatre-vingt-quatre (84) or quatre-vingt-quatorze (94).

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: In the past six lessons, you have expanded your knowledge of the different language registers that you're likely to encounter on a regular basis, either directly through conversation or indirectly through movies, books or videos. When you came back to this lesson series in the second part, or productive phase, of the learning process, you will realise just how much you have learned in a relatively short space of time.

dialogue.french: J'ai trouvé cet article, sur le-foot.fr, qui peut t'intéresser.

dialogue.english: He was born in Spain in 1999. His parents died in a road accident.

dialogue.french: Peu de temps après, Manuel est venu vivre avec son oncle et sa tante dans le sud de la France.
dialogue.english: Shortly after, Manuel came to live with his uncle and his aunt in the south of France.

dialogue.french: Il est allé au lycée à Nice et, ensuite, est monté à Paris pour faire des études à l'université.
dialogue.english: He went to high school in Nice and then went up to Paris to study at university.

dialogue.french: Il est entré à la faculté de droit, où il est devenu un fan de sports d'équipe.
dialogue.english: He went to law school, where he became a fan of team sports.

dialogue.french: Un soir, il est allé voir un match international féminin entre les Pays-Bas et l'Allemagne.
dialogue.english: One evening, he went to see a women's international match between the Netherlands and Germany.

dialogue.french: Le jeu lui a vraiment plu et il a pris la décision de travailler comme entraîneur dans ce sport en Europe."
dialogue.english: He really liked the game and he decided to work as a coach in that sport in Europe."

dialogue.french: Est-ce qu'il est rentré en Espagne ?
dialogue.english: Did he go back to Spain?

dialogue.french: Non, il est retourné à la fac pour étudier la gestion sportive. L'article continue :
dialogue.english: No, he returned to university to study sports management. The article continues:

dialogue.french: "Manuel est sorti trois ans plus tard, avec son diplôme en poche et plusieurs offres d'emploi.
dialogue.english: "Manuel came out three years later, with a diploma in his pocket and several job offers.

dialogue.french: Il est allé travailler avec l'équipe de Rennes, où il est resté longtemps.
dialogue.english: He went to work with the Rennes team, where he stayed a long time.

dialogue.french: Après cette expérience, il est passé d'un club à un autre pendant quelque temps
dialogue.english: After that experience, he went from one club to another for some time

dialogue.french: avant d'être nommé au poste d'entraîneur officiel de l'équipe féminine de Bordeaux.
dialogue.english: before being appointed to the post of official trainer of the Bordeaux women's team.

dialogue.french: Il est arrivé au bon moment. L'équipe est montée très vite en haut du classement,
dialogue.english: He arrived at the right moment. The team quickly rose to the top of the ranking.

dialogue.french: et le directeur lui a demandé de rester."
dialogue.english: and the director asked him to stay."

dialogue.french: Mais alors, pour quelle raison ? Pourquoi choisir un entraîneur et pas une entraîneuse ?
dialogue.english: But then, for what reason? Why choose a male trainer and not a female trainer?

dialogue.english: Good question! I can't explain it easily.

dialogue.french: Personne ne le sait vraiment.
dialogue.english: No one really knows.

notes.content: The names of many sports have been imported into French as masculine nouns. They include le tennis, le golf, le squash and le volley-ball. In a couple of cases, the names have been shortened. This is notably the case of le football → le foot and le basket-ball → le basket (the final t is pronounced). However, not all French sports have English names (for example, la natation, swimming) so always check in a dictionary.

When making the agreement between a compound noun and an adjective, always look for the root word, in this case équipe, which is feminine (une équipe féminine, see line 15). With the noun l'équipe de football, the adjective will agree with the head word, l'équipe, not the second noun, football. By contrast, with a single noun, the agreement is simple le football féminin.

Certain verbs are conjugated in the perfect tense with être instead of avoir when used intransitively. This lesson contains the main ones you will need to know, such as partir, to leave: je suis parti, tu es parti, il est parti, nous sommes partis, vous êtes partis, ils sont partis. Another major difference with avoir-conjugated verbs is that the past participle agrees with the subject, thus Monsieur Grégoire est parti / Madame Grégoire est partie.

naître is a very irregular verb, meaning to be born. It is conjugated with être in the perfect tense, so il est né / elle est née means he / she was born. (The noun from naître is la naissance, birth, see lesson 23). The same conjugation rule applies to mourir, to die: je suis mort/morte, tu es mort/-e, il/elle est mort/-e, I / you / he died, etc.

plaire (past participle: plu) means to please but is used with the preposition à and an indirect object as a synonym for to like. In this case, the word is the reverse of English: La nouvelle maison plaît à la famille, The family likes the new house (literally: "The new house is pleasing to the family"). If a pronoun replaces the noun, it comes before the verb: Le film lui a plu, He liked the film. The same rule applies, for example, to demander à: Je vais demander à l'entraîneur → Je vais lui demander, I'm going to ask the coach. When learning a new verb, make sure to check the preposition, if any, that goes with it.

Masculine adjectives ending in -if form the feminine with -ive: sportif → sportive (sporty), créatif → créative (creative), agressif → agressive (aggressive). Note how the feminine form is often used in English as an adjective.

quelque temps is an indefinite (and thus invariable) adjective describing a short time period: a while, or the literal translation some time: Je ne les ai pas vus depuis quelque temps, I haven't seen them for a while. Contrast this with longtemps (line 13), a long time, written as a single word.

Adding the definite article to bon, good, changes the meaning slightly, to correct, proper, or right: Tu es arrivé au bon moment, You came at the right time. Ce n'est pas la bonne question, That's not the right question. One of the all-time classic guides to French grammar is Le Bon Usage, which translates as proper usage.

Here, personne is not a noun (lesson 19 line 3) but an indefinite pronoun, meaning no one, nobody, anyone. It can be either the subject or the object of verb, and the accompanying verb is always in negative: Personne ne le sait, No one knows; Je ne connais personne ici, I don't know anyone here.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: The French school system is divided into four levels: l'école maternelle (nursery school), l'école primaire (primary school), le collège (roughly equivalent to middle school) and le lycée (secondary school). Students who graduate with the school-leaving certificate, le baccalauréat (known colloquially as le bac) can go on to l'université (university), which comprises several facultés (faculties). However, that word - often abbreviated to la fac - is also used in a familiar register as a synonym for university.

dialogue.french: Si vous n'avez pas un titre de transport électronique, madame,
dialogue.english: If you don't have an e-ticket, madam,

dialogue.french: vous pouvez acheter votre billet dans le distributeur automatique au bout du quai.
dialogue.english: you can buy your ticket in the automatic machine at the end of the platform.

dialogue.french: Merci, mais il m'en faut deux : un aller simple pour moi et un aller-retour pour mon cousin.
dialogue.english: Thanks, but I need two: a single for me and a return for my cousin.

dialogue.french: Dans ce cas, la billetterie est là-bas, vers les consignes et les objets trouvés.
dialogue.english: In that case, the ticket office is over there, towards the left-luggage lockers and lost property.

dialogue.french: Vous voyez les deux files d'attente devant les guichets ?
dialogue.english: You see those two queues in front of the ticket offices?

dialogue.french: Prenez celle de gauche : elle avance plus vite que l'autre... enfin, moins lentement.
dialogue.english: Take the one on the left: it's moving faster than the other... well, less slowly.

dialogue.french: C'est bon, Jules, j'ai les billets. Je vais les composter.
dialogue.english: It's okay, Jules, I have the tickets. I'm going to time-stamp them.

dialogue.french: Fais attention à ne pas les mettre dans le mauvais sens dans la machine !
dialogue.english: Be careful not to put them the wrong way around in the machine!

dialogue.french: C'est énervant ! On affiche partout "Le compostage est obligatoire",
dialogue.english: That's annoying! There are signs everywhere saying "Time-stamping is obligatory,"

dialogue.french: mais toutes les machines sont hors service et je n'ai pas pu valider les billets.
dialogue.english: but all the machines are out of order and I couldn't validate the tickets.

dialogue.french: À ce moment-là, il faut le signaler au contrôleur à bord du train.
dialogue.english: In that case, you have to report it to the inspector on board the train.

dialogue.french: Veux-tu de l'aide pour porter ton sac ? Il a l'air très lourd.
dialogue.english: Do you want help carrying your bag? It looks very heavy.

dialogue.french: Non merci, il est grand mais en réalité il est assez léger.
dialogue.english: No thanks, it's big but in reality it's quite light.

dialogue.french: Attends une minute, je regarde le tableau d'affichage des trains de grandes lignes :
dialogue.english: Wait a minute, I'm looking at the display board for the mainline trains:

dialogue.french: "Circulation perturbée : Nancy : Train en panne ; Colmar : Retard ; Mulhouse : Annulé".
dialogue.english: "Traffic disrupted: Nancy: Train broken down; Colmar: Late; Mulhouse: Cancelled."
dialogue.pronunciation: seerkewlassyonhn pairtewrbay ... nœhnsee ... pan ... kolmahr ... mew/ooz ... ahnewlay

dialogue.french: Il n'y a même pas de trains omnibus ! C'est toujours la même chose et j'en ai marre !
dialogue.english: There aren't even any stopping trains! It's always the same thing and I'm fed up!

dialogue.french: À chaque fois il y a des soucis avec ces lignes, vrai ou faux ?
dialogue.english: Every time there are issues with these lines, true or false?

dialogue.french: Je suis d'accord, mais il vaut mieux prendre notre mal en patience.
dialogue.english: I agree, but it's better to grin and bear it.

dialogue.french: Il n'en est pas question. Je vais plutôt prendre le car !
dialogue.english: Out of the question. I'm going to take the coach instead!

notes.content: sens: the final s is pronounced: [sœhnss], as is the case for fils (son/sons) and for plus (more) at the end of a sentence.

We know that un titre means a title. By extension it also means a deed or a right, found in expressions like un titre de transport, a ticket or travel card. As usual, context is all-important: when buying a train ticket, you would ask for un billet but an inspector would likely ask you to show your titre de transport.

un quai is the origin of the English cognate quay. More broadly, it designates an embankment or riverside road. In a train station, un quai is a platform, although the synonym une voie is also used, particularly in passenger announcements: Le train partira de la voie numéro trois, The train will depart from platform number three.

In a travel context, un billet aller simple (or just un aller simple) means a one-way or single ticket. A return ticket is un (billet) aller-retour. The term for a season ticket is une carte d'abonnement, literally "a subscription card," or simply un abonnement.
In some cases, mauvais, bad, can mean wrong: Vous avez fait un mauvais numéro, You've got the wrong number. There are several other ways of saying wrong, including faux, as we shall see later on.

un sens generally means a sense, but it can also relate to direction: Vous n'allez pas dans le bon sens, You're not going the right way. When buying a train ticket, you can select a seat that faces forward: dans le sens de la marche, literally "the direction of walking," i.e. movement.

The suffix -age can be added to the root of some verbs to make a substantive noun. Thus composter means to time-stamp or punch a ticket, hence the noun le compostage: time-stamping. Likewise, afficher, to display gives us l'affichage, display or billposting.

marre, which is loosely related to la mer, the sea and hence the sense of discomfort brought on by sea-sickness, is found in the idiomatic expression en avoir marre, to be fed up. This can be used simply as an exclamation J'en ai marre !, I'm fed up!, or with a direct object: Il en a marre de son travail, He's fed up with his job.

The adjective faux has several meanings, all related to things that are incorrect, fake or false. For example, un faux billet is a forged ticket while, in music, une fausse note is a wrong note. The expression C'est faux means It's wrong or a falsehood. Remember, however, that to be wrong is avoir tort.
The opposite of être d'accord avec, to agree with is ne pas être d'accord avec, to disagree with: Je ne suis pas d'accord avec vous, I disagree with you. It's simple!

vaut is the third person singular of the irregular valoir, to be worth: je vaux, tu vaux, il/elle vaut, nous valons, vous valez, ils/elles valent. Combien vaut ce tableau ? How much is this painting worth?

mal can be an adverb meaning badly as well as a noun, meaning pain or sickness. It can also be used figuratively, with du): J'ai du mal à le comprendre, I have difficulty understanding him. The expression prendre son mal en patience is equivalent to grin and bear it.

title: Il n'y a pas de métiers inutiles

dialogue.french: On m'a invitée aujourd'hui pour vous parler des réseaux sociaux professionnels.
dialogue.english: I have been invited to talk to you about professional social networks.

dialogue.french: Bien entendu, le monde du travail a changé considérablement depuis vingt ans.
dialogue.english: Of course, the world of work has changed considerably in twenty years.

dialogue.french: Mon père, que j'admire, a travaillé toute sa vie dans la même usine,
dialogue.english: My father, whom I admire, worked all his life in the same factory,

dialogue.french: alors que mon frère, qui a vingt-cinq ans, a déjà changé plusieurs fois d'entreprise.
dialogue.english: whereas my brother, who is twenty-five, has already changed companies several times.

dialogue.french: De nos jours, on peut devenir avocat, ingénieur, journaliste, comptable ou programmeur,
dialogue.english: Nowadays, you can become a lawyer, an engineer, a journalist, an accountant or a programmer,

dialogue.french: mais aussi boucher, coiffeur, cuisinier ou plombier : il n'y a pas de métiers inutiles.
dialogue.english: but also a butcher, a hairdresser, a cook or a plumber: there are no useless jobs.

dialogue.french: Je veux vous parler de ce que je sais faire, plutôt que de ce que je voudrais faire.
dialogue.english: I want to talk to you about what I know how to do, rather than what I would like to do.

dialogue.french: Je suis conceptrice multimédia et consultante,
dialogue.english: I am a multimedia designer and consultant,

dialogue.french: un métier qui demande une excellente formation, et pas mal de connaissances techniques.
dialogue.english: a job that requires excellent training, and quite a lot of technical knowledge.

dialogue.french: Ce que j'apprécie le plus dans mon travail c'est qu'il m'offre la possibilité de rencontrer des gens aussi passionnés que moi.
dialogue.english: What I appreciate the most about my work is that it offers me the possibility to meet people who are as passionate as I am.

dialogue.french: Ce qui est difficile est qu'on est toujours en train de voyager : en avion, en train, parfois en bateau ;
dialogue.english: What is difficult is that we're always in the process of travelling: by plane, train and sometimes boat;

dialogue.french: on n'arrête pas de bouger ! Nous ne pouvons pas prendre de congés,
dialogue.english: we never stop moving! We can't take any holidays,

dialogue.french: et nous ne passons jamais assez de temps dans les villes que nous visitons, ce qui est dommage.
dialogue.english: and we never spend enough time in the cities that we visit, which is a pity.

dialogue.french: Mais, bon, c'est comme ça. Et les choses changent tellement vite !
dialogue.english: But, well, that's the way it is. And things change so quickly!

dialogue.french: Que faites-vous maintenant, madame ? Toujours le même métier ?
dialogue.english: What do you do now, madam? Still the same job?

dialogue.french: Non, je suis entre deux emplois. Ce qui veut dire que je suis au chômage.
dialogue.english: No, I'm between two jobs. Which means that I'm unemployed.

notes.content: The circumflex over the o of chômage lengthens the vowel: [show-mahzh]; compare this sound with the o in dommage: [do-mahzh].

Most adjectives ending in -al, including social and royal, form their masculine plural with -aux: sociaux, royaux, etc. The feminine forms, sociale, royale, simply take an -s (sociales, royales). (Un réseau, a network, forms its plural with an -x, as explained in lesson 29 note 9.)

entendu, the past participle of entendre, to hear, can be used as a response, meaning Of course. The phrase Bien entendu has the same meaning but is more flexible and can be placed at the beginning, middle or end of a complete sentence: Bien entendu, nous comprenons votre décision, Of course, we understand your decision.

qui and que are relative pronouns that can refer both to people and to things, qui as the subject and que as the direct object. It is important not to translate qui systematically as who because the subject can be inanimate: Prenez le bus qui part à dix heures, Take the bus that leaves at ten o'clock.

We know the noun un emploi, a job. Two related words are un métier, which, in addition to an occupation, can also mean a trade or craft, and une profession, a profession. The opposite, unemployment, is le chômage. Être au chômage, to be unemployed.

ce qui and ce que are indefinite pronouns meaning which or what. They introduce a subordinate clause, in the same way as qui and que, but are used in sentences where the noun or phrase referred to by a pronoun is not expressed.
voudrais is the first person singular of the conditional form of vouloir: je veux → je voudrais, I want → I would like.

Masculine job titles ending in -eur that are derived directly from a verb have a feminine form ending in -euse - for example, un programmeur becomes une programmeuse - whereas -eur nouns that do not come from a verb stem have the feminine ending -trice: un concept → un concepteur/une conceptrice.

The invariable phrase pas mal de is useful for describing a fairly large quantity : Il y a pas mal de neige ici en janvier, There's quite a lot of snow here in January. Despite the adjective mal, bad, the term is not pejorative.

aussi ... que is the comparative of equality, equivalent to as ... as: Il est aussi grand que son père, He's as tall as his father. The second noun can be replaced by a stressed pronoun (but never a subject pronoun, as in English: Elle est aussi surprise que moi, She's as surprised as I [am].

We know that French verbs do not have a progressive form. Where necessary, though, the expression en train de, followed by an infinitive, allows us to insist on the ongoing nature of an action: Je ne peux pas venir, je suis en train de regarder le match, I can't come, I'm watching the game. The important element is duration: Voici une bonne adresse pour votre prochain voyage ou votre voyage en cours si vous êtes en train de voyager, Here's a good address for your next trip or your current trip if you are in the process of travelling. Don't confuse en train de with en train, by train!

The compound verb vouloir dire ("to want to say") translates as to mean, i.e. to imply or indicate. Only vouloir is conjugated: Qu'est-ce que tu veux / vous voulez dire ? What do you mean? However, in the sense to denote, we use the verb signifier: Que signifie ce mot ? What does this word mean?

new_words.french: a déjà changé plusieurs fois d'entreprise
new_words.english: has already changed companies several times

new_words.french: il n'y a pas de métiers inutiles

new_words.english: I want to talk to you

new_words.english: people as passionate as I am

new_words.french: nous ne pouvons pas prendre de congés
nous ne passons jamais assez de temps
dans les villes que nous visitons

translate.question: Alain a lu leur lettre et il est aussi surpris que moi.
answers: Alain read their letter and he is as surprised as I am.

translate.question: J'ai pas mal de travail à finir avant ce soir. Heureusement, je travaille vite !
answers: I have quite a lot of work to finish before this evening. Luckily, I work quickly!

translate.question: Prends le train de huit heures, car je voudrais partir plus tôt, si possible.
answers: Take the 8 o'clock train, because I would like to leave earlier if possible.

translate.question: Je comprends ta décision, bien entendu, mais je ne suis pas du tout d'accord.
answers: I understand your decision, of course, but I totally disagree.

translate.question: Ce que j'apprécie le moins dans cette profession c'est que les choses changent trop vite.
answers: What I appreciate the least in this profession is that things change too quickly.

fill.question: You can become a lawyer or an accountant but also a hairdresser or a cook. There are no useless jobs. ... devenir ... ou ... mais aussi ... ou ... Il n'y a pas de métiers inutiles.
fill.full_answer: Tu peux devenir avocat ou comptable mais aussi coiffeur ou cuisinier. Il n'y a pas de métiers inutiles.

fill.question: On social networks, there is much talk about the royal gardens in Brussels at the moment. Sur les ..., on parle beaucoup ... de Bruxelles en ce moment.
fill.full_answer: Sur les réseaux sociaux, on parle beaucoup des jardins royaux de Bruxelles en ce moment.

fill.question: We are going to talk to you about what we can do for you. Nous ... parler ... nous pouvons faire pour vous.
fill.full_answer: Nous allons vous parler de ce que nous pouvons faire pour vous.

fill.question: What does the word "homage" mean? - I think that you mean "chômage." It means that a person has no work. ... le mot "homage" ? - Je pense que tu ... "chômage". ... qu'on n'a pas de travail.
fill.full_answer: Que signifie le mot "homage" ? - Je pense que tu veux dire "chômage". Ça signifie qu'on n'a pas de travail.

fill.question: Hélène is a multimedia designer, and she meets people who are as passionate as she is. Hélène est ... multimédia, et elle ... des gens qui sont aussi passionnés qu'elle.
fill.full_answer: Hélène est conceptrice multimédia, et elle rencontre des gens qui sont aussi passionnés qu'elle.

dialogue.french: J'ai perdu mon mari, monsieur l'agent. Il a disparu.
dialogue.english: I've lost my husband, officer. He has disappeared.

dialogue.french: Quand l'avez-vous vu pour la dernière fois ?
dialogue.english: When did you see him for the last time?

dialogue.french: Hier matin. Nous sommes partis ensemble pour faire les courses.
dialogue.english: Yesterday morning. We left together to go shopping.

dialogue.french: Moi, je suis allée à l'hypermarché près du Boulevard périphérique et lui est allé à la boulangerie.
dialogue.english: Me, I went to the hypermarket near the Boulevard Périphérique and he went to the bakery.

dialogue.french: Et depuis, je n'ai aucune nouvelle, même pas un petit coup de téléphone.
dialogue.english: And since then, I've had no news, not even a little phone call.

dialogue.french: Pouvez-vous décrire votre époux, s'il vous plaît ?
dialogue.english: Can you describe your husband, please?

dialogue.french: Il a une quarantaine d'années, mince et assez grand ; environ un mètre quatre-vingt-cinq.
dialogue.english: He's about 40, thin and quite tall - about 1 metre 85.

dialogue.french: Un visage carré avec des cheveux bruns, longs et raides, et des lunettes noires.
dialogue.english: A square face with brown hair, long and straight, and dark glasses.

dialogue.french: Comment est-il habillé ? En costume ? En jean ?
dialogue.english: How is he dressed? In a suit? In jeans?

dialogue.french: Il porte un pantalon gris et une chemise à manches courtes, une écharpe et une veste en cuir marron.
dialogue.english: He's wearing a pair of grey trousers and a short-sleeved shirt, a scarf and a brown leather jacket.
dialogue.pronunciation: ... pœhntalohn gree ... shuhmeez-ah-mœhnsh-koort ... aysharp ... vest ... kweer mahrohn

dialogue.french: Oh, et des chaussures noires et des chaussettes assorties.
dialogue.english: Oh, and black shoes with matching socks.

dialogue.french: Voilà une description vraiment détaillée, meilleure qu'une photographie !
dialogue.english: That's a really detailed description, better than a photograph!

dialogue.french: Je peux faire mieux : j'ai un petit film sur mon téléphone. Vous voulez le voir ?
dialogue.english: I can do better: I have a little film on my phone. Do you want to see it?

dialogue.french: Non, ce n'est pas la peine. Vous le décrivez très bien. Mieux que moi !
dialogue.english: No, don't bother. You describe him very well. Better than me!

dialogue.french: J'ai l'habitude de le perdre : il déteste faire du shopping et il disparaît une fois sur deux.
dialogue.english: I'm used to losing him: he hates shopping and he disappears half of the time.

dialogue.french: Mais cette-fois-ci je suis vraiment inquiète car il avait une grande valise dans une main et un billet d'avion dans l'autre.
dialogue.english: But this time, I'm really worried because he had a big suitcase in one hand and a plane ticket in the other.

dialogue.french: Aucun souci : nous allons faire de notre mieux pour le retrouver.
dialogue.english: No worries: we're going to do our best to find him.

notes.content: In formal language, monsieur and madame (but not mademoiselle) are used, along with an honorific, when addressing someone in an official position: monsieur le député, ("mister member of parliament",) madame la directrice, ("madam director".) (Un agent de police, a police officer; though masculine, l'agent can apply to officers of both sexes). Likewise, un époux / une épouse, a spouse, is used in official contexts, instead of un mari / une femme, to mean a husband / a wife.

Remember that the past participle of verbs forming their perfect tense with être (lesson 43 note 3) agrees in number and gender with the subject, not the object, of the sentence. If a plural subject comprises both a male and a female agent, as here, then the masculine takes precedence.

The stressed pronouns moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux and elles are used for emphasis, especially when a contrast is involved: Moi, j'aime le café au petit-déjeuner, mais lui préfère le thé. In this context, the pronouns do not necessarily need to be translated into English: I like coffee for breakfast but he prefers tea. Note how the stressed pronoun is followed immediately by a subject pronoun (the second clause in our example could be phrased: ... mais lui, il préfère le thé, in order to add even greater emphasis).

aucun is a useful adjective and pronoun, meaning not any, no, or none. As a determiner, it is not used in the plural and, in a negative construction, is not followed by pas: Ils n'ont aucun problème de santé, They have no health problems. It must, however, agree with the singular noun it determines: Je n'ai aucune idée, I have no idea. In informal language, a sentence with the negative is often shortened: Aucune idée.

A number of nouns are plural in French but singular in English. We already know les toilettes, the toilet, and en vacances, on holiday; another one is les cheveux, hair: Tes cheveux sont trop longs, Your hair is too long. The singular un cheveu is used in certain expressions, such as avoir un cheveu sur la langue ("to have a hair on the tongue"), to have a lisp but rarely in the physical meaning of a hair.

In contrast to note 5, some nouns are plural in English but singular in French. This rule applies in particular to "two-legged" garments, which can be singularised in French: un jean, a pair of jeans, un pantalon, a pair of trousers. Logically, the plural two pairs of trousers is translated as deux pantalons.

meilleur, the comparative form of bon (lesson 42 section 3), is an adjective and therefore agrees with its noun: J'ai une meilleure idée, I have a better idea. Ce café sert les meilleures crêpes et les meilleurs gâteaux de Bretagne, This café serves the best pancakes and the best cakes in Brittany. Don't confuse meilleur with mieux, the comparative adverb of bien, well (see lesson 42 section 3): Comment va ton frère ? - Il va mieux.

The abstract noun la peine has several meanings, ranging from sadness to difficulty. Ce n'est pas la peine is an idiomatic expression meaning something like Don't bother or Don't worry about it. Vous voulez de l'aide ? - Ce n'est pas la peine, merci. Do you want help? - Don't bother, but thanks. In a full sentence, the expression is followed by the preposition de: Ce n'est pas la peine de chercher du sucre, il n'y en a plus. There's no point looking for sugar, there's none left.

Here's another idiomatic expression. Un souci is a concern, a worry. The common interjection Aucun souci is the equivalent of No problem or Don't worry about it. An even commoner form is Pas de souci(s), in which case the noun can be in the plural.

new_words.english: do you want to see it

new_words.french: nous allons faire de notre mieux
new_words.english: we're going to do our best

translate.question: Elle porte un pantalon bleu, une chemise grise à manches longues, une veste en cuir et un sac à main.
answers: She's wearing a pair of blue trousers, a grey long-sleeved shirt, a leather jacket and a handbag.

translate.question: J'ai l'habitude de me perdre en ville. C'est pour ça que j'ai toujours mon téléphone avec moi.
answers: I usually get lost in town. That's why I always have my phone with me.

translate.question: Vous chantez très bien. Enfin, mieux que lui.
answers: You sing very well. At least, better than he does.

translate.question: Ce n'est pas la peine d'appeler Marianne, elle est déjà partie.
answers: No need to call Marianne, she has already left.

translate.question: Nous n'avons aucune nouvelle d'eux depuis une semaine, et nous sommes très inquiets.
answers: We've had no news from them for a week and we're very worried.

fill.question: When did you last see your friend? - I saw him yesterday. Quand avez-vous ... votre ... pour la dernière fois ? - Je ... hier.
fill.full_answer: Quand avez-vous vu votre ami pour la dernière fois ? - Je l'ai vu hier.

fill.question: "Chez Yannick" serves the best pancakes in town. - No, the best pancake house is "Chez Rozenn." "Chez Yannick" sert les ... crêpes de la ville. - Non, la ... crêperie, c'est "Chez Rozenn".
fill.full_answer: "Chez Yannick" sert les meilleures crêpes de la ville. - Non, la meilleure crêperie, c'est "Chez Rozenn".

fill.question: Marianne speaks Japanese better than you and me. She's the best student in the university, and we have to do better. Marianne parle le japonais ... Elle est ... étudiante de la fac, et nous ...
fill.full_answer: Marianne parle le japonais mieux que vous et moi. Elle est la meilleure étudiante de la fac, et nous devons faire mieux.

fill.question: My daughter has no health problems. At least, she has no serious illnesses. ... fille n'a aucun problème de santé. Enfin, elle n'a aucune maladie grave.
fill.full_answer: Ma fille n'a aucun problème de santé. Enfin, elle n'a aucune maladie grave.

fill.question: You're very badly dressed, my son. Your jeans are too short and your hair is too long. Tu es très ..., mon fils. Ton jean est trop ... et tes cheveux sont trop ...
fill.full_answer: Tu es très mal habillé, mon fils. Ton jean est trop court et tes cheveux sont trop longs.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: With a surface area of just 84.5 square kilometres, the city of Paris is small compared with the likes of London or Los Angeles. One of the reasons is that it is surrounded by a 35km ring road called le Boulevard périphérique (in compound proper nouns, only the first word usually takes an initial capital). By contrast, the entire conurbation, known as le Grand Paris (greater Paris), is ten times larger. The city within le Périph' -the ring-road's nickname-is often referred to as Paris intra-muros, from the Latin phrase meaning "within walls."

dialogue.french: J'ai un service à te demander : peux-tu nous aider à déménager samedi et dimanche prochains ?
dialogue.english: I have a favour to ask you: can you help us to move next Saturday and Sunday?

dialogue.french: Je vais aussi demander à Stéphane et à Olivier : plus on est nombreux, plus on s'amuse.
dialogue.english: I'm also going to ask Stéphane and Olivier: the more, the merrier.

dialogue.french: Tu vas leur demander de travailler un samedi ? Bonne chance ! Ce sont de gros paresseux.
dialogue.english: You're going to ask them to work on a Saturday? Good luck! They're big slackers.

dialogue.french: Tu pourrais éventuellement parler à Didier, mon beau-frère : il a une entreprise de déménagement.
dialogue.english: You could maybe talk to Didier, my brother-in-law: he has a removal firm.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que tu peux lui parler, toi ? Je ne le connais pas. Tu penses qu'il peut le faire gratuitement ?
dialogue.english: Can you ask him, you? I don't know him. Do you think he can do it free of charge?

dialogue.french: J'en doute, car ses affaires vont mal, mais je peux toujours essayer. Je te tiens au courant.
dialogue.english: I doubt it, because his business is going badly. But I can always try. I'll keep you posted.

dialogue.french: Mon beau-frère est d'accord pour t'aider. Je lui ai dit que tu es un de mes meilleurs amis.
dialogue.english: My brother-in-law agrees to help you. I told him that you're one of my best friends.

dialogue.french: En plus, il est aimable : il te facture seulement la location du camion et une demi-journée de travail.
dialogue.english: What's more, he's nice: he's charging you only for the rental of the lorry and a half-day's work.

dialogue.french: En gros, cela te fait quelques centaines d'euros, au lieu de mille ou plus, son tarif normal.
dialogue.english: All in all, that makes you a few hundred euros, instead of a thousand or more, his normal rate.

dialogue.french: Il est très bien, ce Didier ! C'est très gentil de sa part. Tu peux le remercier ?
dialogue.english: He's great, that Didier! It's very kind of him. Can you thank him?

dialogue.french: Tu peux lui dire toi-même : il arrive dans une heure et demie avec un collègue. On va les attendre.
dialogue.english: You can say it yourself: he's arriving in an hour and a half with a colleague. We're going to wait for them.

dialogue.french: Maintenant, au boulot ! Tout ce qui est gros et lourd va dans le camion de Didier :
dialogue.english: Now, let's get cracking! Everything that's big and heavy goes in Didier's lorry:

dialogue.french: le canapé, les fauteuils, les lits, les armoires, la machine à laver, le lave-vaisselle et la cuisinière.
dialogue.english: the sofa, the armchairs, the beds, the wardrobes, the washing machine, the dishwasher and the stove.

dialogue.french: Le reste - la vaisselle, les tableaux, la télé, le linge, les rideaux,
dialogue.english: The rest - the dishes, the paintings, the TV, the linen, the curtains,

dialogue.french: les cartons dans l'escalier et les piles de bouquins contre le mur -
dialogue.english: the boxes on the stairs and the piles of books against the wall -
dialogue.pronunciation: ... kahrtohn ... leskahleeyay ... peel ... bookahn ... kohntr ... mewr

dialogue.french: on le prend avec nous dans la camionnette. Je vais la chercher tout de suite.
dialogue.english: we're taking it with us in the van. I'm going to fetch it right away.

dialogue.french: Bon courage à tout le monde : on a une longue journée devant nous.
dialogue.english: Best of luck everyone: we have a long day ahead of us.

dialogue.french: Plus vite on finit, plus vite on peut se coucher.
dialogue.english: The quicker we finish, the quicker we can go to bed.

notes.content: The terminal -er of the masculine noun l'hiver is pronounced [air], as in cher (rather than [ay], as in aller). Don't forget that the half-vowel h is not pronounced (just like hour in English) so, with en hiver, the final n of en runs into the i of hiver: [œhneevair]. nez is pronounced [nay], like the final syllable of the second person plural of an -er verb: venez: [vuhnay].

The English comparative phrase the more ... the more has a direct equivalent in French: plus ... plus. Typically, this type of construction uses the impersonal pronoun on: Plus on gagne de l'argent, plus on dépense, The more money you earn, the more you spend. (Another well-known saying, Plus on est de fous, plus on rit -literally "The more madmen there are, the more we laugh"- is the equivalent of The more, the merrier.)

ce is both a singular demonstrative adjective (lesson 9 note 1) and a demonstrative pronoun. It can replace il/elle est (→ c'est) but also ils/elles sont, (→ ce sont), in which case it is translated by they. Ce sont deux de mes films préférés, They are two of my favourite movies. The differences between il/elle est (ils/elles sont) and c'est (ce sont) are quite complex but, in this case, c'est / ce sont describes a situation ("they're lazy") as opposed to the specific person or object.

As we know, gros / grosse means fat (lesson 14 section 5.2). But it has a much broader meaning, including big (lesson 19 line 11), thick (un gros manteau, a thick coat), and serious (une grosse erreur, a serious mistake). To translate the adjective properly or find an idiomatic equivalent, always check the noun it is qualifying: Tu es un gros paresseux ! You're a slacker/ a lazybones! The expression en gros, usually placed at the beginning of a sentence or clause, means broadly, basically, etc.

éventuellement is a faux-ami. It expresses the idea of possibility: Je ne connais pas la réponse, mais vous pouvez éventuellement la trouver en ligne, I don't know the answer but you may be able to find it online. Nicole vient à la fête demain ? - Éventuellement, Is Nicole coming to the party tomorrow? - Maybe. (The adjective is éventuel, possible). The adverb eventually is rendered finalement or enfin: Il a finalement acheté une nouvelle collection de timbres, He eventually bought a new stamp collection.)

courant is both a noun and an adjective: le courant means the current, while the adjective means ordinary, standard, common, etc. C'est un problème courant, It's a common problem. The expression être au courant, first seen in lesson 32, means to be aware of something: Ma belle-sœur arrive demain. - Oui, je suis au courant. My sister-in-law is arriving tomorrow. - Yes, I'm aware of that. Tenir au courant, to keep someone informed/posted.

Some verbs have no direct equivalent in other languages. That is the case for to charge (money). The most convenient equivalent is probably facturer, to invoice: Le plombier m'a facturé six cents euros de l'heure, The plumber charged me 600 euros an hour. Another possibility is prendre, to take, usually with a monetary amount or an adjective: Combien prend-il pour une leçon privée ? How much does he charge for a private lesson?

When demi, half, comes before a noun, it is invariable: une demi-heure, un demi-kilo. After the noun, however, it can be feminine or masculine depending on the gender -une heure et demie, un kilo et demi- but never plural. In every case, the pronunciation is identical [duhmee].

We have seen bien used as an adverb meaning well, but it can also be an adjective, usually as part of an expression before a noun: Elle est très bien, cette chanteuse, She's very good, that singer. In such cases, however, it does not agree with the subject: Les fauteuils sont très bien, The armchairs are very nice.

We know that indirect object pronouns come before the verb in French (lesson 35 section 5). One slight complication is that some verbs taking an indirect object in English require a direct object in French. Among the most common of these are chercher, to look for, to fetch, and attendre, to wait for. In this case, the noun is replaced by a direct object pronoun: J'attends Didier et son collègue → Je les attends (not Je leur attends).

le boulot is a familiar but very common word for work, for which there is no one-on-one equivalent. Unlike its English counterpart, the noun is both countable and uncountable. J'ai beaucoup de boulot en ce moment, I've got a lot of work at the moment; Jeanne commence son nouveau boulot ce lundi, Jeanne is starting her new job this Monday. The imperative expression Allez, au boulot ! means something like: Let's get cracking! Another slang word that has no equivalent is un bouquin, a book, line 15, used in a familiar register in the same way as un livre.

new_words.english: I'm going to fetch it right away

new_words.french: on a une longue journée devant nous
new_words.english: we have a long day ahead of us

new_words.french: plus vite on peut se coucher
new_words.english: the quicker we can go to bed

translate.question: On est déjà en retard. Je vais chercher le camion en attendant ta belle-sœur.
answers: We're already late. I'll go and fetch the lorry while waiting for your sister-in-law.

translate.question: Il ne fera pas le déménagement aujourd'hui, mais éventuellement demain s'il a le temps.
answers: He won't do the move today, but possibly tomorrow if he has time.

translate.question: Mon comptable m'a facturé huit cents euros, mais son tarif normal est plus cher.
answers: My accountant charged me 800 euros, but his normal rate is more expensive.

translate.question: Bon courage ! Vous avez une longue journée devant vous.
answers: Best of luck! You have a long day ahead of you.

translate.question: Vous savez tous ce que vous devez faire. Maintenant, au boulot !
answers: You all know what you have to do. Now, let's get down to work!

fill.question: Have you asked your brother-in-law? - I'm going to ask him right away. ... vous ... votre beau-frère ? - Je vais ... tout de suite.
fill.full_answer: Est-ce que vous avez demandé à votre beau-frère ? - Je vais lui demander tout de suite.

fill.question: The bus is arriving in half an hour. - But I've been waiting for it for three and a half hours! Le bus arrive ... - Mais je l'attends depuis ... !
fill.full_answer: Le bus arrive dans une demi-heure. - Mais je l'attends depuis trois heures et demie !

fill.question: But these armchairs are very nice. Why don't you like them? Mais ..., ces fauteuils. Pourquoi tu ... ?
fill.full_answer: Mais ils sont très bien, ces fauteuils. Pourquoi tu ne les aimes pas ?

fill.question: My parents agree to help us. I told them that you were my best friends. Mes parents ... pour nous aider. Je ... que vous étiez mes ...
fill.full_answer: Mes parents sont d'accord pour nous aider. Je leur ai dit que vous étiez mes meilleurs amis.

fill.question: There are about a hundred people to help us. - I'm going to thank them: the more, the merrier! Il y a ... de personnes pour nous aider. - Je vais ... : plus on est de fous, plus on rit !
fill.full_answer: Il y a une centaine de personnes pour nous aider. - Je vais les remercier : plus on est de fous, plus on rit !

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: L'argot (slang) was once the language of thieves, footpads and other ne'er-do-wells. In its current acceptance, it means a set of non-technical words and expressions used by a particular social group, whereas familiar language has a much broader reach. In this book, we have largely avoided slang, while introducing familiar words and expressions, such as un bouquin and le boulot, which are widely used across all social classes and groups. Significantly, some of these colloquialisms have no direct equivalents in English, as these two examples show.

dialogue.french: Comme il fait chaud aujourd'hui, nous allons pique-niquer dans la forêt de Fontainebleau.
dialogue.english: As it's warm today, we'll go for a picnic in the Fontainebleau forest.

dialogue.french: Honnêtement, un pique-nique ne me dit rien. C'est tellement inconfortable.
dialogue.english: Honestly, I don't feel like a picnic. It's so uncomfortable.

dialogue.french: On est mal assis par terre et on mange mal : les chips, les salades de riz dégoûtantes,
dialogue.english: You're seated uncomfortably on the ground and you eat badly: crisps, disgusting rice salads,
dialogue.pronunciation: ... mahl-assee par ter ... sheeps ... salad-duh-ree daygootœh"t

dialogue.french: des sandwichs de poulet sans goût, les bananes trop mûres - et des fourmis et des guêpes partout.
dialogue.english: chicken sandwiches with no taste, over-ripe bananas - and ants and wasps everywhere.
dialogue.pronunciation: ... sœh"dweech ... poolay ... goo ... bahnan ... mewr ... foormee ... gep ...

dialogue.french: Et puis on a du mal à manger proprement, avec les assiettes en papier, les gobelets en carton,
dialogue.english: And then it's hard to eat cleanly, with paper plates, cardboard beakers,

dialogue.french: les fourchettes en plastique et les couteaux qui ne coupent rien.
dialogue.english: plastic forks, and knives that don't cut anything.

dialogue.french: Bref, je ne supporte pas les pique-niques.
dialogue.english: In short, I can't stand picnics.

dialogue.french: Mais ce n'est pas vraiment une mauvaise idée, tu sais. Au contraire.
dialogue.english: But it's not really a bad idea, you know. On the contrary.

dialogue.french: D'abord, c'est nous qui allons préparer la nourriture, avec de bons produits sains.
dialogue.english: First, it's us who are going to prepare the food, with good, healthy produce.

dialogue.french: Ensuite, pas de problème de confort. Regarde ceci : c'est notre nouveau panier à pique-nique.
dialogue.english: Next, no comfort problems. Look at this: it's our new picnic hamper.

dialogue.french: Mais ce n'est pas un panier, c'est un sac à dos !
dialogue.english: But it's not a basket, it's a backpack!

dialogue.french: Oui, mais c'est un sac à dos de luxe ! Il y a tout ce qu'il faut pour faire de vrais repas.
dialogue.english: Yes, but a luxury backpack! There's everything you need for a good meal.

dialogue.french: Des couverts en métal, des assiettes en porcelaine, et des verres à vin et à eau.
dialogue.english: Metal cutlery, china plates, water and wine glasses.

dialogue.french: Et regarde cela. Qu'est-ce que tu en penses ?
dialogue.english: And look at that. What do you think of it?

dialogue.french: Qu'est-ce que c'est ?
dialogue.english: What is it?

dialogue.french: C'est un sac spécifique pour les bouteilles de vin blanc. Sophistiqué, non ?
dialogue.english: It's a special bag for bottles of white wine. Sophisticated, isn't it?

dialogue.french: On a aussi quatre chaises pliantes, une table basse et une vraie couverture.
dialogue.english: We also have folding chairs, a low table and a real blanket.

dialogue.french: Mais attends, qu'est-ce qui se passe ? La pluie commence à tomber.
dialogue.english: But hang on, what's happening? The rain is starting to fall.

dialogue.french: Oh, il pleut. Quel dommage ! Pas de pique-nique aujourd'hui...
dialogue.english: Oh, it's raining. What a pity! No picnic today...

notes.content: chips, meaning crisps, is a faux-ami but, above all, a foreign import. Which is why the final s is pronounced. (Remember, too that ch- is pronounced as [sh].) The word is therefore pronounced [sheeps].

We have already seen dire used idiomatically to mean to fancy, like, etc. (lesson 25 note 5). A related expression, Ça ne me dit rien, expresses a lack of interest or enthusiasm: Tu veux venir au cinéma avec nous ? - Ça ne me dit rien, Do you want to come to the movies with us? - I don't feel like it. As in our example (line 2), ça can be replaced by a noun. The same structure can also express a lack of recognition or familiarity: Ce nom ne me dit rien, I'm not familiar with that name.

When describing temperature conditions, French does not use the same verbs as English. We already know avoir chaud and avoir froid (lesson 26 note 3). Likewise, faire chaud / froid describes the ambient temperature: Il y a une grande différence de température dans cette région de France, il fait très chaud en été et il fait très froid en hiver, There is a big difference in temperature in this region of France, it is very hot in summer and very cold in winter. When talking about the weather, chaud by itself can mean both hot and warm.

Like bien (lesson 47 note 8), mal is usually an adverb -Je dors mal quand il fait chaud comme ça, I sleep badly when it's hot like that- but it can sometimes be used as an adjective. In this case, it is usually invariable and can only be used with state-of-being verbs like être: Je n'ai rien fait de mal, I haven't done anything wrong. Don't confuse mal with the adjective mauvais(e): C'est une mauvaise idée, It's a bad idea. And don't confuse avoir mal, to feel pain, with the idiomatic expression avoir du mal.

Remember that the adjective describes the head noun, in this case une salade, and not the complement, de riz. It therefore has to agree in the feminine plural. For example, une salade de fruits délicieuse, a delicious fruit salad. This rule applies to all such compound nouns. (Note that English, which has no grammatical genders, is less precise than French in this respect: does delicious apply to fruit, salad or both? If we want to insist on the first noun, we would have to say a salad of delicious fruit, whereas French makes the distinction through adjective agreement une salade de fruits délicieux.)
The preposition en is used between two nouns to describe what the first object is made of: une assiette en carton, a cardboard plate, deux tasses en porcelaine, two china cups, etc.
supporter, to support, also means to tolerate. The verb is often used idiomatically in the negative: Je ne supporte pas cette émission, I can't stand that show.

When a BAGS adjective (lesson 14 section 5.2) comes before a plural noun, the partitive article des becomes de (or d' before a vowel). For example, Nous achetons des produits sains, We buy healthy produce, but Nous achetons de bons produits sains, We buy good healthy produce.

Ceci and cela (line 14) are demonstrative pronouns meaning this, that, and, in impersonal sentences, it. Ceci is not widely used in everyday language, except when referring to something that is about to be mentioned or presented: Écoutez ceci et dites-moi ce que vous en pensez, Listen to this and tell me what you think. Cela is usually abbreviated to ça in informal language: Ça te fait quelques centaines d'euros ...

à is used in compound nouns to indicate the purpose of a designated object, whereas English strings two nouns together: un panier à pique-nique, a picnic basket, un sac à dos, a backpack ("a bag to the back"). Likewise, un verre à eau, a water glass, etc. Be careful not to confuse à with de (of) in this type of noun: un verre à vin, a wine glass, un verre de vin, a glass of wine. The difference can be disappointing...

passer means to pass, which we have seen in lesson 40 line 12, as well as in the idiom passer un coup de fil (lesson 41 note 4). But se passer (literally: "to pass oneself") is a reflexive verb. In this context, the idiom se passer means to happen: Qu'est-ce qui se passe ?, What's happening? / What's going on?
