translate.question: Est-ce qu'il fait chaud dehors ? Moi, j'ai froid : regarde mes mains !
answers: Is it hot outside? Personally, I'm cold: look at my hands!

translate.question: Qu'est-ce que c'est que ce bruit ? Qu'est-ce qui se passe ?
answers: What's that noise? What's going on?

translate.question: Nous avons tout ce qu'il faut pour faire un vrai café mais ça ne me dit rien.
answers: We've got everything needed to make a real coffee but I don't feel like one.

translate.question: Elle n'aime pas manger dans les assiettes en papier. C'est pour ça qu'elle ne supporte pas les pique-niques.
answers: She doesn't like eating off paper plates. That's why she can't stand picnics.

translate.question: Leur idée n'est pas bonne ; au contraire, elle est très mauvaise.
answers: Their idea isn't good. On the contrary, it's very bad.

fill.question: These folding chairs are so uncomfortable! We're really badly seated. Ces ... sont tellement inconfortables ! On est très mal assis.
fill.full_answer: Ces chaises pliantes sont tellement inconfortables ! On est très mal assis.

fill.question: We ate a salad of fresh fruit served on real china plates. Nous avons mangé une ..., servie sur ...
fill.full_answer: Nous avons mangé une salade de fruits frais, servie sur de vraies assiettes en porcelaine.

fill.question: Come here and listen to this. It's an Indian singer. Do you know him? - No, I'm not familiar with his name. Viens ici et écoute ceci. C'est un chanteur indien. Tu le connais ? - Non, son nom ne me dit rien.
fill.full_answer: Viens ici et écoute ceci. C'est un chanteur indien. Tu le connais ? - Non, son nom ne me dit rien.

fill.question: It's too cold for a picnic and it's raining. - What a pity! Il fait trop froid pour un pique-nique, et il pleut ! - Quel dommage !
fill.full_answer: Il fait trop froid pour un pique-nique, et il pleut ! - Quel dommage !

fill.question: I find it hard to see the difference between the right and the wrong answers. J'ai du mal à voir la différence entre les bonnes et les mauvaises réponses.
fill.full_answer: J'ai du mal à voir la différence entre les bonnes et les mauvaises réponses.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Fifty kilometres southeast of Paris, the city of Fontainebleau is famed for its huge forest, now classified as a national park, and its royal palace, le Château de Fontainebleau, dating from the 12th century.

dialogue.french: Quel est le monument le plus visité de France ?
dialogue.english: What is the most visited monument in France?

dialogue.french: Nous avons cherché sur Internet, mais il y a pas mal de réponses différentes,
dialogue.english: We've searched the internet, but there are quite a lot of different answers,

dialogue.french: ce qui nous fait penser que notre question est mal posée.
dialogue.english: which makes us think that our question is badly phrased.

dialogue.french: En effet, ce n'est pas la bonne question. Vous n'avez pas assez réfléchi.
dialogue.english: Indeed, it's not the right question. You haven't thought enough about it.

dialogue.french: Ce qu'il faut chercher est "quel est le monument le plus visité cette année ?".
dialogue.english: What you have to search for is "which is the most visited monument this year?".

dialogue.french: Regardez l'écran : "Le Louvre reçoit plus de visiteurs que la Tour Eiffel".
dialogue.english: Look at the screen: "The Louvre receives more visitors than the Eiffel Tower".

dialogue.french: Et juste en-dessous "Le monument préféré des Français est le Château de Fontainebleau".
dialogue.english: And just below "The monument preferred by the French is the Fontainebleau château".

dialogue.french: Je suis aussi surprise que vous par Fontainebleau. À mon avis, c'est certainement le Louvre.
dialogue.english: I'm as surprised as you are by Fontainebleau. In my opinion, it's certainly the Louvre.

dialogue.french: Moi, le musée que j'aime le plus est Orsay. Il a la meilleure collection de tableaux impressionnistes du monde.
dialogue.english: Me, the museum that I like best is Orsay. It has the best collection of Impressionist paintings in the world.

dialogue.french: Peut-être, mais le Louvre est plus grand et plus intéressant. Plus je le visite, plus je le trouve superbe.
dialogue.english: Perhaps, but the Louvre is bigger and more interesting. The more I visit it, the more I find it superb.

dialogue.french: Et la Tour Eiffel ? À mon avis, c'est elle qui reçoit le plus grand nombre de visiteurs. Même moi, j'y suis allé !
dialogue.english: And the Eiffel Tower? In my opinion, it's the one that gets the biggest number of visitors. Even I have been!

dialogue.french: Eh non, désolée. J'ai trouvé les dernières informations qui circulent. Le gagnant est ... Le Louvre !
dialogue.english: No, sorry. I've found the latest information. The winner is ... the Louvre!

dialogue.french: Bien sûr. Nous, les Français, nous apprécions les artistes plutôt que les ingénieurs.
dialogue.english: Of course. We French, we appreciate artists rather than engineers.

dialogue.french: Pensez ce que vous voulez, Michel, mais la raison est plus simple : le billet d'entrée du musée est moins cher que celui du monument.
dialogue.english: Think what you like, Michel, but the reason is simple: the ticket for the museum is cheaper than the one for the monument.

notes.content: Verbs conjugated with être instead of avoir in the perfect tense: 1.1 The verbs: A group of verbs expressing either movement or a change of place or condition form their perfect tense with être instead of avoir. There are sixteen main verbs, which can be remembered with a simple mnemonic: meet Dr and Mrs Vandertramp: Devenir (to become); Revenir (to come back); Mourir (to die); Retourner (to return); Sortir (to go out); Venir (to come); Arriver (to arrive); Naître (to be born); Descendre (to come down, descend); Entrer (to enter); Rentrer (to come back); Tomber (to fall); Rester (to rest); Aller (to go); Monter (to go up); Partir (to leave). Five of these have irregular past participles: venir → venu, and its derivatives devenir → devenu and revenir → revenu, as well as naître → né and mourir → mort (see lesson 43 note 4). There are other members of this family, notably the verb passer, to pass, which can be both transitive and intransitive. We'll see these later on. 1.2 Conjugation: All these verbs are conjugated with être instead of avoir in the perfect tense. This edifying little story might serve as another mnemonic (or a cautionary tale). Ce lundi, Paul est arrivé très en retard pour son rendez-vous. Il est entré dans l'immeuble en courant et puis est monté au deuxième étage. Personne. Il est descendu au premier étage. Toujours personne. Il est retourné à l'accueil, où on lui a dit. "Votre client ? Mais il est parti il y a une heure. Il est resté longtemps dans votre bureau mais vous n'êtes pas venu. Il est devenu très impatient et il est sorti en colère. Je pense qu'il est rentré chez lui. Ou il est peut-être allé au café en face. Une chose est sûre : il n'est pas revenu ici". Paul est sorti tellement vite du bâtiment qu'il est tombé sous un camion. Pauvre Paul : il est né pour travailler mais il est mort au travail. This Monday, Paul arrived very late for his appointment. He ran into the building and then went upstairs to the second floor. No one was there. He went down to the first floor. Still no one. He went back to the reception desk, where he was told: "Your client? He left an hour ago. He was in your office for a long time, but you didn't show up. He became very impatient and came out in an angry mood. I think he went home. Or maybe he went to the cafe opposite. One thing is sure: he didn't come back here." Paul ran out of the building so fast that he fell under a lorry. Poor Paul: he was born to work but he died at work. The past participle of these être-conjugated verbs agrees in number and gender with the subject of the sentence. Returning to the above example: Marie est arrivée très en retard pour son rendez-vous, Mary arrived very late for her appointment. Les deux hommes sont montés au deuxième étage, The two men went up to the second floor. Les deux femmes sont retournées à l'accueil, The two women went back to the reception desk. If the plural subjects of an être verb include a masculine and a feminine noun, then the participle agrees in the masculine: Paul et sa femme sont partis après le diner. Paul and his wife left after dinner. Les hommes et les femmes sont arrivés au même moment. The men and the women arrived at the same time. However, this centuries-old rule on masculine precedence -Le masculin l'emporte sur le féminin, The masculine takes precedence over the feminine- is increasingly called into question in today's more egalitarian world, and la règle de proximité, proximity rule, whereby adjectives (including participles) agree with the gender of the nearest noun, is making significant headway. Moreover, there is an ongoing debate about abolishing the rule on past-participle agreement altogether. In the meantime, however, we will stick to the established rule, albeit unenthusiastically.

Stressed, or emphatic, pronouns: These pronouns also exist in English but are not always used in the same way. moi (I, me), toi (you), lui (he, him), elle (she, her), nous (we, us), vous (you), eux (they, them - masc.), elles (they, them - fem.). They are used if the personal pronoun is emphasized (hence emphatic) or contrasted with another pronoun (or noun), which they generally precede. Toi, tu es un vrai ami, You're a real friend. Alain adore les pique-niques mais moi, je ne les supporte pas, Alain loves picnics but I can't stand them. Stressed pronouns are also used in some imperative -or emphatic- constructions: Fais-le, toi ! You do it! Trouvez-la moi, Find her/it for me. The pronouns can also be used with a multiple subject: Ma femme et moi, nous aimons travailler ensemble, My wife and I like working together. In these examples, we can see that the pronoun seems disconnected from the sentence and could, theoretically, be omitted (which is why they are also called disjunctive pronouns). In French they are used almost systematically, which is why it's important to learn them in context. Finally, there is an impersonal third-person emphatic pronoun, soi, which we will see later on.

Relative pronouns: 3.1 qui and que: Qui and que are used to avoid repeating a noun or pronoun. Learners often confuse the two because they can refer both to people and to things. First and foremost, it is important to avoid the common mistake of systematically translating qui as who and que as that. Think grammatically: qui is the subject and que the direct object. In a subordinate clause, the pronouns are equivalent to who (or whom), which and that. • Qui: The subject of a sentence is often a person, but not always: L'homme qui nous aide pour le déménagement est mon beau-frère. The man who is helping us with the removal is my brother-in-law. J'ai horreur des couteaux qui ne coupent pas. I hate knives that don't cut. Since qui refers to the subject, it is always followed by a verb. It can also be used with a preposition to replace an indirect object: Nous sommes une société à qui vous pouvez faire confiance. We're a company that you can trust. La société pour qui j'ai travaillé n'existe plus. The company that I worked for no longer exists. (In informal English, the preposition whom has all but disappeared, yet it is the equivalent of qui in this type of construction. Moreover, English prepositions are often placed alongside the verb ("the company that I work for" rather than "the company for which I work"). In French however, the preposition and the relative always stay together. Which is why, to an English-speaking ear, French often sounds formal. • Que: This pronoun replaces the direct object (thing or person) in a relative clause and is followed by a noun or pronoun: La Tour Eiffel, que tout le monde connaît, est située près de la Seine. The Eiffel Tower, which everyone knows, is located near the Seine. Les personnes que nous allons rencontrer aujourd'hui vont nous aider. The people who[m] we are going to meet today are going to help us. Before a vowel or the letter h, que become qu': La banane est le fruit qu'elle aime le moins. The banana is the fruit that she likes least; La chose qu'Henri déteste le plus est la pluie. The thing (that) Henri hates the most is the rain. In every case, the relative pronouns and their subsequent clauses must be placed immediately after the antecedent, i.e. the word or words that they replace (la tour, les personnes, le morceau, etc.) 3.2 ce qui and ce que: Like qui and que, the indefinite relative pronouns ce qui and ce que introduce a subordinate clause. They mean which/what and are used in sentences where the noun or phrase referred to by a pronoun is not expressed. Ce qui relates to the phrase that is the subject of the verb: Mon mari a disparu, ce qui m'inquiète, My husband has disappeared, which worries me. In this sentence, ce qui replaces the noun phrase his disappearance worries me. Ce que relates to the object of the verb: Je ne comprends pas ce que vous voulez dire. I don't understand what you mean. The pronoun is often used for emphasis at the beginning of a sentence, just like What: Ce qu'il veut dire est que nous sommes en retard. What he means is that we're late. Note that only ce que is abbreviated to qu' before a vowel.

Singular versus plural: Some nouns that are always singular in French are plural in English, and vice versa (lesson 46). In some cases, a French noun exists in both the singular and the plural (i.e. it is countable), for instance une information, a piece of information, les informations, information in general (or the news, see below). One of the most obvious examples concerns garments "with two legs." In addition to un pantalon, this rule also applies to un collant, tights; un jean, jeans; un maillot de bain, swimming trunks; un pyjama, pyjamas; un short, shorts; and un slip, underpants. To form the plural -for example, three pairs of shorts- simply add an s to the noun: trois shorts. Other common examples of this singular-to-plural shift include l'actualité, current affairs, l'escalier, the stairs, la politique, politics, and la vaisselle, dishes; as well as certain sciences, such as la physique, physics and la linguistique, linguistics. Conversely, nouns that are generally plural in French but singular in English include les affaires, business; les conseils, advice; les toilettes, the toilet; les bagages, luggage, and les connaissances, knowledge. It is important to remember these nouns because, in some cases, the singular and plural forms can have slightly different meanings. For example, les affaires, business, une affaire, a firm; la recherche, research, les recherches, searches; or l'actualité, current affairs, les actualités, the news (bulletin) -a synonym of les informations. We'll see some more examples in upcoming lessons.

Prepositions: Prepositions are vitally important because they connect different parts of a sentence and alter the meaning of verbs. French has no equivalent of the phrasal verbs that are so hard to learn in English (go up, down, in, out, through, etc.) but even so it is important to learn the preposition that normally accompanies a verb, especially where there is a difference between English and French: écouter, to listen to, for example. Here is a table showing the main prepositions (some of which, marked with an asterisk, are also adverbs). You have already seen all of them. à to: Passe le carton à Jérôme. Pass the box to Jérôme. après* after: Après vous, madame. After you, madam. avant* before: Venez avant neuf heures. Come before 9 o'clock. avec* with: Elle sort avec mon meilleur ami. She's going out with my best friend. chez to/at (the home of): Je reste chez moi ce soir. I'm staying at home this evening. contre* against: Le tableau est contre le mur. The painting is against the wall. dans in: Ils connaissent un bon restaurant. They know a good restaurant. de of,from: Sorya vient de Marseille. Sorya is from Marseille. depuis* since, from: Je suis là depuis ce matin. I have been here since this morning. derrière* behind: Le magasin est derrière l'église. The shop is behind the church. devant* in front of: Le Louvre est devant vous. The Louvre is in front of you. en in: Êtes-vous libre en avril ou en mai ? Are you free in April or May? entre between: Il y a un match entre la France et l'Espagne. There's a match between France and Spain. par by: J'ai réussi à la joindre par téléphone. I managed to reach her by phone. pendant during, for: Elle a réfléchi pendant quelques secondes. She thought for a few seconds. pour for: Avez-vous un secret pour le loto ? Do you have a secret for the lotto? près de near: Ils sont assis près de la fenêtre. They're sitting near/by the window. sous under: Tes clés sont sous la table. Your keys are under the table. sur on: On a fait des recherches sur Internet. We searched on the Internet. vers towards: Il va vers la gare. He's going towards the station. Use this table to revise the prepositions from time to time, until you can recognise them instantly.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: You have now completed the receptive phase of the method. Lesson 50 marks the beginning of the productive phase, when you start to generate your own phrases and sentences. When you reach the end of a new lesson from 50 to 100, go back to the beginning of the book and study a lesson you have already seen, as instructed, and translate the main dialogue and the first exercise into French. For more information, see the Introduction. Maintenant, continuons (Now, let's continue!).

dialogue.french: Que faites-vous dans la vie ?
dialogue.english: What do you do for a living?

dialogue.french: Je m'ennuie. Tous les jours, sans exception, je fais exactement la même chose.
dialogue.english: I get bored. Every day, without exception I do exactly the same thing.

dialogue.french: Je me réveille à la même heure et je me connecte à Internet pour lire les messages dans ma boîte de réception.
dialogue.english: I wake up at the same time and I log on to the internet to read the messages in my inbox.

dialogue.french: Si j'ai vraiment envie, j'écoute les informations à la radio - je me méfie des journaux télévisés
dialogue.english: If I really want to, I listen to the news on the radio - I'm wary of TV news broadcasts

dialogue.french: car je trouve qu'ils se trompent constamment et, de toute façon, les infos me dépriment.
dialogue.english: because I find that they constantly get things wrong and, in any case, the news depresses me.

dialogue.french: Moins on les écoute, plus on est tranquille.
dialogue.english: The less you listen to it, the calmer you feel.

dialogue.french: De temps à autre, je lis un magazine sur ma tablette, mais je le finis en cinq minutes.
dialogue.english: From time to time, I read a magazine on my tablet, but I finish it in five minutes.

dialogue.french: Au bout d'un quart d'heure, je me lève très lentement - je ne me dépêche pas -
dialogue.english: After half an hour, I get up very slowly - I don't hurry -

dialogue.french: puis je vais dans la salle de bains, où je me rase, me lave et me brosse les dents.
dialogue.english: then I go into the bathroom, where I shave, wash, and brush my teeth.

dialogue.french: Ensuite je m'habille et me prépare à partir au travail, en centre-ville.
dialogue.english: Then I get dressed and prepare to leave for work, in the town centre.

dialogue.french: J'y vais en voiture, jamais en bus ou en métro, et j'y suis en vingt minutes.
dialogue.english: I go by car, never by bus or by metro, and I'm there in twenty minutes.

dialogue.french: En arrivant, je m'installe dans mon bureau et ferme la porte à clé.
dialogue.english: When I arrive, I settle in my office and lock the door.

dialogue.french: Je fais semblant de travailler, mais j'essaie surtout de ne pas m'endormir.
dialogue.english: I pretend to work, but I try above all not to fall asleep.

dialogue.french: Le travail, c'est dur, vous savez. Je me demande si je suis fait pour ça.
dialogue.english: Work is very hard, you know. I wonder whether I'm made for it.

dialogue.french: C'est très stressant de ne rien faire. Je dois me détendre autant que possible.
dialogue.english: It's very stressful doing nothing. I must relax as much as possible.

dialogue.french: Mon médecin m'a conseillé de ne pas m'énerver et de me reposer quand je peux.
dialogue.english: My doctor advised me not to get annoyed and to rest when I can.

dialogue.french: Mais vous vous amusez quand même un peu ? En fait, quel métier faites-vous ?
dialogue.english: But you enjoy yourself a little anyway? In fact, what job do you do?

dialogue.french: Je suis Président-directeur général d'une des entreprises de mon père.
dialogue.english: I'm Chairman and Managing Director of one of my father's companies.

dialogue.french: Ça ne m'étonne pas !
dialogue.english: That doesn't surprise me!

notes.content: tranquille: Despite the seemingly feminine ending (-lle), the adjective tranquille is masculine, so the last syllable is pronounced [-eel].

This lesson looks at pronominal verbs and, more specifically, reflexive verbs, where the subject is the person or thing performing the action. Indicated by se (or s' in the infinitive: s'ennuyer, for example), these verbs always take reflexive pronouns in French, though not necessarily in English. Thus the transitive verb ennuyer quelqu'un, to bore someone, requires a pronoun in the reflexive form: je m'ennuie, which translates as I am bored ("I bore myself") or, in some contexts, I get bored. Likewise, Je connecte mon ordinateur à mon téléphone, I'm connecting my computer to my phone, requires a pronoun if the subject and object are the same person: Je me connecte à Internet, I'm connecting to the internet.

à with the definite article (au with a masculine noun) translates on in the case of communications media such as the TV, radio or phone: à la radio, à la télévision, au téléphone. Be careful not to use sur.

se méfier (de) means to mistrust or be suspicious of (it is the negative form of se fier à, to trust). Elle se méfie des informations qu'elle lit en ligne, She mistrusts the news that she reads online. (Remember: de becomes des because informations is plural). The imperative Méfiez-vous ! is commonly used as a warning - Méfiez-vous du chien !, Beware of the dog - and in certain proverbs: Il faut se méfier de l'eau qui dort ("You need to be wary of water that sleeps"), i.e. Still waters run deep.
Here is another apocope (lesson 17 note 7): les informations, the news, is shortened in everyday speech to les infos.

me is one of the reflexive pronouns, along with te, se (the same as the infinitive), nous and vous. But it is also a direct object pronoun (lesson 24 note 6). For example, Je me réveille à midi, I wake up at noon, but Le bruit me réveille toutes les nuits, The noise wakes me every night.

en, as we know, can be both an adverbial pronoun (lesson 31 note 2) and a preposition meaning on, in, to, etc. Note how it is used in this lesson, and how it contrasts with dans (lines 3 and 12). In particular, en is used with modes of transport -en bus, en voiture, etc.- but not for walking: aller à pied (lesson 25 line 10). We'll look at en vs. dans in greater detail in the next revision lesson.

Many verbs that are reflexive in French but not in English concern everyday actions such as getting up (se lever), shaving (se raser), dressing (s'habiller), brushing one's teeth (se brosser les dents), but also taking a shower (se doucher), applying make-up (se maquiller) and brushing one's hair (se brosser les cheveux).
See lesson 42 section 1.1.

The noun semblant, the origin of semblance, is commonly used in the expression faire semblant (de), to pretend or act as if. Mon enfant fait semblant de ne pas m'entendre quand je dis non, My child pretends not to hear me when I say no.

demander (à), to ask, is used in the pronominal form, se demander, to mean to wonder ("to ask oneself"): Je me demande ce qu'il faut faire, I wonder what to do. The verb is rarely used in the negative.
Note that the English idiom I don't wonder means I'm not surprised and is translated with the verb s'étonner (the root of to astonish): Ça ne m'étonne pas, That doesn't surprise me.

new_words.english: what do you do for a living

new_words.french: je fais exactement la même chose
new_words.english: I do exactly the same thing
I log on to the internet

new_words.french: je trouve qu'ils se trompent constamment
new_words.english: I find that they constantly get things wrong
the less you listen to them

new_words.french: je le finis en cinq minutes
new_words.english: I finish it in five minutes
after a quarter of an hour

new_words.french: je vais dans la salle de bains
je ferme la porte à clé
si je suis fait pour ça
vous vous amusez quand même un peu
new_words.english: you enjoy yourself a little anyway

new_words.french: d'une des entreprises de mon père
new_words.english: of one of my father's companies

translate.question: Quand je me lève, je me douche, ensuite je me maquille, et enfin je me brosse les cheveux.
answers: When I get up, I shower, then I put on my makeup and, lastly, I brush my hair.

translate.question: Plus on écoute les infos, moins on est tranquille. - Et ça t'étonne ?
answers: The more you listen to the news, the less easy you feel. - And that surprises you?

translate.question: Je sais que le travail est un peu stressant, mais détendez-vous !
answers: I know the work is a bit stressful, but relax!

translate.question: Ils s'ennuient, car ils font la même chose tous les jours et toutes les semaines.
answers: They're bored because they do the same thing, every day and every week.

translate.question: Cette journaliste se trompe de temps à autre, mais ses articles sont toujours intéressants.
answers: That journalist gets things wrong from time to time, but her articles are always interesting.

fill.question: He wakes up very early and logs on immediately to his inbox. Il ... très tôt et ... tout de suite ... de réception.
fill.full_answer: Il se réveille très tôt et se connecte tout de suite à sa boîte de réception.

fill.question: Are you going by metro or by car? - I'm going there on foot. ... tu y vas en métro ou en voiture ? - J'y vais à pied.
fill.full_answer: Est-ce que tu y vas en métro ou en voiture ? - J'y vais à pied.

fill.question: When he arrives, he goes into his office, closes the door and pretends to work. ... , il va dans son bureau, ferme la porte et fait semblant de travailler.
fill.full_answer: En arrivant, il va dans son bureau, ferme la porte et fait semblant de travailler.

fill.question: Guy doesn't get worked up. He rests when he can, and he falls asleep easily in the evening. Guy ... Il ... quand il peut, et il ... facilement le soir.
fill.full_answer: Guy ne s'énerve pas. Il se repose quand il peut, et il s'endort facilement le soir.

fill.question: They're pretending to be bored. Still waters run deep! Ils font semblant de s'ennuyer. Il faut se méfier de l'eau qui dort !
fill.full_answer: Ils font semblant de s'ennuyer. Il faut se méfier de l'eau qui dort !

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Job titles are often hard to translate because corporate structures differ from one country to another. Also, since English is widely used in the business world, many French companies anglicise to some extent their in-house vocabulary. Thus a firm may have both un directeur and un manager, who have different positions even though the second title is the translation of the first! But in most public companies, the top job is the same: le Directeur général, or managing director (also known as the chief executive). In some companies, le DG [day-zhay] as he (or she) is often called, can also be the chair of the company, in which case the title is le Président-directeur général, ("chairman and managing director") or le PDG [pay-day-zhay]. Note that most of the nouns used in French corporate job titles are still masculine, even if the person holding the office is female. Today we start the productive phase of our method, as explained at the end of lesson 49. Productive phase: 1st lesson

dialogue.french: Tout le monde est là ? Non, il manque Brice. Où est-il ?
dialogue.english: Is everyone there? No, Brice is missing. Where is he?

dialogue.french: Toujours au lit, mais ne l'attendons pas. Tu sais très bien qu'il ne se lève jamais avant midi.
dialogue.english: Still in bed, but let's not wait for him. You know very well that he never gets up before noon.

dialogue.french: Dépêchons-nous ! Nous perdons du temps.
dialogue.english: Let's hurry up! We're wasting time.

dialogue.french: La pièce commence dans vingt minutes et nous allons manquer le début.
dialogue.english: The play starts in twenty minutes and we're going to miss the beginning.

dialogue.french: On n'est pas encore prêts et le théâtre n'est pas tout près. Comment faire ?
dialogue.english: We're not yet ready and the theatre isn't very close by. What shall we do?

dialogue.french: Il est trop tard pour y aller en transports en commun, donc on va prendre la voiture.
dialogue.english: It's too late to go there by public transport, so we're going to take the car.

dialogue.french: Nous y serons en dix minutes si nous partons dans deux minutes.
dialogue.english: We'll be there in ten minutes if we leave in two minutes.

dialogue.french: Tu plaisantes ! On n'a pas le droit de se garer dans les rues autour du centre.
dialogue.english: You're joking! You're not allowed to park in the streets around the centre.

dialogue.french: Ne vous inquiétez pas : c'est facile comme tout.
dialogue.english: Don't worry: it's as easy as anything.

dialogue.french: Je m'arrête dix secondes dans une place de livraison mais je n'arrête pas le moteur.
dialogue.english: I stop in a delivery space but I don't turn off the engine.

dialogue.french: Vous descendez vite et je vais garer la voiture dans le parking public qui se trouve du côté droit du tribunal.
dialogue.english: You get out quickly and I go to park the car in the public carpark on the right side of the court house.

dialogue.french: Pendant ce temps, vous achetez les billets et on se retrouve dans le foyer.
dialogue.english: Meanwhile, you buy the tickets and we meet up in the foyer.

dialogue.french: Bon, on y va. Bougez-vous !
dialogue.english: Right, let's go. Move yourselves!

dialogue.french: Euh, attendez un instant. Il y a un petit problème.
dialogue.english: Um, wait a moment. There's a slight problem.

dialogue.french: Qu'est-ce qui se passe ? Nous allons être en retard !
dialogue.english: What's happening? We're going to be late!

dialogue.french: Je ne trouve pas mes clés de voiture.
dialogue.english: I can't find my car keys.

dialogue.french: Je ne me souviens pas où je les ai mises. Elles ne sont pas dans ma poche,
dialogue.english: I don't remember where I put them. They're not in my pocket,

dialogue.french: ni dans le tiroir du meuble en bas de l'escalier, où je les mets d'habitude.
dialogue.english: nor in the drawer of the piece of furniture at the bottom of the stairs, where I usually put them.

dialogue.french: Pourquoi souris-tu ?
dialogue.english: Why are you smiling?

dialogue.french: Parce qu'elles sont dans ta main, idiot ! Vite, dépêche-toi. En route !
dialogue.english: Because they're in your hand, dummy! Quick, hurry up. Let's get moving!

notes.content: Despite the different spellings, the adjective prêt (ready) and the adverb près (near) are pronounced identically [pray]. The c of the adjective and noun second(e) is pronounced as a hard "g" [suhgoh"/suhgoh"d].

We saw manquer, to miss, in lesson 27 note 2 (and below, in line 4). Here, however, the verb is used in the impersonal form, so il can be translated in different ways: Il manque un mot dans cette phrase, There's a word missing in this sentence. The pronoun is invariable, even if the subject is plural: Il manque trois personnes, Three people are missing. Compare this with Où est-il ?, were il, of course, means he.

As we saw in the previous lesson, the negative of a reflexive verb is formed by placing the particle ne before the pronoun and pas (or an adverb, lesson 32 note 5) after the verb: Je ne me souviens pas où tu habites, I don't remember where you live, Ils ne s'arrêtent jamais de travailler ! They never stop working!

The imperative form of reflexive verbs depends on whether the sentence is negative or affirmative. In the negative, the pronoun comes before the verb, as usual: Ne nous inquiétons pas, Let's not worry. In the affirmative, however, the pronoun comes afterwards: Dépêchez-vous !, Hurry up!. Remember also that te becomes toi in an affirmative command: Dépêche-toi !; but it does not change if the command is negative: Ne te dépêche pas.

Comment faire ? (literally "How to do?") is a useful, short way of asking what to do or how to do something. It can be used alone -J'ai perdu mon mot de passe. Comment faire ? I've lost my password. What should I do?- or with a complement: Comment faire pour fermer une boîte de dialogue ? What do you do to close a dialogue box?

You know some of the differences between en and dans, summarised in lesson 50. With an expression of time, the difference is quite subtle but very important. While en expresses the time taken to do something -Je peux le faire en cinq minutes, I can do it in five minutes- dans indicates when the action takes, or will take, place: Elle peut le faire dans cinq minutes, She can do it in five minutes' time. Look again at sentence 7: en describes the duration of the trip to the cinema whereas dans specifies when that trip will begin.

le droit means the law (lesson 43 line 7) but also the right (lesson 20 line 6). Thus the expression avoir le droit (de) means to have the right (to) but also, in everyday speech, to be allowed to: Vous n'avez pas le droit de vous garer ici, You're not allowed to park here. (Remember that droit is also an adjective, meaning right, as opposed to gauche, left.)

Here is an example of a verb used in the transitive and the reflexive forms: Où est-ce que tu gares ta voiture ? Where do you park your car? If we drop the direct object of the sentence (i.e if the subject and object are the same), we use a reflexive: Où est-ce que tu te gares ?, Where do you park/Where are you parking?

Some verbs have a slightly different meaning when used in the reflexive form. In this case, Je m'arrête means I pull up (in a car, for example) whereas J'arrête le moteur means I turn off the engine. Similarly, se trouver ("to find itself/oneself"), the reflexive form of trouver, means more or less the same as to be: Trouville se trouve en face de Deauville, Trouville is opposite Deauville.

retrouver (literally "to find again") means to find something that one has lost. But it is often translated simply by to find: J'ai retrouvé mes clés, I've found my keys. The reflexive form generally means to meet up with: On se retrouve au restaurant à vingt heures, We're meeting up at the restaurant at 8pm.

We know that the pronominal verb se passer means to happen (lesson 48 note 10). The actual translation will depend on the context, though, and is often very simple: La fête se passe chez nous demain soir, The party is at our place tomorrow evening.

dans les rues autour du centre
new_words.english: in the streets around the centre
I don't turn off the engine
I'm going to park the car

new_words.english: which is on the right side

new_words.french: on se retrouve dans le foyer
new_words.english: we meet up in the foyer

new_words.french: il y a un petit problème

new_words.english: I can't find my car keys

new_words.french: elles ne sont pas dans ma poche
du meuble en bas de l'escalier
new_words.english: of the piece of furniture at the bottom of the stairs

new_words.french: parce qu'elles sont dans ta main

translate.question: Tu n'as pas le droit de te garer du côté gauche de la rue. Gare-toi en face du tribunal.
answers: You're not allowed to park on the left-hand side of the street. Park opposite the courthouse.

translate.question: Je ne retrouve pas mon grand panier bleu. Je ne sais pas où je l'ai mis.
answers: I can't find my big blue basket. I don't know where I put it.

translate.question: Tout le monde est prêt à partir ? - Non, il manque Jade et Camille.
answers: Is everyone ready to leave? - No, Jade and Camille are missing.

translate.question: Je ne me souviens pas comment faire pour ne pas perdre mes messages.
answers: I don't remember what to do in order not to lose my messages.

translate.question: Où se passe la fête demain soir ? - Chez Simone.
answers: Where is the party tomorrow night? - At Simone's.

fill.question: He is stopping for a couple of seconds in front of the supermarket but he isn't turning off the engine. Il ... deux secondes devant le supermarché mais il ... le moteur.
fill.full_answer: Il s'arrête deux secondes devant le supermarché mais il n'arrête pas le moteur.

fill.question: What's going on? - I forgot to buy the tickets. - You're kidding? ... - ... d'acheter les billets. - ... ?
fill.full_answer: Qu'est-ce qui se passe ? - J'ai oublié d'acheter les billets. - Tu plaisantes ?

fill.question: He has lost his car keys. - I think that they are on the piece of furniture where he usually puts them. Il a perdu ses ... - Je pense qu'elles sont sur le meuble où il les met d'habitude.
fill.full_answer: Il a perdu ses clés de voiture. - Je pense qu'elles sont sur le meuble où il les met d'habitude.

fill.question: Where do you park your car? - I park in the street, beneath my place. Où ... votre voiture ? - Je ... dans la rue, en bas de chez moi.
fill.full_answer: Où est-ce que vous garez votre voiture ? - Je me gare dans la rue, en bas de chez moi.

fill.question: We can walk there in an hour and a half. - In that case, let's leave in ten minutes. On peut y aller à pied en une heure et demie. - Dans ce cas, partons dans dix minutes.
fill.full_answer: On peut y aller à pied en une heure et demie. - Dans ce cas, partons dans dix minutes.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Simple verbs like être and avoir are often shunned - especially in written French- in favour of more complex synonyms such as se trouver and posséder, which have the same meaning but sound more "elegant" than simply be or have. Thus Ils possèdent une grande maison is preferred to Ils ont une grande maison, even though the two sentences are synonymous. This stylistic habit, which underlines the impression of formality discussed in Lesson 7, can be disconcerting at first, but it soon becomes second nature. Productive phase: 2nd lesson

dialogue.french: J'ai accepté de répondre à un sondage sur "les attentes des Français dans le domaine de l'audiovisuel".
dialogue.english: I've agreed to answer a survey on "the expectations of the French in the field of broadcasting."

dialogue.french: Voulez-vous participer ou, au moins, donner votre point de vue sur cette première question :
dialogue.english: Do you want to take part or, at least, give your point of view on this first question:

dialogue.french: "Quelle est votre série préférée, celle qui vous a fait rire ou pleurer le plus ?"
dialogue.english: "What is your favourite series, the one that made you laugh or cry the most?"

dialogue.french: La mienne est l'émission d'humour "Puissance faible".
dialogue.english: Mine is the comedy show "Low Power."

dialogue.french: Et la tienne, Mathilde ? La vôtre, Léon et Adèle ?
dialogue.english: And yours, Mathilde? Yours, Léon and Adèle?

dialogue.french: Moi, je ne suis pas fan de séries. Je préfère les magazines et les documentaires.
dialogue.english: Me, I'm not a fan of series. I prefer magazine programmes and documentaries.

dialogue.french: Et vous deux, vous préférez les divertissements ou les débats politiques ?
dialogue.english: And you two, do you prefer entertainment shows or political debates?

dialogue.french: Ni l'un ni l'autre. Nous regardons la télévision pour nous distraire, pas pour nous instruire !
dialogue.english: Neither one nor the other. We watch television for entertainment, not for education!

dialogue.french: C'est vrai mais j'ai quand même un faible pour les concours de cuisine.
dialogue.english: It's true but even so I have a soft spot for cooking contests.

dialogue.french: Pour moi, la meilleure émission de tous les temps est "Bon appétit !".
dialogue.english: For me, the best show ever is "Enjoy Your Meal!".

dialogue.french: Vous vous en souvenez ? Avec Maïka, la présentatrice célèbre, celle avec l'accent basque.
dialogue.english: Do you remember it? With the famous compere Maïka, the one with the Basque accent.

dialogue.french: La femme maladroite qui s'est coupé la main plusieurs fois en faisant la cuisine ?
dialogue.english: The clumsy woman who cut her finger several times while cooking?

dialogue.french: C'est bien elle. En fait, elle s'est coupée une fois seulement, mais tout le monde s'en souvient.
dialogue.english: That's the one. In fact, she cut herself only once, but everyone remembers it.

dialogue.french: L'émission s'est arrêtée il y a dix ans après huit saisons. Je l'ai regardée du début à la fin,
dialogue.english: The show ended ten years ago after eight seasons. I watched it from the beginning to the end,

dialogue.french: et je ne me suis jamais ennuyée un seul instant. Chère Maïka, vous me manquez !
dialogue.english: and I never got bored for a single moment. Dear Maïka, I miss you!

dialogue.french: Oui, nous nous sommes bien amusés, à l'époque. Maintenant, tout est en ligne.
dialogue.english: Yes, we really enjoyed ourselves at the time. Now, everything is online.

dialogue.french: Je vais écrire ça sur la feuille de réponse.
dialogue.english: I'm going to write that on the answer sheet.

dialogue.french: Zut, mon stylo ne marche plus. Prête-moi le tien, s'il te plaît Mathilde.
dialogue.english: Damn, my pen doesn't work anymore. Lend me yours, please Mathilde.

dialogue.french: Tu peux prendre le nôtre si tu veux. C'est amusant, ce petit jeu ! Continuons.
dialogue.english: You can take ours if you want. This little game is fun! Let's continue.

notes.content: The phoneme euille is pronounced like the noun œil : [uhyy] (see lesson 32).

une attente, from attendre, to wait, has a literal meaning, found in terms such as une salle d'attente, a waiting room, and also in sentences: L'attente à la caisse est trop longue, The wait at the checkout is too long. But it can also mean an expectation: Nous essayons toujours de répondre aux attentes de nos clients, We always try to meet our customers' expectations.

When used as adverbs, the superlatives le plus, the most, and le moins, the least, can be placed before the verb as well as after it: Quelle est l'émission qui vous a le plus fait rire ? Pre-placement is more literary but nevertheless quite common.

Possessive pronouns (yours, mine, etc.) are formed with a definite article (le, la, les) and a pronominal word (mien, tien, etc.). Like possessive adjectives, they agree in number and gender with the noun(s) they replace, not with the possessor. The pronoun vôtre takes a circumflex over the o to distinguish it from the possessive adjective votre: Ceci est votre clé → C'est la vôtre.

The adjective fan, an abbreviation of fanatique, fanatical, is used idiomatically to mean wildly enthusiastic. Je suis fan de foot, I'm a football fanatic. The word can also be used as a noun: C'est un fan de l'équipe de Bordeaux, He's a fan of the Bordeaux team. Both adjective and noun are pronounced [fan] (with a short [a]) because they come from the English word.

We have seen ni used in a negative construction to mean nor (lesson 57 line 78). If the negative verb applies to two things (like neither ... nor), simply repeat the conjunction instead of using pas: Mes enfants n'aiment ni les fruits ni les légumes, My children like neither fruit nor vegetables. Be careful: unlike in English, the verb must always be negative. Ni l'un ni l'autre can often be translated simply as neither: Ni l'un ni l'autre n'est venu, Neither came.

The adjective faible means weak (see lesson 27) but can be used in a broader sense of lacking: Elle a une faible chance de réussir, She has a slim chance of succeeding; Ces légumes sont faibles en calories, These vegetables are low in calories. The corresponding noun is la / une faiblesse, (a) weakness: Ma faiblesse en français est un problème dans mon travail, My weakness in French is a problem in my job. By contrast, the expression avoir un faible pour means to have a weakness for or, idiomatically, a soft spot for: J'ai un faible pour les émissions d'humour, I have a soft spot for comedy shows.

While many verbs can take a reflexive pronoun if the subject and object are identical, others are always (or "essentially") pronominal. For example, se souvenir (de) means to remember and, in everyday usage, is always pronominal: Est-ce que tu te souviens de cette émission ?, Do you remember that show? A near-synonym of se souvenir de is se rappeler (with no partitive article): Je me rappelle cette émission, I remember that show. (In the non-reflexive form, rappeler means to remind: Rappelez-moi votre nom, Remind me of your name.)

The agreement of past participles in pronominal verbs is complex. If the reflexive pronoun (me, se, etc.) is the indirect object of the sentence, the participle does not agree with the reflexive pronoun: Ma femme s'est coupé la main, My wife has cut her hand. But if the pronoun is the direct object, then the participle agrees in gender and number: Ma femme s'est coupée, My wife has cut herself. In some cases, notably first-group verbs, there is no difference in the pronunciation of the past participle (coupé and coupée are both pronounced [koopay]). However, this is not the case for the other verb groups, as we shall see.

The concrete noun une / la cuisine, a/the kitchen (lesson 36 line 6), has an abstract use that means cooking/cookery -or even cuisine! J'adore la cuisine basque, I love Basque cooking/cuisine. Faire la cuisine means to cook, or, more precisely, to do the cooking. Je fais toujours la cuisine pour mes amis, I always cook / do the cooking for my friends.

Used with the past tense and followed by a time period, il y a is the equivalent of ago: J'ai vu ce présentateur il y a vingt ans, I saw that presenter 20 years ago. The action described is always complete.

An idiosyncratic use of manquer (see lesson 57) is when the verb to miss expresses a feeling or regret or sadness. The French sentence structure is the reverse of English: I miss you is Tu me manques (or Vous me manquez), literally "you me are missing." Thus the person (or thing) missed is the subject of the sentence in French but the object in English. Thus if Maïka misses me, I would say Je manque à Maïka.

title: Je ne ferai plus jamais ça !

dialogue.french: Je ferai mes courses comme d'habitude au marché ce vendredi.
dialogue.english: I will do my shopping as usual at the market this Friday.

dialogue.french: J'achèterai des produits frais, du poisson et de la charcuterie - du pâté et du saucisson.
dialogue.english: I will buy fresh produce, fish and deli meats - pâté and sausage.

dialogue.french: Je prendrai aussi des fruits : des pêches, des abricots, et des raisins s'il y en a.
dialogue.english: I'll also take some fruit: peaches, apricots, and grapes if there are any.

dialogue.french: Mais pour les produits du quotidien, j'essaierai de passer une commande en ligne.
dialogue.english: But for everyday products, I'll try to place an order online.

dialogue.french: Dans le rayon épicerie, il me faut des conserves, du sel, du poivre et de la moutarde.

dialogue.english: I need fruit juice, flour, pasta, but also toilet paper and toothpaste.

dialogue.french: Et aussi l'huile d'olive. J'en ajouterai à mon panier si elle est en promotion cette semaine.
dialogue.english: And olive oil, too. I'll add some to my basket if it's on special offer this week.

dialogue.french: Voilà, je valide la commande et je règle mes achats.
dialogue.english: There, I confirm the order and I pay for my purchases.

dialogue.french: Finalement, c'est simple comme bonjour ! [ ... ]
dialogue.english: In the end, it's as easy as pie! [ ... ]

dialogue.french: Bonjour madame, on vient vous livrer les courses que vous avez commandées ce matin.
dialogue.english: Good morning madam, we have come to deliver the shopping that you ordered this morning.

dialogue.french: Où est-ce qu'on les met ? Il y a quatre cartons en tout.
dialogue.english: Where shall we put it? There are four boxes in all.

dialogue.french: Posez-les sur la table contre le frigo dans la cuisine si vous voulez bien.
dialogue.english: Put them on the table against the fridge in the kitchen if you please.

dialogue.french: C'est à gauche tout au fond du couloir.
dialogue.english: It's on the left, right at the end of the corridor.

dialogue.french: Voulez-vous de l'aide pour vider les cartons ?
dialogue.english: Do you want help to empty the boxes?

dialogue.french: D'abord je vais vérifier que tout est là.
dialogue.english: First I'm going to check that everything is there.

dialogue.french: Mais ce ne sont pas les articles que j'ai sélectionnés sur le site !
dialogue.english: But these aren't the items I selected on the site!

dialogue.french: En plus, vous m'avez livré un paquet de lessive et un bocal de cornichons !
dialogue.english: What's more, you've delivered a packet of washing powder and a jar of gherkins!

dialogue.french: Je n'ai commandé aucun des deux. Vous allez tout rapporter.
dialogue.english: I ordered neither of them. You're going to take everything back.

dialogue.french: Je ne ferai plus jamais mes courses à distance : j'irai au magasin et je parlerai avec un être humain.
dialogue.english: I'll never again do my shopping remotely: I'll go to the shop and I'll speak with a human being.

dialogue.french: Ce ne sera pas évident car il y aura beaucoup de monde aujourd'hui.
dialogue.english: That won't be easy because there will be a lot of people today.

notes.content: This is the first person singular of the future tense of the irregular verb faire, to do/make: je ferai, tu feras, il/elle fera, nous ferons, vous ferez, ils feront. In most cases, this tense corresponds to the English future with will. Note the difference between this tense and the "immediate" future formed with aller before the infinitive (in line 4, for example).

The future tense of verbs in the first two groups (ending in -er and -ir) is formed by adding the endings -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont to the stem. (See lesson 41 note 9). In some cases, the stem changes slightly. For example, with acheter, to buy, the first e takes a grave accent (j'achèterai, etc.) while the y in essayer changes to an i (j'essaierai, etc.). Neither of these changes makes a noticeable difference to the pronunciation.

Verbs ending in -re form their future from the infinitive, but drop the final -e. Thus the future tense of prendre, to take, is je prendrai, tu prendras, il/elle prendra, nous prendrons, vous prendrez, ils/elles prendront.

un rayon means a ray or a beam (Enfin un rayon de soleil ! At last a ray of sunshine!). In a shopping context, however, it means a department, section or counter in a store or supermarket. In this case, there is no need for a preposition between rayon and the qualifying noun: le rayon frais, the fresh [food] section.

une promotion has many meanings, including the cognate a promotion (i.e. professional advancement). In stores, it means a special offer. Les meubles de jardin sont en promotion cette semaine, Garden furniture is on special offer this week. The word is often abbreviated to en promo.

Bonjour, Good morning, is used in several idioms, notably C'est simple comme bonjour, It's as easy as pie. It can also be found in the idiomatic exclamation Bonjour les dégâts ! ("Hello the damage"), equivalent to What a mess!

tout, all, everything (see line 18) is also used as an adverb of degree, as seen in lesson 51 line 5. Nous habitons tout près d'ici, We live very near here. As such, it refers not only to physical distance but also to position or intensity: Ton nom est tout en haut de la liste, Your name is right at the top of the list. In this function, tout is invariable.

aucun(e), none, not any (lesson 46 note 4) can also mean neither when followed by de / des and a determiner. The accompanying verb is always negative: Elle n'aime aucune de ces robes, She likes neither of these dresses. (Compare with ni l'un ... ni l'autre, lesson 52).

Here is the future tense of the irregular verb aller, to go: j'irai, tu iras, il/elle ira, nous irons, vous irez, ils/elles iront. Note that only the stem is irregular, while the endings are exactly the same as for regular verbs (see note 1).

Another irregular future: être, to be, becomes je serai, tu seras, il/elle sera, nous serons, vous serez, ils/elles seront. The negative is formed, as usual, with ne ... pas: Les commandes ne seront pas livrées demain matin, The orders will not be delivered tomorrow morning.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: La charcuterie (from the term la chaire cuite, or cooked flesh) is a blanket term for cooked products made from pork (le porc) including ham (le jambon), pâté (le pâté), and sausage, for which French has two words: une saucisse, the generic term for any type of sausage eaten hot, and un saucisson, a hard, cooked sausage usually consumed when cold. The word charcuterie can refer to both the products (deli meats or cold cuts) and the shop where they are bought. The term for a grocery, une épicerie, comes from the noun une épice, a spice, which is what these shops originally sold. (The initial syllable ép- is often -but not always- the equivalent of sp- in English: une éponge, a sponge, les épinards, spinach, épier, to spy, etc.) Productive phase: 4th lesson

title: Votez pour moi : vous ne le regretterez pas ...

dialogue.french: Les élections législatives auront lieu dans quelques mois
dialogue.english: The general election will take place in a few months' time

dialogue.french: et nous aurons sans doute un nouveau gouvernement.
dialogue.english: and we shall probably have a new government.

dialogue.french: Les deux partis de centre-gauche feront cause commune contre la droite
dialogue.english: The two centre-left parties will make common cause against the right

dialogue.french: et présenteront un seul candidat, qui ne sera pas un politique professionnel.
dialogue.english: and field a single candidate, who will not be a professional politician.

dialogue.french: Ils tiendront un grand meeting à Grenoble ce week-end-ci. Est-ce que vous viendrez ?
dialogue.english: They will hold a big meeting in Grenoble this weekend. Will you be coming?

dialogue.french: Non, nous ne viendrons pas. Je ne serai pas en France et Romain ne pourra pas se libérer.
dialogue.english: No, we won't. I won't be in France and Romain won't be able to get away.

dialogue.french: En tout cas, le fameux candidat ne dira rien d'original. C'est toujours la même chanson.
dialogue.english: In any case, the much-vaunted candidate won't say anything original. It's always the same old story.

dialogue.french: En es-tu sûre ? Qui sait ? Il nous surprendra peut-être quand il parlera ce soir.
dialogue.english: Are you sure of that? Who knows? Perhaps he will surprise us when he speaks tonight?

dialogue.french: J'en suis sûre et certaine, et c'est ça qui me rend triste !
dialogue.english: I'm absolutely sure, and that's what makes me sad!

dialogue.french: Alors, contre qui vas-tu t'abstenir cette fois-ci ? [ ... ]
dialogue.english: So, who are you going to abstain against this time? [ ... ]

dialogue.french: "Français, Françaises, mes chers compatriotes, rassurez-vous : je ne serai pas long.
dialogue.english: "Frenchmen, Frenchwomen, my dear countrymen, rest assured: I won't be long-winded.

dialogue.french: Je ne parlerai pas pendant des heures pour ne rien dire.
dialogue.english: I won't talk for hours to say nothing.

dialogue.french: Je ne promettrai pas une solution miracle pour résoudre les problèmes du pays.
dialogue.english: I won't promise a miracle solution to solve the problems of the country.

dialogue.french: Mais c'est mon devoir de dire la vérité : mon adversaire ne sera ni de droite, ni de gauche :
dialogue.english: But it's my duty to tell the truth: my opponent won't be from the right or from the left:

dialogue.french: mon adversaire sera l'argent et la corruption qui l'accompagne.
dialogue.english: my opponent will be money and the corruption that goes with it.

dialogue.french: Vous n'aurez jamais à douter de mon intégrité, je vous le jure.
dialogue.english: You will never have to doubt my integrity, I swear to you.

dialogue.french: Maintenant, vous savez que vous devrez faire le bon choix, pour vous et votre portefeuille, euh, votre pays.
dialogue.english: Now, you know you will have to make the right choice, for you and your wallet, er, your country.

dialogue.french: La situation est urgente et vous ne pourrez plus dire : "Ça ne me concerne pas".
dialogue.english: The situation is urgent and you will no longer be able to say: "It doesn't concern me".

dialogue.french: Vous n'aurez qu'un choix à faire : voter pour moi.
dialogue.english: You will have only one choice to make: vote for me.

dialogue.french: Le capitalisme, c'est l'exploitation de l'Homme par l'Homme, mais ma philosophie, c'est le contraire !"
dialogue.english: Capitalism is the exploitation of man by man, but my philosophy is the opposite!"

notes.content: The abstract noun le doute means doubt. Je n'ai pas de doute sur leur réponse, I have no doubt about their answer. The root verb is douter (de). Il doute de tout, He doubts everything. The phrase sans doute, without a doubt, can be used to mean probably: Elle a raison sans doute, mais nous devons être certains, She's probably right but we have to be certain.

The singular noun la politique, seen in lesson 49, means politics: La politique est l'art du possible, Politics is the art of the possible. In this context, the noun is never plural. But it can also mean a policy: une politique économique, an economic policy, in which case, it takes a regular plural -s: les politiques économiques. Un(e) politique can also mean a (male or female) politician, although the loan word un(e) politicien(ne) is frequently used nowadays. (Remember that politique is also an adjective, see lesson 52 line 7).

The noun un meeting is used to describe a political gathering or assembly. It is also used in athletics (un meeting d'athlétisme). Like most loan words, meeting is masculine. (Don't confuse it with une réunion, a business meeting, lesson 38 line 15).

The demonstrative adverb -ci (lesson 31 note 3) can be used with time periods to emphasise proximity: Ce mois-ci, This coming month. It does not have a plural form: ces jours-ci, [in] the coming days.

The adjective célèbre (the origin of celebrity and celebrated, lesson 52 line 11) means famous or well-known. But fameux has a much broader meaning, often relating to quality: Son pâté est fameux !, His (or Her) pâté is fantastic! The word can also be used to describe something that has been widely discussed: C'est ça, ton fameux plan ? C'est nul ! Is that the plan that you've been talking about? It's rubbish!

In a subordinate clause beginning with a time expression, such as quand, the verb is in the future tense if the verb in the main clause is also in the future. This contrasts with English, which uses the present. Nous serons tous là quand il arrivera, We will all be there when he arrives.

Remember that adjectives always agree in gender with the person they refer to, but those ending in -e (like triste, sad) in both masculine and feminine agree only in the plural: des choses tristes, sad things. (See lesson 14).

devoir is a verb, must (lesson 17 note 5), but also a masculine noun meaning duty: C'est mon devoir de vous le dire, It's my duty to tell you. In a school context, it is used in the plural (les devoirs) to mean homework (because the student is obliged to do the work).

l'homme is used in the general sense to mean mankind. For that reason, it is often written with an initial capital: Le Musée de l'Homme à Paris est fermé le mardi, The Museum of Mankind in Paris is closed Tuesdays. A concerted effort is underway in France to move towards more gender-neutral language, especially in official contexts. So the expression les droits de l'homme (or l'Homme), "the rights of man" is making way for les droits humains, the back translation of human rights. (The Canadian francophone community has long used the term les droits de la personne.)

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Politics is a perennial topic of conversation in France. The country is organised constitutionally as une république, a republic (the current regime, dating from 1958, is fifth republic, la cinquième république, since 1792). It is headed by le Président de la République, elected by universal suffrage every five years (the term of office is known as un quinquennat [kahnkenah], from the Latin word for five). Les élections législatives (a general election) are also held every five years to elect les députés, representatives who sit in the lower house of parliament, la Chambre des députés (although the president has the right to seek an early dissolution of parliament, le parlement). The members of the upper house, le Sénat, are called les sénateurs and are elected every nine years. Productive phase: 5th lesson

dialogue.french: J'ai vu "La Mort atroce" hier. Je te le conseille vraiment.
dialogue.english: I saw "Dreadful Death" yesterday. I really recommend it.

dialogue.french: Il a battu les records d'audience pour un film d'épouvante.
dialogue.english: It has broken viewing records for a horror movie.

dialogue.french: Où l'as-tu vu ? En vidéo ?
dialogue.english: Where did you see it? On video?

dialogue.french: Non, à côté de chez moi il y a un complexe multisalles qui vient d'ouvrir.
dialogue.english: No, next to my place there's a multiplex that has just opened.

dialogue.french: La programmation est variée, les salles sont spacieuses, et on y vend de très bonnes glaces !
dialogue.english: The programming is varied, the theatres are spacious, and they sell very good ice cream!

dialogue.french: De quoi parle le film ?
dialogue.english: What is the movie about?

dialogue.french: L'action se passe à Annecy, où j'ai passé toutes mes vacances quand j'étais petite.
dialogue.english: The action takes place in Annecy, where I spent all my holidays when I was little.

dialogue.french: L'une des vedettes est Jeanne Morteau, un monstre sacré du cinéma.
dialogue.english: One of the stars is Jeanne Morteau, a giant of the movies.

dialogue.french: Elle m'amuse bien avec ses airs de grande dame ! Mais quelle actrice !
dialogue.english: She really amuses me with her fine-lady airs, but what an actress!

dialogue.french: C'est l'histoire d'un vieux couple qui, en apparence, s'entend à merveille.
dialogue.english: It's the story of an old couple who, apparently, get on marvellously.

dialogue.french: Ils habitent dans un hameau qui se trouve tout près d'un vieil immeuble.
dialogue.english: They live in a hamlet which is located right near an old apartment building.

dialogue.french: Ils pensent que ce bâtiment est abandonné, mais les apparences peuvent tromper ...
dialogue.english: They think that this building is abandoned, but appearances can be deceptive ...

dialogue.french: Un jour, la femme est seule à la maison lorsqu'elle entend des bruits étranges dehors.
dialogue.english: One day, the woman is alone in the house when she hears strange noises outside.

dialogue.french: Elle sort dans la cour, où elle trouve des dizaines d'oiseaux morts par terre.
dialogue.english: She goes out into the courtyard, where she finds dozens of dead birds on the ground.

dialogue.french: Elle croit d'abord que c'est son chat qui les a tués.
dialogue.english: She believes at first that it's her cat which has killed them.

dialogue.french: Mais elle se trompe ! Soudain elle voit son mari avec une énorme hache à la main.
dialogue.english: But she is mistaken! Suddenly she sees her husband with a huge axe in his hand.

dialogue.french: Elle se bat avec lui mais il est beaucoup plus fort et il la bat à coups de bâton.
dialogue.english: She fights with him but he is much stronger and he hits her with a stick.

dialogue.french: Mais c'est horrible ! Qu'est-ce qui se passe ensuite ?
dialogue.english: But that's horrible! What happens next?

dialogue.french: Je ne sais pas parce que je me suis cachée sous la table jusqu'à la fin.
dialogue.english: I don't know because I hid under the table until the end.

dialogue.french: Néanmoins, je me suis amusée comme une gosse et j'ai adoré le film.
dialogue.english: Even so, I enjoyed myself like a kid and I loved the movie.

dialogue.french: Du moins, la première moitié...
dialogue.english: At least, the first half ...

notes.content: un monstre sacré ("a holy monster") is an admirative term similar to the English epithet a giant or a legendary figure: Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard Gérard is a giant of French cinema. The expression applies to both men and women. However, the noun un monstre is used pejoratively, as in English: C'est un monstre horrible qui fait peur aux enfants, He's a dreadful monster who scares children.

amuser means to amuse (someone): Il m'amuse avec ses histoires drôles, He amuses me with his funny stories. The pronominal form, seen in lesson 47 line 2, generally means to enjoy or to have fun. Il joue mal de la guitare mais il s'amuse, He plays the guitar badly but he enjoys himself.

entendre means to hear (and we know the past participle entendu, lesson 38 note 8, meaning, I see or I understand). And, as a simple reflexive, s'entendre means to hear oneself. Faites moins de bruit, je ne m'entends pas parler au téléphone, Make less noise, I can't hear myself speaking on the phone. As an idiomatic pronominal verb, s'entendre means to reach an agreement with or, more commonly, to get on well with someone. Je m'entends très bien avec le frère de Jacques, I get on well with Jacques' brother.
See lesson 51 note 8.

se tromper (lesson 50 line 5), to make a mistake, is the pronominal form of tromper, to deceive or mislead: Le vendeur m'a trompé sur le prix des appels téléphoniques, The salesman misled me about the price of phone calls. The transitive verb can also mean to be unfaithful to, cheat on: Si une femme trompe son mari, c'est souvent parce que le mari trompe sa femme: If a wife is unfaithful to her husband, it's often because the husband is cheating on his wife.

We have already seen un coup in several expressions (un coup d'œil, un coup de main, lesson 32, for example). Here we have the original meaning, a blow, a knock, etc.: J'ai pris un coup sur la tête, I banged my head. In these contexts, English is more flexible than French. For instance, donner un coup de poing/de pied ("to give a blow of the fist/foot") is to punch/kick. The expression à coups de describes the thing delivering the blow: Elle le battait à coups de brosse, She was hitting him with a brush.

un(e) gosse, a common informal word for a child, is the equivalent of a kid: Ils vont en vacances sans les gosses ce mois-ci, They're going on holiday without the kids this month. Like monstre sacré (note 4), the noun is masculine but can apply to both sexes. Two common related idioms are un sale gosse (a brat) and un beau gosse, a good-looker. (Be careful: in Canadian French, gosse is a testicular slang word!)

new_words.english: I really recommend it to you

il y a un complexe multisalles
on y vend de très bonnes glaces
new_words.english: they sell very good ice cream there
the action takes place in Annecy

new_words.french: où j'ai passé toutes mes vacances
new_words.english: where I spent all my holidays

new_words.french: avec ses airs de grande dame

new_words.english: they think this building is abandoned

new_words.french: la femme est seule à la maison
new_words.english: the woman is alone at home

new_words.english: when she hears strange noises outside
she goes out into the courtyard

new_words.english: she believes at first that it's her cat

new_words.english: with a huge axe in his hand
