translate.question: Tout le monde dit que la situation économique du pays est épouvantable.
answers: Everyone says that the country's economic situation is dreadful.

translate.question: Je vous conseille son nouveau film : vous vous amuserez beaucoup.
answers: I recommend his/her new film: you'll really enjoy yourselves.

translate.question: As-tu entendu la nouvelle ? Jeanne est morte. Son mari l'a tuée.
answers: Have you heard the news? Jeanne is dead. Her husband killed her.

translate.question: Ils passent toute une semaine seuls, sans les gosses, pour la première fois.
answers: They are spending a whole week alone, without the kids, for the first time.

translate.question: Nous avons battu tous les records de froid pour un mois de décembre.
answers: We have broken all the cold-temperature records for a month of December.

fill.question: What's happening for the holidays this summer? - We're spending them near Annecy. Qu'est-ce qui ... pour les vacances cet été ? - Nous les ... près d'Annecy.
fill.full_answer: Qu'est-ce qui se passe pour les vacances cet été ? - Nous les passons près d'Annecy.

fill.question: We're fighting to help our team but I think that it will be beaten this time. Nous ... pour aider notre équipe mais je pense qu'elle sera battue cette fois-ci.
fill.full_answer: Nous nous battons pour aider notre équipe mais je pense qu'elle sera battue cette fois-ci.

fill.question: A hundred euros? You made a mistake. - No, I believe it's the salesman who cheated me on the sale. Cent euros ? Tu t'es trompé. - Non, je crois que c'est le vendeur qui m'a trompé sur mon achat.
fill.full_answer: Cent euros ? Tu t'es trompé. - Non, je crois que c'est le vendeur qui m'a trompé sur mon achat.

fill.question: In principle they get on well together but I hear people say that it's not true. En principe ils ... mais j'entends des gens qui disent que ce n'est pas vrai.
fill.full_answer: En principe ils s'entendent bien mais j'entends des gens qui disent que ce n'est pas vrai.

fill.question: The knife has fallen. It's under the table. - I've found it. Le couteau est tombé. Il se trouve sous la table. - Je l'ai trouvé.
fill.full_answer: Le couteau est tombé. Il se trouve sous la table. - Je l'ai trouvé.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Productive phase: 6th lesson

dialogue.french: Comment va Hélène ? Est-ce qu'elle est toujours en Allemagne ?
dialogue.english: How's Hélène? Is she still in Germany?

dialogue.french: Non, elle est rentrée il y a six semaines et elle travaille maintenant à Grenoble.
dialogue.english: No, she came back six weeks ago and she's now working in Grenoble.

dialogue.french: Ça se passe bien pour elle ? Elle s'amuse, j'espère ?
dialogue.english: Are things going well for her? She's enjoying herself, I hope?

dialogue.french: Non, elle s'ennuie parce qu'il n'y a pas assez à faire au boulot.
dialogue.english: No, she's bored because there isn't enough to do at work.

dialogue.french: Hier, elle s'est connectée à Internet pour faire ses courses en ligne !
dialogue.english: Yesterday, she went online to do her shopping!

dialogue.french: Pas comme moi, alors ! Je n'ai pas le temps de m'ennuyer.
dialogue.english: Not like me, then! I don't have time to get bored.

dialogue.french: Je me suis levée à cinq heures ce matin pour aller au bureau.
dialogue.english: I got up at five o'clock this morning to go to the office.

dialogue.french: Je ne me suis même pas douchée parce que je n'avais pas assez de temps.
dialogue.english: I didn't even have a shower because I didn't have enough time.

dialogue.french: J'ai pris la voiture pour arriver plus vite mais, en arrivant,
dialogue.english: I took the car to get there earlier but, when I arrived,

dialogue.french: je me suis arrêtée devant l'immeuble et j'ai vu que la porte était fermée à clé.
dialogue.english: I stopped in front of the building and saw the door was locked.

dialogue.french: Je n'ai même pas arrêté le moteur.
dialogue.english: I didn't even turn off the engine.

dialogue.french: Qu'as-tu fait ?
dialogue.english: What did you do?

dialogue.french: Je suis repartie très en colère.
dialogue.english: I left, very angry.

notes.content: Pronominal verbs: Pronominal verbs are divided into two classes: reflexive, where the subject and object of an action are the same, and reciprocal, used when two or more subjects do the same thing to each other. Both classes are preceded by se (s' before an initial vowel or h) in the infinitive and are conjugated in the same way, with a subject pronoun and a reflexive pronoun: se laver, to wash: je me lave, tu te laves, il/elle se lave, nous nous lavons, vous vous lavez, ils/elles se lavent. In the perfect tense, pronominal verbs are conjugated with être and the past participle, which generally agrees with the subject: je me suis lavé/-ée, tu t'es lavé/-ée, il s'est lavé / elle s'est lavée, nous nous sommes lavés/-ées, vous vous êtes lavé/lavés/-ées, ils se sont lavés / elles se sont lavées. To form the negative, place ne before the reflexive pronoun and pas (or jamais, etc.) after the verb: Il ne se lave pas. The first interrogative (rising intonation) is formed with the subject pronoun followed by the reflexive pronoun: Tu te laves ? For the second interrogative, place est-ce que before the subject pronoun: Est-ce que tu te laves ? The third interrogative (inversion, Te laves-tu ?) is slightly more complex and less common. For the imperative, the position of the pronoun depends on whether the command is affirmative or negative. In both cases, the subject pronoun is dropped. With a negative imperative, beginning with Ne ... , the reflexive pronoun comes before the verb, as usual: Ne te lave pas. In the affirmative, however, the reflexive pronoun is replaced by a stressed pronoun, placed after the verb and joined with a hyphen: Lave-toi ! Reflexive verbs: These are much more common in French than in English. In particular, those indicating a change of state (se réveiller, to wake up, s'endormir, to go to sleep) or describing an everyday action or movement (se laver, to wash, s'habiller, to get dressed, se promener, to go for a walk) are not reflexive in English. Some of these verbs are "exclusively pronominal," so that without the reflexive pronoun, they have no meaning. Among the most frequently used are s'absenter, (to leave, go away from), s'enfuir (to run away, to flee), s'envoler (to fly away), se fier (to rely on), and se méfier (to be wary of). In the past tense, the past participle always agrees in number and gender with the subject. By contrast, some verbs are "occasionally pronominal" and can be used transitively or reflexively depending on the direct object. For example: Je lave ma voiture, I'm washing my car; Je me lave le visage, I'm washing my face. Reciprocal verbs are used when the subject or subjects are acting on each other. For example: se comprendre: Nous ne nous comprenons pas, We don't understand each other. Other common reciprocals include se détester (Ils se détestent, They hate each other), se regarder (Nous nous regardons, We are looking at each other) and se sourire (Elles se sourient, They're smiling at each other). Some pronominal verbs have a slightly different meaning when used non-reciprocally, as we saw in lesson 55. These include passer (to pass, to spend time) and se passer (to happen); entendre (to hear) and s'entendre (to get on well with); tromper (to deceive) and se tromper (to make a mistake).

Future tense: The future tense is used in very much the same way as its English equivalent (but remember that French makes no difference between the simple and the progressive). To form the future of regular verbs, the endings of avoir are added to the stem, which, for the first two verb groups, is usually the infinitive: ajouter, to add: j'ajouterai, tu ajouteras, il/elle ajoutera, nous ajouterons, vous ajouterez, ils/elles ajouteront. finir, to finish: je finirai, tu finiras, il/elle finira, nous finirons, vous finirez, ils/elles finiront. Verbs ending in -re use the same endings but drop the final -e from the infinitive: vendre, to sell: je vendrai, tu vendras, il/elle vendra, nous vendrons, vous vendrez, ils/elles vendront. There are a few spelling changes when the future endings are added to certain -er verbs, as we saw in lesson 53 with acheter and essayer. The same changes occur with lever, to lift (the initial e takes a grave accent) and payer, to pay (the y changes to i): j'achèterai, je lèverai; j'essaierai, je paierai. Another change concerns verbs such as jeter and appeler, which double the first letter of the last syllable: je jetterai, j'appellerai. Some important irregular verbs, particularly the two auxiliaries avoir and être, change significantly in the future. Here they are, for reference purposes: j'aurai, tu auras, il/elle aura, nous aurons, vous aurez, ils/elles auront; je serai, tu seras, il/elle sera, nous serons, vous serez, ils/elles seront. Two other important irregular verbs are faire and aller (see lesson 53 notes 1 and 9). A major structural difference between French and English concerns subordinate clauses linked to a future-tense main clause by a time conjunction, particularly quand, when. In French, the verbs in both clauses are in the future: Quand je saurai ce qu'il veut, je te le dirai, When I know what he wants, I'll tell you. The same rule applies to the future perfect, which we see at the end of the course.

Possessive pronouns: Possessive pronouns -the equivalent of yours, mine, etc.- are formed with a definite article and a pronominal word. le mien, la mienne, les miens, les miennes (mine); le tien, la tienne, les tiens, les tiennes (yours); le sien, la sienne, les siens, les siennes (his/hers/its); le nôtre, la nôtre, les nôtres (ours); le vôtre, la vôtre, les vôtres (yours); le leur, la leur, les leurs (theirs). Like possessive adjectives, these pronouns agree in number and gender with the noun(s) they replace, not with the possessor. For example: C'est mon stylo, It's my pen → C'est le mien, It's mine; C'est ma voiture, It's my car → C'est la mienne, It's mine. Because of the agreement, it is not possible to identify the gender of the possessor in third person singular (le sien, la sienne). C'est son manteau → C'est le sien can mean his or hers. Note that the circumflex over the o on nôtre and vôtre makes it possible to distinguish between the pronoun and the possessive adjective (votre, notre): Ceci est votre clé → C'est la vôtre.

Prepositions: en, dans and à: While most French prepositions are easy to master, these three are often confused (in addition, en is also an adverbial pronoun, like y). Let's revise the different prepositional uses we have seen since you started this course. 4.1 Dans and en: • One basic difference is that en is never followed by a definite or partitive article (except in a number of fixed expressions, such as en l'air, in the air). It is used, among others, to talk about languages (en français, en anglais), modes of transport (en avion, en train, etc.), materials (en papier, en or), periods and dates (en 1789, en avril), and seasons with the exception of spring: (en été, en automne, en hiver but au printemps). En can also be used with places, Il est en prison, and countries that are feminine nouns: Je vis en France (masculine countries take au, see lesson 57). Last, it is used before a present participle: en arrivant, on arriving. • In contrast to en, dans has to be followed by an article, whether definite (le/la/les), indefinite (un/une) or partitive (du, de la, des). Dans designates the location of something (dans ma poche) and, in this context, is more precise than en. For example, you would say Mes vacances en montagne, My holidays in the mountains but Mes vacances dans les montagnes du Jura, My holidays in the Jura mountains. When followed by an article and a noun, dans means in: Elle est dans son bureau, She is in her office. Dans is also used for a specific measure of time (dans dix minutes), but remember (lesson 51 note 5) that en can also be used in this context, but with a slight difference in meaning: Je peux faire le travail en vingt minutes, I can do the job in 20 minutes (i.e. it will take me 20 minutes) compared with Je peux faire le travail dans vingt minutes, I can do the job in 20 minutes' time (i.e. in the future). 4.2 Dans and à: Here, too, the differences are subtle. The one that seems to most confuse English-speakers is the use of à to translate in when the pronoun does not mean inside. A typical usage is à la maison, at home. Où est Rosie ? - Elle est à la maison, Where's Rosie? - She's at home. By contrast, J'entends quelqu'un dans la maison, I can hear someone inside the house. Likewise, J'ai vu le film au cinéma, I saw the movie at the cinema, versus, J'ai perdu ma montre dans le cinéma, I lost my watch in (i.e. inside) the cinema. One big difference with English, and a source of many mistakes, concerns parts of the body: Il a un couteau à la main, He has a knife in (not inside) his hand, compared with Tiens-le dans ta main, Hold it in your hand (i.e. close your fingers around it). À is also used to talk about cities or towns: Elle habite à Paris, She lives in Paris. (Remember that à becomes au/aux with le/les: Elle habite au quatrième étage, She lives on the fourth floor.) Last, à is used with certain communications media: à la télévision, à la radio, au téléphone.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: From now on, we will no longer systematically give the translations of words -notably the main verbs such as être, avoir and faire- in our examples. By now, you should be thoroughly familiar with them. Productive phase: 7th lesson

dialogue.french: Comment ça se fait que vous parlez tous les deux le breton ?
dialogue.english: How come you both speak Breton?

dialogue.french: L'explication est simple. Nous avons vécu à Vannes pendant dix ans quand nous étions plus jeunes.
dialogue.english: The explanation is simple. We lived in Vannes for ten years when we were younger.

dialogue.french: J'enseignais au collège et mon mari travaillait dans une entreprise de construction navale.
dialogue.english: I was teaching in middle school and my husband was working in a ship-building company.

dialogue.french: Beaucoup de gens autour de nous parlaient cette langue, ce qui nous a motivé à l'apprendre.
dialogue.english: Many people around us spoke the language, which motivated us to learn it.

dialogue.french: Est-ce que c'était difficile à maîtriser ?
dialogue.english: Was it hard to master?

dialogue.french: Nous avions beaucoup de chance parce que nous habitions un quartier de la ville
dialogue.english: We were very lucky because we were living in a part of the town

dialogue.french: où les commerçants avaient presque tous le breton comme première langue,
dialogue.english: where almost all the shopkeepers spoke Breton as their first language,

dialogue.french: donc nous étions obligés de le parler, rien que pour acheter du pain !
dialogue.english: so we were obliged to speak it, if only to buy bread!

dialogue.french: À propos, saviez-vous que le verbe "baragouiner" était d'origine bretonne ?
dialogue.english: By the way, did you know that the verb baragouiner was of Breton origin?

dialogue.french: C'est vrai ? Je ne le savais pas. Vous m'apprenez quelque chose !
dialogue.english: Is that true? I didn't know. That's news to me!

dialogue.french: Autrefois, beaucoup de Bretons montaient à Paris pour trouver du travail.
dialogue.english: In the past, many Bretons came up to Paris to find work.

dialogue.french: Le voyage était très long et ils arrivaient souvent affamés.
dialogue.english: The journey was very long and they were often famished when they arrived.

dialogue.french: Ils entraient dans le premier café qu'ils trouvaient
dialogue.english: They went into the first café that they came across

dialogue.french: et commandaient du pain et du vin. Mais ils ne parlaient que le breton,
dialogue.english: and ordered bread and wine. But they only spoke Breton,

dialogue.french: donc ils demandaient du bara et du guin - et personne ne les comprenait.
dialogue.english: so they asked for bara and guin - and no-one understood them.

dialogue.french: "Que disent ces Bretons ? On n'y comprend rien", disaient les Parisiens.
dialogue.english: "What are these Bretons saying? We can't make head nor tail of it," the Parisians used to say.

dialogue.french: "Ils baragouinent !". Et voilà d'où vient ce mot.
dialogue.english: "They're talking gibberish!" And that's where the word comes from.

dialogue.french: Elle est absolument fascinante, ton histoire. Je ne la connaissais pas.
dialogue.english: Your story is absolutely fascinating. I didn't know it.

dialogue.french: Pourtant j'allais souvent en Bretagne avec mes parents quand j'étais petit.
dialogue.english: And yet I often used to go to Brittany with my parents when I was little.

dialogue.french: J'aimais bien la région, mais il pleuvait tout le temps et on ne sortait presque jamais de la maison.
dialogue.english: I really liked the region, but it rained all the time and we almost never left the house.

dialogue.french: Il pleuvait sans doute, mais au moins en été la pluie bretonne est plus chaude.
dialogue.english: It was certainly raining, but at least the Breton rain is warmer in summer.

notes.content: commerçant [komairssœhn]: Note how the cedilla beneath the letter c changes the pronunciation from a hard [k] to a sibilant, or soft, [s]. The ç is used only before the vowels a (ça), o (garçon) and u (reçu) because c is automatically sibilant before e, i and y.

Comment ça se fait ? ("How that does itself") is an idiomatic way of asking a question, a little like How come... . Il n'est pas parti en vacances cette année - Comment ça se fait ? He didn't go on holiday this year? - How come? The expression can also be used in a full sentence: Comment ça se fait que le train est en retard ? How come the train is late? In this case, however, it is often necessary to put the main verb into the subjunctive, which we will learn later on.

French does not have a word for both, relying on various constructions according to the grammatical function. As an adjective, you can use les/des/aux deux: J'ai mal aux deux genoux, Both my knees hurt. As a pronoun, use tous les deux: Ils sont bretons, tous les deux, They're both Breton.

Here is the imperfect tense, glimpsed in lesson 55 line 7. It is used to describe situations or actions in the past, without specifying when they began or ended. The imperfect form of the irregular verb être is j'étais, tu étais, il/elle/on était, nous étions, vous étiez, ils/elles étaient. The nearest equivalent in English is was / were.

The imperfect tense of all verbs other than être is formed by dropping the -ons ending from the nous form of the present and adding the following endings: enseignons: j'enseignais, tu enseignais, il/elle enseignait, nous enseignions, vous enseigniez, ils/elles enseignaient. Depending on the context, the translation uses was teaching or used to teach. In this same sentence, travaillait is the third-person singular imperfect of travailler.

un commerçant comes from the noun le commerce, trade. (Here is another example of Latin/Anglo-Saxon variations: trade concerns the exchange of goods and some services, whereas commerce encompasses a broader range of ancillary activities). Depending on the context, un(e) commerçant(e) can be a trader but also a shopkeeper or merchant.
This is the imperfect of savoir: savons → je savais, tu savais, il/elle/on savait, nous savions, vous saviez, ils/elles savaient.
baragouiner is a familiar verb meaning to talk gibberish or, less insultingly, to speak a language badly, mumble a few words: Il baragouinait le russe, He was speaking bad Russian.

We know that apprendre means to learn and enseigner to teach. However, when used with a direct object and an indirect object in the same phrase, apprendre means to teach (something to someone). So, j'apprends le français, I'm learning French but Je leur apprends le français, I'm teaching them French. By contrast, enseigner means only to teach: Je leur enseigne le français. The idiomatic expression Vous m'apprenez / Tu m'apprends quelque chose is the equivalent of That's news to me (or, more simply, I didn't know that).

We know that adjectives of nationality do not take an initial capital, whereas nouns do (lesson 20 note 3). Logically, the lower-case rule applies to adjectives derived from regions, towns, etc., as well as to languages: C'est un Normand qui parle mal l'anglais, He's a Norman who speaks bad English. Be careful not to confuse nouns and adjectives of nationality: Ce sont des Bretons but Ils sont bretons. (Alternatively, Ils sont d'origine bretonne, They're of Breton origin.)

We know that the adverbial pronoun y refers back to a previously mentioned indirect object, usually a thing or place, generally introduced by à (see lesson 35 section 3.1). Thus, in the idiomatic phrase on n'y comprend rien, y replaces that indirect object: À la fin du film, on n'y comprend rien (i.e. au film), By the end of the movie, nobody understands anything. If the indirect object comes after the verb, however, y is not used, and rien returns to its usual place, directly after the verb: On n'a rien compris au film.
To remind yourself of how to use connaître (line 18) and savoir (line 10), look back at lesson 35 section 4.

dialogue.french: Allons à Auxerre ce week-end, puisque mardi est férié.
dialogue.english: Let's go to Auxerre this weekend, since Tuesday is a public holiday.

dialogue.french: Nous pourrons faire le pont du premier novembre et passer quatre jours complets.
dialogue.english: We can take the long weekend of 1st November and spend four full days.

dialogue.french: Ça sera chouette, non ? Un peu de ciel bleu, ça nous changera les idées.
dialogue.english: It will be great, won't it? A bit of blue sky will give us something different to think about.

dialogue.french: Tu n'as pas l'air enthousiaste. Qu'est-ce qu'il y a ?
dialogue.english: You don't seem very keen. What's up?

dialogue.french: D'abord, qu'est-ce qu'il y a à voir à Auxerre ? C'est une ville comme une autre.
dialogue.english: First, what is there to see in Auxerre? It's a town like any other.

dialogue.french: Mais il y a plein de choses à visiter : regarde dans le guide de voyage :
dialogue.english: But there are loads of things to visit: look in this travel guide:

dialogue.french: "La vieille ville, la Tour de l'Horloge, l'abbaye, les beaux hôtels particuliers,
dialogue.english: "The old town, the Clock Tower, the abbey, handsome private mansions,

dialogue.french: et des maisons à pans de bois sont parmi les sites incontournables à apprécier".
dialogue.english: and half-timbered houses are among the key things to enjoy".

dialogue.french: Ça vaut la peine, je t'assure. Tu ne le regretteras pas.
dialogue.english: It's worth it, I assure you. You won't regret it.

dialogue.french: À vrai dire, j'ai la flemme de quitter la maison, surtout pour un long week-end.
dialogue.english: To tell the truth, I can't be bothered to leave the house, especially for a long weekend.

dialogue.french: D'abord, il faudra partir au petit matin car la plupart des Parisiens seront sur la route, et il y aura un monde fou.
dialogue.english: First, we'll have to leave in the early hours because most Parisians will be on the road and there'll be loads of people.

dialogue.french: La dernière fois que je suis allé en Bourgogne, j'ai mis une demi-journée à faire cinquante kilomètres !
dialogue.english: The last time that I went to Burgundy, I spent half a day doing fifty kilometres!

dialogue.french: Je suis resté coincé dans les embouteillages pendant quatre heures et demie, et ce n'était pourtant pas un jour férié.
dialogue.english: I was stuck in traffic jams for four-and-a-half hours, and yet it wasn't a public holiday.

dialogue.french: Pire, il faisait un froid de canard et il tombait des cordes.
dialogue.english: Worse, it was freezing and it was pouring with rain.

dialogue.french: Selon la météo, il fera beau, chaud et ensoleillé toute la semaine.
dialogue.english: According to the weather forecast, it will be fine, hot and sunny all week.

dialogue.french: Peut-être bien, mais les hôtels seront complets ou ruineux,
dialogue.english: Maybe, but the hotels will be either full or ruinously expensive,

dialogue.french: et je n'ai pas les moyens de jeter l'argent par les fenêtres.
dialogue.english: and I can't afford to throw money away.

dialogue.french: J'abandonne ! Tu es têtu comme une mule.
dialogue.english: I give up! You are as stubborn as a mule.

notes.content: enthousiaste: remember that the phoneme th is always pronounced as a t: [œh"tooziast]. a à: the two letters are pronounced identically and each must be sounded: don't try to elide the second one.

Although une chouette means an owl, the word is often used as a familiar adjective meaning great, cool, nice, etc. Il est vraiment chouette, ton nouveau sac, Your new handbag is really nice. Chouette can also be used as an exclamation, sometimes followed by alors: On ira à Auxerre demain. - Chouette alors ! We'll go to Auxerre tomorrow. - Great!

The idiom Qu'est-ce qu'il y a ? means What's wrong? or What's up? Unlike Qu'est-ce qui ne va pas ? (lesson 24 line 1), where something is visibly wrong, Qu'est-ce qu'il y a ? is often a more general way of making an enquiry: Qu'est-ce qu'il y a ? - Je veux vous parler, What is it? - I want to talk to you.

Here are two false friends. The adjective particulier, particular, distinctive, can also mean private when used in the compound noun un hôtel particulier, a private mansion. (In this context, un hôtel takes no guests!) The noun un particulier means a private individual, as opposed to a company. Website contact pages often ask the question: Êtes-vous un particulier ou une entreprise ? Are you an individual or a business?

Here is another use of the third person of valoir (lesson 44 line 18): the impersonal expression Ça vaut ... followed by a noun means It's worth ... . Ça vaut la peine, It's worth the trouble (see lesson 46 note 8). Some tourist guides rate important monuments, sites and even restaurants simply as Vaut le voyage, Worth the trip.

The feminine noun la flemme is derived from the medical term le flegme, phlegm and conveys the idea of laziness. It is used almost exclusively in the expression avoir la flemme: J'ai la flemme de faire le lit, I can't be bothered to make the bed.

We know that le monde, the world, is a synonym for people (tout le monde, beaucoup de monde). The idiomatic expression Il y a un monde fou ("a mad world") means It's really crowded / There are loads of people, etc. Il y a un monde fou dans les magasins à Noël, The shops are really crowded at Christmas.

The adverb pourtant, seen in the previous lesson, is a useful way of expressing a contradiction, like yet or however. It can be used directly after a verb: Il reste pourtant beaucoup de choses à faire, But there's still a lot to do, or at the beginning of a sentence, usually with Et, as a rejoinder. Tu es en retard. - Et pourtant, je me suis levé tôt, You're late. - And yet I got up early.

un canard means a duck: Suzanne vend des poulets et des canards au marché depuis dix ans, Suzanne has been selling chickens and ducks at the market for ten years. Since ducks are apparently easier to hunt in the winter, they have become synonymous with bitter cold: Il fait un froid de canard dehors, It's freezing outside.

une corde means a rope or, on a musical instrument, a string. Since streams of heavy rain can resemble thick ropes, the expression tomber (or pleuvoir) des cordes means to pour with rain. (The English noun a cord is generally translated by un cordon.)

un bulletin météorologique is the official term for a weather forecast. For simplicity, it is usually abbreviated to la météo, another example of the tendency to shorten longer phrases in the everyday language (lesson 17 note 7). As-tu entendu la météo pour aujourd'hui ? Have you heard today's weather forecast? And la météo can also mean simply the weather. Si la météo est mauvaise, nous ne sortirons pas le bateau, If the weather is bad, we won't get the boat out.

translate.question: Le voyage est ruineux. Ce n'est pas la peine de jeter l'argent par les fenêtres.

fill.question: We're going to Burgundy this weekend. - Great! That will give me something else to think about. Nous allons en Bourgogne ce week-end. - Chouette ! Ça me changera les idées.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Although France is a secular country (un pays laïc), it has a strong Catholic tradition, and many public holidays -in addition to Noël (Christmas) and Pâques (Easter)- are religious in origin. Chief among them are le quinze août or l'Assomption, the Assumption on 15 August, and le premier novembre, or la Toussaint, All Saints' Day on 1 November. These, and other holidays, are known collectively as les jours fériés (from a Latin word for leisure). Similarly, une fête, generally translated as a party, is also a feast day or saint's day. The tradition of wishing a person Bonne fête, or "good name-day" on the feast day of the saint after whom they are named is rapidly disappearing as France becomes increasingly multicultural. (For the expression faire le pont, see the Culture Note in lesson 18.) Productive phase: 9th lesson

dialogue.french: Quand mon père était jeune, les moyens de communication que nous avons aujourd'hui n'existaient pas.
dialogue.english: When my father was young, the means of communication that we have today did not exist.

dialogue.french: S'il voulait contacter un parent ou un ami, il devait écrire une lettre ou, en cas d'urgence, envoyer un télégramme ...
dialogue.english: If he wanted to contact a relative or a friend, he had to write a letter or, in case of emergency, send a telegram ...

dialogue.french: Un quoi ? Je pensais qu'un télégramme était un journal breton !
dialogue.english: A what? I thought a telegram was a Breton newspaper!

dialogue.french: Non, imbécile, les télégrammes permettaient... Laisse tomber, tu n'étais même pas né.
dialogue.english: No, idiot, telegrams allowed ... Drop it, you weren't even born.

dialogue.french: Je disais donc que papa ne pouvait jamais s'attendre à une réponse instantanée.
dialogue.english: So I was saying that dad could never expect an instant answer.

dialogue.french: Il ne pouvait pas non plus obtenir une information immédiatement.
dialogue.english: Neither could he get information immediately.

dialogue.french: Comme c'est affreux ! Il n'avait pas le téléphone ? Ça existait alors, non ?
dialogue.english: How awful! He didn't have a phone? It existed back then, didn't it?

dialogue.french: Bien sûr, mais les appels coûtaient très cher donc on ne téléphonait que rarement.
dialogue.english: Of course, but calls were very expensive and people telephoned only rarely.

dialogue.french: Si j'ai bien compris, les forfaits illimités n'existaient pas encore ?
dialogue.english: If I have understood correctly, unlimited phone plans didn't yet exist?

dialogue.french: C'est ça. Et on utilisait des pigeons voyageurs pour communiquer. Non, je plaisante.
dialogue.english: That's right. And we used homing pigeons to communicate. No, I'm joking.

dialogue.french: À cette époque, papa jouait au Tiercé toutes les semaines.
dialogue.english: At that time, dad used to play the Tiercé every week.

dialogue.french: Comme lui et maman vivaient loin du village voisin, il prenait son vélo pour aller au PMU.
dialogue.english: As he and mum lived far from the neighbouring village, he would take his bike to go to the betting office.

dialogue.french: Il ne gagnait jamais mais ça l'amusait de jouer et il aimait bien se balader à la campagne.
dialogue.english: He never won but he enjoyed playing and he liked riding around the country.

dialogue.french: Je suis sûr que tes parents espéraient toucher le gros lot.
dialogue.english: I'm sure that your parents were hoping to get the jackpot.

dialogue.french: C'est possible mais j'en doute. Malheureusement, mon père choisissait ses chevaux au hasard.
dialogue.english: It's possible but I doubt it. Unfortunately, dad used to choose horses at random.

dialogue.french: Il lançait des dés ou jouait à pile ou face, c'est pourquoi il perdait toujours.
dialogue.english: He would throw dice or flip a coin, which is why he always lost.

dialogue.french: Enfin, presque toujours. Une nuit, pendant qu'il dormait, il a fait un rêve ...
dialogue.english: Well, almost always. One night, while he was sleeping, he had a dream ...

dialogue.french: Le lendemain, il ne se souvenait pas exactement du nom du gagnant mais pensait qu'il s'appelait "Fortune".
dialogue.english: The next day, he didn't remember exactly the name of the winner but he thought it was called "Fortune".

dialogue.french: Mais ce jour-là il pleuvait tellement fort que papa n'a pas pu aller au village.
dialogue.english: But that day, it was raining so hard that dad was unable to go to the village.

dialogue.french: Et heureusement, car il n'y avait pas de "Fortune".
dialogue.english: And fortunately, because there was no "Fortune".

dialogue.french: C'est la semaine suivante que "Bonne Fortune" a remporté la course et papa a touché ... une petite fortune !
dialogue.english: It's the following week that "Good Fortune" took the race and dad got ... a small fortune!

notes.content: alors, seen several times with the meaning so or then, literally means at the time, back then (from lors, which we will see later): Je l'ai connue à la fac. Elle était alors étudiante, I knew her at uni. Back then, she was a student. A shorter, more elegant way of saying à cette époque (line 12), alors is never used at the beginning of a sentence in this context.

We know that les moyens can mean financial resources (lesson 37 note 6) but it is also the word for means i.e. a method for achieving something. However, while the English word takes an s, even in the singular, un moyen / les moyens depends on the context. J'ai trouvé un moyen de le contacter, I've found a means of contacting him. Contrast that with Il y a deux moyens de payer : par téléphone ou par carte, There are two means of payment: by phone or by card.

un parent means a parent but also a relative (cousin, niece, etc.). So mes parents et amis can mean my parents OR my relatives and friends. The word is also used in the expression le parent pauvre: Le changement climatique est le parent pauvre des politiques de ce gouvernement, Climate change is the poor relative of this government's policies. Always check the context!

The feminine noun urgence means urgency, and, in some cases, rush: Il n'y a pas d'urgence, ça peut attendre, There's no rush; it can wait. With the partitive d', it means emergency: La mairie a pris des mesures d'urgence, The town hall has taken emergency measures. And the emergency department of a hospital, le service des urgences, is often referred to simply as les urgences.

The compound verb laisser tomber ("to allow to fall") means to drop: Attention, il va laisser tomber le sac par terre !, Watch out, he's going to drop the bag on the floor! Idiomatically, the expression can mean -as in English- to abandon: Il a laissé tomber ses études de médecine, He dropped his medical studies. (Note the construction; we'll meet it again shortly.) As an interjection, Laisse(z) tomber ! is equivalent to Forget it, expressing irritation or boredom: Cet exercice est trop difficile. - Laissez tomber, This exercise is too hard. - Forget it. In this context, there is no need for a direct object.

un forfait basically means an all-in financial package or a fixed sum. It is widely used in two contexts: travel (for example, un forfait avion-hôtel, a flight and hotel package) and telephony, where the basic meaning is a phone plan: J'ai dépassé mon forfait, I've exceeded my plan limit.

papa and the feminine equivalent maman are the equivalents of mum(my) and dad(dy). Papa et maman sont mariés depuis plus de trente ans, Dad and mum have been married for more than 30 years. In everyday usage, une maman is more common that une mère.

un voisin means a neighbour (we saw the feminine form in lesson 36) but the word can also be an adjective, meaning neighbouring, nearby, etc. Ils habitant dans le rue voisine, They live in the next street. By extension, le voisinage means the neighbourhood.

The little-used English word velocipede, a forerunner of the bicycle, comes from the French un vélocipède, which passed long ago into everyday language as un vélo, a bike. The "technical" term is une bicyclette. Such is the longstanding popularity of cycling in France that the affectionate term for the machine is la petite reine ("little queen").

un hasard (lesson 22 line 1) is another faux-ami, meaning chance or luck. Je l'ai rencontré par hasard dans la rue hier, I met him by chance in the street yesterday. The expression au hasard means randomly (or haphazardly). Il répondait toujours au hasard à mes questions, He always used to answer my questions at random. (A hazard is translated as un risque or un danger.)

fill.question: I abandoned my phone plan because it was much too expensive. J'ai laissé tomber mon forfait parce qu'il était beaucoup trop cher.

fill.full_answer: Quand nous étions jeunes, nous devions aller à l'école jusqu'à l'âge de treize ans seulement. - C'est affreux !

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: France has a state-operated system for horse race betting. It is controlled through an organisation called le Pari mutuel urbain, or PMU, named for a mutual betting system (un pari, a bet) also used in North America (although un PMU can also mean a betting office). Bets were originally placed on a combination of three horses, hence the name Tiercé. Although the system has since been expanded to four (Quarté) or five (Quinté) winners, le Tiercé is widely used as a generic term for horse betting. Most punters would love to win le gros lot ("fat prize"), also known as le jackpot ... Although telegrams were phased out in France in 2018, the word is used on the masthead of several popular newspapers in Brittany, notably Le Télégramme de Brest. Productive phase: 10th lesson

title: Le voisinage est de plus en plus dangereux !

dialogue.french: L'incendie a commencé peu après la fermeture du bar, aux environs de minuit.
dialogue.english: The fire began shortly after the bar closed, around midnight.

dialogue.french: Que faisiez-vous à ce moment-là ? Étiez-vous dans les environs ?
dialogue.english: What were you doing at that time? Were you in the vicinity?

dialogue.french: Non, je n'y étais plus. Je rentrais chez moi à pied et je n'ai rien vu du tout.
dialogue.english: No, I was no longer there. I was walking home and I didn't see anything at all.

dialogue.french: J'ai quelque chose à vous dire, monsieur le brigadier ...
dialogue.english: I have something to tell you, officer ...

dialogue.french: Un peu de patience, madame, s'il vous plaît. Et vous monsieur ? Qu'avez-vous vu ?
dialogue.english: A little patience, madam, please. And you, sir? What did you see?

dialogue.french: Je sortais de la bouche du métro lorsque j'ai aperçu un grand nuage de fumée.
dialogue.english: I was coming out of the metro entrance when I noticed a big cloud of smoke.

dialogue.french: Je pensais d'abord que c'était du brouillard car le temps était maussade et il bruinait depuis peu.
dialogue.english: I thought at first that it was fog because the weather was gloomy and it had been drizzling for a short while.

dialogue.french: Mais je me suis rendu compte que l'immeuble à l'angle de l'avenue brûlait.
dialogue.english: But I realised that the building on the corner of the avenue was burning.

dialogue.french: J'ai pris mon téléphone pour appeler les secours mais quelqu'un l'avait déjà fait.
dialogue.english: I took my phone to call the emergency services but someone had already done it.

dialogue.french: Les pompiers ont mis peu de temps à venir - moins de trois minutes, pas plus.
dialogue.english: The firefighters came quickly - less than three minutes, no more.

dialogue.french: En tout cas, ils sont arrivés juste à temps car le toit était complètement en flammes.
dialogue.english: In any case, they arrived just in time because the roof was completely in flames.

dialogue.french: Mais laissez-moi parler ! Saviez-vous que le voisinage était de plus en plus dangereux ?
dialogue.english: But let me speak! Did you know that the neighbourhood was increasingly dangerous?

dialogue.french: Peu importe. Continuez, monsieur. Combien y avait-il de camions, plus ou moins ?
dialogue.english: Never mind. Go on, sir. How many trucks were there, more or less?

dialogue.french: Au début il y en avait peu - trois ou quatre je crois, pas plus -,
dialogue.english: At the beginning, there weren't many - three or four I believe, no more -,

dialogue.french: mais d'autres sont arrivés peu de temps après. J'en ai vu plus d'une dizaine, environ.
dialogue.english: but others arrived shortly afterwards. I saw around ten of them.

dialogue.french: Ils sont arrivés pendant que l'immeuble continuait à brûler de plus en plus fort.
dialogue.english: They arrived while the building continued to burn increasingly fiercely.

dialogue.french: Les équipages ont réalisé un travail formidable.
dialogue.english: The crews did an amazing job.

dialogue.french: S'il vous plaît, j'attends depuis longtemps. Écoutez-moi et, après, je ne dirai plus rien.
dialogue.english: If you please, I've been waiting for a long time. Listen to me and, afterwards, I won't say anything else.

dialogue.french: Encore vous ! Mais que voulez-vous ?
dialogue.english: You again! But what do you want?

dialogue.french: Je voulais vous prévenir que pendant que vous parliez avec ces gens quelqu'un a volé votre voiture, celle qui était garée là-bas.
dialogue.english: I wanted to warn you that while you were talking to these people someone stole your car, the one that was parked over there.

dialogue.french: Et quand aviez-vous l'intention de m'en parler ? Plus tard, peut-être ?
dialogue.english: And when did you intend to talk to me about it? Later, maybe?

notes.content: The pronunciation of the adverb plus changes according to its function. Listen carefully to the text and recorded exercise. Detailed rules can be found in lesson 63. province : Note the open vowel [ahn] in the second syllable: [provahnss]. Don't confuse this word with Provence, the beautiful region of southeastern France, pronounced [provœh"ss].
The adverb peu is used in numerous contexts. On its own in a time clause, it means shortly or just: Je suis arrivé peu avant la fermeture du magasin, I arrived shortly before the shop closed.

We know that environ means about, approximately (lesson 46 line 7). As an adverb, it does not agree. However, it can take an s in the expressions dans les environs, meaning in the vicinity, Il habite dans les environs, He lives in the vicinity/neighbourhood. If further information is added, we need the preposition de: Ils habitent dans les environs de la gare, They live somewhere around the station. When the idea of approximation applies to time we use the expression aux environs de: Il est arrivé aux environs de midi, He arrived at around noon ...

We saw un peu de, a little, a bit of, in lesson 11. Do not confuse the noun un peu with the adverb peu de, which means little/few (remember that French makes no difference between countable and uncountable nouns in this context). Here's a simple mnemonic: J'ai peu d'argent mais un peu de temps, I have little money but a bit of time.
The conjunction lorsque, when, is a synonym of quand. There is no difference in meaning between the two, but lorsque is more elegant. (The root word is lors, seen in alors, lesson 59 note 5.)

depuis with the present tense, meaning for or since, is generally followed by a specific time period (lesson 41 note 2). But it can also be followed by less precise adverbs such as peu and longtemps. In the first case, Je la connais depuis peu, the English equivalent would use either a negative verb or an affirmative with only: I have not known her for long / I have known her for only a short time. With longtemps, the translation is more straightforward: Je les connais depuis longtemps, I have known them for a long time. Note the grammatical simplicity of the French constructions.

The reflexive verb se rendre compte ("to render account to oneself") means to realise or be aware. When followed by a direct object, it takes de: Est-ce qu'il se rend compte de la situation ? Is he aware of the situation? (Because compte is the direct object of the reflexive verb, rendu does not agree in number or gender: Elle s'est rendu compte ...) The verb réaliser is a faux-ami, meaning to do or to carry out.
When plus is used at the end of a sentence to mean more, the final s is pronounced. The pronunciation rules for plus are quite complex (see lesson 63).

The verb importer means to import (goods, etc.) but also to matter, to be important (as in English: a matter of import). It is often found in the exclamation Peu importe, It doesn't matter or Never mind. C'est très cher ! - Peu importe. It's very expensive! - Never mind.

pendant followed by a period of time means during (see table, lesson 49). Followed by the conjunction que it means while. In a past-tense sentence, the verb that follows is generally in the imperfect tense: Le téléphone a sonné pendant qu'ils dormaient, The phone rang while they were sleeping (i.e the action lasted a certain period of time).

translate.question: Nous avons besoin de plus de moyens car nous ne voulons plus travailler vingt-quatre heures sur vingt-quatre.
answers: We need more resources because we no longer want to work 24/7.

translate.question: Comme disait un des candidats à l'époque : "Travailler plus pour gagner plus".
answers: As one of the candidates used to say at the time: "Work more to earn more."

translate.question: Plus tard dans la nuit, les bruits devenaient de plus en plus forts, mais je n'avais plus peur.
answers: Later in the night, the noises got louder and louder, but I was no longer scared.

translate.question: Prenez le métro pour y aller. Tu mettras plus de temps en bus.
answers: Take the metro to get there. You'll take longer by bus.

translate.question: Tu connais l'expression "Plus ça change, plus c'est la même chose" ?
answers: You know the expression "The more things change, the more they stay the same"?

fill.question: I think he was sleeping because I couldn't hear any noise in his bedroom. Je pense qu'il dormait car je n'entendais aucun bruit dans sa chambre.
fill.full_answer: Je pense qu'il dormait car je n'entendais aucun bruit dans sa chambre.

fill.question: My son and my daughter were coming out of the house when they saw flames that were rising towards the roof. Mon fils et ma fille sortaient de la maison quand ils ont vu des flammes qui montaient vers le toit.
fill.full_answer: Mon fils et ma fille sortaient de la maison quand ils ont vu des flammes qui montaient vers le toit.

fill.question: The first firefighters took more or less three minutes to reach the spot. Les premiers pompiers ont mis plus ou moins trois minutes à arriver.
fill.full_answer: Les premiers pompiers ont mis plus ou moins trois minutes à arriver.

fill.question: I realised that you did an amazing job. I just wanted to say thank you. Je me suis rendu compte que vous avez réalisé un travail formidable. Je voulais juste vous dire merci.
fill.full_answer: Je me suis rendu compte que vous avez réalisé un travail formidable. Je voulais juste vous dire merci.

fill.question: While we were looking for a car park, someone stole our truck. Pendant que nous cherchions un parking, quelqu'un a volé notre camion.
fill.full_answer: Pendant que nous cherchions un parking, quelqu'un a volé notre camion.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Les secours d'urgence or, more commonly, les secours (the origin of the English word succour), is the blanket term for the emergency services. In addition to la police, the police force, which in France is organised on a nationwide basis, whence the official name la Police nationale, the other main service is the fire brigade, les sapeurs-pompiers (although the first part of the title -the root of the English word a sapper- is generally omitted in everyday speech). The origin of the term un pompier is une pompe, a pump. Productive phase: 11th lesson

title: La célébrité ne me dit pas grand-chose

dialogue.french: Mon invitée est la photojournaliste Isabelle Rossi, pour son nouveau livre 'THistoire en images''.
dialogue.english: My guest is the photojournalist Isabelle Rossi, for her new book "History in Pictures".

dialogue.french: Bonjour, Isabelle. Pouvez-vous raconter à nos auditeurs comment vous avez choisi votre carrière ?
dialogue.english: Hello, Isabelle. Can you tell our listeners how you chose your career?

dialogue.french: Avec plaisir. Je suis née il y a soixante-dix ans à Ajaccio dans une famille plutôt modeste.
dialogue.english: With pleasure. I was born seventy years ago in Ajaccio to a fairly modest family.

dialogue.french: Je savais très jeune que je voulais devenir peintre et que, pour ça, il fallait quitter la Corse,
dialogue.english: I knew very young that I wanted to be a painter and, for that, it was necessary to leave Corsica,

dialogue.french: m'installer en Métropole, et fréquenter des gens avec les mêmes intérêts que les miens.
dialogue.english: settle on the mainland, and keep company with people with the same interests as mine.

dialogue.french: C'était dur au début car j'étais plutôt timide et je ne connaissais pas grand monde.
dialogue.english: It was tough at the beginning because I was quite shy and I didn't know many people.

dialogue.french: Mais petit à petit je me suis fait des amis et aussi j'ai fait la connaissance d'autres gens comme moi.
dialogue.english: But little by little I made friends and also made the acquaintance of other people like me.

dialogue.french: Mais vous avez laissé tomber assez vite la peinture. Pourquoi cette décision ?
dialogue.english: But you abandoned painting quite quickly. Why that decision?

dialogue.french: Je me suis rendu compte que, en définitive, je n'étais pas si douée que ça.
dialogue.english: I realised that, in fact, I wasn't as gifted as that.

dialogue.french: En revanche, j'avais un don pour la photographie - pas des paysages ou la mode -
dialogue.english: By contrast, I had a gift for photography - not landscapes or fashion -

dialogue.french: mais plutôt pour, à la fois des portraits et des scènes de la vie ordinaire.
dialogue.english: but both portraits and scenes from ordinary life.

dialogue.french: Cependant il y avait peu de femmes photographes professionnelles à ce moment-là ;
dialogue.english: However, there were few professional women photographers at that time

dialogue.french: et, pour réussir, j'ai dû surmonter des obstacles et apprendre à ne jamais baisser les bras.
dialogue.english: and, to succeed, I had to overcome obstacles and learn to never give up.

dialogue.french: J'ai enfin trouvé ma voie - et mon bonheur - en devenant photographe de rue.
dialogue.english: I finally found my way in life - and my happiness - by becoming a street photographer.

dialogue.french: J'adorais flâner à travers la ville, le matin de bonne heure, quand la journée commençait,
dialogue.english: I loved wandering through the city, early in the morning, when the day was beginning,

dialogue.french: et prendre en photo les gens qui s'occupaient de leurs affaires et vivaient leur vie.
dialogue.english: and photographing people who were going about their business and living their lives.

dialogue.french: Il fallait leur demander la permission d'être photographiés mais la plupart d'entre eux disaient oui.
dialogue.english: It was necessary to ask their permission to be photographed, but most of them said yes.

dialogue.french: Certains refusaient, souvent par timidité, et je savais que ça ne valait pas la peine d'insister.
dialogue.english: Some would refuse, often out of shyness, and I knew that it wasn't worth insisting.

dialogue.french: Mais vous avez réussi brillamment et vous êtes devenue très vite célèbre.
dialogue.english: But you succeeded brilliantly and you quickly became very famous.

dialogue.french: Vous savez, la célébrité ne me dit pas grand-chose. En fait ça m'énerve,
dialogue.english: You know, fame doesn't mean much to me. In fact, it annoys me,

dialogue.french: car une belle photo vaut mieux qu'un long discours ou le succès médiatique.
dialogue.english: because a beautiful photo is worth more than a long speech or media success.

notes.content: Since an initial h is a half-vowel, it is usually mute when liaised to final consonant of the previous word. Thus bonne heure is pronounced identically to bonheur [bonuhr].

We are familiar with falloir, to be necessary / to have to. This impersonal verb is also defective, meaning that it has only once conjugation in each tense: the third person singular. The imperfect form is fallait: Il ne fallait pas inviter ce journaliste, That journalist shouldn't have been invited.

fréquenter is a good example of a French verb with both a Latin-based and an Anglo-Saxon translation, depending on the level of formality. De nombreux oiseaux fréquentent cette région; Numerous birds frequent this region or, less formally, Évite de fréquenter les bars du centre-ville, Avoid going to the bars in the city centre. The verb can also be translated as to keep company with, spend time with, etc. By contrast, the adjective fréquent simply means frequent.

We know that the adjective grand, big, can also mean great, eminent, etc. (lesson 16 note 2). It can also mean main, principal. Note the subtle difference in meaning in this sentence: La grande difficulté est de trouver une salle assez grande, The main difficulty is finding a room that is big enough. In short, grand designates anything of size, importance, value or seniority.

In the expression faire la connaissance, to make the acquaintance of, connaissance is still abstract. But it can also be a concrete noun: Simon n'est pas un ami, plutôt une connaissance, Simon is an acquaintance rather than a friend. (The noun remains feminine, regardless of the acquaintance's sex.) Remember that the root verb is connaître.

We know laisser tomber means to drop (lesson 59 note 5). In the perfect tense, it is not possible to put two participles together, so, in any two-word compound verb -faire cuire, to cook, for example- the second verb stays in the infinitive: J'ai fait cuire des pommes de terre, I've cooked some potatoes, or, as in lesson 59, Il a laissé tomber son sac, He dropped his bag.

Faux-ami alert: un(e) photographe, a photographer, une photographie, a photograph (often abbreviated to une photo, a photo), and la photographie (or la photo), photography. The latter is classified as either numérique (digital) or argentique, a neologism from l'argent, silver, usually translated as film or analogue.

à la fois means at the same time, at once: N'essaie pas de faire trop de choses à la fois, Don't try to do too many things at once. The expression can also translate both in the case of two nouns: La solution est à la fois simple et pas chère, The solution is both simple and inexpensive. In a longer construction, however, the expression can simply be dropped, but the meaning remains: La solution est à la fois simple, rapide et pas chère, The solution is simple, quick and inexpensive.

dû is the past participle of devoir: Nous avons dû partir car nous étions en retard, We had to leave because we were late. The circumflex is important in order to distinguish the word from the partitive du, just as the grave accent allows us to distinguish between ou (or) and où (where). In neither case does the accent change the pronunciation.
valait is the imperfect of valoir (lesson 44 note 10).

The expression pas grand-chose means not much, nothing much. On ne sait pas grand-chose de lui, We don't know much about him. / Not much is known about him. The expression Ça ne me dit pas grand-chose means It doesn't mean very much to me or, in a more familiar register, It doesn't grab me. Note that we use grand even though the noun is feminine. The same form is found in a couple of other nouns, notably une grand-mère, a grandmother (the masculine being un grand-père, grandfather).

answers: They realised their mistake but it was already too late.

translate.question: La plupart d'entre vous ont déjà entendu cette histoire, mais ça vaut la peine de la raconter.

translate.question: En cas d'urgence, deux têtes valent mieux qu'une, et même s'il n'y a pas d'urgence.
answers: In case of emergency, two heads are better than one, and even if there isn't an emergency.

translate.question: Le restaurant est excellent : les plats sont à la fois simples, bons et pas chers.
answers: The restaurant is excellent: the dishes are simple, good and inexpensive.

fill.question: Where is the cinema? You must have told me but I've forgotten. Où est le cinéma ? Tu as dû me le dire, mais j'ai oublié.
fill.full_answer: Où est le cinéma ? Tu as dû me le dire, mais j'ai oublié.

fill.question: Juliette became a grandmother at the age of fifty but we don't know a lot about her. Juliette ... à l'âge de cinquante ans mais on ne sait pas grand chose d'elle.
fill.full_answer: Juliette est devenue grand-mère à l'âge de cinquante ans mais on ne sait pas grand chose d'elle.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: La Corse (Corsica both in English and the Corsican language: le corse) is an island 170 kilometres off the Mediterranean coast of France. Known as l'île de Beauté the Isle of Beauty, it is famed for dramatic scenery, mountainous terrain, sandy beaches and lush vegetation. Corsica has its own language (le corse -note the masculine gender) and a very strong cultural identity combining influences from all over the Mediterranean and beyond. Corsicans -among others- often refer to mainland France as la Métropole ("metropolitan France"), often without a capital m, since the word was once used to differentiate a country from its colonies or possessions. Productive phase: 12th lesson

title: J'ai mis le doigt sur ton problème ...

dialogue.french: On m'a dit "Apprends à coder et les entreprises t'accueilleront les bras ouverts !".
dialogue.english: I was told "Learn to code and companies will welcome you with open arms!"

dialogue.french: "Elles font les yeux doux aux informaticiens qui ont les dents longues."
dialogue.english: "They make sweet eyes at ambitious computer scientists who have long teeth."

dialogue.french: J'ai eu un coup de cœur pour le métier de programmateur - eh oui, ça arrive -
dialogue.english: I was taken with the job of programmer - oh yes, it happens -

dialogue.french: et j'ai fait la sourde oreille à ceux qui me déconseillaient de poursuivre.
dialogue.english: and I turned a deaf ear to those who advised me against continuing.

dialogue.french: J'ai commencé une formation mais j'ai vite appris que ce n'était pas si évident que ça.
dialogue.english: I started a training session but I quickly learned that it wasn't as easy as that.

dialogue.french: Si certains concepts sont simples, d'autres me passent au-dessus de la tête.
dialogue.english: If some concepts are simple, others go over my head.

dialogue.french: Je me creuse la tête pour essayer de comprendre,
dialogue.english: I rack my brains to try to understand,

dialogue.french: mais c'est souvent impossible et je m'arrache les cheveux.
dialogue.english: but it's often impossible and I tear my hair out.

dialogue.french: J'ai envie de prendre mes jambes à mon cou et fuir l'école,
dialogue.english: I want to take to my heels and flee the school,

dialogue.french: mais j'ai peur de mettre les pieds dans le plat parce que mon prof est très sympa.
dialogue.english: but I'm scared to put my foot in it because my teacher is very nice.

dialogue.french: Par contre, les autres étudiants ne lèvent pas le petit doigt pour m'aider.
dialogue.english: By contrast, the other students don't lift a finger to help me.

dialogue.french: Je pourrais serrer les dents et leur demander de l'aide, mais l'idée me fait froid dans le dos.
dialogue.english: I could grit my teeth and ask them for help, but the idea sends a shiver down my spine.

dialogue.french: Si seulement quelqu'un voulait me donner un coup de main ...
dialogue.english: If only someone would give me a hand ...

dialogue.french: Doucement ! Ne te casse pas la tête.
dialogue.english: Take it easy! Don't worry about it.

dialogue.french: Je te conseille de changer ton fusil d'épaule et d'essayer une approche différente.
dialogue.english: I advise you to change your stance and to try a different approach.

dialogue.french: D'abord il faut mettre un peu d'huile de coude, travailler davantage,
dialogue.english: First you need to use a little elbow grease and to work harder,

dialogue.french: mais en utilisant des nouveaux outils pédagogiques pour surmonter les difficultés.
dialogue.english: but using new teaching tools to overcome the difficulties.

dialogue.french: On pense souvent qu'ils coûtent les yeux de la tête mais c'est faux !
dialogue.english: It's often thought that they cost a fortune but that's wrong!

dialogue.french: Tu peux même en avoir certains à l'œil. Mais il faut les utiliser.
dialogue.english: You can even get some of them free. But you have to use them.

dialogue.french: Oh, quelle barbe ! J'en ai marre d'étudier continuellement.
dialogue.english: Oh, what a pain! I'm fed up with studying continuously.

dialogue.french: C'est décourageant, peut-être, mais fais ce que je te dis maintenant.
dialogue.english: It may be disheartening, but do what I tell you now.

dialogue.french: Tu seras bientôt diplômé et tu pourras dormir sur tes deux oreilles.
dialogue.english: You'll soon have your diploma and you'll be able to rest easy.

notes.content: The adjective doux (fem. douce; adverb doucement) means gentle, sweet, mild, soft, etc. (Think dulcet). Il fait un temps doux et sec, The weather is mild and dry. Ce savon laisse vos mains douces, This soap leaves your hands soft. The idiomatic expression faire les yeux doux (à) is the equivalent of to make eyes at someone. By extension it can mean to woo, to cosy up to, etc. L'équipe fait les yeux doux au jeune joueur marocain, The team is eyeing up the young Moroccan player.

avoir les dents longues ("to have long teeth") means to be ambitious: Il ira loin, Jean, il a les dents longues. Jean will go far. He's ambitious. Be careful: it is not the translation of to be long in the tooth, translated by ne pas être tout jeune ("not to be very young").

avoir un coup de cœur (pour quelque chose / quelqu'un) ("have a blow of the heart") means to fall in love with or have a crush on (something or someone) or, more abstractly, to be taken with. A common usage of the noun un coup de cœur is as synonym for a favourite. Nos coups de cœur de cette semaine: This week's favourites.

The adjective sourd means deaf. Faire la sourde oreille, to turn a deaf ear to. (French can use adjectives as nouns, which is not always possible in English: un sourd, a deaf person, un jeune, a young person, etc. We will see more examples in another lesson.)

la tête, the head, is used in many French idioms. With the reflexive form of the verb creuser, to dig, se creuser la tête is the equivalent of to rack one's brains. Je me suis creusé la tête mais je ne m'en souviens pas, I've racked my brains but I can't remember. By contrast, se casser la tête ("to break one's head") means to worry or fret. The verb is often used in the expression Ne te casse / vous cassez pas la tête, Don't worry (about it).

prendre ses jambes à son cou ("take one's legs to one's neck") paints a cartoonish image of a person running away quickly: to take to one's heels. Note that, unlike many other idioms, this one does not use the reflexive form of the verb.

Another idiom that resembles its English equivalent: mettre les pieds dans le plat, to put one's foot in it. Spot the little differences: a plural noun (pieds) and an extra bit of detail (le plat, the dish).

Despite the final -s, le dos, the back (lesson 48), like le bras (lesson 61), is singular and does not change in the plural. The expression faire froid dans le dos means to send shivers down the spine. It can be used impersonally (Ça fait froid dans le dos) or with an indirect object pronoun: Ce qu'ils ont trouvé leur fait froid dans le dos, What they've found sends shivers down their spine.

Here are two more French words that have passed into English: une épaule, a shoulder (think an epaulette, a shoulder-worn decoration) and un fusil, a gun (a fusilier, a type of soldier). The idiom changer son fusil d'épaule ("to change one's gun to the other shoulder") means to shift one's stance, to have a change of heart: Le ministre n'est pas prêt à changer son fusil d'épaule au sujet de la santé, The minister isn't prepared to shift his stance on the health issue.

Many idioms are formed from un œil / les yeux. If something is obtained à l'œil, it is free (tradespeople used to judge a person's eligibility for free credit by casting an eye over them). By contrast, if something is exorbitantly expensive, il coûte les yeux de la tête, it costs the earth ("eyes of the head"). Interestingly, British English has integrated the American expression to cost an arm and a leg, which French has now transformed into coûter un bras.
une barbe, a beard (the origin of barber) is used as an exclamation La barbe ! / Quelle barbe !, What a drag! Whence the idiomatic adjective barbant, boring.

If you sleep soundly, you hear nothing. This is probably the origin of dormir sur ses deux oreilles ("to sleep on both ears"), which also means to rest easy. And while English-speakers sleep like a log, a francophone will dormir comme une souche, literally "to sleep like a tree stump"!

new_words.french: les entreprises t'accueilleront les bras ouverts
new_words.english: companies will welcome you with open arms

new_words.french: j'ai eu un coup de cœur

new_words.english: to those who advised me against

new_words.french: que ce n'était pas si évident que ça
new_words.english: that it wasn't as easy as that

new_words.french: d'autres me passent au-dessus de la tête
j'ai envie de prendre mes jambes à mon cou
new_words.english: I want to take to my heels

new_words.french: mais j'ai peur de mettre les pieds dans le plat
new_words.english: but I'm scared to put my foot in it

new_words.french: parce que mon prof est très sympa
new_words.english: because my teacher is very nice

new_words.french: les autres étudiants ne lèvent pas le petit doigt
new_words.english: the other students don't lift a finger

new_words.french: mais l'idée me fait froid dans le dos
new_words.english: but the idea sends a shiver down my spine

new_words.french: quelqu'un voulait me donner un coup de main
new_words.english: someone would give me a hand

new_words.french: ne te casse pas la tête
il faut mettre un peu d'huile de coude
new_words.english: you need to use a little elbow grease

new_words.french: on pense souvent qu'ils coûtent les yeux de la tête
new_words.english: it's often thought they cost a fortune

but you have to use them
