translate.question: Tu peux dormir sur tes deux oreilles. Demain, il fera doux et sec.
answers: You can rest easy. Tomorrow it will be fine and dry.

translate.question: Je pense que j'ai mis les pieds dans le plat. J'ai dit que Max n'est pas tout jeune.
answers: I think I've put my foot in it. I said that Max was long in the tooth.

translate.question: Ne te casse pas la tête, mon frère peut me donner un coup de main.
answers: Don't worry, my brother can give me a hand.

translate.question: Il faut faire davantage pour aider les sourds à surmonter les obstacles qu'ils rencontrent.
answers: We have to do more to help deaf people overcome the hurdles that they meet.

translate.question: Mon patron n'a pas levé le petit doigt pour m'aider ! C'est très décourageant.
answers: My boss didn't lift a finger to help me! It's very disheartening.

fill.question: We're tearing our hair out because the mayors are turning a deaf ear to our problems. Nous ... car les maires font la sourde oreille à nos difficultés.
fill.full_answer: Nous nous arrachons les cheveux car les maires font la sourde oreille à nos difficultés.

fill.question: I fell in love with his last book, even if some of the texts are over my head. J'ai eu ... pour son dernier livre, même si certains textes me passent au-dessus de la tête.
fill.full_answer: J'ai eu un coup de cœur pour son dernier livre, même si certains textes me passent au-dessus de la tête.

fill.question: Maurice wants to succeed; he's very ambitious. - Yes, but he's a bit long in the tooth. Maurice veut réussir ; il a les dents longues. - Oui, mais il n'est pas tout jeune.
fill.full_answer: Maurice veut réussir ; il a les dents longues. - Oui, mais il n'est pas tout jeune.

fill.question: I'm racking my brains to find a solution. - It's easy: use some elbow grease. Je me creuse la tête pour trouver une solution. - C'est simple : mettez de l'huile de coude.
fill.full_answer: Je me creuse la tête pour trouver une solution. - C'est simple : mettez de l'huile de coude.

fill.question: You've got to grit your teeth and smile. - The idea sends a shiver down my spine. Il faut serrer les dents et sourire. - L'idée me fait froid dans le dos.
fill.full_answer: Il faut serrer les dents et sourire. - L'idée me fait froid dans le dos.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: We're now beginning to learn the kinds of idiomatic expressions that are used on a regular basis by native speakers. Some of the ones in this lesson will look familiar; others may surprise you. But once you get the hang of them (vous en prenez l'habitude), you'll be speaking truly colloquial French. Productive phase: 13th lesson

dialogue.french: Nous avons vécu en Bretagne quand nous étions jeunes.
dialogue.english: We lived in Brittany when we were young.

dialogue.french: Jacques enseignait le français et moi, je travaillais dans le tourisme.
dialogue.english: Jacques taught French and I worked in tourism.

dialogue.french: Vous comprenez le breton, alors ?
dialogue.english: So you understand Breton?

dialogue.french: Non, je n'y comprends rien. Pourtant, j'ai essayé de l'apprendre quand j'y habitais.
dialogue.english: No, I don't understand a thing. And yet I tried to learn it when I lived there.

dialogue.french: J'aimais beaucoup cette région, même s'il faisait souvent un froid de canard !
dialogue.english: I used to like the region a lot, even though it was often freezing cold!

dialogue.french: Les gens étaient très sympathiques : ils nous ont accueillis les bras ouverts,
dialogue.english: The people were very nice: they welcomed us with open arms,

dialogue.french: même s'ils n'ont pas levé le petit doigt pour nous aider avec leur langue !
dialogue.english: even if they didn't lift a finger to help us with their language!

dialogue.french: J'ai commencé à prendre des leçons, mais j'ai laissé tomber au bout de quelques mois.
dialogue.english: I started to take lessons, but I dropped them after a few months.

dialogue.french: Il fallait continuer ! Il ne faut jamais baisser les bras.
dialogue.english: You should have continued! You must never give up.

dialogue.french: Non, ça ne valait pas la peine parce que je savais que nous n'allions rester que deux ou trois années.
dialogue.english: No, it wasn't worth it because I knew that we were going to stay only two or three years.

dialogue.french: En fait, nous avons dû revenir à Paris au bout d'un an. Ensuite, nous sommes repartis en Corse.
dialogue.english: In fact, we had to come back to Paris after a year. Then we went off to Corsica.

dialogue.french: J'ai eu tout de suite un coup de cœur pour l'île de Beauté : c'est un des plus beaux endroits du monde.
dialogue.english: I fell in love straight away with the Isle of Beauty: it's one of the most beautiful places in the world.

notes.content: L'imparfait: the imperfect tense: The imperfect is used to describe actions performed regularly or for an extended period in the past, with no mention of when they began or ended. (The tense owes its name to the fact such actions are not "perfected," or completed). There is no direct equivalent in English. Depending on the context, the imperfect can be translated by the past continuous (I was teaching) or the past habitual (I used to teach, I would teach). In some cases, particularly descriptions, the best equivalent is the past simple. The imperfect for all three verb groups (including irregular verbs) is formed from the first person plural of the present tense, deleting the -ons ending and adding -ais, ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, and -aient to the stem. Here are examples from the three groups: enseigner, to teach → enseign-: j'enseignais, tu enseignais, il/elle enseignait, nous enseignions, vous enseigniez, ils/elles enseignaient. finir, to finish→ finiss-: je finissais, tu finissais, il/elle finissait, nous finissions, vous finissiez, ils/elles finissaient. savoir, to know→ sav-: je savais, tu savais, il/elle savait, nous savions, vous saviez, ils/elles savaient. The only exception is être: j'étais, tu étais, il/elle était, nous étions, vous étiez, ils/elles étaient. There are two special cases: the impersonal verb falloir and the defective verb pleuvoir, to rain, are both used in the third person singular only: il fallait; il pleuvait. The negative and interrogative forms of the imperfect are formed in the usual way: tu ne connaissais pas ?; est-ce-que tu connaissais ? / connaissais-tu ?, etc. In some case, the stem changes very slightly. In the case of -er verbs like lancer, to throw (lesson 59), the c takes a cedilla before the letter a: il lançait (if not, the c would be a hard k). Likewise, in verbs such as manger, to eat, and déranger, to disturb, the g becomes ge before a and o: tu mangeais, il dérangeait (otherwise, the g would be hard g). The imperfect tense is used for continuous or habitual actions in the past, notably those that, in English, are in the past continuous (Elle vivait à Lyon à l'époque, She was living in Lyon at the time). It is used particularly in sentences where a continuing action is interrupted by a single action in the past: Elle dormait quand son téléphone a sonné. She was sleeping when her phone rang. One very common use is narration: Quand nous habitions à Grenoble, j'enseignais le français et mon ami travaillait à Colombe. Nous avions beaucoup de chance car nous habitions à trente kilomètres de la montagne. En été, j'allais faire de la randonnée toutes les semaines. In this case, the imperfect is translated by the past simple tense or with would because the actions are all habitual: When we lived in Grenoble, I taught French and my friend worked at Colombe. We were very lucky because we lived thirty kilometres away from the mountains. In summer, I would / used to go hiking every week. Note the difference between En été, j'allais faire de la randonnée toutes les semaines and this sentence: En été, je suis allé faire de la randonnée quatre fois, In summer, I went hiking four times. In both cases, there is a repetitive action, but the first sentence describes a habitual (and less precise) action.

valoir and falloir: These two irregular verbs, which are sometimes confused, are used idiomatically. Let's take a look: • valoir: Present: je/j' vaux, tu vaux, il/elle vaut, nous valons, vous valez, ils/elles valent. Future: je/j' vaudrai, tu vaudras, il/elle vaudra, nous vaudrons, vous vaudrez, ils/elles vaudront. Imperfect: je/j' valais, tu valais, il/elle valait, nous valions, vous valiez, ils/elles valaient. Past: j'ai valu, tu as valu, il/elle a valu, nous avons valu, vous avez valu, ils/elles ont valu. Valoir means to be worth: Cette maison vaut deux millions, This house is worth two million. The derived noun, value, is la valeur: Je n'ai aucune idée de la valeur de ce produit. I have no idea of the value of this product. Pecuniary value aside, valoir conveys the notion of merit or quality: Que vaut ce nouveau jeu ? How good is this new game? Ces nouvelles mesures ne valent pas grand-chose. These new measures don't mean very much. In this context, the impersonal idiom valoir par is used to emphasise the main quality of something, expressed in a noun: Cet hôtel vaut par son ambiance. The good thing about this hotel is its atmosphere. The impersonal third-person singular, vaut, is coupled with the comparative mieux, better, to talk about something preferable or more desirable: Il vaut mieux partir de bonne heure. It's better to leave early. Another common impersonal use is the expression Ça vaut la peine, It's worth it, or, more idiomatically, Ça vaut le coup. There are many other idiomatic uses of valoir, some of which we will see in future lessons, but the underlying theme is that of worth or value. Try to learn as many as you can: Ça vaut le coup ! • falloir (with a double l) is an impersonal verb whose basic meaning is to need or to be necessary. It is conjugated only in the third person singular: Present: il faut. Future: il faudra. Imperfect: il fallait. Past: il a fallu. The verb is impersonal -it can never take a subject- and is generally followed by a noun or an infinitive, just like need. The translation depends, as always, on the context. For example, Il faut beaucoup d'argent pour voyager can be translated formally with one or informally with the impersonal "you": One needs / You need a lot of money to travel. Another possibility is to use the passive: A lot of money is needed, etc. To personalise falloir, add an indirect object pronoun (me, lui, etc.) before the conjugated form: Il me/lui faut de l'argent (see lessons 44 line 3 and 53 line 5). And just as American English uses need to when giving instructions, so French uses falloir: Il vous faut tourner à gauche. You need to take a left. In the interrogative, the inverted question form is very common, particularly in a polite register. When shopping, for instance, you might be asked: Vous faut-il autre chose ? Do you need anything else? rather than Est-ce qu'il vous faut autre chose ? Naturally, falloir is used in idiomatic expressions, many of which concern the idea of appropriate action. For example: Il a dépensé une fortune ! - Il faut ce qu'il faut. He spent a fortune - Whatever it takes ("it's necessary what is necessary"). Another concise expression -so concise that it has been imported into English- is comme il faut, suggesting proper or necessary behaviour: Le travail doit être fait comme il faut. The work has to be done properly. Keep an eye out for other falloir expressions, and make a note of them.

When to pronounce the "s" in plus: The pronunciation, or not, of the final s in the adverb and noun plus depends, among other things, on the word's grammatical function and position in the sentence or expression. A basic rule is that s is voiced if plus has a positive meaning and unvoiced if negative. • Pronounced: The s is pronounced [plewss] when plus means more: Il faut plus de moyens ... More resources are needed ... Similarly, at the end of a sentence: Ce n'est pas assez ; il faut un peu plus. It's not enough; you need a little more. ... and as a noun: Ce stage est un plus pour ta carrière. That course is a plus for your career. More generally, it is pronounced -always as a [z]- when followed by adjective starting with a vowel: plus_intéressant, [plewzah"teressœh"]. • Silent: The s is silent ([pl/ew]) when plus is used in the negation ne ... plus or, at the end of a phrase in non plus: Nous n'avons plus d'argent, We have no more money. - Et nous n'en avons pas, non plus, And we have none either. Likewise, it is silent in the comparative form if the adjective it precedes starts with a consonant: Ils sont plus grands, They are bigger. However, in the intensifier plus en plus, the first s is pronounced (as a z) to make the liaison: [duh plewz-œh"-plew]. In short, it is very important to remember the grammatical function of plus: Est-ce qu'il y a un peu plus ([plewss]) de lait ? - Non, je n'en ai plus ([plew]).

Conjunctive adverbs (pourtant, cependant, etc.): Conjunctive adverbs can modify or qualify the statements or sentences they precede or follow. Here are some of the ones you have met so far: • Pourtant is used to contrast or contradict, in the same way as yet, however, still, or even so. Je ne la connais pas bien. Pourtant, je lui fais confiance. I don't know her well, and yet I trust her. The contrast is often highlighted with mais: C'est difficile à croire mais pourtant vrai. It's hard to believe and yet it's true. or, especially, et: "Et pourtant elle tourne!" disait Galilée. "And yet it moves ("turns"), "said Galileo. The adverb is often used before the verb, rather than after it, or at the beginning of the phrase, as in English: Il faut pourtant le faire. It has to be done even so / And yet it has to be done. • Cependant has almost exactly the same meaning but is more formal than pourtant. Most importantly, it is not preceded by et: C'est difficile à croire, cependant c'est vrai. It's hard to believe, and yet it's true (see above). • Néanmoins is almost a direct translation of nevertheless (néant means nothingness): J'espère néanmoins que vous vous êtes amusés. I hope you had fun nevertheless. • Alors has several meanings. As an adverb of consequence, it means so or then. In the same way that many English speakers begin a sentence with So ... , francophones will say: Alors, tu viens ?, So, are you coming? The adverb is frequently used in exclamations, too: Alors ça, ça me fait plaisir, Now that makes me happy. But alors also refers back to the past, like at that time, back then: Je l'ai connu en 2018. Il était alors mon directeur. I knew him in 2018. At that time, he was my director. In every case, the translation will depend on the context, the register and the person speaking. (And remember, there may be no precise English equivalent.) We recommend that you note down any examples of usage that surprise you.

Idioms (1): body parts: Idioms are a vital part of a language, but they can be hard to understand because their overall meaning is different from the literal meaning of the individual words they are composed of. Because of the similarities between French and English, some idioms are common to both languages, as seen in lesson 62 (s'arracher les cheveux); others are almost the same but vary slightly (mettre les pieds dans le plat); and still others are either totally different (avoir les dents longues) or have no direct equivalent (à l'œil). One of the best ways of learning idioms is to group them by category. In the last few lessons, we saw some of those in the "body part" category: accueillir (quelqu'un) les bras ouverts (welcome someone with open arms), baisser les bras (give up, abandon), coûter un bras (cost an arm and a leg), s'arracher les cheveux (tear one's hair out), avoir un coup de cœur (fall in love with / be smitten by), mettre de l'huile de coude (use elbow grease), serrer les dents (grit one's teeth), ne pas lever le doigt (not lift a finger), mettre le doigt sur quelque chose (put one's finger on something), faire froid dans le dos (make one's blood run cold), changer son fusil d'épaule (change one's stance), prendre ses jambes à son cou (take to one's heels), donner un coup de main (à quelqu'un) (give someone a hand), avoir quelque chose à l'œil (get something for free), dormir sur ses deux oreilles (sleep soundly), faire la sourde oreille (turn a deaf ear), mettre les pieds dans le plat (put one's foot in it), se casser la tête (worry / fret about), se creuser la tête (rack one's brains), passer au-dessus de la tête de quelqu'un (go over someone's head), coûter les yeux de la tête (cost the earth), faire les yeux doux (à) (make eyes at).

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: Productive phase: 14th lesson

dialogue.french: Entrez, entrez monsieur. Est-ce que je peux vous renseigner ?
dialogue.english: Come in, come in sir. Can I give you some information?

dialogue.french: Il me faut des vacances parce que je suis épuisé. Qu'est-ce que vous pouvez me proposer ?
dialogue.english: I need a holiday because I'm exhausted. What can you offer me?

dialogue.french: Tout dépend : combien de temps pouvez-vous partir ? À quelles conditions et à quel moment ?
dialogue.english: It all depends: how long can you go away for? On what terms and at what period?

dialogue.french: Si vous partez avant le mois de juillet, normalement vous serez tranquille,
dialogue.english: If you leave before the month of July, you should have some peace and quiet,

dialogue.french: mais si vous attendez les grandes vacances, ça sera réellement l'enfer.
dialogue.english: but if you wait for the summer holiday, it really will be hellish.

dialogue.french: Tout est bondé en cette période, les prix grimpent et les températures aussi.
dialogue.english: Everything is crowded during that period, prices climb and temperatures too.

dialogue.french: Les moments les plus agréables sont soit début-juin soit fin-septembre.
dialogue.english: The nicest times are either early June or late September.

dialogue.french: Ce sont les moments où il y a le moins de monde. Pour moi, ce sont les plus beaux mois.
dialogue.english: These are the times when there are fewer people. For me, they are the nicest months.

dialogue.french: Et quel est le pire mois de l'année, d'après vous : juillet ou août ?
dialogue.english: And what's the worst month of the year, according to you: July or August?

dialogue.french: Ils sont aussi mauvais l'un que l'autre, mais je pense qu'août est pire que juillet.
dialogue.english: Each is as bad as the other, but I think that August is worse than July.

dialogue.french: S'il fait beau, les plages sont saturées de touristes, et s'il fait mauvais on ne peut pas se baigner.
dialogue.english: If it's fine, the beaches are inundated with tourists, and if the weather's bad you can't go swimming.

dialogue.french: Pour éviter ça, si vous avez envie, vous pourrez essayer quelque chose de plus original.
dialogue.english: To avoid that, if you want to, you can try something more original.

dialogue.french: Par exemple, vous pourrez faire de la plongée sous-marine dans le Var,
dialogue.english: For example, you can go underwater diving in the Var,

dialogue.french: ou bien de la randonnée à cheval dans le Massif central. C'est merveilleux à cette période de l'année.
dialogue.english: or horse trekking in the Massif Central. It's wonderful at that time of year.

dialogue.french: Non, aucun des deux ne me tente. Je ne sais pas nager,
dialogue.english: No, neither of the two tempts me. I can't swim,

dialogue.french: je ne sais pas monter à cheval, et je n'ai pas envie de prendre des cours non plus.
dialogue.english: I can't ride, and I don't want to take lessons either.

dialogue.french: J'ai une autre possibilité, qui est très reposante, très confortable mais pas donnée.
dialogue.english: I have other possibility, which is very relaxing, very comfortable, but not cheap.

dialogue.french: Une semaine dans un grand hôtel, Le Domaine des Dieux, sur la Côte d'Azur.
dialogue.english: A week in a grand hotel, the Domain of the Gods, on the Riviera.

dialogue.french: C'est un des plus beaux palaces de la région.
dialogue.english: It's one of the nicest luxury hotels in the region.

dialogue.french: Il y a une offre de dernière minute avec une réduction exceptionnelle de deux pour cent.
dialogue.english: There is a last-minute offer with an exceptional reduction of two per cent.

dialogue.french: Et c'est combien ? Je crains le pire ...
dialogue.english: And how much is it? I fear the worst ...

dialogue.french: La formule tout compris est à dix mille euros, hors taxes pour quatre jours en demi-pension.
dialogue.english: The all-in package is at ten thousand euros, before tax for four days at half-board.

dialogue.french: Mm, je vois. Et des cours de plongée, ça serait combien ?
dialogue.english: Mm, I see. And how much would diving lessons cost?

notes.content: grand hôtel: Since h is a half-vowel, the pronunciation rule explained in lesson 16 applies here, i.e. the terminal d is pronounced [t]: [grœh"t-ohtel].

renseigner is the verb from which the masculine noun renseignement (lesson 37 line 7) is derived. It means to give information or directions to. Pouvez-vous me renseigner ? is a formal way of asking for information. The question Est-ce que je peux / Puis-je vous renseigner ? is used by service industry staff to greet customers: Can I help you? The information desk in a public building is signposted RENSEIGNEMENTS.

Here is the first, or "zero," conditional which, like English, uses the present+future construction: Si vous partez maintenant, vous arriverez avant midi, If you leave now, you'll arrive before noon. In some cases, the si clause introduces a simple observation, in which case the present is used in both parts of the sentence, and can be replaced by quand: S'il fait / Quand il fait mauvais, les plages sont vides, If/when the weather is bad, the beaches are empty.

normalement, normally (lesson 8 line 4) is also used to express a condition: Il vient tout à l'heure ? - Normalement oui, Is he coming later? - Yes, he should do. In this context, it can be understood as under normal circumstances or if all goes well.

The construction of a superlative clause depends on whether the adjective comes after the noun, which is the general rule, or before it (the BAGS rule, lesson 14). In the first case, the article is repeated: les mois les plus agréables; but not in the second: les plus beaux mois.

après vous means after you, Je suis arrivé(e) après vous, I arrived after you. It is also a polite way of allowing someone to go ahead of you. But d'après with a noun or pronoun means according to: D'après Jarnel, il y a beaucoup de monde au mois d'août, According to Jarnel, August is very crowded.

faire is used when talking about sports or physical activity, whereas in English we generally use to go: Je fais du ski deux fois par an, I go skiing twice a year. In other cases, faire du/de la/de l' + sport can be translated by a single verb in English, faire de la natation: to go swimming OR to swim; faire du cheval, to go horse-riding OR to ride. Quand elle était au lycée, elle faisait de la natation tous les jours, When she was at school, she used to swim every day.

Another difference with English is the use of savoir to translate the notion of ability (i.e. to know how to): Je ne sais pas nager et ma femme ne sait pas conduire, I can't swim and my wife can't drive.
un cours (singular, despite the s) is a synonym of une leçon, a lesson. We can say un cours de français or une leçon de français.

The idiom Ce n'est pas donné means It's not cheap ("not given [away]"). And, as in our example, pas donné can be incorporated into a sentence: Leurs produits ne sont pas donnés mais ils sont de très bonne qualité, Their products aren't cheap but the quality is very good.

The technical term une formule, a formula, is frequently used in a commercial context to mean a package, an offering: Notre formule "tout compris" comprend les vols, les hôtels et les repas, Our all-in deal or package includes flights, hotels and meals. The term is commonly found in restaurants offering une formule déjeuner, the equivalent of the "English" term a prix fixe lunch.

title: Cent pour cent des gagnants ont tenté leur chance !

dialogue.french: Voici une bonne occasion de partir en vacances gratuitement !
dialogue.english: Here's a good opportunity to go on holiday for free!

dialogue.french: La bibliothèque municipale organise un jeu-concours avec un tirage au sort des billets gagnants.
dialogue.english: The municipal library is organising a game-competition with a random draw of the winning tickets.

dialogue.french: Si tu gagnais le premier prix, tu pourrais passer une semaine aux sports d'hiver.
dialogue.english: If you won the first prize, you could spend a week at winter sports.

dialogue.french: J'irais tous les ans à la montagne si je pouvais. Pas toi ?
dialogue.english: I would go to the mountains every year if I could. Wouldn't you?

dialogue.french: Je n'y suis jamais allée, et je dois dire que ça ne m'attire pas vraiment.
dialogue.english: I've never been, and I must say that it doesn't really attract me.

dialogue.french: J'aurais besoin d'équipements spéciaux et je suppose qu'ils me coûteraient une fortune.
dialogue.english: I would need special equipment and I suppose that it would cost me a fortune.

dialogue.french: Pas nécessairement : on peut soit les acheter, soit les louer, et la location serait beaucoup moins chère.
dialogue.english: Not necessarily: you can either buy it or rent it, and rental would be much cheaper.

dialogue.french: Si tu voulais, tu pourrais demander à Amir et Yasmine s'ils accepteraient de te prêter le nécessaire.
dialogue.english: If you wanted, you could ask Amir and Yasmine if they would agree to lend you what you need.

dialogue.french: Je suis sûr qu'ils diraient oui. De quoi d'autre aurais-tu besoin?
dialogue.english: I'm sure that they would say yes. What else would you need?

dialogue.french: Tout, absolument tout. Il me faudrait une combinaison, des gants, des chaussures ...
dialogue.english: Everything, absolutely everything. I would need a ski suit, gloves, boots ...

dialogue.french: Je vois ce que tu veux dire ! Te faut-il autre chose ?
dialogue.english: I see what you mean. Do you need anything else?

dialogue.french: Si j'allais dans une station à la mode, comme celles en Suisse,
dialogue.english: If I went to a fashionable resort, like the ones in Switzerland,

dialogue.french: il me faudrait au moins un nouveau tailleur ainsi qu'un ensemble décontracté pour l'après-ski.
dialogue.english: I would need at least another suit and a casual outfit for the après-ski.

dialogue.french: Je devrais me renseigner pour la tenue vestimentaire, d'ailleurs.
dialogue.english: I should enquire about the dress code, actually.

dialogue.french: Penses-tu que les stations françaises sont aussi huppées que les stations suisses ?
dialogue.english: Do you think that the French resorts are as posh as the Swiss resorts?

dialogue.french: Elles sont pareilles, je crois. Elles sont toutes fréquentées par les mêmes millionnaires !
dialogue.english: They're the same, I believe. They are all frequented by the same millionaires!

dialogue.french: Et si tu allais ailleurs ? Si tu avais la possibilité, où choisirais-tu d'aller ?
dialogue.english: What if you went somewhere else? If you had the possibility, where would you choose?

dialogue.french: Je n'en sais rien. Qu'est-ce que tu me conseillerais, toi ?
dialogue.english: I've no idea. What would you advise me?

dialogue.french: Je serais prête à aller n'importe où.
dialogue.english: I'd be willing to go anywhere.

dialogue.french: Si j'étais toi, je choisirais une petite station quelque part dans les Pyrénées.
dialogue.english: If I were you, I would choose a small resort somewhere in the Pyrenees.

dialogue.french: Mais, avant de faire tous ces projets, tu devrais acheter un billet pour le jeu...
dialogue.english: But before making all these plans, you should buy a ticket for the game...

notes.content: pareille the feminine and the masculine (pareil) are pronounced identically in both the singular and the plural: [parayy].

This is the second conditional, expressing the possible result of a hypothetical action. Pouvoir is an irregular verb, the conditional mood being je pourrais, tu pourrais, il/elle pourrait, nous pourrions, vous pourriez, ils/elles pourraient. In an if-then sentence, French uses the imperfect tense (never the conditional) after si, instead of the past simple in English: Il pourrait t'aider si tu voulais, He could help you if you wanted.

The present conditional of aller is j'irais, tu irais, il/elle irait, nous irions, vous iriez, ils/elles iraient. There is no need for an auxiliary (e.g. would). The first person singular is pronounced identically to the future form (j'irai), i.e. the final s is silent. The same rule applies to all verbs.
The conditional of avoir is j'aurais, tu aurais, il/elle aurait, nous aurions, vous auriez, ils/elles auraient. (Remember j'aurais is a homophone of the future form j'aurai.)

With most ordinary verbs, the conditional is formed by adding the endings of the imperfect tense to the infinitive. Thus the conditional of coûter is je coûterais, tu coûterais, il/elle coûterait, nous coûterions, vous coûteriez, ils/elles coûteraient.
The conditional of être is je serais, tu serais, il/elle serait, nous serions, vous seriez, ils/elles seraient.
You'll find the conditional of vouloir in lesson 70. See also lesson 45 note 6.
For the conditional of -re verbs, the final e is dropped (like for the future): Thus dire → je dirais, tu dirais, il/elle dirait, nous dirions, vous diriez, ils/elles diraient.
The conditional of the defective verb falloir (see lesson 63 note 2) is il faudrait.

la mode generally means fashion (clothing, etc., see lessons 33 and 61), so the adjectival phrase à la mode means fashionable, trendy: Les investissements verts sont à la mode cette année, Green investments are fashionable this year.

The masculine adjective pareil (fem pareille) means the same or similar. Les deux mots sont pareils, The two words are the same. The basic difference between pareil and même is that the former is used alone while the latter requires an article and a noun, usually chose. Tu viens jeudi ou vendredi ? - Pour moi, c'est pareil / c'est la même chose. The expression C'est du pareil au même ("It's from the same to the same") means It's the same thing, etc. Lire ou écouter un livre audio, c'est du pareil au même, Reading or listening to an audio book is six of one, half a dozen of the other.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Les Pyrénées, the Pyrenees, is a range of mountains forming a natural border between France and Spain and spanning several départements, including les Pyrénées Orientales ("eastern Pyrenees"), les Hautes-Pyrénées ("high Pyrenees") and les Pyrénées-Atlantiques (bordering the Atlantic Ocean). One section of the range is a beautiful national park, le parc national des Pyrénées. Productive phase: 16th lesson

dialogue.french: Les élèves d'un lycée lyonnais sont invités à participer au débat sur le climat à l'Assemblée nationale,
dialogue.english: The students from a high school in Lyon are invited to take part in the debate on the climate in the National Assembly,

dialogue.french: avec, comme thème, "Que feriez-vous si vous étiez Président de la République ?"
dialogue.english: on the theme 'What would you do if you were President of the Republic?'

dialogue.french: "Mesdames et messieurs les députés, j'invite Mademoiselle Noura Bouris à prendre la parole.
dialogue.english: Fellow Members of Parliament, I invite Ms Noura Bouris to take the floor.

dialogue.french: Alors, Noura, si vous étiez présidente, que feriez-vous pour combattre le changement climatique ?
dialogue.english: Well, Noura, if you were president, what would you do to fight climate change?

dialogue.french: Est-ce qu'il serait possible, à votre avis, de prendre rapidement des mesures efficaces ?
dialogue.english: Would it be possible, in your opinion, to quickly take effective measures?

dialogue.french: Nous croyons que oui, et il faudrait en effet agir vite car nous sommes tous en danger.
dialogue.english: We believe so, and we should act quickly because we are all in danger.

dialogue.french: Que feriez-vous ? Interdiriez-vous à tous les véhicules à essence et diesel de rouler en ville ?
dialogue.english: What would you do? Would you prohibit all petrol and diesel vehicles from driving in cities?

dialogue.french: Je n'interdirais pas les petits véhicules utilitaires car les artisans ont besoin de se déplacer,
dialogue.english: I wouldn't prohibit small goods vehicles because artisans need to travel,

dialogue.french: mais je ne permettrais pas aux camions de livraison de circuler pendant la journée.
dialogue.english: but I wouldn't allow delivery trucks to drive during the day.

dialogue.french: Et que feriez-vous pour approvisionner les magasins ? Interdiriez-vous n'importe quel camion
dialogue.english: And what would you do to supply the shops? Would you prevent any lorry

dialogue.french: à n'importe quelle heure, n'importe où dans la ville ? Ça ne serait pas très pratique.
dialogue.english: at any time, anywhere in town? That wouldn't be very practical.

dialogue.french: Peut-être, mais je n'aimerais pas que mes enfants respirent constamment de l'air pollué.
dialogue.english: Perhaps, but I wouldn't like my children to constantly breathe polluted air.

dialogue.french: Tout le monde est contre les voitures particulières, mais elles sont un mal nécessaire, non ?
dialogue.english: Everybody is against passenger cars, but they are a necessary evil, aren't they?

dialogue.french: Nous ne sommes pas contre les voitures, monsieur, mais contre le réchauffement planétaire.
dialogue.english: We're not against cars, sir, but against global warming.

dialogue.french: Tous les véhicules polluent mais certains sont pires que d'autres, et de loin.
dialogue.english: All vehicles pollute but some are worse than others, and by far.

dialogue.french: Et, au bout d'un certain temps, les résultats seront catastrophiques.
dialogue.english: And, after a certain time, the results will be disastrous.

dialogue.french: Il faut à tout prix réduire notre empreinte carbone.
dialogue.english: It's necessary to reduce our carbon footprint at all costs.

dialogue.french: N'importe quoi ! Si nous savions comment éliminer les gaz à effet de serre, nous l'aurions fait il y a longtemps.
dialogue.english: Nonsense. If we knew how to eliminate greenhouse gases, we would have done it a long time ago.

dialogue.french: Nous avons la nette impression que vous ne nous écoutez pas.
dialogue.english: We have the distinct impression that you are not listening to us.

dialogue.french: Mais si, mais si, ma petite. Sinon, nous ne vous aurions pas invités aujourd'hui.
dialogue.english: But yes, but yes, my little one. Otherwise, we wouldn't have invited you here today.

dialogue.french: Maintenant, deux autocars attendent dehors pour vous ramener à la maison.
dialogue.english: Now, two coaches are waiting outside to take you home.

dialogue.french: C'est bien ce que nous pensions ! Vous ne nous prenez pas au sérieux.
dialogue.english: That's exactly what we thought! You don't take us seriously.

notes.content: net is irregular because the final t is pronounced, exactly the same as the feminine nette: [net].

We know that adjectives do not take an initial capital. This applies not only to nationalities (lesson 65: la Suisse → suisse) but also to adjectives of location, here the city of Lyon → lyonnais. Like all adjectives, agreement is obligatory: les écoles lyonnaises, schools in Lyon. But remember that, with an indefinite article (often preceded by un(e)), such words are nouns, and therefore take a capital: Notre magazine est lu par un Lyonnais sur deux, Our magazine is read by one Lyonnais in two.
The third interrogative (inversion) is commonly used with the conditional form because it is easier on the ear than the est-ce que form (qu'est-ce que vous feriez), which is nevertheless used.

une parole, like un mot, means a word, but refers more to the underlying thoughts or meaning than to the words themselves. Combien de mots y a-t-il dans cette phrase ? How many words are there in this sentence, but Je te donne ma parole, I give you my word. Prendre la parole ("to take the word") is a formal way of saying to speak or to take the floor, usually in a public forum.

As in English, croire, to believe, can be a synonym for penser, followed by que if used in a clause: Je pense/crois qu'il était surpris, I think/believe that he was surprised. The relative is also used when either verb is followed by oui or non: Je crois/pense que non, I believe/think not.

interdire means to forbid (the origin of the English technical word interdict) and is usually used in the impersonal form with de: Il est interdit de fumer dans la plupart des lieux publics, Smoking is forbidden in most public places. If the verb has a direct object, however, it needs the preposition à: Je vais interdire à mon fils d'utiliser son téléphone le soir, I'm going to forbid my son to use his phone in the evening. The past participle, interdit, is used in warning signs: INTERDIT DE VAPOTER, NO VAPING.

circuler literally means to circulate: L'air circule partout dans la chambre, Air circulates throughout the bedroom. But the verb is used more broadly in the sense of to walk or move around, or, for traffic, to drive: N'oubliez pas qu'en Grande-Bretagne, on circule à droite, Don't forget that in Great Britain, they drive on the right. (Thus la circulation can mean traffic (see lesson 44): La circulation est dense ce soir, Traffic is heavy this evening.) The difference with conduire, seen in the previous lesson, is that circuler is intransitive.

In lesson 65 line 19, we saw the indefinite pronominal expression n'importe où, anywhere. N'importe can be followed by other prepositions or pronouns: Choisissez n'importe quelle carte, Choose any card. N'importe qui peut entrer dans mon bureau, Anyone can come into my office. In such cases, the basic meaning is any. N'importe quoi ! is a dismissive exclamation: Nonsense!

We know that un mal can mean a pain, an ache, harm, etc. (lesson 34 note 3). But the root is le mal, evil (think: malevolent, etc.). So un mal nécessaire is a necessary evil. Less diabolically, le mal means wrong: le bien et le mal, right and wrong or even just a difficulty: Nous avons réussi à la joindre, mais non sans mal, We managed to get hold of her, but not without difficulty.

certain means not only certain (as in line 16: un certain temps, a certain time) but, more broadly, some. In this case, the adjective must agree with its noun: Certaines personnes, notamment des écologistes, sont végétariennes, Some people, particularly ecologists, are vegetarian. In some cases, either translation is appropriate (dans certains cas, in some/certain cases), but you should be aware of the basic difference.

net (feminine nette) means neat or tidy (see nettoyer, to clean, lesson 30 line 9 and lesson 36 note 8). By extension, the adjective can mean clear, sharp or precise. Je n'aime pas les photos numériques car les images sont moins nettes, I don't like digital photos because the pictures are less sharp.

answers: I shouldn't drive my car any more. In any case, you can no longer drive around Paris.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Inhabitants of villages, towns and cities throughout France can all be referred to by a gentilic, derived from the place name and usually formed by adding the suffix -ien(ne) or -ais(e): un(e) Parisien(ne), un(e) Lyonnais(e), etc. When mayors make a formal address to their fellow citizens, they will begin with these proper nouns (always putting the feminine first): "Parisiennes, Parisiens, je compte sur vous". The nearest English equivalent would be "My fellow Parisians, I am counting on you." l'Assemblée nationale (National Assembly) is the lower house of France's two-chamber parliament. Its lawmakers, les député(e)s, sit in le Palais Bourbon, the official seat, on the left bank of the River Seine. (The upper house is le Sénat, as we saw in lesson 54.) Productive phase: 17th lesson

dialogue.french: Madame Claudie Marchand est rentrée à la maison lundi dernier après avoir passé un mois à l'hôpital.
dialogue.english: Mrs Claudie Marchand came home last Monday after having spent a month in hospital.

dialogue.french: Claudie est toujours coincée dans son appartement au douzième étage, la pauvre.
dialogue.english: Claudie is still stuck in her flat on the twelfth floor, the poor thing.

dialogue.french: Comment ça ?
dialogue.english: How so?

dialogue.french: L'ascenseur est tombé en panne il y a trois semaines et il ne marche toujours pas.
dialogue.english: The lift broke down three weeks ago and it still isn't working.

dialogue.french: C'est pénible - surtout pour elle, qui n'est pas sortie de chez elle depuis trois jours.
dialogue.english: It's annoying - especially for her, who hasn't left her house for three days.

dialogue.french: Quand je suis rentré hier, je suis monté la voir pour savoir si elle avait besoin de quelque chose.
dialogue.english: When I got back yesterday, I went up to see if she needed anything.

dialogue.french: J'ai rentré ses plantes, qui étaient sur le balcon depuis un mois et demi, et j'ai descendu sa poubelle.
dialogue.english: I brought in her plants, which had been on the balcony for a month and a half, and I took down her rubbish bin.

dialogue.french: J'ai même sorti son petit chien pour le promener.
dialogue.english: I even took out her little dog to walk it.

dialogue.french: Moi, je suis passée la voir ce matin. J'ai monté quelques courses et son courrier.
dialogue.english: I went by to see her this morning. I brought up some shopping and her mail.

dialogue.french: Après, j'ai passé l'aspirateur, fait un peu de ménage et rangé le désordre.
dialogue.english: After, I passed the vacuum cleaner, did a bit of housework and cleared up the mess.

dialogue.french: Magali, sa voisine du dessus, est descendue la voir et elles ont bavardé pendant une bonne heure.
dialogue.english: Magali, her upstairs neighbour, came down to see her and they chatted for a good hour.

dialogue.french: Je suis sûr que, à son âge, Claudie n'a pas d'ordinateur.
dialogue.english: I'm sure that, at her age, Claudie doesn't have a computer.

dialogue.french: Sinon, elle aurait pu entrer sa liste de courses dans une appli et demander à être livrée à domicile.
dialogue.english: Otherwise, she could have entered her shopping list into an app and asked to be delivered at home.

dialogue.french: Tu serais surpris ! Madame Marchand est très bien équipée pour une sénior.
dialogue.english: You'd be surprised! Mrs Marchand is very well equipped for a senior.

dialogue.french: La première fois que je suis allée chez elle, je suis entrée dans une petite pièce à côté de la cuisine
dialogue.english: The first time I went to her place, I went into a little room next to the kitchen

dialogue.french: et j'ai vu qu'elle avait un nouveau portable, une imprimante, et même une connexion Internet.
dialogue.english: and I saw that she had a new laptop, a printer and even an internet connection.

dialogue.french: Le problème, c'est qu'après un mois à l'hôpital, Claudie a oublié ses identifiants.
dialogue.english: The problem is that after a month in hospital, Claudie has forgotten her login details.

dialogue.french: Je suis retournée avant-hier et j'ai regardé partout, sur les étagères,
dialogue.english: I went back the day before yesterday and I looked everywhere, on the shelves,

dialogue.french: dans ses classeurs et ses boîtes de rangement, mais je n'ai rien trouvé.
dialogue.english: in her filing cabinets and storage boxes, but I didn't find anything.

dialogue.french: En dernier ressort, j'ai retourné son tapis de souris.
dialogue.english: As a last resort, I turned over her mouse mat.

dialogue.french: Bingo ! Les codes et les mots de passe étaient collés là-dessous.
dialogue.english: Bingo! The code and passwords were glued underneath.

dialogue.french: Comme on dit : "L'encre la plus pâle vaut mieux que la meilleure mémoire" !
dialogue.english: As they say: "The palest ink is worth the strongest memory"!

notes.content: verrouillé looks difficult to pronounce, but take the word apart: verr is pronounced as in very, -ou- is [oo], the i feeds into the double-l, pronounced [yy], and the final acute é is [ay]: [verooyay]. Because French is syllable-timed, it's easier to hear the different sounds that make up a word.

A handful of verbs can be conjugated with both avoir and être, but with a slight change of meaning. In the intransitive form, rentrer means to go / come back: Tu es rentré(e) tard hier soir ! You came back late last night! The transitive form means to bring in: As-tu rentré ta voiture au garage ? Did you bring your car into the garage?

The interrogative Comment ça se fait ? (lesson 57 note 1) is often shortened to Comment ça ? in idiomatic French when asking for an explanation: Il n'est pas dans son bureau. - Comment ça ? He's not in his office. - How come?. Other, similar forms can be used, notably Où ça ? - Regarde ce petit chien ! - Où ça ? Look at that little dog? - Whereabouts?

The adjective pénible, from la peine (lesson 46 note 8) describes something that is difficult, laborious, etc. Le voyage en avion était pénible, The flight was difficult. When used to describe a person, it means tiresome, irritating, etc. Qu'est-ce qu'il est pénible !, He's so annoying!

Used intransitively for to go out (lesson 35 section 1.3), sortir is conjugated with être (Elle est sortie, She's gone out). The transitive form -note the direct object (clés)- means to put / take out: Il a sorti ses clés de sa poche, He took his keys from his pocket.

The intransitive monter, to go up, has a similar meaning in the transitive form: to bring / take up: J'ai laissé mes lunettes en bas. Peux-tu les monter s'il te plaît ? I've left my glasses downstairs. Can you bring them up please? (Remember that monter à cheval means to ride).

The opposite of monter is descendre, to get off / to go/come down (lesson 16 line 10). It follows the same pattern when used with a direct object: Pouvez-vous m'aider à descendre ma valise ? Can you help me bring down my suitcase?

We know the intransitive use of passer: A quelle heure passe le bus pour le centre-ville ?, What time does the bus for the town centre come by? The transitive form is self-evident: Passe-moi le savon, Hand me the soap. Remember the difference between passer and se passer, to happen (lesson 56 section 1).

The regular verb bavarder means to chat or, depending on the context, to gossip. On a bavardé un bon quart d'heure, We chatted for a good half hour. However, both the adjective (bavard(e)) and the noun (un(e) bavard(e)) are slightly more judgmental: Qu'est-ce qu'il est bavard !, What a chatterbox!
The transitive form of entrer is used, as in English, to mean to introduce or insert something: Entrez votre code PIN et validez, Enter your PIN and press OK.

In the intransitive form, retourner (generally with à) means to return, to go back (to): Nous sommes retournés à notre hôtel après la réunion, We went back to our hotel after the meeting. The transitive verb means to turn over, up, etc. Retournez le poisson au bout de deux minutes et ajoutez les légumes, Turn the fish over after two minutes and add the vegetables.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: You will have noticed that we are gradually whittling down the pronunciation section because, by now, you should be familiar with most of the sounds in French, and, in particular, the various verb tenses. From now on, we will indicate only the new words that we introduce and any unusual or irregular pronunciations. You can gauge yourself just how much progress you have made so far. (If in doubt about how a particular word or group of words is pronounced, listen carefully to the recordings.) Productive phase: 18th lesson

dialogue.french: Salut Papy, salut Mamie. Ça fait longtemps que je ne vous ai pas vus !
dialogue.english: Hi Grandad, hi Granny. I haven't seen you for a long time!

dialogue.french: Vous allez bien tous les deux ? En tout cas, vous avez bonne mine.
dialogue.english: Are you both well? In any case, you look well.

dialogue.french: Bonjour ma petite ! C'est très gentil de nous rendre visite.
dialogue.english: Hello my dear! It's very kind of you to visit us.

dialogue.french: Ton grand-père et moi sommes tous les deux contents.
dialogue.english: Your grandfather and I are both happy.

dialogue.french: On te voit si peu ces jours-ci : tu dois être très occupée.
dialogue.english: We see so little of you these days: you must be very busy.

dialogue.french: Oui, je fais un tas de choses et je cours à droite et à gauche, comme toujours,
dialogue.english: Yes, I'm doing loads of things and I'm running around left, right and centre, as always,

dialogue.french: mais dès que j'ai un moment, je viens vous voir, vous le savez bien.
dialogue.english: but as soon as I have a moment, I come and see you, as you know very well.

dialogue.french: Mais tu viens toujours seule : tu n'as pas un amoureux ? Un petit-ami ? Ou même une petite-amie ?
dialogue.english: But you always come on your own: don't you have a sweetheart? A boyfriend? Or even a girlfriend?

dialogue.french: Tu sais bien que tu peux nous présenter qui que ce soit : nous sommes des vieux jeunes !
dialogue.english: You know very well that you can introduce us to anyone: we're young old people!

dialogue.french: Mamie, je t'en prie ! Chaque chose en son temps.
dialogue.english: Granny, please! Everything in its own time.

dialogue.french: J'ai une blague à te raconter : "Un muet dit à un sourd qu'un aveugle les espionne,
dialogue.english: I've got a joke to tell you: "A dumb person says to a deaf person that a blind person is spying on them,

dialogue.french: en se cachant derrière les cheveux d'un chauve". C'est drôle, non ?
dialogue.english: hiding behind the hair of a bald person." That's funny, isn't it?

dialogue.french: Papy, comment te dire ... ? Je ne suis pas morte de rire.
dialogue.english: Grandad, how can I put this? I'm not dying of laughter.

dialogue.french: Tu devrais savoir que notre vocabulaire a changé depuis que toi et Mamie étiez jeunes.
dialogue.english: You should know that our vocabulary has changed since you and Granny were young.

dialogue.french: Par exemple, on ne dit plus un aveugle mais un "non-voyant";
dialogue.english: For instance, we no longer say "a blind person" but a "non-sighted person";

dialogue.french: et "un malentendant" est plus juste que "un sourd", tu comprends ?
dialogue.english: and "a hearing impaired person" is more accurate than "a deaf person," you understand?

dialogue.french: Bon, changeons de sujet : comment vas-tu rentrer à Paris ?
dialogue.english: Right, let's change the subject: how are you going to get back to Paris?

dialogue.french: Une amie m'a déposée ici en voiture mais je vais rentrer en train.
dialogue.english: A friend dropped me here by car but I'm going to go back by train.

dialogue.french: Je crois qu'il y en a un vers dix-huit heures. Avez-vous un horaire ?
dialogue.english: I think that there is one around 6pm. Do you have a timetable?

dialogue.french: Oui il circule toutes les heures dès treize heures. Je t'amènerai à la gare.
dialogue.english: Yes, it runs every hour from 1pm onwards. I'll take you to the station.

dialogue.french: Merci, je reviendrai dès que possible, c'est promis. Au fait, peux-tu me prêter vingt euros ?
dialogue.english: Thanks, I'll come back as soon as possible, I promise. By the way, can you lend me twenty euros?

dialogue.french: Je suis temporairement sans ressources. Autrement dit, fauchée.
dialogue.english: I'm temporarily without resources. In other words, broke.

notes.content: un tas: as usual, the final s is silent [ta]. Of course, the plural is pronounced identically. [puhteet-amee / puhteet-amee]. Both the masculine and the feminine forms have the same pronunciation so it is important to listen to the article (un/une).

une petite-fille (masc. un petit-fils) means a granddaughter (resp. grandson). The hyphen is essential because both words are compound nouns; without it, une petite fille (un petit fils) would mean a small girl (resp. small son). The plural forms are petites-filles and petits-fils. There is almost no difference in pronunciation between the compound noun and the adjective+noun formation, in both the singular and the plural, so context is all-important.

papy (or papi) et mamie are the equivalents of grandad and granny. As in English, the terms are sometimes used pejoratively (old man, old woman) or humorously: le papy boom, the grey boom (retirement of the baby boomers).

Ça fait... can introduce a time clause (lesson 37 line 1). On its own Ça fait longtemps means It's been a long time. It can also be used in a sentence, usually with the present or the perfect: Ça fait longtemps que tu apprends le français ? Have you been learning French for long? Ça fait longtemps qu'elle a quitté son pays, She left her country a long time ago. Note how the clause comes at the beginning of the French sentence but the end of the English one.

We know that there is no word for both (lesson 57 note 2). Where English uses the word as an adverb (both you and I, for example), we translate it as tous les deux / toutes les deux, generally after the verb: Michel et moi aimons tous les deux la campagne française, Both Michel and I love the French countryside. (Note that French uses the stressed pronoun moi, not the subject pronoun je.)

une mine refers to a person's face or physical aspect: Elle a mauvaise mine. Qu'est-ce qu'elle a ? She doesn't look well. What's the matter with her? Ne faites pas cette mine triste, Don't pull that sad face. (Une mine also means a mine, but the context will clear up any ambiguity.)

Adjectives can easily be used as singular nouns in French. Mon petit (fem. ma petite) is usually affectionately to address a younger person, a bit like my dear. Other examples in this lesson of adjectives used as nouns include: Il est amoureux de toi, He's in love with you → C'est un amoureux de la musique, He's a music-lover. In many cases, English needs to add a noun to convey the same idea: aveugle, blind → un(e) aveugle, a blind man/woman; sourd, deaf → un(e) sourd(e), a deaf man/woman.

un tas means a heap or pile. Il y avait un grand tas de livres dans le coin de la chambre, There was a big pile of books in the corner of the bedroom. In a familiar register, though, un tas de means loads of or heaps of: J'ai un tas de choses à vous raconter, I've loads of things to tell you. The plural is des tas: J'ai des tas de choses ...

Used in a time clause, the preposition dès -note the grave accent- means (as) from: L'agence ouvrira ses portes dès midi, The agency will open its door from noon onwards. Dès always marks the start of the action but is not used in a from-to context (L'agence ouvrira ses portes de midi à dix-huit heures.) Dès que means as soon as or as early as: Donne-moi des nouvelles dès que possible, Send me news as soon as possible.

soit is the third person singular subjunctive of être (the equivalent of est in the present tense). The phrase ... que ce soit, preceded by a pronoun like qui, quoi or où, has the indefinite sense of any+pronoun: La règle est la même, où que ce soit en France, The rule is the same, anywhere in France. We'll learn more about the subjunctive later on.

The verb faucher literally means to mow, reap or cut down. However, the adjectival participle fauché(e) is used idiomatically to mean broke or hard up. The link between the literal and metaphorical meanings is evident in the expression être fauché comme les blés, i.e. to be stony-broke.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Imported from the US and the UK, terminology designed to avoid offence to particular groups of people has entered French. This mode of expression applies in particular to certain forms of physical and mental disadvantage (for example non-voyant, non-seeing, rather than aveugle, blind). Culturally sensitive language is increasingly common, particularly in the media, and is worth paying close attention to. In Canada, in particular, a major effort has been made to find appropriate terminology in French. Productive phase: 19th lesson

dialogue.french: As-tu le temps de manger un morceau avec moi ce midi ?
dialogue.english: Do you have time to get a bite to eat at noon?

dialogue.french: Je devais déjeuner avec mon fils, mais il m'a posé un lapin.
dialogue.english: I was supposed to have lunch with my son but he stood me up.

dialogue.french: Je pense qu'il a d'autres chats à fouetter que de sortir avec son vieux papa.
dialogue.english: I think that he's got other fish to fry rather than to go out with his old dad.

dialogue.french: Ça serait avec le plus grand plaisir !
dialogue.english: It would be with great pleasure!

dialogue.french: Il y a quelques jours, j'étais malade comme un chien.
dialogue.english: A few days ago, I was as sick as a dog.

dialogue.french: J'avais une fièvre de cheval et plein de courbatures.
dialogue.english: I had a raging temperature and I was aching all over.

dialogue.french: Je commençais à avoir le cafard parce que j'étais clouée au lit depuis une semaine.
dialogue.english: I was beginning to feel down in the dumps because I'd been stuck in bed for a week.

dialogue.french: Dehors, il faisait toujours gris et moche - un vrai temps de chien.
dialogue.english: Outside, it was always grey and dull - really lousy weather.

dialogue.french: Je m'ennuyais comme un rat mort et j'avais un appétit d'oiseau.
dialogue.english: I was bored to death and I was eating like a bird.

dialogue.french: Julien, mon voisin du dessous, venait parfois me rendre visite,
dialogue.english: Julien, my downstairs neighbour, sometimes came to see me,

dialogue.french: mais il est bavard comme une pie et j'avais juste envie de dormir comme un loir.
dialogue.english: but he's a chatterbox and I just wanted to sleep like a log.

dialogue.french: Mais depuis deux jours, je vais mieux
dialogue.english: But for the past two days I've been getting better

dialogue.french: et je commence tout doucement à reprendre du poil de la bête
dialogue.english: and I'm beginning quite slowly to pick up again

dialogue.french: Surtout, j'ai une faim de loup !
dialogue.english: Above all, I'm famished!

dialogue.french: Je passe du coq à l'âne, mais savais-tu que Patrice a trouvé un nouveau boulot ?
dialogue.english: I'm jumping from one thing to another, but did you know that Patrice has found a new job?

dialogue.french: Il est malin comme un singe, celui-là !
dialogue.english: He's as smart as a whip, that one!

dialogue.french: Il a l'air doux comme un agneau, mais il est vachement ambitieux. Qu'est-ce qu'il va faire ?
dialogue.english: He looks as gentle as a lamb but he's damned ambitious. What's he going to do?

dialogue.french: Tu ne devineras jamais. Vas-y : essaie !
dialogue.english: You'll never guess. Go on, try!

dialogue.french: Je sais qu'il court toujours plusieurs lièvres à la fois et il peut tout faire.
dialogue.english: I know that he's got several irons in the fire at once and he can do anything.

dialogue.french: Bijoutier ? Chirurgien ? Éboueur ? Chauffeur de taxi ? Jardinier ?
dialogue.english: Jeweller? Surgeon? Refuse collector? Taxi driver? Gardener?

dialogue.french: Non ? Alors je donne ma langue au chat ! Dis-le-moi s'il te plaît.
dialogue.english: No? Then I give up! Tell me please.

dialogue.french: Il va être vétérinaire, et il est heureux comme un poisson dans l'eau.
dialogue.english: He's going to become a vet, and he's as happy as a lark.

notes.content: A symbol of strength or size, un cheval, a horse (plur. des chevaux, lesson 22) turns up in many idioms and similes, like une fièvre de cheval, a raging fever, and its antidote un remède de cheval, a drastic remedy or cure.

un lapin means a rabbit, and poser, to put or place (lesson 53 line 12). The idiomatic expression poser un lapin (à quelqu'un) means to stand someone up or, simply, not to show up: La chanteuse Nala a posé un lapin à ses fans, The singer Nala didn't turn up to meet her fans.

un fouet means a whip, whence the verb fouetter (note the double t). Avoir d'autres chats à fouetter ("to have other cats to whip") is one of several idioms using different animals to convey the same idea as an English equivalent: to have other fish to fry.

un cafard is the universally unpopular insect a cockroach. And since a roach infestation can cause depression, avoir le cafard means to be feeling low or down in the dumps. Another common expression is avoir un coup de cafard, to have a fit of the blues.

The idiomatic adjective moche is useful for describing ugliness (Il est moche comme un pou ("ugly as a louse"), He's as ugly as sin), or unpleasantness in general: Le match contre Lyon était assez moche, The game against Lyon was pretty awful. The word is often used to describe bad weather.

In French as in English, dogs feature in many idioms, generally expressing drudgery, hardship etc. Une vie de chien, a dog's life. Likewise, J'ai eu un mal de chien à le convaincre, I had a tough time convincing him.
une pie, a magpie, features in the metaphor être bavard comme une pie, which can be translated simply as to be a chatterbox.

Some French idioms are confusing because they resemble English expressions but mean something different. Here, reprendre du poil de la bête shares the same origin as to take a hair of the dog but has nothing to do with hangovers. It means to regain strength, to pick up, etc. Le marché a repris du poil de la bête depuis le début du mois, The market has rallied since the start of the month. Note that un poil / des poils refers to body and animal hair, whereas un cheveu / des cheveux is used for human head hair.

The link (or lack of one) between the two animals in this expression -un coq, a cock (rooster), and un âne, a donkey- is lost in the mists of time, but the expression passer (or sauter) du coq à l'âne means to go or jump from one subject to another, unrelated one.

The adjective malin (fem maligne) comes from le mal, evil, seen in lesson 66 note 8 (whence the English cognate malignant). In everyday French, though, it means clever, smart: Ce n'est pas très malin, ce que tu viens de faire, What you've just done isn't very clever. Because of the word's "devilish" origins, the emphasis is on shrewdness rather than just intelligence. Un singe, a monkey.

vachement, from une vache, a cow, is an idiomatic intensifier commonly used both with adjectives, Elle est vachement intelligente, She's really smart, and with verbs, Il pleut vachement, It's raining really hard. The word should be used with caution because it is quite familiar.

une lièvre means a hare. The verb courir, to run or to race, has entered English as to course, i.e. to hunt, as in hare coursing. "Hunting several hares at a time" -courir plusieurs lièvres à la fois- means to concentrate on more than one thing, or to have several irons in the fire. (One can also have trop de lièvres, too many irons.)

donner sa langue au chat ("to give one's tongue to the cat") means to give up or stop guessing: Si tu donnes ta langue au chat, je te donnerai la réponse, If you give up, I'll tell you the answer. The same idea of being unable to speak is found in the English idiom Has the cat got your tongue? but the scope is broader than in French.

translate.question: Je la trouve moche comme un pou mais elle est maligne comme un singe !
answers: I find her as ugly as sin but she's as smart as a whip!

translate.question: Stéphane s'ennuie comme un rat mort dans son boulot.
answers: Stéphane is getting bored to death in his job.

translate.question: Elle a dormi comme un loir cette nuit et maintenant elle a une faim de loup.
answers: She slept like a log last night and now she's famished.

translate.question: J'ai du mal à vous suivre car vous sautez du coq à l'âne tout le temps.
answers: I find it hard to follow you because you're jumping from one thing to another.

translate.question: J'ai eu un mal de chien à trouver un taxi ce matin.
answers: I had a heck of a time finding a taxi this morning.

dialogue.french: Salut Gérard, ça fait longtemps ! Comment vas-tu ?
dialogue.english: Hi Gérard, it's been a long time! How are you?

dialogue.french: Ça va mieux, merci. J'étais malade comme un chien toute la semaine dernière,
dialogue.english: I'm doing better, thanks. I was as sick as a dog all last week,

dialogue.french: mais je reprends du poil de la bête depuis quelques jours.
dialogue.english: but I've been picking up in the past few days.

dialogue.french: Maintenant, ma femme et moi aimerions beaucoup aller quelque part en vacances.
dialogue.english: Now my wife and I would very much like to go somewhere on holiday.

dialogue.french: D'après mon fils, nous devrions tous aller dans le sud, quelque part où il ferait meilleur.
dialogue.english: According to my son, we should all go south, somewhere the weather would be better.

dialogue.french: C'est vrai que si vous alliez dans le Midi, vous trouveriez peut-être du soleil.
dialogue.english: It's true that if you went to the Midi, you'd perhaps find some sun.

dialogue.french: Qu'est-ce que tu me conseillerais ?
dialogue.english: What would you advise me?

dialogue.french: Quels sont les endroits les plus agréables pour passer une semaine ?
dialogue.english: What are the nicest places to spend a week?

dialogue.french: Si tu vas dans le Vaucluse, vers Avignon, tu pourrais avoir du beau temps.
dialogue.english: If you go to the Vaucluse, towards Avignon, you might get good weather.

dialogue.french: Mais dès que tu descendras vers la côte, tu trouveras certainement ce que tu cherches.
dialogue.english: But as soon as you go down towards the coast, you'll certainly find what you are looking for.

dialogue.french: La température de la mer est agréable, donc vous pourriez vous baigner n'importe quand.
dialogue.english: The sea is a nice temperature, so you could go swimming any time.

dialogue.french: Ce n'est pas la peine : je ne sais pas nager. Et ma femme n'aimerait pas la plage en hiver.
dialogue.english: Don't bother: I can't swim. And my wife wouldn't like the beach in winter.

dialogue.french: Dans ce cas, pourquoi ne pas aller à la montagne ?
dialogue.english: In that case, why not go to the mountains?

dialogue.french: Mais qu'est-ce que je ferais s'il n'y avait pas de neige, ou s'il faisait un temps de chien ?
dialogue.english: But what would I do if there were no snow, or if the weather were dreadful?

dialogue.french: Je m'ennuierais comme un rat mort ! Finalement, on ferait mieux de rester chez nous.
dialogue.english: I'd be bored to tears! In the end, we'd do better to stay at home.

notes.content: Le conditionnel: the conditional: So far, we have seen three types of conditional sentence formed with a si (if) clause and a main clause: • The "zero conditional" simply expresses a general truth, using the same verb tenses as in English: S'il fait froid, les plages sont vides, If it is cold, the beaches are empty. In most case the first part of the proposition can be introduced by quand (when) rather than si (if). • The first conditional describes a cause-effect proposition (i.e. if the condition is fulfilled, then the result is certain). In this case, the verb in the si clause is in the present tense and the second verb is in the future, as in English: Si vous achetez ce sac en ligne, vous le payerez plus cher. If you buy that bag online, you will pay more for it. • The second conditional is a hypothetical, describing what would happen if a condition were satisfied. In an "if → then" construction, the verb governed by si is in the imperfect tense (see lesson 63) and the verb in the main clause is in the conditional. To form the conditional tense, the imperfect endings (-ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient) are added to the infinitive of the verb. For Group 3 verbs ending in -re, the final letter is omitted before adding the endings: gagner: je gagnerais, tu gagnerais, il/elle gagnerait, nous gagnerions, vous gagneriez, ils/elles gagneraient. finir: je finirais, tu finirais, il/elle finirait, nous finirions, vous finiriez, ils/elles finiraient. boire: je boirais, tu boirais, il/elle boirait, nous boirions, vous boiriez, ils/elles boiraient. This rule applies to all verbs. However, four of the most important irregular verbs change their stems: avoir: j'aurais, tu aurais, il/elle aurait, nous aurions, vous auriez, ils/elles auraient. être: je serais, tu serais, il/elle serait, nous serions, vous seriez, ils/elles seraient. aller: j'irais, tu irais, il/elle irait, nous irions, vous iriez, ils/elles iraient. vouloir: je voudrais, tu voudrais, il/elle voudrait, nous voudrions, vous voudriez, ils/elles voudraient. These are not the only exceptions forms -major verbs such as devoir, pouvoir, venir and voir are all irregular- so please check the appendix. As regards spelling, a handful of Group 1 verbs change very slightly. This applies to verbs ending in -eler and -eter, which double the final consonant: j'appellerais, vous appelleriez; je jetterais, vous jetteriez, etc. As we saw with the future (lesson 56 section 2), verbs such as acheter and lever take a grave accent (j'achèterais, tu lèverais). Finally, the conditional of -ayer verbs, like essayer, can be spelled in two ways: j'essayerais or j'essaierais. Both forms are correct and are pronounced identically. The most important thing to remember with a second-conditional construction is that the verb following si is in the imperfect, not the conditional. This is easy to see in a sentence with être: Si j'étais vous, je serais content de partir. If I were you I would be happy to leave. As in English, the conditional is used in many expressions of politeness (Je voudrais un café s'il vous plait). However, for the "tag" response to an invitation (Would you like to come? - Yes I'd like to.), we do not use the conditional in French because it would suggest a reserve: (I would like to but I can't). The usual answer to such an invitation, Voudriez-vous venir ? is usually Avec plaisir. Similarly, where English uses would to express a habitual action in the past (When we were young we would go on holiday together), would go is not a real conditional; in French we use the imperfect for both actions: Quand nous étions jeunes, nous allions en vacances ensemble. There is a fourth construction, the past conditional, which we will not see in this course.

Verbs that can be conjugated with être and avoir: As we saw in lesson 67, the passé composé of some common verbs can be formed with être or avoir, depending on the meaning. The basic difference is between transitive and non-transitive usage. To remember the difference, meet a colleague of Dr Vandertramp's: DR PRE MS: Descendre, Rentrer, Passer, Retourner, Entrer, Monter, Sortir. Il est descendu du bus. (He got off the bus). Il a descendu l'escalier. (He went down the stairs). Nous sommes rentrés tard. (We came back late). Nous avons rentré la poubelle. (We've brought in the bin). Elle est passée tout à l'heure. (She came by earlier). Elle m'a passé le sel. (She passed me the salt). Je suis retourné en Bretagne. (I went back to Brittany). J'ai retourné la crêpe. (I turned over the pancake). Ils sont entrés sans payer. (They went in without paying). Ils ont entré toutes les données. (They entered all the data). Elles sont montées au premier étage. (They went up to the first floor). Elles ont monté les courses. (They brought up the shopping). Je suis sorti trop tard. (I went out too late). J'ai sorti la voiture du parking. (I brought the car out of the carpark). In each pair, the two meanings are related but different. In terms of construction, the intransitive form is followed immediately by a preposition or an adverb while the transitive form takes a direct object. Like entrer and rentrer, some of these dual-auxiliary verbs can take a prefix: descendre / redescendre (to come down again), monter / remonter (to go back up), and passer / repasser (to come by again -although repasser can also mean to iron!). All these prefixed verbs follow the same rules.

All about tout: Tout, meaning all or every (and in some cases very), can be confusing because it has several functions and can be used as an adjective, adverb, pronoun or noun. • As an adjective, tout always precedes and agrees in gender and number with the nouns it qualifies. The four forms are tout (masculine singular), toute (feminine singular), tous (masculine plural) and toutes (feminine plural). Here's a simple sentence to help you remember: Tout le temps et toute la journée, tous mes frères et toutes mes sœurs sont à la maison. (Where tout refers to two nouns of different genders (here, frères, sœurs), it must be repeated in the appropriate form.) In addition to nouns, tout can qualify adjectives, both possessive: Toute ma famille vit à Marseille, All my family lives in Marseille; and demonstrative: Tous ces gens sont ici pour nous aider, All these people are here to help us. Regarding pronunciation, the adjectives tout and tous are identical ([tao]) as are toute and toutes ([foot]). • As an adverb, tout is usually invariable: Allez tout droit et tourner au feu rouge. Go straight on and turn at the traffic light. If used with another adverb, tout means very or quite: Je commence tout doucement à m'ennuyer. I'm very slowly getting bored. Three other common expressions in which tout is an adverb are tout à fait, absolutely (lesson 23), tout droit, straight on (lesson 25), and tout à l'heure, later (lesson 30). • As a pronoun, there are only three forms: tout, tous and toutes. The singular pronoun means everything, anything, all, etc. Tout est possible ! Everything/Anything is possible! Les enfants sont tous dans la chambre, The children are all in the bedroom. Les commandes sont toutes arrivées en retard. All the orders arrived late. In terms of pronunciation, tout and toutes are pronounced in the same way as the adjectival forms but the final s of tous is voiced: [tooss]: Tous [too] les hommes sont pareils. / Les hommes sont tous [tooss} pareils. All men are the same / Men are all the same. When tout is followed by a relative clause starting with que, we have to add ce, whereas English usually omits the relative that/which: Tout ce que vous voulez, Everything you want. • Other expressions seen so far include: tout le monde, everybody (lesson 20), tout de suite, right away (26), tout le temps, all the time (34), tout à l'heure, earlier/later (30), en tout cas, in any case (54), also written en tous cas, and rien du tout, nothing at all (66). C'est tout pour le moment, That's all for now!

N'importe: We know (lesson 60 note 8) that importer means to be important / of import / to matter. In the affirmative, the verb is quite formal and often used with the subjunctive. But in the negative, it is included in many common indefinite expressions. The simplest are N'importe and Peu importe. Generally used in response to a question, they mean No matter, I don't mind, etc. Que voulez-vous boire ? - N'importe. What do you want to drink? - Anything. (i.e. "It matters not.") A number of indefinites are formed simply by adding a pronoun or adverb after n'importe -the equivalent of anyone, anywhere etc. J'irais n'importe où avec lui, I'd go anywhere with him. N'importe qui peut y aller, Anyone can go there. Tu peux venir à n'importe quelle heure ou n'importe quel jour. You can come at any time or on any day. En fait, tu peux venir n'importe quand, In fact, you can come anytime. Il faut cliquer sur une page, n'importe laquelle. You have to click on a page, any one. With quoi, there are several possibilities. The literal meaning is anything at all: Il ferait n'importe quoi pour attirer mon attention. He would do anything to attract my attention. En ligne, on trouve tout et n'importe quoi. You can find anything and everything online. But n'importe quoi can also refer to something that is absurd or nonsensical. Tu dis n'importe quoi, You're talking rubbish. In this sense, it is often used as an exclamation to criticise or dismiss a previous statement: Les Français sont les meilleurs ! - N'importe quoi ! The French are the best! - Rubbish. A longer form, but with the identical meaning, is C'est du n'importe quoi.

Idioms (2): animals: As we saw in the last revision lesson, it is a good idea to learn idiomatic expressions in thematic groups. This second instalment looks at animal-based idioms, which feature in most languages. être doux comme un agneau (be as gentle as a lamb), reprendre du poil de la bête (pick up, to recover), avoir le cafard (be down in the dumps), donner sa langue au chat (give up), avoir d'autres chats à fouetter (have other fish to fry), avoir une fièvre de cheval (have a raging fever), être malade comme un chien (be as sick as a dog), faire un temps de chien (be / have lousy weather), passer / sauter du coq à l'âne (jump from one thing to another), poser un lapin (stand someone up), courir plusieurs lièvres à la fois (have several irons in the fire), dormir comme un loir (sleep like a log), avoir une faim de loup (be famished), avoir un appétit d'oiseau (eat like a bird), être bavard comme une pie (be a chatterbox), être heureux comme un poisson dans l'eau (be as happy as a lark / clam), s'ennuyer comme un rat mort (be bored to tears), être malin comme un singe (be as smart as a whip). One word seen in lesson 69 is the adjective vachement, which apparently comes from une vache, a cow. The word was originally an adverb meaning nastily or unpleasantly (the adjective vache still has that meaning: une remarque vache, a nasty comment) but is now used as an intensifier. Although very common, vachement is nevertheless familiar and, depending on the register of language, can be the equivalent of bloody or damned. Thus, as with slang and idioms in general, it should not be used by anyone who does not already have a good grasp of standard French.
