dialogue.french: Écoutons le commentaire sur ce nouvel audioguide touristique qu'on m'a recommandé.
dialogue.english: Let's listen to the commentary on this new tourist audio guide that was recommended to me.

dialogue.french: "La Tour Eiffel, le monument emblématique de Paris, est construite à la fin du dix-neuvième siècle.
dialogue.english: "The Eiffel Tower, the emblematic monument of Paris, was built at the end of the nineteenth century.

dialogue.french: Aujourd'hui, elle est visitée par à peu près sept millions de visiteurs, bon an, mal an.
dialogue.english: Today, it is visited by nearly seven million visitors, year in, year out.

dialogue.french: Mais peu connaissent la vraie histoire de la Dame de Fer, comme on l'appelle affectueusement.
dialogue.english: But few know the true story of the Iron Lady, as she is affectionately known.

dialogue.french: D'abord, elle n'est pas conçue par Gustave Eiffel lui-même mais par deux de ses collègues.
dialogue.english: First of all, it was not designed by Gustave Eiffel himself but by two of his colleagues.

dialogue.french: Inspirée par deux ingénieurs américains, elle est d'abord baptisée "La tour de trois cents mètres"
dialogue.english: Inspired by two American engineers, it was first named "The Three Hundred Meter Tower"

dialogue.french: et construite en deux ans pour l'Exposition universelle de mil huit cent quatre-vingt-neuf.
dialogue.english: and built in two years for the Universal Exhibition of 1889.

dialogue.french: La tour n'est pas destinée à durer plus de vingt ans, mais l'Histoire en décide autrement.
dialogue.english: The tower was not intended to last more than twenty years, but history decided otherwise.

dialogue.french: Comme elle est utilisée non seulement pour la météorologie mais aussi pour la téléphonie,
dialogue.english: As it was used not only for meteorology but also for telephony,

dialogue.french: la décision est prise de ne pas la démolir.
dialogue.english: the decision was taken not to demolish it.

dialogue.french: La Tour Eiffel est restée le monument le plus élevé du monde pendant quarante ans,
dialogue.english: The Eiffel Tower remained the highest monument in the world for forty years,

dialogue.french: mais, à l'époque, elle n'est pas appréciée par tout le monde.
dialogue.english: but at the time it was not well-liked by everyone.

dialogue.french: Beaucoup de gens la trouvent laide, et elle est vivement critiquée par des écrivains et d'autres artistes.
dialogue.english: A lot of people found it ugly, and it was sharply criticised by writers and other artists.

dialogue.french: Rira bien qui rira le dernier, car elle est déjà plus que centenaire !
dialogue.english: He who laughs last laughs longest, because it is already more than a hundred years old!

dialogue.french: Depuis qu'elle est ouverte, la Tour a reçu plus de deux cent cinquante millions de visiteurs !
dialogue.english: Since it opened, the Tower has welcomed more than two hundred and fifty million visitors!

dialogue.french: Elle est même "vendue" dans les années mil neuf cent vingt par un escroc à un entrepreneur naïf,
dialogue.english: It was even "sold" in the 1920s by a crook to a naive entrepreneur,

dialogue.french: qui pense faire une bonne affaire en l'achetant pour récupérer la ferraille !
dialogue.english: who thought he was getting a good deal by buying it for scrap metal!

dialogue.french: Plus récemment, elle est convoitée par une jeune femme, qui l'épouse et adopte son nom !
dialogue.english: More recently, it was lusted after by a young woman, who married it and adopted its name!

dialogue.french: Même si des copies ont été construites par d'autres villes dans le monde, il n'y a qu'une seule Tour Eiffel.
dialogue.english: Even if copies have been built by other cities in the world, there is only one Eiffel Tower.

dialogue.french: Alors "gigantesque cheminée d'usine" ou magnifique symbole dont la France est fière ?
dialogue.english: So 'gigantic factory chimney' or a magnificent symbol of which France is proud?

dialogue.french: La réponse est donnée d'avance par des millions de touristes qui viennent chaque année rendre hommage à la belle Dame de fer."
dialogue.english: The response is a foregone conclusion for millions of tourists who come every year to pay tribute to the beautiful Iron Lady."

notes.content: The p in baptisé is not pronounced in any of the verb's different forms: here, [bateezay]. The masculine adjective fier and the feminine fière are both pronounced [fyair].

French often uses the present tense to describe past actions (le présent historique, lesson 42). Here, être is the auxiliary of the passive voice, along with the past participle, as in English: Eiffel construit son monument en deux ans → Le monument est construit en deux ans par Eiffel. Eiffel built the monument in two years → The monument was built in two years by Eiffel. Since the past participle behaves as an adjective, it has to agree with the subject: Eiffel construit sa tour... → Sa tour est construite...

à peu près ("at little near") is a useful adverbial phrase meaning approximately. Il est à peu près huit/vingt heures, It's around 8 o'clock. Although a synonym of environ (lesson 60 note 2), it can also be used to qualify adverbs, replacing presque: Cet acteur est connu à peu près partout, That actor is known almost everywhere.

The idiom bon an, mal an ("good year, bad year") means year in, year out, in other words, the average of good and bad years taken together. Bon an, mal an, la tour attire des millions de touristes, Year in, year out, the tower attracts millions of tourists. Always use an, never année, in this expression.

The impersonal pronoun on often does the same job as the passive voice. Thus Il est appelé l'Homme de fer and On l'appelle l'Homme de fer both mean He's called the Iron Man. Look again at the first sentence: Le guide qu'on m'a recommandé, The guide that was recommended to me. In neither case would on be the equivalent of the impersonal one in this type of passive-equivalent construction.

The adjective vif (fem. vive) means lively or keen. The adverb vivement means keenly, vividly, sharply, etc. En ville, il est vivement recommandé d'utiliser les transports en commun, In town, it is strongly/highly recommended to use public transport.

Proverbs in French often use old or literary constructions. Rira bien qui rira le dernier is the equivalent of He who laughs last laughs longest. Note how the two clauses are inverted in the French proverb.

As you already know, les affaires can mean business in the sense of an economic activity (lesson 47) or personal affairs (lesson 61). In the singular, une affaire generally means a matter, a case or an affair. The expression une bonne affaire means a good deal, a bargain. It is often used with faire: Nous avons fait une bonne affaire en vendant notre maison, We got a good deal when we sold our house.

The adjective fier (fem. fière) means proud. Elle est fière de ses origines basques, She's proud of her Basque heritage. The word can also be used with the subjunctive mood, as we will see later on. One very common, invariable expression, similar to its English counterpart, is Il n'y a pas de quoi être fier, That's nothing to be proud of. The noun is la fierté, pride. (Do not confuse fier with the verb se fier, pronounced [feeyay], to trust, lesson 50 note 3.)

answers: You can find good cheap laptops almost everywhere now.

translate.question: On l'appelle tous Willi : il est très fier de ses origines alsaciennes.
answers: We all call him Willi: he's very proud of his Alsatian origins.

translate.question: Tu n'as payé que cinq euros pour cet audioguide ? Tu as vraiment fait une bonne affaire.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: One of France's - and the world's - biggest tourist attractions, the Eiffel Tower was inspired by the work of two American engineers who wanted to design and build a 300-metre-tall tower. Completed to coincide with the hundredth anniversary of the 1789 French Revolution, the monument was to have been dismantled after a decade but Gustave Eiffel made every effort to ensure that his work would endure, despite opposition from writers, architects and intellectuals, who likened it to a factory chimney. The anecdotes about the tower being sold off for scrap metal by a confidence trickster and being wedded to an eccentric American are both true. And although a certain British politician once tried to make the Iron Lady sobriquet her own, there is really only one Dame de fer! Productive phase: 22nd lesson

dialogue.french: Éric Camus nous quitte à la fin du mois et nous allons organiser un pot de départ avec tous ses amis
dialogue.english: Éric Camus is leaving us at the end of the month and we're going to organise a farewell drinks party with all his friends

dialogue.french: pour lui souhaiter bonne chance dans les années à venir.
dialogue.english: to wish him good luck in the years to come.

dialogue.french: Depuis combien de temps est-ce qu'il gère le service ? On me dit qu'il est là depuis des lustres.
dialogue.english: How long has he managed the department for? They say that he's been here for ages.

dialogue.french: Il ne faut pas exagérer. Éric nous a rejoints il y a une dizaine d'années.
dialogue.english: Let's not exaggerate. Éric joined us about ten years ago.

dialogue.french: Auparavant, il était salarié dans une compagnie d'assurances en province,
dialogue.english: Beforehand, he was an employee in an insurance company in the provinces,

dialogue.french: mais on lui a fait comprendre qu'il n'aurait pas d'avancement parce qu'il était trop jeune.
dialogue.english: but he was made to understand that he wouldn't get a promotion because he was too young.

dialogue.french: C'est un de ses amis qui a mentionné notre entreprise : Éric a postulé et on l'a embauché aussitôt.
dialogue.english: One of his friends mentioned our company: Éric applied and was hired right away.

dialogue.french: On dit qu'il est très apprécié de ses collègues. Est-ce vrai ?
dialogue.english: They say that his colleagues appreciate him very much. Is that true?

dialogue.french: Oui, Éric est aimé par tous ceux qui ont travaillé avec lui, surtout les chefs d'équipe.
dialogue.english: Yes, Eric is liked by everyone who has worked with him, especially the team leaders.

dialogue.french: Il est toujours de bonne humeur et ne se dispute jamais avec personne ;
dialogue.english: He's always in a good mood and never argues with anyone;

dialogue.french: il sait écouter et prendre des décisions. Il a fait de grandes choses pour cette société.
dialogue.english: he knows how to listen and take decisions. He has done great things for this company.

dialogue.french: Pour le pot, à quel moment allez-vous le faire ? En fin de journée ?
dialogue.english: For the reception, what time are you going to do it? At the end of the day?

dialogue.french: Il est préférable de le faire à midi : comme ça il y aura plus de monde.
dialogue.english: It's preferable to do it at noon: that way there will be more people.

dialogue.french: Qu'allez-vous faire : l'organiser vous-mêmes ou faire appel à quelqu'un ?
dialogue.english: What are you going to do: organise it yourselves or call on someone?

dialogue.french: C'est nous qui allons tout préparer, à part le buffet, qui a été commandé chez un traiteur.
dialogue.english: We're going to prepare everything, apart from the buffet, which has been ordered from a caterer.

dialogue.french: Des petits fours salés et sucrés seront livrés vers onze heures, onze heures trente au plus tard.
dialogue.english: Savoury and sweet petits-fours will be delivered around 11 am, 11.30 at the latest.

dialogue.french: Tout se mange froid, donc il n'y a pas besoin de cuisine.
dialogue.english: Everything is eaten cold so there's no need for a kitchen.

dialogue.french: Et c'est vous qui achetez le cadeau de départ ?
dialogue.english: And you're going to buy the farewell gift?

dialogue.french: Non, c'est un autre collègue qui s'en charge, mais il sera choisi par un comité de quatre personnes,
dialogue.english: No, another colleague is taking care of it, but it will be chosen by a four-person committee,

dialogue.french: et un discours sera prononcé lors du buffet.
dialogue.english: and a speech will be made during the buffet.

dialogue.french: Et il se fera en anglais. Après tout, nous sommes une entreprise moderne et dynamique.
dialogue.english: And it will be done in English. After all, we're a modern and dynamic company.

dialogue.french: Ah non, ça ne se fait pas ! Ici, on parle français, monsieur.
dialogue.english: Ah no, that's not the done thing! We speak French here, sir.

notes.content: province: Note the open vowel [ahn] in the second syllable: [provahnss]. Don't confuse this word with Provence, the beautiful region of southeastern France, pronounced [provœh"ss].

un pot means a pot - un pot de fleurs, a pot of flowers - or a jar, un pot de confiture a jar of jam. (Remember the important difference between à and de in this case, lesson 48 note 9). In a familiar register, un pot means a drink (usually alcoholic): Tu viens boire un pot avec nous ? Coming for a drink with us? In a work context, it means a drinks party, or, specifically, un pot de départ or d'adieu, an office farewell/leaving party.

un lustre means shine (or lustre). However, the familiar expression depuis des lustres (from the Latin word for a five-year period, and always plural) simply means for a long time / for ages. Il est ici depuis des lustres, He's been here for ages.

One way of avoiding the passive voice in a sentence is to put the emphasis on the subject (the person performing the action). For this, we use c'est (sing.) or ce sont (plur.) at the beginning of the clause followed by qui/que. So instead of Votre entreprise a été mentionnée par un ami we say C'est un ami qui a mentionné votre entreprise. In English, c'est / ce sont ... qui does not need translating: A friend mentioned your firm.

The adverb aussitôt, formed from aussi, as, and tôt, early, is an elegant way of saying straight away, immediately. La neige est tombée cette nuit mais a disparu aussitôt, The snow fell last night but disappeared immediately. It can often be replaced by immédiatement. One common and easy-to-remember expression is Aussitôt dit, aussitôt fait, No sooner said than done.

The passive agent is generally introduced with par: La décision a été prise par le directeur, The decision was taken by the manager (See lesson 71 note 1). But if the verb expresses a feeling or emotion, par may be replaced by de: Il est aimé par or de ses collègues, He is liked by his colleagues. There is no difference in meaning.

Be careful not to confuse the masculine noun l'humour, humour (see lesson 52 for pronunciation) with the feminine l'humeur, mood. Elle est de bonne humeur ce matin, She's in a good mood this morning. The word can also refer to a person's temperament: Il est d'humeur changeante depuis qu'il a arrêté de fumer, He's been moody since he stopped smoking. Another "animal" expression (lesson 69) is être d'une humeur de chien: Le chef est d'une humeur de chien depuis deux jours, The boss has been in a foul mood for two days.

We know that grand means not only big but also great, eminent (lesson 16 note 2). Thus faire / accomplir de grandes choses means to do / accomplish great things. Don't confuse this adjective-plus-noun construction with the idiom ne pas faire grand-chose (always singular), seen in lesson 61.

un appel, from the verb appeler, to call, means a call (phone, appeal, etc., lesson 38, 16). Thus to make a phone call is passer un appel (téléphonique). The expression faire appel à thus means to call upon or appeal to. The direct object of the verb can be a person - Nous avons fait appel à un expert pour nous aider, We called on an expert for help - but also an abstract noun: Je fais appel à vos connaissances en informatique, I'm calling on your computer skills.

Another way to avoid the passive is with the pronominal verb form in the third person singular: Tout se fait en français dans notre entreprise, Everything is done in French in our company. This workaround is generally used in situations where the agent is unknown or unimportant: Le billet s'achète en ligne, The ticket is bought online. It can also be used if the agent is "understood," notably when addressing them directly, in which case the previous sentence means You buy the ticket online. The expression Ça ne se fait pas, meaning That's not the done thing, can be used in a variety of contexts (it's a no-no, You can't do that, etc.)

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Productive phase: 23rd lesson

dialogue.french: Mais qu'est-ce que tu as ? On dirait que ça ne va pas fort.
dialogue.english: What's wrong with you? It looks like things aren't going well.

dialogue.french: Effectivement. J'en peux plus. J'en ai plein le dos.
dialogue.english: It does. I can't take it anymore. I'm sick and tired.

dialogue.french: Avant-hier, je me réveille en pleine nuit en mauvais état avec une rage de dents
dialogue.english: The day before yesterday, I woke up in the middle of the night in a bad state with a toothache

dialogue.french: et je n'ai rien dans l'armoire à pharmacie pour soulager la douleur.
dialogue.english: and I had nothing in the medicine cabinet to relieve the pain.

dialogue.french: J'ai dû attendre le matin pour appeler mon dentiste, mais j'avais oublié que son cabinet est fermé le week-end.
dialogue.english: I had to wait until the morning to call my dentist, but I had forgotten that his office closes at weekends.

dialogue.french: Tout comme la pharmacie du coin. Ce sont tous des fainéants !
dialogue.english: Just like the local pharmacy. They're all lazy!

dialogue.french: J'ai voulu prendre le métro pour aller aux urgences, mais il était en grève.
dialogue.english: I wanted to take the metro to go to the emergency room, but it was on strike.

dialogue.french: J'ai réussi à trouver un taxi mais on a été pris dans les bouchons pendant une heure.
dialogue.english: I managed to find a taxi but we were stuck in traffic for an hour.

dialogue.french: À l'hôpital, les urgences étaient débordées et ils m'ont fait patienter pendant trois heures.
dialogue.english: At the hospital, the emergency room was overwhelmed and they made me wait for three hours.

dialogue.french: Trois heures ? C'est pas possible ! Quelle galère !
dialogue.english: Three hours? That's not possible! What a drag!

dialogue.french: Après, ils m'ont cassé les pieds parce que je n'avais pas ma carte d'assurance maladie.
dialogue.english: Afterwards, they pestered me because I didn't have my health insurance card.

dialogue.french: Pour couronner le tout, j'ai été soigné par un stagiaire, pas un vrai dentiste, et j'ai toujours mal.
dialogue.english: To top it all off, I was treated by a trainee, not a real dentist, and I'm still in pain.

dialogue.french: C'est dingue ! Quelle histoire de fou.
dialogue.english: That's mad! What a crazy story.

dialogue.french: Tout est comme ça maintenant, et il y en a marre.
dialogue.english: Everything is like that nowadays, and I'm fed up with it.

dialogue.french: N'importe qui fait n'importe quoi, n'importe comment - c'est toujours pareil.
dialogue.english: Anyone can do anything, anyhow - it's always the same.

dialogue.french: Rien ne marche comme il faut : tout est sens dessus dessous, et ça m'énerve.
dialogue.english: Nothing works as it should: everything's upside down, and that annoys me.

dialogue.french: Et quand tu le fais remarquer aux gens, ils te répondent "Ah bon ? Nous, on ne peut pas se plaindre".
dialogue.english: And when you point this out to people, they say, "Really? We mustn't grumble."

dialogue.french: Qu'est-ce qu'ils sont fatigants ! Même toi, tu commences à me fatiguer !
dialogue.english: They're so tiring they are. Even you are starting to tire me!

dialogue.french: Qu'est-ce que je pourrais faire pour te remonter le moral ? T'inviter à dîner ?
dialogue.english: What could I do to cheer you up? Invite you to dinner?

dialogue.french: Ne t'en fais pas. C'est vrai que j'en ai ras le bol,
dialogue.english: Don't worry. It's true that I'm fed up,

dialogue.french: mais tu connais les Français : nous ne sommes heureux que lorsque nous nous plaignons.
dialogue.english: but you know the French: we're only happy when we're complaining.

notes.content: In informal French, the adverb ne is often omitted from a negative construction when speaking quickly. Thus, j'en peux plus, pronounced [zhoeh" puh plew], is the informal way of saying je n'en peux plus [zhuh noeh" puh plew]. Similarly, c'est pas possible (line 10) replaces ce n'est pas possible. You will find these grammatically incorrect forms written out in song lyrics, blog entries, social media postings and the like. Needless to say, do not imitate them, but do be prepared for a missing ne.

We saw le dos, the back, in lesson 62 note 8. The idiomatic expression en avoir plein le dos basically means to be fed up, to be sick and tired. It can be used as an exclamation, as here, or with a direct object, in which case it takes de: J'en ai plein le dos de leurs excuses, I'm sick and tired of their excuses. Likewise, Je n'en peux plus expresses inability to deal with unpleasantness, using the same image as English expressions like I can't take any more, I've had it up to here.

une grève means a strike, a work stoppage. It is generally used either with faire (sometimes without an article) or être en (never with the article): Les ouvriers font [la] grève / L'usine est en grève, The workers are striking / The plant is on strike.

un bouchon means a cork. While English uses a bottleneck to describe traffic congestion, French prefers the image of a bottle stopper. Un bouchon is more or less synonymous with une embouteillage (lesson 58 line 13), which literally means bottling and is generally used to describe urban congestion. However, only bouchon can be used in a verb: Ça commence à bouchonner sur la route des vacances, Traffic jams are starting to form on holiday roads.

une galère is literally a galley (an oar-propelled vessel). The sheer effort and exertion made by the ship's oarsmen provides the context for a word that describes anything from hardship and difficulty to a hellish journey. For now, simply learn the exclamation Quelle galère !, What a pain/mess/nightmare!

The idiom casser les pieds ("break the feet") is an idiomatic way of saying that someone is importuning you, i.e. to bore, annoy, irritate, pester, etc. Il me casse les pieds avec toutes ces questions ! He's pestering me with all those questions! There is also a derived adjective - C'est casse-pieds de changer son mot de passe chaque mois, It's a drag to change your password every month - and noun: Mon nouveau voisin est un vrai casse-pieds, My new neighbour is a real pain.

The familiar adjective dingue, derived from an old slang word for malaria, basically means crazy, barmy, incredible, etc. Ce truc me rend dingue ! This thing is driving me nuts! In this sense, it is a synonym for fou (lesson 58). Derived meanings include unbelievable, extraordinary, etc. Les prix dans ce restaurant sont dingues ! That restaurant charges unbelievable prices! The nominal form is un(e) dingue: C'est un dingue de moto, He's a motorcycle nut.
en avoir marre, as we saw in lesson 44 note 7, means to be fed up with. Il y en a marre ! is an impersonal exclamation that translates roughly as That's enough!

The adverbs dessus, on top of, and dessous, underneath, are combined in the expressions sens dessus dessous, upside down, the word order being the same as in English (or, in some cases, topsy-turvy): Tu as mis ma vie sens dessus dessous, You have turned my life upside down. Note the order of the two adverbs: the one with the extra letter (o) comes second.

plaindre means to pity, feel sorry for: Si vous n'avez pas encore la réponse, je vous plains, If you still don't have the answer I feel sorry for you. The pronominal form se plaindre, with its past participle plaint, is the origin of to complain and a complaint (une plainte). Il se plaint de maux de tête, He's complaining of headaches. It can also mean to grumble, moan, etc. Arrête de te plaindre tout le temps, Stop moaning all the time. The expression On ne peut pas se (or Je ne peux pas me) plaindre is the equivalent of the stoic response Mustn't grumble.

le moral means morale, i.e. enthusiasm, confidence: Qu'est qu'il y a ? Tu n'as pas le moral ? What's the matter? Are you feeling down? Be careful not to confuse it with la morale, morals, moral standards.

s'en faire, to worry / to be concerned about, is a synonym of s'inquiéter (lesson 51 line 9). It is mainly used in the expression Ne vous en faites pas (fam. Ne t'en fais pas): Don't worry. A direct object, introduced by pour, can be added: Ne t'en fais pas pour lui, il va bien, Don't worry about him, he's fine.

new_words.french: on dirait que ça ne va pas fort
new_words.english: it looks like things aren't going well

new_words.french: je me réveille en pleine nuit
new_words.english: I woke up in the middle of the night

new_words.french: son cabinet est fermé le week-end
new_words.english: his office is closed at weekends

new_words.french: tout comme la pharmacie du coin

new_words.english: I wanted to take the metro
to go to the emergency room

new_words.french: j'ai réussi à trouver un taxi
new_words.english: I managed to find a taxi

new_words.french: on a été pris dans les bouchons
parce que je n'avais pas ma carte d'assurance maladie
new_words.english: because I didn't have my health insurance card

new_words.french: rien ne marche comme il faut
quand tu le fais remarquer aux gens
new_words.english: when you point this out to people

new_words.french: nous, on ne peut pas se plaindre
même toi, tu commences à me fatiguer
new_words.english: even you are starting to tire me

new_words.french: nous ne sommes heureux que lorsque nous nous plaignons
new_words.english: we're only happy when we're complaining

translate.question: Ils font grève juste avant Noël. J'en ai plein le dos !
answers: They're striking just before Christmas. I'm sick and tired.

translate.question: Ces jeunes nous cassent les pieds avec ces histoires de réchauffement climatique.
answers: These youngsters really annoy me with these climate change stories.

translate.question: Quelle galère ! Nous étions coincés dans les bouchons sur l'autoroute pendant trois heures.
answers: What a pain! We were stuck in traffic jams on the motorway for three hours.

translate.question: Je n'en peux plus de ce bruit, qui me réveille à trois heures toutes les nuits.
answers: I can't stand any more of this noise, which wakes me up at three o'clock every night.

translate.question: Le service est débordé et, pour couronner le tout, ils ne répondent plus au téléphone.
answers: The department is overwhelmed and, to top it all off, they're no longer answering the phone.

fill.question: They made me wait for 20 minutes but I mustn't grumble. On ... vingt minutes, mais je ne peux pas me plaindre.
fill.full_answer: On m'a fait patienter pendant vingt minutes, mais je ne peux pas me plaindre.

fill.question: They complain all the time and it's driving me mad because the problem isn't as serious as that. Ils ... tout le temps et ça me rend dingue, car le problème n'est pas si grave que ça.
fill.full_answer: Ils se plaignent tout le temps et ça me rend dingue, car le problème n'est pas si grave que ça.

fill.question: It was pointed out to me that everything is topsy turvy, and it's true. What a crazy story! On ... que tout est sens dessus dessous, et c'est vrai. Quelle histoire de fou !
fill.full_answer: On m'a fait remarquer que tout est sens dessus dessous, et c'est vrai. Quelle histoire de fou !

fill.question: You're beginning to tire me with your questions. You're a real pain in the neck. Vous ... avec vos questions. Vous êtes vraiment un casse-pieds.
fill.full_answer: Vous commencez à me fatiguer avec vos questions. Vous êtes vraiment un casse-pieds.

fill.question: I'm down in the dumps: anybody can do anything, any old how. Je n'ai pas le moral : n'importe qui fait n'importe quoi, n'importe comment.
fill.full_answer: Je n'ai pas le moral : n'importe qui fait n'importe quoi, n'importe comment.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: We often stress the importance of etymology and association in building up a substantial vocabulary. Take une grève, a strike. Until the 18th century, one of the main public gathering places in Paris was a patch of sandy ground on the right shore, or strand (grève), of the River Seine, where jobless labourers would gather in search of a day's work unloading barges at the nearby docks. The place became known as Place de Grève (just as London's Thames riverbank was named The Strand), and the name eventually became synonymous with idleness and, ironically, with deliberate work stoppages. More ominously, the square was also the venue for public executions. (It was renamed Place de l'Hôtel de Ville, i.e. City Hall Square, at the beginning of the 19th century.) Productive phase: 24th lesson

dialogue.french: Ce sont les soldes en janvier, et ça tombe bien parce que j'ai grand besoin d'un nouveau chapeau.
dialogue.english: It's the sales in January, and it's good timing because I really need a new hat.

dialogue.french: Ah bon ? Tu es vraiment courageuse d'aller à la chasse aux bonnes affaires.
dialogue.english: Really? You're really brave to go bargain-hunting.

dialogue.french: Ce n'est pas une question de courage. Il y a des bons plans à saisir partout.
dialogue.english: It's not a question of courage. There are good deals to be had everywhere.

dialogue.french: Si tu voulais, tu pourrais m'accompagner. Ça me ferait plaisir.
dialogue.english: If you wanted, you could come with me. I'd like that.

dialogue.french: Qui sait ? Peut-être que tu trouveras ces caleçons que tu cherchais.
dialogue.english: Who knows? Perhaps you'll find those boxer shorts that you need.

dialogue.french: Je hais les centres commerciaux, tu le sais bien. C'est la pagaille à tous les étages !
dialogue.english: I hate shopping centres, you know that. It's chaos on every floor!

dialogue.french: Et les grands magasins sont pires car il y a toujours la foule.
dialogue.english: And the department stores are worse because they're always crowded.

dialogue.french: Quant aux boutiques de mode, n'en parlons pas. On peut à peine respirer, tellement il y a de monde.
dialogue.english: As for the fashion stores, don't mention them. You can barely breathe, there are so many people.

dialogue.french: Arrête de râler tout le temps et viens avec moi. [ ... ]
dialogue.english: Stop grumbling all the time and come with me. [ ... ]

dialogue.french: Bonjour madame, bonjour monsieur. Vous cherchez quelque chose ? Puis-je vous aider ?
dialogue.english: Hello ma'am, hello sir. Are you looking for something? Can I help you?

dialogue.french: Merci, mais pas pour l'instant. Nous ne faisons que regarder.
dialogue.english: No thank you, but not at the moment. We're just looking.

dialogue.french: Toi, peut-être. Mais moi, je suis à la recherche de mon chapeau et, éventuellement, de bottes.
dialogue.english: You, perhaps. But I'm looking for my hat and possibly some boots.

dialogue.french: Et pourquoi pas un nouveau manteau pendant que tu y es ?
dialogue.english: And why not a new coat while you're at it?

dialogue.french: J'ai justement ce qu'il vous faut, madame. Par ici, s'il vous plaît.
dialogue.english: I've got just the thing for you, ma'am. This way, please.

dialogue.french: J'hésite entre cette parka et cet imperméable. Lequel des deux me va le mieux ?
dialogue.english: I'm hesitating between this parka and this raincoat. Which of the two suits me the best?

dialogue.french: Prenez l'imperméable. Il est bien à votre taille, il tombe bien, et il vous va à merveille.
dialogue.english: Take the raincoat. It's your size, it hangs well and it suits you perfectly.

dialogue.french: Est-ce qu'il me va vraiment ? Je ne suis pas folle de la couleur. L'avez-vous en gris clair ?
dialogue.english: Does it really suit me? I'm not crazy about the colour. Do you have it in light grey?

dialogue.french: Non, mais il se trouve que ce modèle est soldé à moins soixante pour cent aujourd'hui.
dialogue.english: No, but it just so happens that this model is on sale at a sixty per cent discount today.

dialogue.french: Je le prends ! Et toi, Paul : ne veux-tu pas cette belle casquette, décorée de plumes ...
dialogue.english: I'll take it! And you, Paul. Don't you want this nice cap, decorated with feathers?

dialogue.french: Il n'en est pas question ! Où se trouve le rayon bricolage ?
dialogue.english: No way! Where's the DIY department?

notes.content: The verb haïr, to hate, is awkward. The diaresis, showing that both a and i must be pronounced, is present in the infinitive [eye-ear] and the three present-plural forms (haïssons, [eye-eessoh"], haïssez [eye-eessay], haïssent [eye-eess]) but not the singular: hais and hait, both of which are pronounced [ay].

The masculine noun les soldes, usually plural in this context, means sales (reduced-price goods). The singular, un solde, means a balance, i.e. a remaining amount. Thus le solde des marchandises means remaining goods. The verb solder means to wind up or pay off a balance, while the participle adjective soldé generally means reduced in price: Cette veste est soldée à moins quinze pour cent, This vest is reduced by 15% in the sales.

The expression tomber bien ("to fall well") is used in two ways. Applied to clothing (coat, skirt, etc.), it means to hang well (line 16). By extension, the idiomatic meaning is to come at the right moment. Ce chèque tombe bien : j'ai besoin d'argent: This cheque has arrived at just the right moment. I need cash. A useful exclamation is Ça tombe bien ! meaning That's convenient! or Great timing! The opposite, of course, is Ça tombe mal.

un plan means a map (lesson 5 line 3) or a plan (lesson 54 note 5). But the idiomatic expression un bon plan has a similar meaning to une bonne affaire (lesson 71 note 7). Depending on the context, it can mean a good deal or bargain or, in tourism, a top tip or recommendation, etc. On est déjà vendredi ! Voici nos bons plans pour le week-end, It's already Friday. Here are our top tips for the weekend.

la / une pagaille means mess, shambles, chaos. À cause de la grève, c'est la pagaille dans les transports en commun, Public transport is a mess because of the strike. Preceded by the preposition en, the meaning is huge amounts or loads of: Elle a des idées en pagaille, She has loads of ideas. (An alternative spelling is une pagaïe, but this should be avoided because it is too similar to une pagaie [pagay], a paddle or short oar.)

quant à means with regard to, as for: Quant à lui, il n'est même pas venu, As for him, he never came. This adverb is often used to stress a contrast, in which case it does not need to be translated: J'aime les sports mais mon mari, quant à lui, les hait, I love sports but my husband hates them. Do not confuse quant with quand, when (tip: it's always followed by à).

Merci, Thank you, is also a polite way to express refusal. Vous voulez un thé ? - Merci. Do you want some tea? - No thanks. This can be confusing for English-speakers, who often use merci thinking that it is a positive response (yes thank you). If in doubt, you can always insist: Non merci.

Here is a common example of how the masculine gender dominates the feminine. If two nouns with different genders are used together, any subsequent pronoun - especially lequel/laquelle, etc. - is in the masculine form: Je peux vous montrer un passeport ou une carte d'identité. Lequel voulez-vous ? I can show you a passport or an identity card. Which one do you want?

French has no direct equivalent of to suit. Convenir can be used to express the notion of being acceptable or convenient (lesson 26 note 2). But, if the meaning is to enhance or look good on, we use a paraphrase such as aller bien: Cet imperméable bleu vous va très bien, That blue raincoat really suits you. The usual preposition is à, so the pronoun is indirect: La parka lui va bien, The parka suits him/her.

se trouver, to be located (lesson 51 note 8) can also be used in the third person to express a coincidence or occurrence, like to happen or turn out (that). Il se trouve que j'ai le même prénom que leur mère, It turns out that I have the same first name as their mother. As a rule, if the usual translation of a word doesn't help you to understand the sentence (like tomber bien in note 2), always look at the broader context. And, of course, check in a good dictionary!

le bricolage, from the root verb bricoler, is an institution in France. It is the nearest equivalent to the English concept of home repairs and do-it-yourself (DIY). Le dimanche, j'adore bricoler / faire du bricolage, I love doing stuff around the house on Sundays. Used disparagingly, the meaning is to do something hastily, mess about with: Le travail est mal fait: c'est du bricolage, The work is badly done; it's a rush job.

translate.question: Jacques, tu tombes bien. Tu peux m'aider à faire un peu de bricolage ?
answers: Great timing, Jacques. Can you help me with a bit of DIY?

translate.question: Voulez-vous manger quelque chose ? Moi, j'ai mangé tout à l'heure et je n'ai plus faim.
answers: Do you want something to eat? Me, I ate earlier and I'm not hungry any more.

translate.question: Vous ne pourriez pas sortir, même si vous le vouliez. Il fait un temps de chien.
answers: You couldn't go out, even if you wanted to. The weather is dreadful.

translate.question: Le ministre a dit : "Nous haïssons cette taxe et nous allons la faire disparaître".
answers: The minister said: "We hate this tax and we're going to make it go away."

translate.question: Mon mari râle toujours quand je l'amène dans le rayon lingerie du magasin.
answers: My husband always grumbles when I take him to the shop's underwear department.

fill.question: You're going to the shopping centre? Get some coffee and milk while you're at it. Vous ... ? Prenez du café et du lait pendant que vous y êtes.
fill.full_answer: Vous allez au centre commercial ? Prenez du café et du lait pendant que vous y êtes.

fill.question: My wife likes the grey coat, over there. Myself, I prefer this one. It suits me. Ma femme aime le manteau gris, là-bas. Quant à moi, je préfère celui-ci. Il me va bien.
fill.full_answer: Ma femme aime le manteau gris, là-bas. Quant à moi, je préfère celui-ci. Il me va bien.

fill.question: We have what you need, sir. Look, these parkas are reduced by 30 per cent in the sales. Nous avons ce qu'il vous faut, monsieur. Regardez, ces parkas sont soldées à moins trente pour cent.
fill.full_answer: Nous avons ce qu'il vous faut, monsieur. Regardez, ces parkas sont soldées à moins trente pour cent.

fill.question: Have you got any good tips for the weekend? - No, I'm staying home. Public transport is a mess. Avez-vous des bons plans pour le week-end ? - Non, je reste chez moi. C'est la pagaille dans les transports.
fill.full_answer: Avez-vous des bons plans pour le week-end ? - Non, je reste chez moi. C'est la pagaille dans les transports.

fill.question: I'm just looking. But, who knows? Perhaps I'll find something that I like. Je ne fais que regarder. Mais, qui sait ? Je trouverai peut-être quelque chose qui me plaît.
fill.full_answer: Je ne fais que regarder. Mais, qui sait ? Je trouverai peut-être quelque chose qui me plaît.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Productive phase: 25th lesson

dialogue.french: Bienvenue à l'émission qui récompense tous les talents de toutes les musiques.
dialogue.english: Welcome to the show that rewards all talents in all types of music.

dialogue.french: Nous avons l'immense plaisir d'accueillir le célèbre chanteur Alain Poupon
dialogue.english: We have the great pleasure of welcoming the famous singer Alain Poupon

dialogue.french: qui entame sa toute dernière tournée après une carrière longue de quatre décennies.
dialogue.english: who is beginning his final tour after a four-decade career.

dialogue.french: Il recevra le prix "Accord majeur", décerné à un musicien ou une musicienne d'exception pour l'ensemble de sa carrière.
dialogue.english: He will receive the "Major Chord" prize, awarded to an outstanding musician for his or her entire career.

dialogue.french: Bonsoir Alain, et merci d'être venu. Vous avez eu une vie fabuleuse, n'est-ce pas ?
dialogue.english: Good evening Alain, and thanks for coming. You've had a fabulous life, haven't you?

dialogue.french: Vous avez même dit dans une interview que personne n'avait accompli autant de choses que vous.
dialogue.english: You even said in an interview that no one has accomplished as much as you have.

dialogue.french: Je doute d'avoir dit exactement ça, mais je n'ai jamais douté de mon talent.
dialogue.english: I doubt that I said exactly that, but I never doubted my talent.

dialogue.french: Je m'en doutais ! Vous avez eu une carrière passionnante, et très mouvementée.
dialogue.english: I thought so! You've had an exciting and very eventful career.

dialogue.french: Ça vous pouvez le dire ! J'ai fait un tas de choses que je n'aurais jamais imaginées
dialogue.english: You can say that again! I've done a lot of things that I would never have imagined

dialogue.french: avant de devenir chanteur, mais j'ai toujours été très apprécié.
dialogue.english: before I became a singer, but I've always been very appreciated.

dialogue.french: À l'école, j'avais beaucoup de succès et plein de potes.
dialogue.english: At school, I was very popular and I had loads of mates.

dialogue.french: Il faut dire que j'étais très fort dans toutes les matières, pas seulement la musique.
dialogue.english: It has to be said that I was very good at all subjects, not only music.

dialogue.french: J'ai quitté le lycée à seize ans parce qu'on ne pouvait plus rien m'apprendre.
dialogue.english: I left high school at sixteen because no one could teach me anything else.

dialogue.french: Ensuite, j'ai touché à tout. J'ai bossé dans une librairie, puis comme maçon, et électricien.
dialogue.english: After that, I did a bit of everything. I worked in a bookstore, then as a builder and electrician.

dialogue.french: Enfin j'ai rencontré le guitariste Charlot, avec qui j'ai entamé ma brillante carrière.
dialogue.english: Finally I met the guitarist Charlot, with whom I started my brilliant career.

dialogue.french: J'ai d'abord été pianiste et trompettiste avant de me mettre à chanter.
dialogue.english: I started out as a pianist and trumpet player before starting to sing.

dialogue.french: Vous saviez faire tout ça ? Mais vous étiez surdoué !
dialogue.english: You knew how to do all that? You were exceptionally gifted!

dialogue.french: J'étais bien obligé ! Vous savez, il faut avoir confiance en soi et en son talent
dialogue.english: I had to! You know, you have to have confidence in yourself and in your talent

dialogue.french: et je n'en ai jamais douté. Je dois avouer que je suis confiant de nature.
dialogue.english: and I never doubted it. I must admit that I am confident by nature.

dialogue.french: Qui suis-je pour vous contredire ? Et maintenant, notre Questionnaire de Proust :
dialogue.english: Who am I to contradict you? And now to our Proust's Questionnaire:

dialogue.french: "Quelle qualité appréciez-vous le plus chez les gens ?"
dialogue.english: "What quality do you appreciate most in people?"

dialogue.french: La modestie. Il faut aimer les autres comme soi-même.
dialogue.english: Modesty. You have to love other people as you do yourself.

notes.content: The regular verb entamer literally means to cut / slice into (la tranche d'entame is the first slice of a loaf or ham). It is often used as a synonym for commencer, i.e. to begin an action lasting a certain length of time (Nous avons commencé / entamé notre voyage). But while commencer can be both transitive and intransitive (Le voyage a commencé), entamer is transitive only.

Time periods are not used as preceding adjectives in French. For example, a three-year programme is un programme de trois ans. But they can be used with the partitive de to add extra information: longue de trois ans. (See also lesson 77.)

The regular verb décerner (the root of discern) means to award: Le Prix Goncourt a été décerné à Proust en 1919, The Goncourt Prize was awarded to Proust in 1919. Moreover, un prix, a prize (lesson 39 line 12), can also be translated as an award. In certain contexts, especially music and business prizes, French has unfortunately adopted les Awards. To be avoided at all costs!

douter (the root of doubt) can be used transitively and pronominally, with a slight difference in meaning. The transitive form (lesson 54 line 16) means to doubt and, with a direct object, is followed by de: Malgré trois excellents albums, elle a toujours douté de ses qualités de chanteuse, Despite three excellent albums, she has always doubted her abilities as a singer. By contrast, se douter de means to suspect or guess something: Je ne savais pas mais je m'en suis douté, I didn't know but I suspected it. A useful expression, used as a rejoinder, is Je m'en doute ! I can imagine, I'm sure you do/are!, etc.

Remember that the past participle behaves as an adjective and agrees with the direct object (here, the feminine plural choses) if it comes before the verb. Compare that with Il a utilisé des mots (m.) que je n'aurais jamais imaginés, He used words that I never would have imagined. Of course, the two forms of the participle are pronounced identically.

Another commonly used idiomatic noun is un pote, a mate, a buddy. Unlike un copain (lesson 34 note 6) it does not imply a romantic interest. Formerly used only in closed circles, the word came back to prominence in the late 20th century with the anti-racist catchphrase Touche pas à mon pote, Don't touch my buddy, now the official slogan of an NGO collective.

toucher means to touch: Cette armoire touche le plafond, This cupboard is touching the ceiling. As a transitive indirect verb (which acts on or for its object), it requires the preposition à: Il n'a pas touché à sa nourriture, He hasn't touched his food. The idiom meaning toucher à tout means to try one's hand at everything.

Yet another common slang word, bosser means to work and is used instead of travailler. Depending on the context, it can also mean to slog away, to swot, etc. Tu viens ? - Non, je bosse mes cours, Are you coming? No, I'm swotting up on my lessons.

The pronominal form of mettre, like most common verbs, has several meanings. When followed by à (thus au/aux) and either an object or an infinitive, it can mean to start or get down to something: Je vais me mettre au travail dès demain, I'm going to get down to work tomorrow. (See lesson 77 for more about difference between base and pronominal verbs.)

The impersonal third-person emphatic pronoun soi (referenced in lesson 49 section 2) means one or oneself but is much less formal in French than in English. It is often used with an indefinite pronoun: Chacun pour soi, Every person to themselves or in a sentence with no antecedent: Il faut tout faire soi-même, You have to do everything yourself (very formally, One has to do everything oneself). This impersonal context is underlined by the use of the indefinite pronoun on: On est toujours mieux chez soi, There's no place like home.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Marcel Proust (1871-1922) is one of the greatest French novelists and prose stylists. His masterwork, À la recherche du temps perdu (In Search of Lost Time aka Remembrance of Things Past), was published between 1913 and 1927. Proust coined the term la mémoire involontaire, involuntary memory, to describe the recollections triggered by cues encountered by chance, specifically the memories of childhood triggered when the novel's protagonist dips a cake, une madeleine, into his tea. Today, the expression la madeleine de Proust refers to a tiny event that stirs up strong emotions. Proust was a fan of an English game called Confessions, where seemingly trivial questions (Quel est votre animal préféré ? What's your favourite animal?) are framed to discover a person's inner nature. The game subsequently became known as le questionnaire de Proust and is often used today by chat show hosts to find out more about their guests. Productive phase: 26th lesson

dialogue.french: Cette semaine nous sommes dans le beau village de Montluc, avec deux candidats : Marthe et Lucien.
dialogue.english: This week we're in the beautiful village of Montluc, with two players: Marthe and Lucien.

dialogue.english: But first, a little clarification because an error crept into our last show.

dialogue.english: Dozens of emails have been sent to us to specify that Napoleon was born in Corsica.

dialogue.english: So our question was answered brilliantly. We're the ones who aren't brilliant!

dialogue.english: Now, on with the game! Marthe, we'll start with you. And we're off!

dialogue.french: Bonne tentative mais, non. Celle-ci ne mesure que quatre-vingts centimètres de hauteur et un peu plus de cinquante de longueur.
dialogue.english: Nice try, but no. That one is only 80 centimetres high and a little over 50 in length.

dialogue.french: La bonne réponse sera donnée à la fin de l'émission. Lucien, c'est à votre tour.
dialogue.english: The correct answer will be given at the end of the show. Lucien, it's your turn.

dialogue.english: What is the deepest river in France, which rises in Switzerland?

dialogue.french: Euh ... je l'ai sur le bout de la langue ...
dialogue.english: Er ... it's on the tip of my tongue ...

dialogue.french: On se dépêche s'il vous plaît !
dialogue.english: Please hurry up!

dialogue.french: Vous n'êtes pas très patient !
dialogue.english: You are not very patient!

dialogue.french: Au contraire, j'attends patiemment depuis deux minutes mais votre temps est écoulé.
dialogue.english: On the contrary, I have been waiting patiently for two minutes but your time is up.

dialogue.french: C'est une question fréquente dans ce type de jeux : on la pose fréquemment.
dialogue.english: This is a frequent question in this type of game: it is asked frequently.

dialogue.french: Et quand on y réfléchit, la réponse est évidente : c'est le Rhône, évidemment !
dialogue.english: And when you think about it, the answer is obvious: it's the Rhône, obviously!

dialogue.french: Qui a écrit "Les Misérables" et a créé quelques-uns des personnages les plus mémorables de notre littérature ?
dialogue.english: Who wrote "Les Misérables" and created some of the most memorable characters in our literature?

dialogue.french: Mm ... Ça me dit quelque chose. Est-ce un auteur récent ?
dialogue.english: Mm ... Sounds familiar. Is it a recent author?

dialogue.french: Non, il n'a rien écrit récemment car il est mort depuis presque un siècle et demi.
dialogue.english: No, he hasn't written anything recently because he's been dead for almost a century and a half.

dialogue.french: Pour moi, la réponse est évidente mais pas pour vous évidemment : c'était Victor Hugo.
dialogue.english: For me, the answer is obvious, but obviously not for you: it was Victor Hugo.

dialogue.french: On va recommencer l'émission la semaine prochaine avec deux candidats un peu plus cultivés.
dialogue.english: We're going to start the show again next week with two slightly more cultured candidates.

dialogue.french: Au revoir et à bientôt !
dialogue.english: Goodbye and see you soon!

notes.content: Two common adverbial suffixes are -emment, formed from adjectives ending -ent (frequent → fréquemment), and -amment, from adjectives ending in -ant (brillant → brillamment). In both cases, the initial vowel of the suffix (e, a) is pronounced as an open [a]: [breeyahmœhn] / [fraykahmœhn]. Listen carefully to sentences 4 and 15.

We know the adjective précis (lesson 29 line 8) and the adverb précisément (lesson 17 line 9). But the regular verb préciser has no direct equivalent. It means to specify, clarify, etc. Nous voudrions préciser quelques détails, We would like to clarify a few details. Thus, while the general noun la précision means precision, accuracy, the countable form means a clarification, detail, etc.: J'ai besoin d'une précision, I need a clarification.

Unlike précis/précisément, some adverb formations are slightly irregular. Adjectives ending in -ant form their adverb by replacing the last two letters with m then adding the usual -ment suffix: brillant → brillamment (lesson 61 line 19). See also note 8 and the pronunciation notes.

Unlike in English, the French passive voice can't be used with an indirect object. So a sentence like My question has been answered by the audience is not directly translatable. The options are to use the impersonal pronoun: On a répondu à ma question, in which case the indirect object public is dropped, or the demonstrative c'est ... + qui: C'est le public qui a répondu à ma question.

faire place à means to make way for or give way to: La croissance économique rapide a fait place à une expansion plus modérée. Rapid economic growth has made way for a more moderate expansion. The expression Place à (Place aux jeunes, Make way for the young) is often used as an introduction. Et maintenant, place à la fête, Now it's party time.

Dimensions, such as height (la hauteur), length (la longueur) and width (la largeur), are among the kinds of information introduced by de (lesson 75 note 2). For example: L'horloge de la gare mesure neuf mètres de hauteur, The station is 9 meters high. But we can also use the adjectives haut, large and long as (masculine) nouns: ... neuf mètres de haut. There is no difference in meaning between the two forms.

que can be used, like quel, to introduce an exclamatory expression, often as the equivalent of how: Que c'est beau ! How beautiful! When followed by a personal pronoun, however, que is not necessarily translated: Que je suis fatigué ! I'm really tired!

Literally, se connaître means to know oneself: Je me connais : si je ne le fais pas maintenant, je ne le ferai pas du tout, I know myself: if I don't do it now, I won't do it at all. But with the pronoun y, the meaning is to know about or be skilled in something. Elle s'y connaît en littérature française, She knows a lot about French literature. Note the use of en to introduce the complement.
Adjectives ending in -ent form their adverb by replacing the last two letters with m then adding the usual -ment suffix: patient → patiemment. See also note 2 and the pronunciation notes.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Victor Hugo (1802 - 1885), a novelist, poet, dramatist, politician and social historian, is one of France's greatest literary figures. Outside his home country, he is best known for his novels Notre-Dame de Paris (The Hunchback of Notre Dame), published in 1831, and Les Misérables (literally: "the wretched poor," "the dispossessed," 1862). The latter book is so sweeping in its scale and scope (one chapter even recounts the Battle of Waterloo) that is justifiably known as une épopée du peuple, a people's epic. (To answer the first quiz question, the world's largest painting is La Fée Électricité, The Electricity Fairy, by Raoul Dufy, which hangs in le Musée d'Art Moderne in Paris.) Productive phase: 27th lesson

notes.content: Le passif: the passive voice: The passive is used if the object - a person or thing - of an active statement is more important than the subject. The French passive voice is almost identical to its English counterpart in terms of construction. But it is used less frequently, for various reasons. 1.1 Formation: The passive voice of a verb is formed with être and the past participle. Les gens l'aiment, People love him → Il est aimé, He's loved. In the past tense, être becomes été and the two participles follow each other: Ils ont fermé le pont hier, They closed the bridge yesterday → Le pont a été fermé hier, The bridge was closed yesterday. In these two examples, the passive is used because the fact of being loved or closed is more important than whoever is doing the loving or closing. One slight difference with the English construction is that the past participle, being an adjective, has to agree with the subject of the passive verb: Ils ont fermé la fenêtre → La fenêtre a été fermée. (Now's the time to revise your past participles!) If the active subject is incorporated as an agent into the passive sentence, it is preceded by par, by: Le maire a fermé le pont, The mayor closed the bridge → Le pont a été fermé par le maire, The bridge was closed by the mayor. Of course, the participle agreement rule does not change (L'école a été fermée par le maire). Where the agent is mentioned, par is used for almost all verbs. However, those expressing a feeling or an emotion (aimer, respecter, admirer) or a state (entourer, couvrir, décorer, etc.) tend to use de rather than par: Le metteur en scène est respecté de tout le monde. The director is respected by everybody. There are only a handful of such verbs, so they are easy to learn. The negative and first and second interrogatives follow the usual rules: Le pont n'a pas été fermé ; Est-ce que le pont a été fermé ? The third interrogative is slightly more complicated - Le pont a-t-il été fermé ? - but is used much less than the other two forms. 1.2 Use: The passive is used less in French than in English, firstly because active forms are preferable when describing actions, and secondly because French has several ways of sidestepping passive constructions. There are three main workarounds: with on, with the pronominal form, and with c'est ... qui. • with on: The third-person impersonal pronoun on is used when the subject is implied or unimportant. It replaces nous, ils/elles, quelqu'un, tout le monde, etc. So a sentence like Ici, on parle anglais can be used by a shop to inform potential customers that all its staff speak English (We speak English here), or by residents of an area to insist that everyone is anglophone (Everyone here speaks English). As you know, on is also commonly used instead of nous, simply because the first-person plural verb form often sounds too formal. In very formal situations, on can be translated by its English cognate, one. For example, On dit que ... can be the equivalent of One says that ... but it would generally be rendered by the passive It is said that ... In a more colloquial setting, the usual equivalent is They say that ... • with the pronominal verbs, formed with se: These are more common in French than in English precisely because they are used instead of the passive voice. In this case, there is no notion of myself, itself, etc. For example: Ce plat se mange chaud ou froid means This dish is eaten hot or cold. This form is possible only with the third person singular or plural: Les voitures neuves se vendent bien cette année, New cars are selling well this year. One very common set of short passive phrases is formed with ça (or, more formally, cela) followed by a pronominal verb: Il est français. - Ça s'entend, He's French. - You can hear it. Elle a très mal. - Cela se voit, She's in great pain. - That's obvious, etc. • with c'est (plural: ce sont) ... qui: This workaround avoids the passive by emphasising the subject. So a sentence like The report was written by my German colleague can be translated literally by Le rapport a été écrit par mon collègue allemand, which is grammatically correct, or, more colloquially, by C'est mon collègue allemand qui a écrit le rapport. A word-for-word translation ("It's my German colleague who . . . ," etc) does not have the same resonance, precisely because French prefers the active to the passive. The main difference between the two languages is that the passive cannot be used with an indirect object in French, unlike English. For example, the English sentence Someone has sent me an email can, if necessary, be turned into a passive by converting the indirect object to a subject: I have been sent an email by someone. This is not possible in French. In cases like this, on can be used as a workaround: On m'a envoyé un mail, I have been sent an email. Likewise, if an English passive sentence does not contain a direct object (for example, The email has been answered), it cannot be passive in French: On a répondu au mail.

Adverbs of time: Let's review this important category of adverbs, most of which you have already learned and assimilated. Remember that many adverbs are formed from the feminine form of adjectives (dernier → dernière → dernièrement). They are placed after a verb consisting of a single word and between the component parts of two-word verbs or a conjugated verb and its auxiliary. Now that you have been using most of these words for several months, the etymological explanations below will make them clearer and help you remember them. - actuellement, now, at present, at the moment, currently: From the adjective actuel, current: Elle travaille actuellement à Brest, She's currently working in Brest. Do NOT confuse actuellement and actually (en réalité / en fait). - alors: From the adverb lors (see below), alors means at the/that time: Il était alors directeur de l'usine, He was the factory manager at the time. It also means so, then: Alors, tu viens ? So, are you coming? - après, next or after(wards): Je l'ai revue longtemps après, I saw her a long time afterwards. - auparavant, before(hand), previously, earlier: Elle avait annoncé son départ une semaine auparavant. She had announced her departure a week earlier. A synonym of avant (as an adverb, not a preposition), auparavant is more common in writing than in speech. - aussitôt: a synonym of immédiatement, meaning straight away: J'ai appelé un taxi et il est arrivé aussitôt. I called a taxi and it came immediately. Formed from aussi and tôt but always written as a single word. - autrefois, formerly, in the past: Autrefois, on vivait différemment, In the past, people lived differently. (from autre, other, and fois, time). Synonyms include dans le passé, in the past and avant (see below) - avant: as an adverb, avant is a less formal synonym of autrefois and auparavant (with which it shares an etymology). See dorénavant. - bientôt, soon, shortly: On arrive bientôt, We'll be arriving shortly. Used commonly in the expression à bientôt, See you soon. - d'abord, first (of all): Nous allons d'abord t'acheter une casquette. First we're going to buy you a cap. When citing the main reason for something, d'abord means in the first place: D'abord tu es trop jeune, In the first place, you're too young. - déjà, already: Il est déjà dix-sept heures ! It's 5pm already. - depuis, for / since, depending on the context: depuis Noël, since Christmas; depuis dix ans, for ten years. - dernièrement, lately, recently: Je ne l'ai pas vue dernièrement, I haven't seen her lately. - dorénavant, from now on: Dorénavant, c'est moi qui prendrai les décisions, From now on, I'll take the decisions. (Note the root word, avant.) - enfin, at last, finally: Ils sont enfin arrivés, They've arrived at last. (en + fin, in the end). Also used in declarative or explicative contexts: C'est mon mari, enfin, mon copain, He's my husband, well, my boyfriend. - ensuite, next, afterwards: Je téléphonerai d'abord et j'écrirai ensuite. I'll phone first, then afterwards I'll write. (suite, from suivre, means continuation, follow-up). - jamais, never: Elle n'a jamais lu "Notre Dame de Paris", She has never read "The Hunchback of Notre Dame". In some cases, jamais can be used without ne : Jamais plus ! Never again. - longtemps, for a long time, (for) long: Je n'ai pas attendu longtemps, I didn't wait long. The root adjective (long) is masculine; it cannot be used as an adverb, unlike in English. - lors de, during, at the time of: L'ascenseur a été installé lors de la construction de la tour. The lift was installed during the construction of the tower. In modern French, lors is not used alone.

Base and pronominal verbs with different meanings: We know that French uses pronominal (i.e. reflexive or reciprocal) verbs extensively in order to avoid the passive voice. In some cases, there are slight idiomatic differences in meaning between the base verb and its pronominal form. As always, the most important factor is context. - Faire: The base verb means to do or make, so the literal meaning of the reflexive form is to make oneself: Je me fais un sandwich au jambon, I'm making myself a ham sandwich. Idiomatically, se faire un film or un restaurant means to go out to a movie or for a meal. Another very common idiom is s'en faire, to worry, be concerned about. It is often used in the negative: Ne t'en fais pas, je t'aiderai, Don't worry, I'll help you. And the expression Ça se fait / Ça ne se fait pas states what is or is not a proper thing to do: Chez nous, ça ne se fait pas, We don't do that sort of thing at our house. (See also lesson 57 note 1). - The reciprocal form of mettre, to put, is used in relation to clothing: Je n'ai rien à me mettre, I've got nothing to wear. It can also indicate movement towards something: Mettez-vous au chaud, Get into the warm. Followed by à, se mettre means to take up (an activity): Il s'est mis au tennis, He's started playing tennis (see lesson 75 note 9). And where the object is understood, it is replaced by y. Je vais m'y mettre, I'll get started on it. - Other common verb pairs with slight differences include attendre, to wait, and s'attendre, to expect (lesson 59); entendre, to hear, and s'entendre, to get along with (lesson 55); plaindre, to pity, and se plaindre, to complain; douter (de), to doubt, and se douter (de), to guess or suspect (both in lesson 73). A simple rule of thumb is that if you can't extrapolate the meaning of a pronominal verb from its base form, then look in a good bilingual dictionary.

Adjectives of dimension: Dimensions can be expressed in several ways. The adjectives long(ue), haut(e), large and profond(e) are placed before the noun, along with the partitive de: Le bâtiment est haut de trente étages, The building is 30 stories high. Of course, they have to agree: La Tour Eiffel est haute de trois cent vingt mètres. The Eiffel Tower is 320 meters high. Another possibility is to put the nouns la longueur (length), la hauteur (height), la largeur (width) and la profondeur (depth), preceded by de, after the noun: Le salon fait trois mètres de largeur or Une tour de cent mètres de hauteur. In this case, however, we still use an adjective when translating into English: The living room is three metres wide, A 100-metre high tower. Lastly, and slightly confusingly, long, haut and large are also masculine nouns (le haut du visage, the upper part of the face, for example) and can be used in the same predicative position as the equivalent adjectives. Thus twenty kilometres long is either vingt kilomètres de long or de longueur; one metre high is un mètre de haut or de hauteur; and two centimetres wide is deux centimètres de large or de largeur. The exception to this duality is profondeur, depth: trois mètres de profondeur (*de profond is incorrect). The only, slight difference is register: the substantive is considered slightly more elegant than the adjective. In practical terms, though, the two are equivalent.

Proverbs and sayings: Maxims are an integral part of any language and are used regularly, either directly or obliquely ("You know what they say about rolling stones ... "). In any case, they are an important part of popular culture. Because French and English are closely related, many proverbs are identical or very similar in both languages. We've learned Rira bien qui rira le dernier, He who laughs last laughs longest. Here are five more: Pierre qui roule n'amasse pas mousse, A rolling stone gathers no moss; À mauvais ouvrier point de bons outils, A bad workman always blames his tools; Tout ce qui brille n'est pas or, All that glitters is not gold; Mieux vaut tard que jamais, Better late than never; Il ne faut pas mettre tous ses œufs dans le même panier, Don't put all your eggs in one (or the same) basket. We have also learned an axiom which, though not identical, does have a direct equivalent: C'est l'hôpital qui se moque de la charité ("It's the hospital that is mocking the charity"), The pot calling the kettle black. In upcoming lessons, we'll learn more about sayings with the same meaning but slightly different wording.

extras.title: Smile!
extras.content: Productive phase: 28th lesson

dialogue.french: Vous avez des projets pour le pont de l'Ascension ? Je suis à la recherche d'inspiration.
dialogue.english: Do you have plans for the long weekend of the Ascension? I'm looking for inspiration.

dialogue.french: Nous allons rendre visite à mes neveux et ma nièce à Nîmes, enfin pas à Nîmes même, mais juste à côté.
dialogue.english: We're going to visit my nephews and niece in Nîmes, well not in Nîmes itself but just next door.

dialogue.french: Vous y allez avec les enfants, j'imagine. Quelle chance ils ont !
dialogue.english: You're going with the children, I imagine. How lucky they are!

dialogue.french: Non, pas cette fois-ci.
dialogue.english: No, not this time.

dialogue.french: Nous le leur avons proposé mais ils n'étaient pas très chauds.
dialogue.english: We suggested it to them, but they didn't seem too keen.

dialogue.french: Ils sont difficiles, vos gamins. Moi, je sauterais sur l'occasion de visiter la Provence.
dialogue.english: Your kids are difficult. Me, I'd jump at the opportunity to visit Provence.

dialogue.french: Je ne leur en veux pas. On les y a souvent emmenés quand ils étaient jeunes.
dialogue.english: I don't hold it against them. We often took them there when they were young.

dialogue.french: Ils s'y sont beaucoup amusés.
dialogue.english: They had a lot of fun there.

dialogue.french: Mais maintenant ils préfèrent s'amuser avec les copains plutôt que de rester avec leurs parents !
dialogue.english: But now they prefer to have fun with their friends rather than stay with their parents!

dialogue.french: Et pourtant, les voyages forment la jeunesse, dit-on.
dialogue.english: And yet, they say that travel broadens the mind.

dialogue.french: Je le leur ai dit mais ils n'en ont rien à faire et je n'arrive pas à les convaincre.
dialogue.english: I told them that, but they don't care and I can't manage to convince them.

dialogue.french: Ils me disent qu'ils ont peur de s'embêter avec les vieux, qu'ils trouvent trop ennuyeux.
dialogue.english: They tell me they are afraid of getting bored with old people, whom they find too boring.

dialogue.french: Je leur réponds que c'est dans les vieux pots qu'on fait la meilleure soupe.
dialogue.english: I tell them that the best wine comes in old bottles.

dialogue.french: Qu'importe. Ce sont eux les perdants dans l'histoire.
dialogue.english: It doesn't matter. It's they who are the losers in the story.

dialogue.french: Revenons à notre sujet. Vu que vous serez dans la région nîmoise,
dialogue.english: Let's get back to the subject. Since you'll be in the Nîmes region,

dialogue.french: est-ce que vous pouvez m'acheter du miel de lavande et des herbes de Provence ?
dialogue.english: can you buy me some lavender honey and Provence herbs?

dialogue.french: Bien sûr, on en rapporte aussi pour Alex et Théo. Nous leur en avons promis.
dialogue.english: Of course, we're also bringing some back for Alex and Théo. We promised them some.

dialogue.french: Et si vous voyez un beau vase en céramique avec un motif de cigales dessus
dialogue.english: And if you see a beautiful ceramic vase with a cicada design on it

dialogue.french: le souvenir touristique par excellence -, vous pouvez me le prendre ?
dialogue.english: the ultimate tourist souvenir - can you get it for me?

dialogue.french: Oui, je connais une boutique qui en vend : nous y en avons déjà acheté pour d'autres amis.
dialogue.english: Yes, I know a shop that sells them: we've already bought them for other friends.

dialogue.french: Mais vu les prix, ça risque de te revenir cher, tu sais.
dialogue.english: But given the prices, it could cost you a lot, you know.

dialogue.french: Alors je vais te faire un chèque tout de suite.
dialogue.english: So I'll do you a cheque right away.

dialogue.french: Ne me le donne pas maintenant ; donne-le-moi à la fin du mois.
dialogue.english: Don't give it to me now; give it to me at the end of the month.

notes.content: la recherche, research (see lesson 37 note 3) is used in the expression à la recherche de, to be in search of or, simply, to be looking for: Nous sommes à la recherche de nouveaux locaux, We're looking for new premises.

It is important not to confuse rendre visite, to visit or to go and see a person (see lessons 68 line 3 and 69 line 10), and visiter, to visit a place (lesson 37 line 8). One way of avoiding rendre visite is simply to say aller voir: Nous sommes allés voir Théo hier, We went to see Théo yesterday.

When a sentence contains both a direct object pronoun and an indirect object pronoun, they have to follow a strict order. In an affirmative sentence, the direct object le has to precede the indirect leur (In the singular, and with a feminine direct object, the sentence would read Nous la lui avons proposée.) In a negative sentence (line 23), ne precedes the first pronoun and pas follows the verb: Ne me la propose pas ... . Look at the examples in this lesson (noted 3), then check lesson 84 for a fuller explanation.

être chaud pour has nothing to do with temperature; it means to be enthusiastic about or keen to do something. Fabien est chaud pour venir avec nous à Nîmes, Fabien is enthusiastic about coming with us to Nîmes. Arguably, the expression is used more often in the negative. Tu viens ? - Je ne suis pas très chaud. You coming? - I'm not too keen. (Of course, to be hot is avoir chaud.)

un gamin is a synonym of un(e) gosse, (lesson 55 note 10), both of which mean a kid. (The feminine form, une gamine, is the origin of our word gamine, meaning an attractively boyish girl or woman.) The two words are familiar, not slang.

Here's yet another idiomatic construction with faire: n'en avoir rien à faire means not to care or be bothered. Ils sont trop occupés ou, peut-être, ils n'en ont rien à faire, They're too busy, or perhaps they don't care. The clue to the idiomatic nature of the expression is en, because Ils n'ont rien à faire simply means They have nothing to do.

Another meaning of arriver, to arrive, is to manage or succeed. In this case, it is followed à, and any subsequent verb is in the infinitive: Je n'arrive pas à la joindre par téléphone, I can't get through to her on the phone.

Here is another verb that changes meaning slightly in the pronominal form (see lesson 77): embêter means to pester, annoy, etc.: N'embêtez pas votre frère, il travaille, Don't pester your brother, he's working. But s'embêter, a synonym of s'ennuyer, means to be or get bored: Elle s'embête en cours d'anglais, She gets bored in English lessons. In the sense of to bother, the pronominal and base forms can have the same meaning: Ne vous embêtez pas avec ça, Don't bother with that.
Qu'importe is a more elegant form of Peu importe. Both mean It doesn't matter (lesson 60 note 8).

vu, the past participle of voir, to see, is useful way of saying in view of (or, more simply, since). It can be followed by a noun: Vu le temps, on restera à la maison, In view of the weather, we'll stay at home, or by que and a verb clause: Vu qu'il fait beau, on ira à la plage, Since the weather's fine, we'll go to the beach. In the que construction, vu que can be used like puisque (lesson 58 line 1).

revenir, to come back, is used idiomatically to talk about cost: Avec l'essence, le voyage reviendra à cent euros, With petrol, the journey will work out at 100 euros. A common expression is Ça revient cher. That costs a lot.
