fill.question: They won the first game, for sure, but there's no need to make a big deal of it. Ils ont gagné le premier jeu, mais ce n'est pas la peine d'en faire tout un fromage.
fill.full_answer: Ils ont gagné le premier jeu, mais ce n'est pas la peine d'en faire tout un fromage.

fill.question: We've decided to meet halfway and to share the two-thousand-euro prize pool between the three of us. Nous avons décidé de couper la poire en deux et de partager la cagnotte de deux mille euros entre nous trois.
fill.full_answer: Nous avons décidé de couper la poire en deux et de partager la cagnotte de deux mille euros entre nous trois.

fill.question: He earns only the minimum wage, so he's looking to top up his income. Il ne gagne que le salaire minimum, donc il cherche à mettre du beurre dans les épinards.
fill.full_answer: Il ne gagne que le salaire minimum, donc il cherche à mettre du beurre dans les épinards.

fill.question: Don't be a nitwit. I've got a lot on my plate and I have to finish by midnight. Ne fais pas l'andouille ! J'ai du pain sur la planche et je dois terminer avant minuit.
fill.full_answer: Ne fais pas l'andouille ! J'ai du pain sur la planche et je dois terminer avant minuit.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: One problem arising from the shortage of French epicene nouns - those having only one form for the male and the female - is the need to use both forms, notably in public address. In these contexts, particularly speeches and media commentary, a noun's feminine and the masculine forms (in that order) are used jointly to ensure gender inclusivity (see lesson 66 line 3). TV presenters, for example, often start their shows with the greeting Chères téléspectatrices, chers téléspectateurs (whereas the English equivalent would be Dear viewers), and radio presenters say Chères auditrices, chers auditeurs (Dear listeners). Likewise, a magazine director might start an editorial with Chères lectrices, Chers lecteurs (Dear readers), while a public official in, say, Lyon, might start a blogpost with Chères Lyonnaises, Chers Lyonnais. Cumbersome as they might seem, these dual forms are very important, for political reasons. Productive phase: 34th lesson

dialogue.french: Je vous appelle à propos des travaux de peinture que vous nous avez commandés.
dialogue.english: I'm calling you about the painting work you ordered from us.

dialogue.french: Je crains une mauvaise nouvelle ... Dites-la-moi.
dialogue.english: I fear bad news ... Tell me.

dialogue.french: Personne ne vous a prévenue ? Je pensais que mon collègue avait appelé.
dialogue.english: Didn't anyone warn you? I thought my colleague had called.

dialogue.french: Non, personne ne m'a appelée. De quoi s'agit-il ?
dialogue.english: No, nobody called me. What's this about?

dialogue.french: Bon, il n'est pas certain que nous puissions venir la semaine prochaine.
dialogue.english: Well, it's not certain that we can come next week.

dialogue.french: Je suis dans les choux car j'ai deux ouvriers qui sont malades et personne pour les remplacer.
dialogue.english: I'm in a bit of a bind because I have two workers who are ill and no one to replace them.

dialogue.french: Je veux que vous sachiez que je ferai tout pour venir, comme prévu, mais il faut que vous soyez prête à reporter le rendez-vous.
dialogue.english: I want you to know that I'll do everything I can to come, as planned, but I need you to be prepared to postpone the appointment.

dialogue.french: Eh bien, vu que je n'ai pas le choix. Que me proposez-vous comme nouvelle date ?
dialogue.english: Well, since I have no choice. What new date are you offering me?

dialogue.french: Je suis désolé de ne pas pouvoir être plus précis, mais ça ne dépend pas que de moi.
dialogue.english: I'm sorry I can't be more precise, but it's not up to me alone.

dialogue.french: Je vais faire appel à un collègue, mais il est sur un autre chantier en ce moment.
dialogue.english: I'm going to call in a colleague, but he's on another site at the moment.

dialogue.french: Quand il aura terminé, peut-il se libérer rapidement ?
dialogue.english: When he's finished, can he be free quickly?

dialogue.french: Je ne sais pas quoi vous dire. Il n'est pas certain que son chantier soit terminé d'ici à la fin de la semaine.
dialogue.english: I don't know what to tell you. He's not sure that his job will be finished by the end of the week.

dialogue.french: Je sens que nous ne sommes pas sortis de l'auberge !
dialogue.english: I feel that we're not out of the woods!

notes.content: Le subjonctif: the subjunctive: The subjunctive mood describes a hypothetical situation. It is generally used in a subordinate clause to talk about actions or events that are desirable, imagined or possible, and that generally depend on an initial verb in the indicative. The subjunctive exists in English but, being almost identical to the indicative, is rarely identified as such (except in phrases such as I wish I were on holiday). In French, however, it is both common and obligatory. This is especially the case in subordinate clauses where the subject is different from the one in the main clause and where the action being described is in some way conditional, theoretical or subjective. In this case, the subordinate verb is preceded by que. For example, Je veux que tu sois heureuse, I want you to be happy (i.e. the person I'm talking to seems miserable -my subjective opinion- and I wish they weren't). There are four subjunctive tenses (present, past, imperfect and pluperfect) but in modern, conversational French, we generally use the present form (and occasionally the past). 1.1 Formation: The present subjunctive is formed by taking the third person plural of the present tense, dropping the final -ent and adding the endings shown below. aimer: j'aime, tu aimes, il/elle aime, nous aimions, vous aimiez, ils/elles aiment. finir: je finisse, tu finisse, il/elle finisse, nous finissions, vous finissiez, ils/elles finissent. attendre: j'attende, tu attendes, il/elle attende, nous attendions, vous attendiez, ils/elles attendent. Note that the first and second persons plural of the subjunctive are identical to the equivalent imperfect forms, but the structure of the sentence (and, often, the presence of que) should make it easy to distinguish between the two. The negative follows the usual pattern (vous n'aimiez pas, etc.), while the interrogative is almost never used. These rules apply to all regular and most irregular verbs. Nevertheless there are some irregular forms, especially with major verbs, which have to be memorised: être: (que) je sois, tu sois, il/elle soit, nous soyons, vous soyez, ils/elles soient. avoir: (que) j'aie, tu aies, il/elle ait, nous ayons, vous ayez, ils/elles aient. faire: (que) je fasse, tu fasses, il/elle fasse, nous fassions, vous fassiez, ils/elles fassent. pouvoir: (que) je puisse, tu puisses, il/elle puisse, nous puissions, vous puissiez, ils/elles puissent. vouloir: (que) je veuille, tu veuilles, il/elle veuille, nous voulions, vous vouliez, ils/elles veuillent. aller: (que) j'aille, tu ailles, il/elle aille, nous allions, vous alliez, ils/elles aillent. There are other, stem-changing verbs, which we will see in the coming lessons. 1.2 Use: One of the most common uses of the subjunctive is when the subject of a sentence expresses a personal feeling, such as regret, hope, preference or expectation, about an action performed -or not- by another person. In this case the first verb is in the indicative, followed by que, and the second is in the subjunctive. Here are some examples: Je suis content / heureux / ravi que vous veniez ce soir. I'm pleased / happy / delighted that you're coming this evening. Elles veulent que vous téléphoniez tout à l'heure. They want you to phone later on. Il espère que son film puisse être distribué en France. He is hoping that his movie can be distributed in France. Nous préférons que vous leur demandiez. We prefer you to ask them. Similar verbs include aimer que (to wish that), attendre que, s'attendre à ce que (expect), demander que (ask that), douter que (doubt that), être désolé que (be sorry that), être triste que (be sad that), être dommage que (be a shame that), exiger que (demand that) and insister pour que (insist that). Go back to lesson 80 and review these verbs in context, noting how the subjunctive in the subordinate clause is "sub-joined" to the indicative verb in the main clause. - The subjunctive is also obligatory with impersonal verbs, especially falloir: to be necessary that, etc. Il faut que tu finisses rapidement, You have to finish quickly. Other impersonal forms, such as être certain / être sûr que, logically take the indicative in the affirmative: Il est certain que nous pouvons les aider, We can certainly help them. In the negative, however, the outcome is in doubt, so the subjunctive is used: Il n'est pas certain que puissions les aider. It's not certain that we can help them. - The subjunctive is used after subordinating conjunctions, such as bien que, quoi que and pourvu que, that introduce a hypothesis or condition. These, too, are followed by the subjunctive. Bien que nous soyons amis, je ne peux pas vous aider. Even though we're friends, I can't help you. Quoi que tu fasses, rien ne changera. Whatever you do, nothing will change. Pourvu qu'il vienne ! As long as he comes! We'll see other examples in the coming lessons. - Similarly, the subjunctive is necessary if we use an imperative to posit a condition: Supposons que quelqu'un vienne te dire : "Vous avez gagné au loto". Que ferais-tu ? Let's assume that someone comes along and says: "You've won the lotto." What would you do? - The subjunctive is also obligatory with certain time conjunctions: Faites-vite, avant qu'elle parte en vacances. Do it quickly, before she leaves on holiday. Je reste ici jusqu'à ce que tu me dises la vérité, I'm staying here until you tell me the truth. In the next six lessons, we will look at other types of construction in which the subjunctive is obligatory, as well as cases where it may or may not be used, depending in the shade of meaning.

Pronoun order: When several indirect pronouns are used before a verb, they have to follow a strict order. You are already familiar with the basic patterns, seen in the course of previous lessons. One way to remember the proper sequence is to visualise a soccer team playing in a 5-3-2 formation, with a goalkeeper (y) and the ball (en). The sequence is: me / te / se / nous / vous, le / la / les, lui / leur, y, en. The sequence is actually easier than it might seem. In most cases only two indirect pronouns are ever used together (the maximum is three), with the most usual combination being Column 1 and Column 2. The pronouns in the first and third columns never go together. Similarly, pronouns in the same column cannot be placed together. For example, Ils se présentent à nous, They introduce themselves to us (never *Ils nous se présentent). Look at these examples, all of which start with a subject pronoun, which is closest to the verb. Je te donnerai l'adresse de la boutique. → Je te la donnerai. I'll give you the address of the shop. → I will give it to you. Elle veut donner le vase à Alex. → Elle veut le lui donner. She wants to give the vase to Alex. → She wants to give it to him. Nous avons proposé le voyage à nos enfants. → Nous le leur avons proposé. We offered the trip to our children. → We offered it to them. Vous me parlez souvent de vos amis. → Vous m'en parlez souvent. You often tell me about your friends. → You often tell me about them. Nous emmenons nos enfants à Nîmes demain. → Nous les y emmenons demain. They're going to Nîmes tomorrow. → They're going there tomorrow. In the negative, the pronouns come immediately after the first negative particle, ne, while pas, comes after the verb, as usual: Je ne te le donnerai pas, Il ne la lui donnera pas, etc. With an imperative, the word order is slightly different. First, we know that me and te become moi and toi (lesson 33 note 3). Second, the second-column pronouns precede those in the first column: Passez-moi la tablette. → Passez-la moi. Donnez-lui le miel. → Donnez-le lui.

Negative constructions: The simple way to negate a verb is with ne ... pas (Il comprend → Il ne comprend pas). But there are other constructions in which pas is replaced by an adverb. Let's review them: • ne ... jamais, never: Ils ne veulent jamais venir en vacances avec nous. They never want to come on holiday with us. For emphasis, the adverb sometimes comes first: Jamais ils ne veulent venir en vacances ... Never do they want to come on holiday. • ne ... plus, no longer, no more: On ne peut plus continuer comme ça, We can't go on like this any longer. Remember that a definite or indefinite article coming after plus becomes de: Il y a une grève / Il y a des grèves → Il n'y a plus de grève/grèves. • ne ... que, only: Je n'ai que deux minutes à vous accorder, I can only give you two minutes. In some cases, ne ... que can be replaced by seulement: J'ai seulement deux minutes à vous accorder, but this is considered less elegant. • ne ... aucun(e), no, none: Nous n'avons aucune nouvelle de lui, We have no news from him. Aucun(e) can be used alone in response to a question: Avez-vous des nouvelles de lui ? - Aucune. Have you had any news from him? - None at all. • ne ... rien, nothing: Je n'ai rien à vous dire, I've got nothing to say to you. When negating a compound verb, such as avoir besoin, rien comes after the partitive article: Je n'ai besoin de rien. However, where rien is used as the direct object of a sentence, it is not part of the negative construction: Elle ne mange jamais rien, She never eats anything. • ne ... personne, no one / anyone, nobody / anybody: Il n'aime personne, He doesn't like anyone. If personne is the subject of the sentence, then the verb needs only the particle ne (remember the rule against double negatives): Personne ne l'aime, No one likes him/her. • ne ... ni ... ni, either ... or: Je n'ai ni le temps ni l'envie, I have neither the time nor the inclination. As with ne ... personne, ni ... ni ... can negate the subjects of the sentence. In this case, unlike English, the verb is generally in the plural: Ni la pluie ni la neige ne me gênent. Neither the rain nor the snow bothers me. In some cases, though, the singular can also be used, especially if each of the subjects is considered individually (Ni Rachida ni Anne n'est la mère de cet enfant). But the rules for this are quite complex, so, if in doubt, use the plural. In some cases, two negative adverbs can be used together -for example, Je ne l'ai plus jamais revu, I never saw him again- but the meaning will always be clear. Finally, in spoken French, the ne part of a negative construction is often "swallowed," leaving only the adverb ("il vient jamais", for example). Of course, this is incorrect and should not be imitated, but you will certainly hear it.

The euphonic t in the interrogative: Whereas est-ce que is the most common interrogative construction in everyday French, especially for oral expression (Est-ce qu'il vit à Paris ?), the inverted interrogative is more frequent in written and formal contexts. In terms of pronunciation, this poses no problem if the verb form ends in a consonant (vit-il...), which can be liaised with the initial vowel of the pronoun ([veet-eel]); but if the verb ends in a vowel, Il parle le français, the liaison is impossible and an inverted form would be hard (and ugly) to pronounce. So, to avoid this, a euphonic t, which does not change the meaning, is inserted between the verb and the pronoun and joined to them by hyphens: Parle-t-il le français ? (In the third person plural, however, this device is unnecessary: Parlent-ils ?). Lastly, if the third person singular ends in d (il prend, for example), a simple inversion is sufficient (prend-il); however, that d is pronounced as a euphonic t: [prœhn-teel].

More idioms: food: Since France has an enviable gastronomic reputation, it is hardly surprising that many of the idioms in its language are concerned with food and eating. Here is a brief recap of what you have learned: avoir du pain sur la planche (have one's work cut out), avoir la pêche / frite (be full of beans / on top form), couper la poire en deux (meet halfway, compromise), être dans les choux (be in a tough spot, in trouble), faire l'andouille (act the fool), en faire tout un fromage (make a song and dance), mettre du beurre dans les épinards (earn a little extra), ne pas être sorti de l'auberge (not to be out of the woods), ne pas gagner un radis (not to earn a bean), tomber dans les pommes (pass out). All these expressions are used in everyday communications. Two other common expressions relating to food are un navet (literally a turnip but, idiomatically, applied to a film, a flop, a turkey, etc.: Son dernier film est un vrai navet, His/Her latest movie is rubbish) and avoir un cœur d'artichaut, (literally "to have an artichoke's heart") to be flighty, to fall in love easily. In the latter expression, the person so described (Il a un cœur d'artichaut, celui-là) has as many paramours as an artichoke heart has leaves. And don't forget the two proverbs seen in lesson 78: Les voyages forment la jeunesse, Travel broadens the mind, and C'est dans les vieux pots qu'on fait la bonne/meilleure soupe. The best wine comes in old bottles.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Productive phase: 35th lesson

title: Comment ça, je massacre le français ?

dialogue.french: Tu sors par ce temps, Michel ? Tu ne vois pas qu'il fait un froid glacial ?
dialogue.english: You're going out in this weather, Michel? Can't you see it's freezing cold?

dialogue.french: Et ils annoncent à nouveau de gros orages pour tout à l'heure.
dialogue.english: And they're again forecasting big storms for later on.

dialogue.french: Il est où ton pardessus ? Et, de toute façon, où tu vas à cette heure-ci ?
dialogue.english: Where's your overcoat? And, anyway, where are you going at this time of day?

dialogue.french: Pourquoi tu veux savoir ? Ce ne sont pas tes oignons, mon frère.
dialogue.english: Why do you want to know? It's none of your business, my brother.

dialogue.french: C'est quoi, ton problème ? Pourquoi tu es si grognon ce matin ?
dialogue.english: What's your problem? Why are you so grumpy this morning?

dialogue.french: Je vais à ma banque, si tu veux savoir. Ça te va comme explication ?
dialogue.english: I'm going to my bank, if you must know. How's that for an explanation?

dialogue.french: On m'a appelé et on m'a demandé de passer à l'agence au plus vite.
dialogue.english: I got a call and was asked to come by the branch as soon as possible.

dialogue.french: Il paraît que mon compte est à découvert. Je suis tombé des nues quand je l'ai appris.
dialogue.english: It appears that my account is overdrawn. I was stunned when I found out.

dialogue.french: Ils t'ont appelé quand ? C'est quand le rendez-vous ?
dialogue.english: When did they call you? When's the appointment?

dialogue.french: Tu pars à quelle heure ? Ton conseiller de clientèle sera là-bas, au rendez-vous ?
dialogue.english: What time are you leaving? Will your customer adviser be there at the meeting?

dialogue.french: Tu poses toutes ces questions pourquoi ? Tu mets ton nez partout.
dialogue.english: Why are you asking all these questions? You stick your nose into everything.

dialogue.french: Tu ne sais pas qu'il ne faut pas être curieux et se mêler des affaires des autres ?
dialogue.english: Don't you know not to be curious and to meddle in other people's business?

dialogue.french: Bon, assez discuté. Quelle heure il est ? Il faut que je file.
dialogue.english: Right, enough talk. What time is it? I've got to dash.

dialogue.french: Arrêtez, vous deux, pour l'amour du ciel ! Où avez-vous appris le français ?
dialogue.english: Stop it, you two, for heaven's sake! Where did you learn French?

dialogue.french: Je vous écoute depuis cinq minutes, et vous massacrez la langue de Molière !
dialogue.english: I've been listening to you for five minutes, and you're slaughtering the language of Molière!

dialogue.french: On doit dire "Où allez-vous ?" ou "Quand est ton rendez-vous ?".
dialogue.english: You have to say: "Where are you going?" or "When is your appointment?".

dialogue.french: De même, on ne dit pas "Il est arrivé quand ?" mais "Quand est-il arrivé ?".
dialogue.english: Likewise, you don't say "He arrived when?" but "When did he arrive?".

dialogue.french: Et "À quelle heure pars-tu ?" est dix fois plus élégant que "Tu pars à quelle heure ?".
dialogue.english: And "What time are you leaving?" is ten times more elegant than "What time you off?".

dialogue.french: Pour en revenir à toi, Michel. Pourquoi tu vas ... euh ... vas-tu à la banque ?
dialogue.english: To get back to you, Michel. Why're ... Erm, why are you going to the bank?

dialogue.french: C'est-à-dire que j'ai emprunté ta carte de crédit et je me suis acheté quelques petits trucs ...
dialogue.english: That is to say that I borrowed your credit card and bought myself a few things ...

dialogue.french: Tu as dépensé combien, espèce de petit voyou ? Pardon : combien as-tu dépensé, mon frère adoré ?
dialogue.english: How much did you spend, you little brat? Excuse me: how much did you spend, my beloved brother?

dialogue.french: Occupe-toi de tes affaires. Je veux dire : La curiosité est un vilain défaut, cher ami.
dialogue.english: Mind your own business. I mean: Curiosity killed the cat, dear friend.

dialogue.french: Vilain défaut ? Et comment !
dialogue.english: Ugly flaw? You said it!

notes.content: The verb haïr, to hate, is awkward. The diaresis, showing that both a and i must be pronounced, is present in the infinitive [eye-ear] and the three present-plural forms (haïssons, [eye-eessoh"], haïssez [eye-eessay], haïssent [eye-eess]) but not the singular: hais and hait, both of which are pronounced [ay].

comment, how, is used as an interjection to express surprise, annoyance or disbelief (lesson 40 line 15), just like What ... ?. Comment, tu as perdu mes clés de voiture ? What, you've lost my car keys? It can be used like Comment ça (lesson 67 note 2): Comment ça, je parle trop fort ? What do you mean, I'm talking too loudly? Comment can be used as a positive intensifier, Et comment !, in response to a comment or question, like I should say so!, You bet!, etc. Donc tu as aimé le jeu ? - Et comment ! So you enjoyed the game? - Didn't I just! (Don't confuse the intensifier and the interrogative: see the exercises below.)

un pardessus is an overcoat. The word comes from the hyphenated preposition par-dessus ("by above"), meaning over: Mets un pull par-dessus ta chemise, il fait un froid glacial, Put a sweater over your shirt, it's freezing cold.

In a familiar register, where the subject of an interrogative sentence is a personal pronoun (je, me, etc), the question can be formed simply by starting with an interrogative word and avoiding the inversion or est-ce que: Où vous allez ? Where are you going? instead of Où allez-vous / Où est-ce que vous allez ? Though not recommended, particularly when writing, these idiomatic interrogatives are very common.

un oignon (sometimes spelled ognon), an onion, is used in several idiomatic expressions where the English equivalent is business. The most common are Ce ne sont pas (or simply Ce n'est pas) mes oignons, It's none of my business, and Occupe-toi de tes oignons, Mind your own business.

Another idiomatic way of forming a question is to precede the interrogative word (qui, comment, etc.) with C'est ... : C'est qui, ton meilleur ami ? Who's your best friend? Comment tu es venu ce matin ? How did you get here this morning? These idiomatic forms are often used in advertisements, song lyrics, etc. to reflect everyday speech.

découvrir, (from couvrir, to cover, see lesson 41, note 5), means both to discover and to uncover. The past participle, découvert, is used as an adjective: une piscine découverte, an open-air swimming pool. But the noun un découvert means an overdraft (i.e. outgoings not covered by incoming amounts). The adjective is être à découvert, to be overdrawn, to have an overdraft.

The adjective nu means naked, bare (and, of course, nude), etc. Il faisait tellement chaud que nous étions torse nu, It was so hot that we were shirtless ("bare torso"). However, in the expression tomber des nues, it is the plural of an old word for a cloud (un nuage in modern French). The meaning of the idiom is to be flabbergasted, stunned: Je suis tombé des nues quand j'ai appris la nouvelle, I was flabbergasted when I got the news.

filer literally means to spin (cotton, etc.), hence un fil a thread, a wire (remember passer un coup de fil, lesson 41 note 4). Used idiomatically, the verb means to leave, run off, etc. A very common expression is Il faut que je file, I have to dash.

In technical French, une espèce is a species. But in ordinary conversation, it means a sort/kind of. La galette est une espèce de crêpe au blé noir, The "galette" is a sort of buckwheat pancake. The word is also used in a familiar register, without the indefinite article and followed by an interjection (just like you little ... in English). Espèce d'idiot ! You little idiot! (Un voyou means a lout or ruffian, and, when applied to a child, a rascal.)

new_words.english: can't you see it's freezing cold

new_words.french: où tu vas à cette heure-ci
new_words.english: where are you going at this time of day
why do you want to know

new_words.french: ce ne sont pas tes oignons
on m'a demandé de passer à l'agence
new_words.english: I was asked to come by the branch

new_words.french: il paraît que mon compte est à découvert
new_words.english: it appears my account is overdrawn
will be there at the meeting

new_words.french: tu poses toutes ces questions pourquoi
new_words.english: why are you asking all these questions
you stick your nose into everything

new_words.french: tu ne sais pas qu'il ne faut pas être curieux
new_words.english: don't you know not to be curious

new_words.french: et se mêler des affaires des autres
new_words.english: and meddle in other people's business

new_words.french: je vous écoute depuis cinq minutes
new_words.english: I've been listening to you for five minutes

new_words.french: vous massacrez la langue de Molière
new_words.english: you're slaughtering the language of Molière

new_words.french: on ne dit pas il est arrivé quand
new_words.english: you don't say he arrived when
and what time are you leaving

new_words.french: que tu pars à quelle heure
pourquoi tu vas à la banque
new_words.english: why are you going to the bank

new_words.french: j'ai emprunté ta carte de crédit
je me suis acheté quelques petits trucs
new_words.english: I bought myself a few things

new_words.french: la curiosité est un vilain défaut

translate.question: Gérard est tombé des nues quand il a découvert que son compte en banque était à découvert.
answers: Gérard was stunned when he discovered that his account was overdrawn.

translate.question: Où est-ce que vous avez appris le français ? Et comment ?
answers: Where did you learn French? And how?

translate.question: Je sais que je ne dois pas me mêler des affaires des autres, mais je suis quand même curieux.
answers: I know that I'm not supposed to meddle in other people's business, but I'm still curious.

translate.question: Pourquoi êtes-vous si grognon aujourd'hui ? - Ce ne sont pas vos oignons.
answers: Why are you so grumpy today? - It's none of your business.

translate.question: Assez discuté, il faut qu'on file. - Quand est-il votre rendez-vous ?
answers: Enough talk, we need to go. - When is your appointment?

fill.question: Why didn't they write to you? - Why are you asking me all these questions. Pourquoi ne vous ont-ils pas écrit ? - Pourquoi me posez-vous toutes ces questions ?
fill.full_answer: Pourquoi ne vous ont-ils pas écrit ? - Pourquoi me posez-vous toutes ces questions ?

fill.question: He has been listening to us, without saying anything, for half an hour. - Curiosity killed the cat. Il nous écoute sans rien dire depuis une demi-heure. - La curiosité est un vilain défaut.
fill.full_answer: Il nous écoute sans rien dire depuis une demi-heure. - La curiosité est un vilain défaut.

fill.question: What do you mean, you borrowed 1,000 euros from your best friend? - Mind your own business. Comment ça, tu as emprunté mille euros à ton meilleur ami ? - Occupe-toi de tes oignons.
fill.full_answer: Comment ça, tu as emprunté mille euros à ton meilleur ami ? - Occupe-toi de tes oignons.

fill.question: How did you two get here yesterday? And at what time did you leave? Comment êtes-vous venus hier, vous deux ? Et à quelle heure êtes-vous partis ?
fill.full_answer: Comment êtes-vous venus hier, vous deux ? Et à quelle heure êtes-vous partis ?

fill.question: Does that satisfy you as an answer or do you need more explanations? Est-ce que ça vous va comme réponse ou vous faut-il plus d'explications ?
fill.full_answer: Est-ce que ça vous va comme réponse ou vous faut-il plus d'explications ?

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Molière, the stage name of Jean-Baptiste Poquelin, is one of France's greatest playwrights, who lived from around 1622 to 1673. In works such as Tartuffe (The Imposter), Le Misanthrope (The Misanthropist) and L'Avare (The Miser), he skewered pretention, power and hypocrisy with biting satire and brilliant wit. His influence on the language is such that French is often referred to figuratively as la langue de Molière (for comparison, English is la langue de Shakespeare). Much of the language you will be learning in these last sixteen lessons is idiomatic and colloquial. By now, you should be able not only to detect these registers but also to recognise more or less instinctively when and where they are used. Nevertheless, you can count on us to continue informing and advising you. Amusez-vous ! (Enjoy!) Productive phase: 36th lesson

title: Un entretien avec un chasseur de têtes

dialogue.french: Nous sommes à la recherche d'un responsable du commerce international
dialogue.english: We are looking for an international trade manager

dialogue.french: qui s'occupera plus particulièrement des pays voisins de la France.
dialogue.english: who will take care in particular of France's neighbouring countries.

dialogue.french: La Suisse est un des marchés que nous essayons de pénétrer depuis très longtemps.
dialogue.english: Switzerland is one of the markets that we have been trying to penetrate for a very long time.

dialogue.french: En fait, c'est le marché le plus difficile que nous ayons connu jusqu'à présent.
dialogue.english: In fact, it's the most difficult market we have experienced so far.

dialogue.french: La personne à qui nous confierons le poste sera quelqu'un d'exceptionnel -
dialogue.english: The person we will entrust the job to will be someone exceptional -

dialogue.french: une femme ou un homme dont les compétences techniques sont reconnues et dont nous pouvons être sûrs.
dialogue.english: a woman or a man whose technical skills are acknowledged and of whom we can be sure.

dialogue.french: Nous n'avons pas encore finalisé le profil de ce poste ni le périmètre dont le nouveau directeur sera responsable
dialogue.english: We haven't yet finalised the profile of this position nor the scope for which the new director will be responsible

dialogue.french: car cela dépendra en partie de la personne que nous trouverons ou qui nous sera présentée par votre cabinet.
dialogue.english: because it will depend in part on who we find or who is introduced to us by your firm.

dialogue.french: Il se peut que ce soit quelqu'un en interne, qui travaille dans le groupe, mais ce n'est pas la piste que nous privilégions pour l'instant.
dialogue.english: It may be someone in-house, working in the group, but that is not the avenue that we favour at the moment.

dialogue.french: En tout état de cause, il ou elle aura une excellente expression écrite et orale,
dialogue.english: In any case, he or she will have excellent written and oral expression,

dialogue.french: une parfaite connaissance des marchés français, suisse et belge et de leurs différentes langues,
dialogue.english: a perfect knowledge of the French, Swiss and Belgian markets and their different languages,

dialogue.french: ainsi que de la rigueur, une vision stratégique, et, surtout, l'esprit cartésien.
dialogue.english: as well as discipline, a strategic vision, and, above all, a rational mindset.

dialogue.french: C'est pour cette raison que nous avons besoin de vos services, dont on nous dit le plus grand bien.
dialogue.english: That's why we need your services, which are highly spoken of.

dialogue.french: Le recrutement en ligne a ses limites, dont vous êtes certainement au courant.
dialogue.english: Online recruitment has its limits, of which you are certainly aware.

dialogue.french: Je vous remercie de votre confiance, dont nous nous montrerons dignes, je vous le promets.
dialogue.english: I thank you for your confidence, which we will prove worthy of, I promise you.

dialogue.french: Nous saurons dénicher la personne dont vous avez besoin, celui ou celle qui correspondra au mieux à vos exigences.
dialogue.english: We'll track down the person you need, the one who best suits your requirements.

dialogue.french: Nous avons plusieurs possibilités, dont un Belge. Mais je pense connaître LE candidat qu'il vous faut.
dialogue.english: We have several possibilities, including a Belgian. But I think I know THE candidate you need.

dialogue.french: Il s'agit d'une femme ayant une grande expérience à l'export et dont les qualités sont reconnues par tous ses collègues.
dialogue.english: This is a woman with huge experience in export and whose qualities are recognized by all her colleagues.

dialogue.french: Je la connais personnellement. Je peux vous affirmer qu'elle pourra fidéliser les clients,
dialogue.english: I know her personally. I can tell you that she will be able to build customer loyalty,

dialogue.french: accroître les ventes et développer votre groupe, dont l'activité est en pleine croissance.
dialogue.english: increase sales and develop your group, whose business is growing strongly.

dialogue.french: Parmi ses nombreux atouts, elle est trilingue et, surtout, elle est suisse.
dialogue.english: Among her many assets, she is trilingual and, above all, she is Swiss.

dialogue.french: Il me la faut ! Qui est-ce, cette perle rare, et quand pouvez-vous me la présenter ?
dialogue.english: I need her! Who is this rare gem, and when can you introduce her to me?

dialogue.french: Rien de plus facile : elle est assise en face de vous en ce moment !
dialogue.english: Nothing could be easier: she's sitting in front of you right now!

notes.content: Remember that, in a relative clause, qui replaces the subject and que the object (lesson 49 section 3). Both can refer to either people or things. And whereas the relative can often be omitted in English (one of the markets we have been trying to penetrate), this is not the case in French (un des marchés que nous essayons de pénétrer). Qui can also replace the indirect object after a preposition: la personne à qui nous confierons le poste, the person to whom we will give the job.

The relative pronoun dont, seen in lesson 71 line 20, basically means of whom, whose or of which, in reference to the object of a relative clause: Je suis fier de cette jeune équipe → L'équipe dont je suis fier est jeune. The team of which I am proud is young. Dont can also be used, as in line 17, to translate including (or of which): Deux chambres d'hôtel, dont une avec une salle de bain, Two hotel rooms, one of which with a bathroom.

The idiomatic construction il se peut que (from pouvoir) indicates a possibility, like it may be that, etc. And because of the conditionality, the accompanying verb is in the subjunctive: Il se peut que je sois en retard car j'ai une réunion à midi, I may be late because I have a noon meeting.

une piste means a track or trail. Among the many other meanings are an airport runway (piste d'aéroport), a dance floor (piste de danse) or, for skiers, a run, slope (or... piste!). But the noun is often used figuratively, as in Il faut trouver une solution rapidement, et nous avons plusieurs pistes à explorer, We need to find a solution quickly and there are several avenues to explore. In short, une piste is something that leads somewhere!

The regular verb privilégier (the root of privilege) is a faux-ami, meaning to favour: Notre société privilégie une approche éthique, Our company favours an ethical approach. However, the past participle retains the idea of privileged or special: La France a une relation privilégiée avec plusieurs pays du sud, France has a special relationship with several southern countries. As always, the context will dictate the appropriate translation.

une cause, a cause, a reason, features in a number of expressions, notably à cause de, because of (lesson 18 line 6) and en tout état de cause ("in any state of cause"), in any case, anyway: En tout état de cause, vous devez prendre vos vacances avant la fin du mois de décembre, In any case, you must take your holidays before the end of December. The expression is synonymous with quoi qu'il en soit (lesson 81 note 7), and both are quite formal.

In many ways, French is more grammatically precise than English. Here is a good example: les marchés suisse et belge refers to the Swiss market and the German market, each considered individually, whereas les marchés suisses et belges implies several markets in both countries. In both cases, the English translation is the Swiss and German markets. In spoken French, there is no difference in pronunciation between the singular and plural forms, but the difference is obvious in the written language.

When penser means to think about, consider, it can be followed directly by an infinitive: Je pense acheter une voiture neuve, I'm thinking about buying a new car. Of course, if we repeat the pronoun, the verb that follows penser has to be conjugated: Je pense que je vais acheter une voiture neuve, but there is no difference in meaning between the two constructions. Another common verb that can be followed by the infinitive is croire, to believe: Je crois savoir que vous êtes avocat, I understand that you are a lawyer.

Here's another example of a verb that is formed from an adjective and can be tricky to translate: fidèle means faithful, so fidéliser ("make faithful"), means something along the lines of to develop/build loyalty: Nous devons fidéliser notre clientèle et analyser ses besoins, We need to develop our customers' loyalty and analyse their needs. As is often the case with this type of verb, there is no word-for-word translation. You need to identify the root word in order to find the right shade of meaning.

This is the personalised form of falloir (lesson 63 section 2) with the indirect object pronoun (me) and the direct object pronoun replacing the noun (here, la femme, line 18). So, for example, if the direct object is masculine plural (les atouts) and the indirect object pronoun is nous, the sentence is Il nous les faut, We must have them. When faced with a sentence containing multiple pronouns, always try to break it down into its component parts.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: René Descartes was a 17th century mathematician and philosopher who argued that knowledge can be achieved only through rational reasoning. His most famous formulation, Je pense, donc je suis (I think, therefore I am), is part of French culture, both highbrow and popular. Descartes' influence and his reputation for rational reasoning is such that the nation prides itself on being cartésien (Cartesian), that is to say dispassionate and logical. Productive phase: 37th lesson

dialogue.french: Mes chers collègues, j'ai la nette impression que la guerre froide vient de se réchauffer.
dialogue.english: Dear colleagues, I have the distinct impression that the cold war has just warmed up.

dialogue.french: Un agent secret travaillant pour les services de sécurité de nos ennemis
dialogue.english: A secret agent working for the security services of our enemies

dialogue.french: a été trouvé sans vie sur les marches du Trocadéro.
dialogue.english: was found lifeless on the steps of the Trocadero.

dialogue.french: Tu veux dire qu'on a descendu un de leurs espions ? C'est super !
dialogue.english: You mean we have taken out one of their spies? That's great!

dialogue.french: Pas si vite. L'histoire est plus compliquée qu'il ne paraît.
dialogue.english: Not so fast. The story's more complicated than it appears.

dialogue.french: L'homme qu'on a retrouvé mort était un agent double, un des nôtres,
dialogue.english: The man we found dead was a double agent, one of ours,

dialogue.french: qui avait obtenu des renseignements à propos d'une arme secrète redoutable,
dialogue.english: who had obtained information about a formidable secret weapon,

dialogue.french: capable de contaminer les réserves d'eau potable de nos plus grandes agglomérations.
dialogue.english: capable of contaminating the drinking water supplies of our biggest urban areas.

dialogue.french: Antoine était sur le point de nous transmettre ces informations vitales quand ...
dialogue.english: Antoine was about to pass us this vital information when ...

dialogue.french: C'est honteux ! Voilà quelqu'un qui a risqué sa peau en travaillant dans l'ombre, et nous étions incapables de le protéger.
dialogue.english: It's shameful! Here's someone who risked his life working in the shadows, and we were unable to protect him.

dialogue.french: Taisez-vous, Lucas ! Vous ne connaissez pas le fin mot de l'histoire. Antoine a été trahi par quelqu'un de chez nous.
dialogue.english: Shut up, Lucas! You don't know the real story. Antoine was betrayed by one of our people.

dialogue.french: Qui oserait faire une chose pareille ?
dialogue.english: Who would dare to do such a thing?

dialogue.french: C'est justement ce qu'on va essayer de savoir. Vous pouvez compter sur moi.
dialogue.english: That's exactly what we're going to try to find out. You can count on me.

dialogue.french: Je ne lâcherai pas l'enquête tant qu'on n'aura pas mis la main sur le tueur et le commanditaire du meurtre.
dialogue.english: I won't let go of the investigation until we've got our hands on the killer and the person who ordered the murder.

dialogue.french: Est-ce qu'on sait au moins comment est mort notre collègue ? Abattu ? Poignardé ?
dialogue.english: Do we at least know how our colleague died? Shot? Stabbed?

dialogue.french: Selon toute vraisemblance, il a été empoisonné.
dialogue.english: In all likelihood, he was poisoned.

dialogue.french: Sur le lieu du crime, il flottait comme une drôle d'odeur - on aurait dit des amandes amères.
dialogue.english: At the crime scene, there was a strange smell - it was like bitter almonds.

dialogue.french: Tout laisse à penser qu'il a bu quelque chose, probablement une tisane, avec son assassin, inconscient du danger qui le menaçait.
dialogue.english: Everything suggests that he drank something, probably a herbal tea, with his murderer, unaware of the danger that was threatening him.

dialogue.french: Il a disparu de la circulation, jusqu'à ce qu'on trouve le cadavre.
dialogue.english: He disappeared from circulation until we found the corpse.

dialogue.french: Avant de me lancer à la recherche du meurtrier, vous devez savoir que vous êtes tous des suspects.
dialogue.english: Before I start looking for the murderer, you should know that you are all suspects.

dialogue.french: Je veux que vous sachiez que je n'épargnerai personne dans mon enquête et que le coupable n'ira pas en prison.
dialogue.english: I want you to know that I will spare no one in my investigation and that the culprit won't go to jail.

dialogue.french: Comment ? Vous allez le laisser filer ?
dialogue.english: What? Are you going to let him get away?

dialogue.french: Surtout pas ! Mais les accidents malheureux sont si vite arrivés, vous savez...
dialogue.english: Certainly not! But unfortunate accidents happen so quickly, you know...

notes.content: Beware of words that are spelled the same in both languages. Although you may know the rules, it's so easy to confuse the English and French pronunciations. Thus assassin and suspect are pronounced [assassahn] and [sewspay], respectively.

Remember that descendre is one of those verbs that can used transitively and intransitively (lesson 70 section 2). Among its transitive meanings, based on the notion of to lower, is to shoot down or, in slang, to bump off.

Like its English cognate, super can be used as an (invariable) adjective - Elle est super, la nouvelle pharmacienne, The new pharmacist is great - and an intensifier, before an adjective: Elle est super sympa, She's super nice. The word can also be used as an exclamation: Super ! Excellent! Great, etc. Don't confuse it with another invariable adjective, superbe, superb, glorious, etc. Quelle journée superbe !, What a glorious day!

With some verbs, ne is used as an expletive, a word that fills out a sentence without adding to the meaning. This usage is often found in subjunctive constructions: Le mauvais temps a empêché qu'ils ne partent, The bad weather prevented them from leaving. More details in an upcoming lesson.

This is the pluperfect tense, formed with the imperfect of avoir (or être, depending on the verb) and the past participle. It is used in more or less the same way as its English counterpart: Elle était déjà partie quand je suis arrivé, She had already left when I arrived.

The irregular verb se taire means to be/fall silent or keep quiet: Quand je demande qui va payer, tout le monde se tait, When I ask who is going to pay, everyone keeps quiet. The imperative Taisez-vous (familiar: Tais-toi) means Be quiet or Shut up, depending on the context. The past participle (following the same pattern as plaire, plu, to please/pleased) is tu: Tout à coup, il s'est tu, Suddenly, he fell silent.

Don't confuse the feminine noun la fin, the end, with the adjective fin(e), meaning thin: Ajoutez une fine couche de chocolat, Add a thin layer of chocolate. By extension, it can also mean fine, delicate, high-class, etc. Notre magasin propose une gamme complète de vins fins, Our shop offers a full range of fine wines. Fin can also be used as an intensifier, meaning last and final: Ils habitent au fin fond de la campagne, They live deep in the countryside. Whence the meaning of the expression le fin mot de l'histoire, "the last word of the story," i.e. the real truth, the bottom line.

The adverb tant que means as long as: Tant que vous buvez avec modération, vous n'avez pas de soucis, As long as you drink in moderation, you have nothing to worry about. In some contexts, tant que can also mean until: Vous ne pouvez pas accéder à votre compte tant que vous n'avez pas payé, You can't access your account until you have paid. In this case, it has the same meaning as jusqu'à ce que: ... jusqu'à ce que Laurent soumette un document ... (see lesson 81 line 14).

Formed from vrai, true, and sembler, to seem, la vraisemblance means likelihood. It is generally used in the expression selon toute vraisemblance, in all likelihood/probability. The adjective vraisemblable therefore means likely: Il est très vraisemblable qu'elle perdra son travail, It's very likely that she will lose her job.
We know that drôle means funny (lesson 41 line 15). And, as in English, the underlying meaning can be strange, peculiar: C'est un drôle de type, ton ami, Your friend is a strange guy.

One way to avoid the subjunctive is to change the structure of the sentence. For example, Avant qu'il soit un espion, notre agent était comptable, Before he was a spy, our agent was an accountant can be recast as Avant de devenir espion, etc. We'll see more ways of getting around the subjunctive later on.

translate.question: Nous étions sur le point de sortir quand, tout à coup, j'ai aperçu un homme dans l'ombre.

translate.question: C'est un drôle de type, Arnaud. Il habite au fin fond de la campagne, sans voiture ni téléphone.

translate.question: C'est honteux : elle était si faible qu'on aurait dit un cadavre.

translate.question: Est-ce que tu pourrais me parler de ton expérience avec les élections, avant que je me lance, moi aussi ?

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: French uses the present tense for narrative and descriptive purposes when talking about past events, as Gaëtan's story shows. Known as le présent historique, or historical present, this usage is not confined to spoken or colloquial contexts, as is often the case in English (though both languages use the tense in news headlines). One of the reasons for the historical present - apart from bringing past events vividly to life - is to avoid le passé simple (also known as le prétérit), which is used almost exclusively in written prose, particularly literature. We will look briefly at the passé simple in the last set of lessons. Productive phase: 38th lesson

dialogue.french: Qui qu'il soit, où qu'il se cache, et quoi qu'il fasse pour s'échapper
dialogue.english: Whoever he is, wherever he's hiding, and whatever he does to escape,

dialogue.french: on le retrouvera, ce flingueur, quelle que soit sa vraie identité.
dialogue.english: we will find him, this gunman, whatever his true identity.

dialogue.french: Il fera sans doute une gaffe. Et, là, je lui mettrai personnellement trois balles dans le corps.
dialogue.english: He's bound to make a mistake. And then I will personally put three bullets in his body.

dialogue.french: Vous êtes avec moi, les gars ? Je ne veux pas faire le sale boulot tout seul.
dialogue.english: Are you with me, guys? I don't want to do the dirty work alone.

dialogue.french: Carrément ! Mais Gaëtan a quelque chose à nous dire, il me semble.
dialogue.english: Too right! But it seems to me that Gaëtan has something to tell us.

dialogue.french: C'est exact. Un mec vient me voir hier et me dit qu'il a un tuyau sur l'assassinat d'Antoine.
dialogue.english: That's right. A guy came to see me yesterday and told me he had a tip-off about Antoine's murder.

dialogue.french: Je crois qu'il bosse pour une boîte plus ou moins réglo qui revend des bagnoles d'occasion.
dialogue.english: I think he works for a more or less straight-up company that sells used cars.

dialogue.french: Je lui demande combien il veut et il me répond qu'il va me faire une fleur,
dialogue.english: I asked him how much he wanted and he told me he's going to do me a favour,

dialogue.french: qu'il ne veut pas de fric parce qu'Antoine était un de ses potes.
dialogue.english: that he didn't want any dosh because Antoine was one of his buddies.

dialogue.french: D'après lui, les services secrets ont démasqué notre homme et découvert son secret.
dialogue.english: According to him, the secret service unmasked our man and discovered his secret.

dialogue.french: Il était fichu, le pauvre Antoine. Il ne portait même pas de flingue pour se défendre.
dialogue.english: He was a goner, poor Antoine. He didn't even carry a gun to defend himself.

dialogue.french: Ce type avec qui tu as causé, il était comment ?
dialogue.english: The guy you chatted to, what was he like?

dialogue.french: Grand, costaud et mal fringué, pas très propre, avec une vraie gueule de bandit.
dialogue.english: Tall, strong and badly dressed, not very clean, with a real gangster's face.

dialogue.french: Ça se voyait qu'il n'était pas un enfant de chœur, si tu vois ce que je veux dire.
dialogue.english: You could tell he was no altar boy, if you see what I mean.

dialogue.french: C'est bizarre, tu ne trouves pas ? Pourquoi il n'est pas allé voir les flics directement ?
dialogue.english: It's weird, don't you think? Why didn't he go straight to the cops?

dialogue.french: Je suis d'accord que c'est une histoire dingue, et ce type est carrément louche,
dialogue.english: I agree that it's a crazy story, and this guy's totally shady,

dialogue.french: mais ça ne veut pas dire pour autant qu'il raconte des salades.
dialogue.english: yet that doesn't mean he's spinning a yarn.

dialogue.french: C'est clair, et c'est le seul renseignement plus ou moins fiable que nous ayons reçu jusqu'à présent,
dialogue.english: That's clear, and it's the only more or less reliable information that we've received so far,

dialogue.french: la meilleure piste que nous puissions suivre. Mais aussi, peut-être, le plus gros mensonge qui soit.
dialogue.english: the best lead we can follow. But also, perhaps, the biggest lie.

dialogue.french: N'empêche, il faut y aller carrément, coûte que coûte. La tâche est difficile, mais je m'en fiche.
dialogue.english: Still, we've got to go all the way, no matter what. The task is difficult, but I don't care.

dialogue.french: J'en ferai mon affaire, patron. Je suis l'unique personne qui connaisse notre indic.
dialogue.english: I'll make it my business, boss. I'm the only person who knows our informer.

dialogue.french: Si tu insistes, mais sois prudent. Il ne faut pas que tu boives une infusion avec lui !
dialogue.english: If you insist, but be careful. You mustn't drink herbal tea with him!

notes.content: Certain adverbs are always followed by the subjunctive. We have already seen quoi que (lesson 81 note 7); others include qui que, whoever, où que, wherever, and quel que, whatever (the latter agreeing in number and gender with the following noun).

This lesson is based around a number of common slang terms, which are explained in detail in lesson 91. For now, just read the translation of the dialogue, bearing in mind that many colloquial terms in French have no direct equivalents in English, and vice versa.

un gars is a familiar word, derived from un garçon, and is sometimes used like a lad. But the most common usage is as a synonym of un type, a guy (lesson 87 note 9), especially in the plural: Venez voir, les gars, Hey guys, come and look. A synonym is un mec: Ce mec n'a jamais lu un bouquin de sa vie, That guy's never read a book in his life. (Note how the use of the familiar un bouquin, seen in lesson 47 note 10, reinforces the familiar register of the sentence.) Un mec can also be used in the sense of boyfriend: Elle a quitté son mec, She's left her man.

Derived from un carré, a square, the adverb carrément means candidly, bluntly, etc.: Je lui ai dit carrément ce que je pensais de lui, I told him straight out what I thought of him. The word is widely used as an intensifier: Ce gars est carrément dingue, That's guy's totally nuts. (The "standard" word would be complètement, completely). Lastly, carrément can be used as an assertion expressing agreement or support: Tu aimes ce chanteur ? - Carrément ! Do you like that singer? - Totally!

une fleur, a flower (seen in lesson 41 line 1), is used in several familiar expressions where, as in English, it embodies something prime or excellent. Les deux comédiens sont dans la fleur de l'âge, The two actors are in the prime of life. Thus faire une fleur (à quelqu'un) means to do someone a good turn or favour. L'hôtel nous a fait une fleur, en nous donnant une suite au lieu d'une chambre double, The hotel did us a favour by giving us a suite instead of a double room.

In a familiar register, ficher (which, in standard French means to put on file) replaces standard verbs, particularly faire, meaning to do, to get up to, etc. Qu'est-ce qu'il fiche, Georges ? Il est en retard, What's Georges up to? He's late. The pronominal form, s'en fiche(r) (line 21), is usually found in expressions such as Je m'en fiche, I don't care. But the past participle, fichu, used with être, means to be finished, done for, etc. Si les joueurs entrent sur le terrain avec la peur au ventre, ils sont fichus, If the players are afraid when they take to the field, they're done for.

The familiar adjective dingue (see lesson 73 note 6) means crazy, and, as in English, can be both meliorative - On a passé une soirée dingue chez eux, We had a crazy evening at their house - and pejorative: Cette assurance nous coûte un fric dingue, That insurance is costing us a stupid amount of money. The noun un dingue means a nutcase.

The subjunctive is used in relative clauses with superlatives (le plus gros, le meilleur) and other expressions of uniqueness (le seul, l'unique). For example, C'est l'hiver le plus froid qu'on ait connu depuis longtemps, It's the coldest winter we have known in a while; or Tu es la seule personne qui puisse m'aider, You're the only person who can help me.
The imperative form of être is identical to the subjunctive. Il faut que vous soyez / tu sois là à midi, You must be there at noon; Soyez / Sois là à midi, Be there at noon.

translate.question: Êtes-vous avec moi, mes amis ? Je ne peux pas faire ce travail difficile tout seul.
answers: Are you with me, my friends? I can't do this tough job on my own.

translate.question: Il me semble que ce monsieur travaille dans une entreprise qui fait le commerce de voitures d'occasion.
answers: It seems to me that this gentleman works in a company dealing in used cars.

translate.question: À quoi ressemblait cet homme avec qui vous avez eu une conversation ?
answers: What did the man you had a conversation with look like?

translate.question: On lui a dit carrément de quitter son petit-ami parce qu'il était méchant avec elle, mais elle s'en fiche.
answers: We told her straight out to leave her boyfriend because he was being mean to her, but she didn't care.

translate.question: Il faut que vous soyez prêt à terminer la tâche, coûte que coûte, car il reste peu de temps.
answers: You have to be ready to finish the task at all costs, because there isn't much time left.

fill.question: You mustn't drink any alcohol. If not, you'll have a big problem with the cops. Il ne faut pas que vous buviez d'alcool. Sinon, vous aurez un gros problème avec les flics.
fill.full_answer: Il ne faut pas que vous buviez d'alcool. Sinon, vous aurez un gros problème avec les flics.

fill.question: Whoever they are and wherever they live, I don't care. They're spinning a yarn, I'm sure of it. Qui qu'ils soient, et où qu'ils habitent, je m'en fiche. Ils racontent des salades, j'en suis sûr.
fill.full_answer: Qui qu'ils soient, et où qu'ils habitent, je m'en fiche. Ils racontent des salades, j'en suis sûr.

fill.question: That's right. I asked him where they lived, but he didn't answer. - That's crazy! C'est exact. Je lui ai demandé où ils habitaient mais il ne m'a pas répondu. - C'est dingue !
fill.full_answer: C'est exact. Je lui ai demandé où ils habitaient mais il ne m'a pas répondu. - C'est dingue !

fill.question: Whatever you may think, I'm the only person who knows the whole truth and who knows their true identity. Quoi que vous pensiez, je suis la seule personne qui sache toute la vérité et qui connaisse leur vraie identité.
fill.full_answer: Quoi que vous pensiez, je suis la seule personne qui sache toute la vérité et qui connaisse leur vraie identité.

fill.question: It's obvious that they're no angels, if you get our meaning. Ça se voit que ce ne sont pas des enfants de chœur, si vous voyez ce que nous voulons dire.
fill.full_answer: Ça se voit que ce ne sont pas des enfants de chœur, si vous voyez ce que nous voulons dire.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Productive phase: 39th lesson

dialogue.french: Oh là là, pourquoi est-ce que tu fais cette tête ? Qu'est qu'il t'arrive, ma pauvre amie ?
dialogue.english: Oh dear, why are you pulling that long face? What's happened to you, my poor friend?

dialogue.french: Je devais partir hier aux sports d'hiver avec une bande de copains.
dialogue.english: I was supposed to leave for winter sports yesterday with a bunch of friends.

dialogue.french: On allait faire la fête - et peut être un peu de sport - dans un super chalet au pied des pistes.
dialogue.english: We were going to party -and maybe do a bit of sport- in a great chalet at the foot of the slopes.

dialogue.french: J'avais fait les valises, préparé mes affaires de ski et commandé un taxi pour cinq heures -
dialogue.english: I had packed my bags, prepared my ski gear and ordered a taxi for 5 a.m.

dialogue.french: il n'est pas question de faire la grasse matinée quand on est en vacances à la neige !
dialogue.english: out of the question to lie in when on a winter holidays!

dialogue.french: Je quittais la maison quand j'ai reçu un message du bureau disant que ma présence était demandée.
dialogue.english: I was leaving the house when I received a message from the office saying that my presence was requested.

dialogue.french: J'avais fait le point avec mes collègues la veille, mais ils m'ont quand même fait venir aux aurores.
dialogue.english: I had updated my colleagues the day before, but they still made me come in at the crack of dawn.

dialogue.french: Du coup, j'ai dû faire une croix sur les vacances. Voilà pourquoi je fais la tête.
dialogue.english: As a result, I had to forgo the holidays. That's why I'm pulling a long face.

dialogue.french: Mais à vrai dire, ça me soulage un petit peu aussi car je devais partir avec Gaspard et Noé.
dialogue.english: But to tell the truth, I'm a little bit relieved, too, because I was supposed to leave with Gaspard and Noé.

dialogue.french: Partir avec ces deux-là, ensemble ? Il faut le faire !
dialogue.english: Leaving with those two, together? That's quite something!

dialogue.french: Gaspard s'est fait connaître pour son esprit de compétition bien développé.
dialogue.english: Gaspard made himself known for his well-developed competitive spirit.

dialogue.french: Pour ne pas dire esprit agressif ! Quand il est en compétition, celui-là, il ne fait pas de cadeau !
dialogue.english: Not to say aggressive spirit! When he's in competition, he gives people a tough time!

dialogue.french: D'ailleurs, il s'est fait un certain nombre d'ennemis à cause de ça.
dialogue.english: Moreover, he's made a number of enemies because of that.

dialogue.french: Mais il sait aussi se faire respecter. Gaspard est ami avec toutes les stars du sport, à qui il fait la bise.
dialogue.english: But he also knows how to make people respect him. Gaspard is friends with all the sports stars, with whom he's on kissing terms.

dialogue.french: Comment se fait-il qu'il soit devenu un si bon skieur ? Il a dû se faire aider par quelqu'un, non ?
dialogue.english: How is it that he's become such a good skier? He must have had help from someone, mustn't he?

dialogue.french: Non, il s'est fait tout seul, sans entraîneur, sans équipe et sans sponsor.
dialogue.english: No, he's self-made, without a coach, without a team and without a sponsor.

dialogue.french: Et Noé ?
dialogue.english: What about Noé?

dialogue.french: Il me fait de la peine, celui-là : c'est un désastre ambulant.
dialogue.english: I feel sorry for him: he's a walking disaster.

dialogue.french: On lui a souvent dit de faire gaffe mais il n'en fait qu'à sa tête.
dialogue.english: We've often told him to be careful but he does just as he pleases.

dialogue.french: L'année dernière, il n'a fait que quelques mètres avant de tomber et se fracturer les deux bras.
dialogue.english: Last year, he only went a few meters before he fell and broke both his arms.

dialogue.french: Il a dû se faire secourir par hélicoptère.
dialogue.english: He had to be rescued by helicopter.

dialogue.french: Cela dit, il a visiblement fait des progrès car l'année d'avant, il s'était cassé la cheville en descendant du train à la station de ski.
dialogue.english: Having said that, he has obviously made progress because the year before, he had broken his ankle getting off the train at the ski resort.

notes.content: The idiomatic expression faire la tête means to sulk, pull a long face, etc. Pourquoi est-ce qu'il me fait la tête ? Je ne lui ai rien fait ! Why's he sulking? I haven't done anything to him! You can also use an indirect object: Pourquoi est-ce que tu me fais la tête ? Why are pulling that face at me? See also note 10.

Derived from le gras, fat, blubber, etc. (and the origin of grease), the adjective gras(se), greasy, means fatty (and, by extension, fatty foods): Évitez de manger gras avant une activité physique, Avoid eating fatty foods before physical activity. The expression faire la grasse matinée, to lie in, sleep late: J'aime faire la grasse matinée le dimanche, I love lying in on Sundays.

faire un/le point is a navigational term meaning to take a precise bearing. That notion of precision is echoed in the expression's colloquial usage, where the basic meaning is to review or sum up a situation, to bring someone up to date, etc. Nous allons faire un point sur les orages dans le Midi, We're going to update you on the storms in the south of France. Logically, the idiom is often used in connection with news coverage.

une croix, a cross, is found in the idiomatic expression faire une croix sur, meaning to forgo, write off, say goodbye to, etc. (like a valedictory sign of the cross): On devra faire une croix sur les vacances à la montagne cette année, We'll have to forgo our mountain holiday this year. If the object noun has already been mentioned, it is replaced by dessus, on it, to avoid repetition: Si tu lui prêtes ta moto, tu peux faire une croix dessus, If you lend him your motorbike, you can kiss it goodbye.

Il faut le faire literally means It has to be done: Si vous voulez le faire, il faut le faire maintenant, If you want to do it, you've got to do it now. Used as an exclamation, however, the meaning is something like That takes some doing/beating! It can be admirative or ironic, depending on the context: Il a perdu non seulement ses clés mais aussi son portefeuille : il faut le faire !, He's lost not only his keys but also his wallet. That's quite an achievement. (In spoken French, the third-person pronoun is often dropped: Faut le faire !)

This "causative" form of the reflexive, se faire followed by an infinitive (lesson 82 line 17), tells us that the subject of the sentence is acted upon by something or someone. As often with the reflexive, the equivalent structure in English uses got: Il s'est fait aider par un journaliste pour écrire l'article, He got help from a journalist to write the article.

faire cadeau de means to give something away, often as a present: Mon copain m'a fait cadeau de cette ceinture, My boyfriend gave me this belt. In the negative, the expression is used figuratively with the meaning not to spare someone or let off lightly. L'équipe n'a pas fait de cadeau à ses adversaires, The team gave their opponents a hard time.

This is the inverted -hence, more elegant- form of comment ça se fait... ? (lesson 57 note 1). Here, however, the following verb is in the subjunctive because the speaker is expressing doubt or uncertainty.

The fishery term une gaffe, a gaff or hook, is used in the expression faire une gaffe, to make a blunder (lesson 88 line 3). However, faire gaffe (without a definite article) means to pay attention to or be wary of: Il faut faire gaffe parce que l'étagère est très lourde, We have to be careful because the shelf is very heavy. The verb is more commonly used as an imperative: Faites/Fais gaffe : c'est vachement lourd ! Be careful, it's damn heavy. Whatever the context, the expression is familiar, replacing the more formal faire attention (lesson 44 line 8).

The idiomatic expression n'en faire qu'à sa tête has nothing to do with sulking (note 1): it means to do exactly as one pleases/wants (listening only to what's in one's head, presumably): Quoi qu'on lui dise, elle n'en fera qu'à sa tête, Whatever we say to her, she'll do exactly as he wants.
