dialogue.french: Nous avions le nez dans le guidon, si bien que nous avons failli déclarer forfait au bout de six mois,
dialogue.english: We were totally immersed in what we were doing, so much so that we almost gave up after six months

dialogue.french: car les choses se sont compliquées. Je pensais alors que nous avions fait un faux départ.
dialogue.english: because things got complicated. I thought then that we had made a false start.

dialogue.french: J'étais même prêt à jeter l'éponge car le projet devenait de plus en plus coûteux.
dialogue.english: I was even ready to throw in the towel because the project was getting more and more expensive.

dialogue.french: Mais mes équipes ont refusé de changer de cap :
dialogue.english: But my teams refused to change course:

dialogue.french: chaque fois que je leur disais qu'il fallait tout arrêter, ils m'ont renvoyé dans les cordes.
dialogue.english: every time I told them they had to stop everything, they sent me packing.

dialogue.french: Je me rends compte maintenant qu'ils ont reculé pour mieux sauter.
dialogue.english: I realise now that they were taking a step back in order to better move forward.

dialogue.french: Vous avez soumis un prototype au Fonds régional de capital-investissement, n'est-ce pas ?
dialogue.english: You submitted a prototype to the Regional Private Equity Fund, didn't you?

dialogue.french: Oui. Mais il a botté en touche, sous prétexte que les fonds nécessaires n'étaient plus disponibles
dialogue.english: Yes. But they sidestepped the issue, on the pretext that the necessary funds were no longer available

dialogue.french: car un autre projet similaire était plus avancé que le nôtre.
dialogue.english: because another similar project was more advanced than ours.

dialogue.french: Ça alors ! Qu'avez-vous fait ?
dialogue.english: Gosh! What did you do?

dialogue.french: Rebelote. Nous avons modifié légèrement le dossier et l'avons présenté à nouveau,
dialogue.english: The same as before. We slightly modified the file and resubmitted it,

dialogue.french: mais cette fois-ci au Ministère de l'innovation.
dialogue.english: but this time to the Ministry of Innovation.

dialogue.french: Là, nous avons visé en plein dans le mille ! Le ministre ne pouvait pas rester sur le banc sans rien faire.
dialogue.english: There, we hit the bull's-eye! The minister couldn't sit on his hands and do nothing.

dialogue.french: Maintenant que nos résultats initiaux sont convaincants, la balle est dans leur camp.
dialogue.english: Now that our initial results are convincing, the ball is in their court.

dialogue.french: Et je suis sûr que nous allons tirer notre épingle du jeu. C'est la dernière ligne droite !
dialogue.english: And I'm sure we're going to hold our own. We're entering the home stretch!

dialogue.french: Alors ça, c'est formidable !
dialogue.english: That's really great!

dialogue.french: On a failli vous mettre KO, mais vous avez encaissé les coups. Chapeau !
dialogue.english: They almost knocked you out, but you rolled with the punches. Hats off to you!

notes.content: Like psychiatre and its derivatives (lesson 96), the initial p in words beginning with pn- is voiced. For example, to pronounce pneu, start by saying [puh-nuh], then gradually shorten the first vowel sound and run it into the p: [p-nuh]. The same applies, of course, for pneumatique.
une pousse is a shoot or sprout (des pousses de bambou, bean sprouts). But the term une jeune pousse was coined to describe a newly formed company, in order to avoid the loan word une start-up.

mettre au point has the same notion of precision as in faire le point (lesson 89 note 3). In photography, it means to focus. But in everyday French, the meaning is much broader: to develop, finalise, perfect, etc. and the translation changes with the context: Nous allons mettre au point de nouvelles formes d'énergie respectueuses de l'environnement, We are going to develop new, environmentally friendly forms of energy. Don't confuse the past tense (mis au point) with the feminine noun la mise au point, development, finalisation, etc.

un dispositif (from disposer, to arrange) literally means an arrangement or layout. By construction, it refers to a mechanism or device (un dispositif de pointage, a pointing device, "mouse"). But the word is widely used in the media and advertising as a blanket term for all kinds of plans, systems, arrangements, etc. Un dispositif d'assurance can be an insurance system, scheme, measure (or set of measures), etc.

The adjective pneumatique has the same meaning as its English cognate, but also means inflatable (un matelas pneumatique, an air bed). As a noun, it means a tyre and is almost always contracted to un pneu: Ma voiture n'est pas équipée de pneus neige, My car isn't fitted with snow tyres.

The intensifier si bien que indicates an outcome (so that, with the result that) described in a subordinate clause: Elle est arrivée en retard, si bien qu'elle a manqué le début du film, She arrived and, as a result, missed the beginning of the movie. (In an elevated register, tant et si bien que is used, with no change in meaning.)

failli, the past participle of faillir (the origin of to fail), is used with an infinitive to express the idea of very nearly: Nous avons failli partir avant la fin, We very nearly left before the end. If you hear failli, always listen out for the following verb to find out what was almost done!

Ça alors ! ("that then") is an exclamation of surprise or disbelief, like Goodness! or How about that! Used disapprovingly, it means Well, really! Finalement, il n'est pas venu à la fête, malgré ses promesses.- Ça, alors ! - In the end, he never came to the party, despite his promises. - Well really! Here, it's the tone rather than the context that determines the meaning. Don't confuse this exclamation with Alors ça ... generally followed by c'est and a qualifier, which means simply Now that ... Alors ça, c'est formidable ! Now that's really good.

la belote is a very popular French card game. Belote and Rebelote are the announcements made by a player taking two consecutive tricks. In colloquial speech, rebelote means something like here we go again, the same thing, etc. Deux heures pour aller au boulot le matin, et rebelote le soir pour rentrer, Two hours to get to work in the morning and the same in the evening to get home. (In "standard" French ... et la même chose le soir, etc.)

KO or K-O, pronounced [ka-oh], is a direct, untranslated import from the world of boxing. Le poids lourd tunisien a gagné par KO, The Tunisian heavyweight won by a knockout. Outside the ring, être KO means to be worn out, shattered: Après une journée entière avec les gamins, je suis KO, After a whole day with the kids, I'm exhausted.

The expression tirer un coup de chapeau à means to take one's hat off to, i.e. congratulate, acknowledge, etc. The expression is usually shortened simply to Chapeau ! or, in some cases, Chapeau bas ! (as if bowing deeply with hat in hand). While sometimes translatable by hats-off or hat-tip to, the actual choice of word or term will be context-driven (Well done!, Nice one!, etc.).

new_words.english: took a step back to move forward

new_words.french: visé en plein dans le mille

translate.question: Les étudiants ont mis au point un dispositif simple pour photographier des petits objets sans mise au point.
answers: The students developed a simple device for photographing small objects without focusing.

translate.question: J'ai le nez dans le guidon depuis des mois avec ce prototype car je ne veux pas qu'on me coiffe au poteau.
answers: I've had my nose to the grindstone for months with this prototype because I don't want to be pipped at the post.

translate.question: Nous ne déclarons pas forfait. Nous reculons pour mieux sauter car nous ne voulons pas brûler les étapes.
answers: We are not giving up. We're taking a step back because we don't want to cut corners.

translate.question: Ils ont placé la barre très haut mais je pense qu'ils vont réussir.
answers: They've set the bar very high but I think they're going to make it.

translate.question: Tu pensais que le candidat allait tirer son épingle du jeu, n'est-ce pas ?
answers: You thought the candidate was going to hold his own, didn't you?

fill.question: You know that I nearly got funding from the ministry, but the minister preferred to sidestep the issue. Tu sais, j'ai ... du ministère, mais ... a préféré ...
fill.full_answer: Tu sais, j'ai failli obtenir des fonds du ministère, mais le ministre a préféré botter en touche.

fill.question: Reza has lost his voice, so he has had to withdraw from this evening's concert. - What a pity! Reza ... voix, si bien qu'il a dû ... pour le concert de ce soir. - ... !
fill.full_answer: Reza a perdu sa voix, si bien qu'il a dû déclarer forfait pour le concert de ce soir. - Quel dommage !

fill.question: Because we refused to change course, the project manager sent us packing. Puisque nous ... , le responsable du projet nous a renvoyés dans les cordes.

blanks.correct: avons refusé de changer de cap

fill.full_answer: Puisque nous avons refusé de changer de cap, le responsable du projet nous a renvoyés dans les cordes.

fill.question: Well done, you were bang on target. Now the ball is in their court. Bravo, vous ... . Maintenant, ...

blanks.correct: la balle est dans leur camp

fill.full_answer: Bravo, vous avez visé dans le mille. Maintenant, la balle est dans leur camp.

fill.question: I'll be worn out if we continue working like this, without a safety net. Je vais être KO si nous continuons à travailler comme ça, sans filet.
fill.full_answer: Je vais être KO si nous continuons à travailler comme ça, sans filet.

extras.title: Productive phase
extras.content: Productive phase: 48th lesson

dialogue.french: Qu'est-ce qu'il y a, Driss ? Tu as l'air complètement KO. Ça ne va pas ?
dialogue.english: What's up? You look completely exhausted. Things not going well?

dialogue.french: Si si, mais tout est sens dessus dessous en ce moment. J'ai le nez dans le guidon depuis cinq jours parce que j'écris un article sur les Dauphins.
dialogue.english: Yes, things are a little upside down right now. I've been totally immersed in things for five days because I'm writing an article about the Dolphins.

dialogue.french: Cette équipe de foot dont le capitaine, Dulong, a disparu dans la nature en 1980 ?
dialogue.english: That football team whose captain, Dulong, disappeared into the wild in 1980?

dialogue.french: Je m'en souviens bien. C'est une histoire qui a défrayé la chronique à l'époque.
dialogue.english: I remember it well. It was a story that made the headlines at the time.

dialogue.french: C'est vrai, elle a fait couler beaucoup d'encre mais, d'après moi, on n'a jamais su toute la vérité.
dialogue.english: It's true, a lot has been written about it, but in my opinion, we never knew the whole truth.

dialogue.french: Je n'ai pas réussi à parler avec Dulong, ou, plutôt, il a exigé une somme faramineuse pour une interview.
dialogue.english: I didn't manage to talk to Dulong, or rather, he demanded a huge sum of money for an interview.

dialogue.french: Mais son ancien agent, Jacques Cartier, lui, a donné son accord tout de suite. Bingo !
dialogue.english: But his former agent, Jacques Cartier, agreed immediately. Bingo!

dialogue.french: Je pensais qu'il allait accepter du bout des lèvres, mais pas du tout ! Il en avait très envie. J'aurais dû m'en méfier, tant il était enthousiaste.
dialogue.english: I thought he would do it reluctantly but not at all! He really wanted to. He was so enthusiastic that I should have been suspicious.

dialogue.french: Je lui posais des questions vachement précises mais, à chaque fois, il bottait en touche. Il me racontait la vérité, soit. Mais sa vérité à lui !
dialogue.english: I asked him really precise questions, but each time he evaded the issue. He told me the truth, sure. But his truth!

dialogue.french: Tu aurais dû me demander de t'accompagner. "Au secours ! J'ai besoin d'aide !"
dialogue.english: You should have asked me to come with you. "Help! I need assistance!"

dialogue.french: Je serais venue tout de suite. Avec des mecs comme ça, c'est toujours le plus fort qui l'emporte.
dialogue.english: I would have come straight away. With guys like that, it's always the strongest who wins.

notes.content: Le passé simple: Le passé simple, or passé historique, is almost never used in everyday conversation or communication but is worth learning in order to be able to read novels, non-fiction and, in some cases, the media. Known in English as the past historic or the preterite, the tense has exactly the same function as le passé composé. 1.1 Formation: To form the past historic, remove the infinitive markers from the verb (-er, -ir, -re) and add the following endings: fermer: je fermai, tu fermas, il/elle ferma, nous fermâmes, vous fermâtes, ils/elles fermèrent. finir: je finis, tu finis, il/elle finit, nous finîmes, vous finîtes, ils/elles finirent. descendre: je descendis, tu descendis, il/elle descendit, nous descendîmes, vous descendîtes, ils/elles descendirent. Note that -ir and -re verbs share the same endings. Of course, there are several irregular forms. Here are the main verbs: être: je fus, tu fus, il/elle fut, nous fûmes, vous fûtes, ils/elles furent. avoir: j'eus, tu eus, il/elle eut, nous eûmes, vous eûtes, ils/elles eurent. faire: je fis, tu fis, il/elle fit, nous fîmes, vous fîtes, ils/elles firent. In all three cases, the initial vowel of the first and second persons plural takes a circumflex. However, don't confuse fut with fût, the (increasingly rare) imperfect subjunctive, or with the noun un fût, meaning, among other things, a barrel: Vin élevé en fût de chêne, Wine aged in oak barrels, used much more frequently. 1.2 Use: The passé simple is used to describe a completed, discrete action in the past that has no link to or is unimportant in the present: Elle fut choisie par le roi, qui l'épousa. She was chosen by the king, who married her. Toute la Cour fit un grand banquet. The entire court held a huge banquet. Le roi et sa reine eurent quatre enfants ensemble. The king and his queen had four children together. But despite the "historic" tag, the tense is not confined to history books. Nowadays, most young French speakers learn only the third person singular and plural; and most contemporary authors narrate using le passé composé and le présent historique (lesson 42 note 2), prompting some grammarians to predict the imminent demise of le passé simple. Even so, a basic knowledge of how to conjugate the main verbs in this tense is important. 1.3 Differences with English: All the actions described with le passé simple are completed, whereas repeated actions in the past require the imperfect. In English, though, we can use the past simple in both cases. Although the actions described with le passé simple are completed, they are not necessarily short.

Use of affixes: Throughout this course, we have seen how prefixes and suffixes modify the meanings of many verbs. Some of these are obvious because they are also used in English (for example, visiter → revisiter, to visit → to revisit). But English also uses phrasal verbs to the same effect. So chausser, déchausser is translated as to put on / take off shoes. This type of construction is not possible in French, which has no equivalent of these verb-preposition/adverb constructions. In addition, whereas English has a diglossic, or dual-context, vocabulary, consisting of formal Graeco-Latin and informal Anglo-Saxon words, such as regal and kingly (see lesson 7 section 4) French does not. So, for example, améliorer can be translated as to improve, to make better or to ameliorate. This is why it is so important to identify the correct register in French: knowing the meaning of a word is not necessarily the same as knowing how and where to use it. Here is an overview of some of the main prefixes and suffixes that we have seen so far. 2.1 Prefixes: a-/an- negation: moral → amoral; a- / an- movement or direction: doux → adoucir; dé / dés- undo, separate: boucher → déboucher; é- movement toward/away: loin → éloigner; em- movement into: ménage → emménager; en- change/movement to: dette → s'endetter; com- / con- together: pâtir → compassion; im-/in- negation: mobile → immobile; mal- bad: heureux → malheureux; mé- negative: se fier → se méfier; pro- movement toward: jeter → projeter; sous- under: payer → sous-payer; super- above: poser → superposer. 2.2 Suffixes: -aille result of an action: se battre → bataille; -ateur / -atrice person performing an action: présenter → présentateur / présentatrice; -âtre approximation, -ish: bleu → bleuâtre; -er/ère profession: boulange → boulanger / ère; -erie shop: boucher → boucherie; -ée result of an action: rentrer → la rentrée; -esse noun derived from adjective: gentil → gentillesse; -ier / ière profession: police → policier; -ment result of an action: abonner → abonnement; -ure result of an action: brûler → brûlure. Some of these suffixes do several jobs, but the context will almost always clarify the function if not provide the precise meaning. Spelling: In many cases, when a prefix is added, the initial consonant of the root word has to be doubled: a + léger → alléger. Likewise, the initial c of most words is doubled: a + calme → accalmie. Six consonants are never doubled (h, j, q, v, w, x), while five others double only rarely (b, d, g, k, z).

The past conditional - Conditionnel passé: The past conditional (I would have bought/come) is relatively straightforward. It comprises the conditional of the verb's usual past auxiliary (avoir, être), and its past participle: acheter: j'aurais acheté, tu aurais acheté, il/elle aurait acheté, nous aurions acheté, vous auriez acheté, ils/elles auraient acheté. venir: je serais venu/e, tu serais venu/e, il/elle serait venu/e, nous serions venus/-ues, vous seriez venus/-ues, ils/elles seraient venus/-ues. In a full conditional sentence (I would have ... if you had ... , etc.), the second verb is in the pluperfect (lesson 87 note 4), as in English: Je serais venu si tu m'avais invité. I would have come if you had invited me. The negative and interrogative forms follow the usual rules: Si j'avais su, je ne serais pas venu. I would never have come if I had known. Est-ce que tu serais / Serais-tu venu si tu avais su ? Would you have come if you had known? The past conditional, like the present, can be used to talk about a possible action: Aurais-tu oublié quelque chose ? Might you have forgotten something? More idiomatically, it is frequently used in the media when reporting on events that have not yet been confirmed. The English equivalent in this case is generally the present perfect tense with a conjectural adverb: L'accident aurait fait deux morts, The accident has apparently/reportedly caused two deaths. or, more idiomatically, Two people have apparently/reportedly died in the accident.

Gentilics: The rules on the formation of gentilics are quite complex, especially those governing towns and cities. Country adjectives are more straightforward. Rather than a long explanation, here is a list of some of the most common masculine and feminine forms, organised by their endings. Remember that, unlike English, gentilic adjectives do not take an initial capital letter but agree in number and gender with their noun. Suffix -ain/aine: l'Amérique → Américain(e) (American); la Jamaïque → Jamaïcain(e) (Jamaican); le Mexique → Mexicain(e) (Mexican). Suffix -ais/aise: l'Angleterre → Anglais(e) (English); l'Écosse → Écossais(e) (Scottish); la France → Français(e) (French); le Portugal → Portugais(s) (Portuguese). Suffix -ois/oise: la Chine → Chinois(e) (Chinese); le Danemark → Danois(e) (Danish); la Suède → Suédois(e) (Swedish). Suffix -ien/ienne: l'Australie → Australien(ne) (Australian); le Canada → Canadien(ne) (Canadian); l'Inde → Indien(ne) (Indian); l'Italie → Italien(ne) (Italian). The "official" gentilic for Les États-Unis is Étatsunien but this is rarely used. The most common gentilic for le Royaume-Uni is Britannique (British), although many French-speakers persist in using Anglais for all four UK countries.

Idioms (3): Sports: All the idioms in lesson 97 are related to sports. Some have direct (or almost direct) English equivalents, while others are not immediately obvious. avoir le nez dans le guidon (Cycling): "to have one's nose in the handlebars": to be totally immersed in what one is doing. avoir le vent en poupe (Sailing): "to have the wind on the stern": to have the wind in one's sails i.e. to be on a roll. botter en touche (Rugby): "to boot in touch": to kick into touch, to sidestep an issue. brûler les étapes (Cycling): "to burn steps/stages": to race ahead or to cut corners. changer de cap (Sailing): "to change cape": to change course or direction. coiffer au poteau (Horse racing): "to do hair at the post": to pip at the post. encaisser les coups (Boxing): "to encash blows": to roll with the punches. faire un parcours sans faute (Horse racing): "to do a course without fault": to do/get everything right. jeter l'éponge (Boxing): "to throw the sponge": to throw in the towel. placer la barre haut (Athletics): "to place the bar high": to set a high standard. reculer pour mieux sauter (Athletics): "to move back in order to jump better": to make a strategic withdrawal or to put off the inevitable. renvoyer dans les cordes (Boxing): "to send back in the ropes": to be thrown on the ropes, to put someone (back) in their place. tirer son épingle du jeu (Game): "to pull one's pin from the game": to emerge unscathed or to get out of a tight spot. viser plein dans le mille (Archery): "to aim fully in the thousand": to score a bull's-eye. Now look back at lesson 97 to review these expressions in context.

extras.title: Productive phase
extras.content: Productive phase: 49th lesson

dialogue.french: Tu aurais pu me faire signe ou me texter : c'est la moindre des choses, entre amis.
dialogue.english: You could have got in touch with me or texted me: that's the least of things, between friends.

dialogue.french: Ça fait des semaines que tu devais m'appeler mais tu n'as pas fait le moindre effort.
dialogue.english: It's been weeks since you were supposed to call me but you haven't made the slightest effort.

dialogue.french: Bon, passons. Quoi de neuf ? Qu'est-ce que tu deviens ?
dialogue.english: But let's move on. What's new? What have you been up to?

dialogue.french: Moi et mes associés travaillons depuis des mois sur quelque chose d'hyper-sensible.
dialogue.english: Me and my partners have been working for months on something hyper-sensitive.

dialogue.french: Quel genre de chose ? Pas le projet dont tu m'as parlé dernièrement ?
dialogue.english: What kind of thing? Not the project you told me about recently?

dialogue.french: Non, pas celui-là. Un autre, qui n'a rien à voir avec ça.
dialogue.english: No, not that one. Another one, which has nothing to do with it.

dialogue.french: Dès que nous aurons finalisé les derniers détails, et quand j'aurai complété le dossier,
dialogue.english: As soon as we have finalised the last details, and when I have completed the file,

dialogue.french: je pourrai t'en parler plus longuement. Pour l'instant, je ne peux te donner que les grandes lignes.
dialogue.english: I will be able to tell you about it at greater length. For now, I can only give you the broad outlines.

dialogue.french: Vas-y ! Je suis tout ouïe.
dialogue.english: Go ahead! I'm all ears.

dialogue.french: Te souviens-tu du concours Concevons la ville de demain dont tout le monde parle depuis un an ?
dialogue.english: Do you remember the Designing Tomorrow's City competition that everyone has been talking about for a year?

dialogue.french: Bien sûr que je m'en souviens ! Et alors ?
dialogue.english: Of course I remember! And so?

dialogue.french: Disons que nous avons un certain avantage par rapport à nos concurrents.
dialogue.english: Let's say we have a certain advantage over our competitors.

dialogue.french: Nous avons tout pensé dans les moindres détails, contrairement à eux.
dialogue.english: We've thought of everything, down to the last detail, unlike them.

dialogue.french: Certains ont misé sur l'informatique - les logiciels et les technologies numériques - ainsi que les solutions haut débit.
dialogue.english: Some have bet on IT - software and digital technologies - as well as broadband solutions.

dialogue.french: Ils envisagent l'avenir sans billets ni pièces de monnaie : que de l'argent virtuel.
dialogue.english: They see a future without notes and coins: just virtual money.

dialogue.french: D'autres portent leur attention sur une question et une seule : la circulation automobile.
dialogue.english: Others are focusing their attention on one issue and one issue only: car traffic.

dialogue.french: Ils pensent que la solution miracle serait d'interdire tous les véhicules, qu'ils soient particuliers ou professionnels,
dialogue.english: They believe that the miracle solution would be to ban all vehicles, whether private or professional,

dialogue.french: et d'obliger tout le monde de se déplacer autrement, ou à pied, ou par les transports collectifs.
dialogue.english: and to oblige everyone to travel by other means, either on foot or by public transport.

dialogue.french: Un des grands avantages, disent-ils, est qu'il n'y aura plus d'accidents de la route.
dialogue.english: One of the great advantages, they say, is that there will be no more road accidents.

dialogue.french: Et aussi, il n'y aura plus de PV !
dialogue.english: And also, there'll be no more parking tickets!

dialogue.french: J'ai le sentiment que tu ne prends pas la chose au sérieux.
dialogue.english: I get the feeling that you are not taking things seriously.

dialogue.french: Mais si, je te taquinais. Que propose ton équipe ?
dialogue.english: Yes I am, I was teasing you. What is your team proposing?

dialogue.french: Nous avons une solution radicale pour s'attaquer aussi bien à la circulation qu'à la pollution :
dialogue.english: We have a radical solution to tackle both traffic and pollution:

dialogue.french: nous allons construire les villes à la campagne, où l'air est plus pur.
dialogue.english: we're going to build cities in the countryside, where the air is purer.

notes.content: The adjective moindre, meaning less, lesser, least, is used as a comparative or a superlative and also in set expressions: Merci pour votre aide ! - C'est la moindre des choses, Thanks for your help! - That's the least I could do. Being an adjective, it agrees with the noun: les moindres détails (line 13).

The first person plural of passer (lesson 48 note 10) is used as a standalone imperative expression meaning Let's move onto something else, Let's say no more. Le travail n'est pas très bien fait mais, bon, passons ("let's pass"): The job hasn't been done well, but let's say no more about it.

Here is another idiomatic expression, with the verb devenir, to become. Qu'est-ce que tu deviens / vous devenez ? ("what are you becoming") How are you doing? / What have you been up to? If a noun is used instead of a pronoun, it comes directly after the verb: Que devient Richard ? What's Richard up to? In the past tense, however, the interrogative Qu'est-ce qu'il est devenu ? has the same meaning as its English equivalent: What became of him? As always, the context (and tense) are vital clues.

French uses voir (instead of faire) in the expression n'avoir rien à voir, have nothing to do with. Mon mari n'a rien à voir avec cette affaire, My husband has nothing to do with this business. The expression Ça n'a rien à voir, often shortened to Rien à voir, is a rejoinder, meaning That's beside the point / That's irrelevant.

Here is the futur antérieur, or future perfect, composed of the future auxiliary and the past participle. Similar in construction to its English equivalent, this tense describes a future event in terms of being completed. J'aurai pris ma décision avant ce soir, I will have taken my decision before this evening. Like the simple future, it is used after time conjunctions (quand, lorsque, dès que, etc.), whereas English uses the present perfect: Je viendrai te voir dès que j'aurai terminé cet exercice, I will come to see you as soon as I have finished this exercise. See lesson 56 section 2.

The feminine noun l'ouïe means hearing (it is related to l'oreille, ear, lesson 24 line 3). The word is often used in the expression être tout ouïe, to be all ears. The other four senses are la vue (sight), l'odorat (smell, masc.), le toucher (touch) and le goût (taste).

Like many French abstract nouns, l'informatique (fem.) covers a broad spectrum of meanings, in this case relating to information technology. We have seen it translated as computer, but it also refers to IT, computer science, data processing, etc. For example, France's data protection statute is called la loi Informatique et Liberté. (The word is also the origin of the technical English term informatics.)

Another way of saying either ... or is ou ... ou: Ou tu m'aimes ou tu ne m'aimes pas, Either you love me or you don't. While in English either can come before or after the noun / pronoun, in French it must come first. An alternative construction with exactly the same meaning is soit ... soit (lesson 64 line 7).

un PV is short for un procès-verbal. In a motoring context, the initialism means a parking ticket (PV de stationnement) or speeding ticket (PV pour vitesse excessive). The full term has a broader meaning, usually a statement or a report, and, in a business context, the minutes of a meeting (un procès-verbal de réunion). Despite the literal translation ("verbal trial"), the offender goes to court only if they don't pay l'amende (fem.), the fine.

We know aussi ... que as a comparative of equality or inequality: Il joue aussi bien/mal que moi, He plays as well/badly as me. However, the phrase aussi bien ... que ... is yet another way of saying both (lesson 57 note 2). À cause de la panne, nous avons tout perdu, aussi bien les données que les logiciels, Because of the breakdown, we lost everything, both the data and the software. Emphatic pronouns can be used instead of nouns: aussi bien lui que moi, both he and I.

new_words.english: what have you been up to

translate.question: Que devient Martin ? - Il est revenu en France il y a deux mois. Il aurait dû te faire signe.
answers: What is Martin up to? - He came back to France two months ago. He should have got in touch with you.

translate.question: Est-ce le livre dont vous m'avez parlé ? - Non, ça n'a rien à voir.
answers: Is this the book you told me about? - No, it has nothing to do with it.

translate.question: Elle ne s'est pas excusée mais, bon, passons. Nous avons d'autres chats à fouetter.
answers: She didn't apologise but, well, never mind. We've got bigger fish to fry.

translate.question: Raconte-moi ton histoire. Je suis tout ouïe. - Je t'en parlerai plus longuement un autre jour.
answers: Tell me your story. I'm all ears. - I'll tell you more about it another day.

translate.question: Quoi de neuf ? - Mes amis et moi sommes en train de finaliser les derniers détails de notre projet.
answers: What's new? - My friends and I are putting the final touches to our project.

fill.question: When we have taken our decision, we will keep you posted. Quand nous ... notre décision, nous vous tiendrons au courant.
fill.full_answer: Quand nous aurons pris notre décision, nous vous tiendrons au courant.

fill.question: We know each of our products down to the last detail, which gives an advantage over our competitors. Nous connaissons ... de nos produits dans ..., ce qui nous donne un avantage par rapport à nos concurrents.
fill.full_answer: Nous connaissons chacun de nos produits dans les moindres détails, ce qui nous donne un avantage par rapport à nos concurrents.

fill.question: We design software and digital solutions, both my husband and I. Nous ... et des solutions ..., aussi bien mon mari que moi.
fill.full_answer: Nous concevons des logiciels et des solutions numériques, aussi bien mon mari que moi.

fill.question: Either you love IT or you hate it: I have been telling you that for years. Ou vous aimez ... ou vous la détestez : ça fait des années que je vous le dis !
fill.full_answer: Ou vous aimez l'informatique ou vous la détestez : ça fait des années que je vous le dis !

fill.question: Will you have finished the paint work before this evening? I hope there will be no more delays. Est-ce que vous aurez terminé les travaux de peinture avant ce soir ? J'espère qu'il n'y aura plus de retard.
fill.full_answer: Est-ce que vous aurez terminé les travaux de peinture avant ce soir ? J'espère qu'il n'y aura plus de retard.

extras.title: Culture note
extras.content: Much of the vocabulary connected with advanced technology is imported directly from English. In information technology, however, French has succeeded in coining native words, such as un ordinateur and haut débit ("high flow," broadband), that have taken root. But some of these terms do not always have a one-on-one equivalent. For example, un logiciel is a software program or package, whereas the uncountable English noun "software" would usually be rendered by a plural. She designs software, Elle conçoit des logiciels. Another example is l'informatique (see note 7). As always, it is the context that will dictate not just the term but its grammatical form. Productive phase: 50th lesson

dialogue.french: Nous voici à la fin du livre, mais pas à la fin du voyage.
dialogue.english: Here we are at the end of the book, but not at the end of the journey.

dialogue.french: Pour assurer votre réussite, vous devez continuer à lire, parler, écouter et écrire le plus possible.
dialogue.english: To ensure your success, you must continue to read, speak, listen and write as much as possible.

dialogue.french: À propos de voyages, vous pouvez désormais profiter de vos connaissances en faisant un tour du monde francophone.
dialogue.english: Speaking of travel, you can now take advantage of what you've learned by going on a trip around the French-speaking world.

dialogue.french: Vous serez le bienvenu non seulement en Suisse, en Belgique et au Luxembourg mais partout où l'on parle le français.
dialogue.english: You will be welcome not only in Switzerland, Belgium and Luxembourg but everywhere where French is spoken.

dialogue.french: Bien sûr, vous trouverez des différences d'accent et de vocabulaire relatif à des spécificités culturelles.
dialogue.english: Of course, you will find differences in accent and vocabulary relating to specific cultural features.

dialogue.french: Au Canada, par exemple, vous pourrez faire vos emplettes chez un dépanneur, manger un chien-chaud ou encore conduire votre char.
dialogue.english: In Canada, for example, you will be able to do your shopping at a convenience store, eat a hot dog or drive your car.

dialogue.french: En Afrique francophone, "un alphabète" est une personne qui sait lire et écrire
dialogue.english: In French-speaking Africa, a "literate person" is one who can read and write.

dialogue.french: et "un taxieur" vous conduira à votre destination - mais pas avant d'avoir fait le plein à une "essencerie".
dialogue.english: and "a taxi driver" will drive you to your destination -but not before filling up at a "gas station."

dialogue.french: Des mots de diverses origines ont toujours enrichi notre vocabulaire : de "arobase" à "vasistas"
dialogue.english: Words from various origins have always enriched our vocabulary: from the "At" symbol to "fanlight"

dialogue.french: en passant par "le caoutchouc", "le goudron" ou encore "le paquebot"
dialogue.english: along with "rubber," "tar" or "ocean liner"

dialogue.french: le français est riche, varié et très accueillant.
dialogue.english: French is rich, varied and very welcoming.

dialogue.french: C'est aussi une langue précise et nuancée, forte de son héritage de langue diplomatique :
dialogue.english: It is also a precise and nuanced language, with a strong diplomatic heritage:

dialogue.french: "Ce qui n'est pas clair n'est pas français", disait un grand auteur.
dialogue.english: "What is not clear cannot be French," said a great author.

dialogue.french: Et puis il y a la Francophonie, un "pays sans frontières" où des centaines de millions de gens partagent leur passion pour cette belle langue.
dialogue.english: And then there is La Francophonie, a "country without borders" where hundreds of millions of people share their passion for this beautiful language.

dialogue.french: Maintenant, vous aussi, vous faites partie de cette communauté francophone,
dialogue.english: Now, you too are part of this French-speaking community,

dialogue.french: composée de femmes et d'hommes qui peuvent se permettre de déclarer :
dialogue.english: made up of women and men who can afford to declare:

dialogue.french: "Ma patrie, c'est la langue française !"
dialogue.english: "My homeland is the French language!"

notes.content: The suffix -phone can be used to form nouns and adjectives relating to speakers of particular languages. Thus the noun un francophone is a French-speaker, and the adjective means French-speaking: un pays francophone, a country where French is spoken. La Francophonie is the usual title of an organisation promoting the French language internationally. For more information, visit www.francophonie.org.

bienvenu ("well come") is an adjective: un accord bienvenu, a welcome agreement. As such, when used in greetings, it agrees in number and gender: Vous êtes le bienvenu and Soyez la bienvenue. But in the expression souhaiter la bienvenue, it is a noun and always takes a final "e": Je vais leur souhaiter la bienvenue, I'm going to welcome them. At the beginning of a sentence, spoken as a greeting, we always use bienvenue alone: Bienvenue dans notre nouvel appartement, Welcome to our new flat (see lesson 6).

Canadian French has a distinctive accent and vocabulary. For example, un dépanneur, meaning a repairman or mechanic in France, is a convenience store. Some words come from old French (un char, a car, derives from the word for a cart), while others are imported from America and then -importantly- translated: un chien-chaud, a hot dog!

French is an official language in nearly 30 countries on the African continent. Many words and expressions from these regions find their way into "official" French via mainstream dictionaries. Thus un alphabète, a literate person, is a logical back-formation from analphabète, illiterate; un taxieur is a local equivalent to un chauffeur de taxi while une essencerie (from l'essence, lesson 66) is a petrol station (rather than une station-service). Though not used in a formal register, these idioms nevertheless show that French is a living language.

Derived from an Arabic word, une arobase is the official term for the At symbol, used in email addresses. When spelling out an address, however, some French speakers say at rather than arobase. We insist on the latter (listen to the first sentence of the first exercise).

un vasistas is a fanlight or small window. The word comes from the German interrogative Was ist das?, What is it?, the gruff greeting supposedly shouted at thirsty French visitors knocking at the spy window of local taverns. Another "gallicised" loan word, this time from English, is un paquebot, an ocean liner, derived from the old term a packet boat. These, and dozens of other borrowings, show how the French vocabulary has been enriched through interactions with other languages and cultures over the centuries.

The adjectival phrase fort(e) de is widely used -usually at the beginning of a sentence- in descriptive writing to emphasise a strength, quality or advantage. Fort de son expérience, il a tout organisé, Drawing on a wealth of experience, he organised everything. The actual translation will depend as always on the context because, as with many idiomatic constructions, there is no single translation.

extras.title: Final message
extras.content: We hope you have enjoyed the course - but it isn't over yet! You still have to complete the Productive Phase, from lesson 51 through to lesson 100. And, of course, you should take every opportunity to read, listen to and practise French. As we said before, Bonne continuation!
